topics.title: Radiation Mechanism from an Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Radiation Mechanism from an Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Radiation Mechanism from an Antenna** is the physical process by which time-varying currents (or accelerating charges) on a conducting structure produce time-varying electromagnetic (EM) fields that detach from the source and propagate into free space at the speed of light, enabling wireless transmission of information.

Mathematically, any charge undergoing **acceleration** ($\frac{dv}{dt} \neq 0$) or any **time-varying current** ($\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \neq 0$) acts as a source of EM radiation governed by **Maxwell's equations** and the **retarded potential** formulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental postulate is that *a stationary or uniformly moving charge does NOT radiate*. Only **accelerating charges** produce radiation. This is the seed principle of the entire Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Click

Imagine you are holding one end of a long rope that is stretched tight, and the other end is tied to a wall:

- If you **gently move your hand side-to-side slowly**, the wave you create has a wavelength much larger than the rope's length, and the motion is "felt" along the rope as a quasi-static disturbance — energy stays local.
- If you **shake your hand rapidly** (high frequency), the wavelength becomes comparable to or smaller than the rope, and **pulses of energy break free and travel down the rope independently** — this is "radiation."

In an antenna, the **time-varying voltage source** is like your shaking hand. The electrons in the metal conductor are the "rope." When driven at RF frequencies, the EM fields detach and propagate as **transverse electromagnetic (TEM) waves** in free space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Boat in a Pond:**
> A boat moving at constant velocity on a still pond creates a smooth, localized wake (no radiation). But if the boat suddenly accelerates, decelerates, or oscillates (like a speedboat zig-zagging), it sends out circular waves that propagate independently across the entire pond. These propagating waves are analogous to **radiated EM energy**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Parameters

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** |
| $\eta_0$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω** |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** |
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** |
| $f$ | Operating frequency | **Hz** |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength ($\lambda = c/f$) | **m** |
| $k$ | Wavenumber ($2\pi/\lambda$) | **rad/m** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Hint:** KTU examiners LOVE testing $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s and $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω. These two constants appear in almost every antenna derivation.

## 1.4 The Two-Current Concept: Conduction & Displacement Current

Inside an antenna, the total current is the sum of two components:

$$\vec{J}_{total} = \vec{J}_{c} + \vec{J}_{d}$$

where $\vec{J}_{c} = \sigma \vec{E}$ is the **conduction current** (flow of free electrons in the metal) and $\vec{J}_{d} = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} = \varepsilon \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$ is the **displacement current** (time-varying electric field, even in the gap between conductors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Displacement Current Matters:** At the open end of a dipole antenna or inside a capacitor-like region, there are NO free electrons, so $\vec{J}_{c} = 0$. However, the time-varying $\vec{E}$ still produces $\vec{J}_{d} \neq 0$, ensuring the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \vec{J} + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ is satisfied and the antenna circuit "closes." **This was Maxwell's crowning insight (1865).**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do Antennas Radiate? — The Three-Step Logic

### Step 1: Source Excitation Creates Time-Varying Current
A high-frequency voltage source is connected across the feed terminals of the antenna. This drives a **time-varying current** $I(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ along the conductor.

### Step 2: Accelerating Electrons Produce Time-Varying Fields
The oscillating electrons are constantly changing direction (acceleration $\vec{a} = \frac{d^2\vec{r}}{dt^2} \neq 0$). Each electron radiates a small EM wave, and the **superposition** of waves from all electrons forms the antenna's radiated field.

### Step 3: Fields Detach and Propagate at Speed of Light
In the **near field**, fields are quasi-static and bound to the antenna. In the **far field** ($r \gg \lambda$, $r \gg 2D^2/\lambda$), the **radiation fields** (proportional to $1/r$) dominate and propagate as TEM waves with $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{r}$.

## 2.2 The Physical Picture of Radiation from a Half-Wave Dipole

Consider a center-fed dipole of length $L = \lambda/2$:

1. At $t = 0$, charge accumulates at the two ends → strong **$\vec{E}$ field** between arms (capacitive).
2. At $t = T/4$, charge flows rapidly toward the center → strong **current in the middle** (inductive).
3. This oscillating $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ field "kinks" in the conductor, and a small **transverse wave breaks off** into space.
4. The cycle repeats at frequency $f$, producing a continuous train of EM waves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Analogy:** Think of the antenna as a vibrating tuning fork. The fork itself is the "near field" (energy oscillates locally), but the sound you hear far away is the "far field" (energy radiated away).

## 2.3 Retarded Potentials — The Heart of Radiation Theory

Because information travels at finite speed $c$, the fields observed at distance $r$ and time $t$ depend on the source conditions at the **earlier (retarded) time**:

$$t' = t - \frac{r}{c}$$

The **scalar** and **vector retarded potentials** are:

$$V(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_0} \int \frac{\rho(\vec{r}', t_r)}{r'} \, dV'$$

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)}{r'} \, dV'$$

where $r' = \vert \vec{r} - \vec{r}' \vert$ and $t_r = t - r'/c$ is the retarded time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The $1/r$ Dependence:** In the far-field region, the $1/r'$ amplitude factor is the *geometric spreading* term. This is the signature of **radiation** — energy density falls as $1/r^2$, but integrated over a sphere of area $4\pi r^2$, the total radiated power is conserved.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$ | Faraday's Law (time-varying B induces E) | V/m |
| 2 | $\nabla \times \vec{H} = \vec{J}_c + \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t}$ | Ampère-Maxwell Law (source of EM waves) | A/m |
| 3 | $V(\vec{r},t) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \int \frac{\rho(\vec{r}', t_r)}{r'} dV'$ | Retarded scalar potential | V |
| 4 | $\vec{A}(\vec{r},t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\vec{J}(\vec{r}', t_r)}{r'} dV'$ | Retarded vector potential | Wb/m |
| 5 | $\vec{E} = -\nabla V - \frac{\partial \vec{A}}{\partial t}$ | E-field from potentials | V/m |
| 6 | $\vec{B} = \nabla \times \vec{A}$ | B-field from vector potential | T (Tesla) |
| 7 | $k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \frac{\omega}{c}$ | Wavenumber | rad/m |
| 8 | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = 120\pi$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | Ω |
| 9 | $E_\theta = j \eta_0 \frac{k I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$ | Hertzian dipole far-field (classic form) | V/m |
| 10 | $H_\phi = j \frac{k I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$ | Hertzian dipole far H-field | A/m |
| 11 | $P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{I_0 l}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Radiated power of Hertzian dipole | W |
| 12 | $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Radiation resistance of Hertzian dipole | Ω |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Equations 9–12 are the **Hertzian dipole** formulas — these are the most-tested results in Module 1. Memorize the $\sin\theta$ pattern (typical dipole donut shape) and the $(l/\lambda)^2$ dependence.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Radiation Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- |
| **5G/6G Base Stations** | Massive MIMO arrays rely on coherent radiation from hundreds of dipole/patch elements. |
| **Satellite Communication** | Horn and parabolic reflectors radiate highly directional beams using the same physical principle. |
| **Radar Systems** | Pulse radiation from a parabolic dish enables target detection via reflected echoes. |
| **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth** | PCB antennas on routers use miniaturized dipoles/IWAs (Inverted-F) — same physics, smaller scale. |
| **Medical (Hyperthermia)** | Phased arrays focus radiated EM energy to heat cancerous tissue non-invasively. |
| **RFID Tags** | Passive tags harvest radiated energy from the reader's field — radiation is the power source. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Radiated Fields from a Hertzian (Infinitesimal) Dipole

A **Hertzian dipole** is a thin, straight wire of length $l \ll \lambda$ carrying a uniform time-harmonic current $I_0 e^{j\omega t}$. We will derive its far-field components step-by-step.

### Step 1: Set Up the Vector Potential

For a thin wire carrying current along the $z$-axis, the volume element becomes $dV' = dz' \, da$. Assuming a filamentary current, $\vec{J}(\vec{r}') dV' \rightarrow I_0 \hat{z} \, dz'$. The vector potential in spherical coordinates reduces to:

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}) = \hat{z} \frac{\mu_0 I_0}{4\pi} \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} \frac{e^{-jkr'}}{r'} \, dz'$$

### Step 2: Apply the Far-Field Approximation

For an observation point in the far field ($r \gg l$):
- In the **amplitude** term: $r' \approx r$ (geometric approximation).
- In the **phase** term: $r' \approx r - z' \cos\theta$ (first-order expansion is kept because phase is sensitive).

So:

$$r' \approx r - z' \cos\theta$$

### Step 3: Integrate the Phase Term

$$\vec{A} \approx \hat{z} \frac{\mu_0 I_0 e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} e^{jkz' \cos\theta} \, dz'$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_{-l/2}^{l/2} e^{jkz' \cos\theta} \, dz' = \left[ \frac{e^{jkz'\cos\theta}}{jk\cos\theta} \right]_{-l/2}^{l/2}$$

For $l \ll \lambda$, $kl \ll 1$, so $e^{\pm jkl\cos\theta/2} \approx 1 \pm jkl\cos\theta/2$. The integral simplifies to $l$ (zero-order approximation).

### Step 4: Vector Potential in Spherical Coordinates

Substituting and converting $\hat{z} = \hat{r}\cos\theta - \hat{\theta}\sin\theta$:

$$\vec{A} \approx \hat{r} \frac{\mu_0 I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \cos\theta - \hat{\theta} \frac{\mu_0 I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$$

### Step 5: Compute the Magnetic Field $\vec{H}$

In the far field, only the $\hat{\phi}$ component survives (since $\nabla \times \vec{A}$ in spherical coordinates retains only the $\hat{\phi}$ term for a $1/r$ potential):

$$H_\phi = j \frac{k I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$$

### Step 6: Compute the Electric Field $\vec{E}$

Using the far-field relation $E_\theta = \eta_0 H_\phi$ (plane-wave condition $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{k}$):

$$E_\theta = j \eta_0 \frac{k I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$$

### Step 7: Time-Average Radiated Power Density (Poynting Vector)

$$\vec{W}_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \text{Re}\{\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*\} = \hat{r} \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{2\eta_0}$$

$$W_{avg} = \hat{r} \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi r}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta$$

### Step 8: Total Radiated Power (Integrate Over a Sphere)

$$P_{rad} = \oint_S \vec{W}_{avg} \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^\pi W_{avg} \, r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi}\right)^2 (2\pi) \int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta$$

Using the standard integral $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta \, d\theta = \frac{4}{3}$:

$$P_{rad} = \eta_0 \pi \left(\frac{k I_0 l}{4\pi}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{I_0 l}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{I_0 l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \approx 40\pi^2 \left(\frac{I_0 l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \text{ W}}$$

### Step 9: Radiation Resistance

By definition, $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_{rad}$. Solving:

$$R_{rad} = \frac{2P_{rad}}{I_0^2} = \frac{2 \eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \;\; \Omega$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Far-Field vs. Near-Field vs. Reactive Near-Field

| Region | Range Criterion | Dominant Field Type | Energy Behavior | KTU Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Reactive Near-Field** | $r < 0.62 \sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ | Stored $\vec{E}$ and $\vec{H}$ (quasi-static) | Oscillates back to antenna | Antenna matching, coupling |
| **Radiating Near-Field** | $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} < r < 2D^2/\lambda$ | Mixed (radiation + reactive) | Angular distribution forms | Holography, near-field probing |
| **Far-Field (Fraunhofer)** | $r > 2D^2/\lambda$ | Pure radiation ($\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{r}$) | Power density $\propto 1/r^2$ | Antenna pattern measurement |

where $D$ is the largest antenna dimension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $2D^2/\lambda$?** The far-field criterion comes from ensuring the maximum phase error across the antenna aperture is $\le \pi/8$ radians (22.5°), which is acceptable for most engineering applications.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Time-Varying Field Equation (Continuity Check)

Starting from Maxwell's equations in phasor form, eliminating $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{D}$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \vec{E} = j\omega\mu \vec{J} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\nabla \rho$$

The right-hand side is the **source term**. In source-free space ($\vec{J} = 0$, $\rho = 0$):

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\omega^2 \mu \varepsilon \vec{E} = -k^2 \vec{E}$$

This is the **Helmholtz wave equation**, whose spherical solutions are outgoing spherical waves of the form $\frac{e^{-jkr}}{r}$ — **the mathematical signature of radiation.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Antenna Radiation Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source: Time-Varying Voltage V(t)] --> B[Excites Conduction Current in Conductor]
    B --> C[Accelerating Electrons in Metal]
    C --> D[Time-Varying Charge Distribution]
    D --> E[Time-Varying Electric Field E]
    B --> F[Time-Varying Magnetic Field H]
    E --> G[Coupling via Maxwells Equations]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Near Field Region}
    H -->|r small| I[Reactive Energy Oscillation]
    H -->|r  2D2 over lambda| J[Far Field Region]
    J --> K[Radiation Fields Detach]
    K --> L[TEM Wave Propagates at c]
    L --> M[Energy Flows in Direction of Propagation]
    M --> N[Receiving Antenna Intercepts]
    N --> O[Signal Recovered at Load]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Field Structure Around a Hertzian Dipole

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hertzian Dipole: length l, current I0 along z-axis] --> B[Electric Field Lines]
    A --> C[Magnetic Field Lines]
    B --> D[Radial Component Er: 1 over r2]
    B --> E[Theta Component Etheta: 1 over r]
    C --> F[Phi Component Hphi: 1 over r]
    D --> G[Reactive Near Field]
    E --> H[Radiation Field]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Poynting Vector S: 1 over r2]
    I --> J[Radiated Power]
```

## 4.3 Process Diagram: Retarded Potential Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Source at time t-prime] --> P1[Field travels distance r at speed c]
    P1 --> P2[Observer at time t]
    S1 -.->|retarded time t minus r over c| P1
    P2 --> R[Radiation Field Detected]
    style S1 fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style P2 fill:#bbf,stroke:#333
    style R fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
```

## 4.4 Concept Map: Key Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Accelerating Charge] --> B[Time Varying Current]
    B --> C[Retarded Vector Potential A]
    C --> D[Far Field Etheta and Hphi]
    D --> E[Power Density S]
    E --> F[Total Radiated Power Prad]
    F --> G[Radiation Resistance Rrad]
    A --> H[Displacement Current]
    H --> I[Continuity of Current]
    I --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Mermaid is rendered as a structural/flow representation. For physical field-line diagrams, refer to your textbook (Balanis, *Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design*, 4th Ed., Chapter 4).
