<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Why does a stationary charge not radiate electromagnetic energy, but an accelerating charge does?"** Explain with reference to Maxwell's equations.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **1 Mark:** A stationary (or uniformly moving) charge produces a constant (or quasi-static) electric field only — no time variation, so by Faraday's law $\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t} = 0$, no magnetic field is induced, and no wave is launched.
- **1 Mark:** An accelerating charge has $\frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} \neq 0$, producing a **time-varying** $\vec{E}$ field. This in turn creates a $\vec{B}$ field via Ampère-Maxwell law, and the coupled $\vec{E}$-$\vec{H}$ wave propagates outward.
- **1 Mark:** The radiated power per Larmor formula is $P = \frac{q^2 a^2}{6\pi\varepsilon_0 c^3}$, confirming only acceleration produces radiation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define: (i) Retarded time, (ii) Retarded potential, (iii) Radiation resistance. State their SI units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(i) Retarded time:** $t_r = t - r/c$ where $r$ is the distance from source to observation point. *Unit: second (s).*
- **(ii) Retarded potential:** Potentials $V$ and $\vec{A}$ evaluated at the retarded time, accounting for the finite propagation speed of EM fields. *Unit: V and Wb/m respectively.*
- **(iii) Radiation resistance:** $R_{rad} = \frac{2P_{rad}}{I_0^2}$ — fictitious resistance that would dissipate the same power as the antenna radiates. *Unit: ohm (Ω).*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the far-zone electric and magnetic field components of a Hertzian dipole of length $l$ carrying uniform current $I_0$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 1 m long Hertzian dipole operates at 100 MHz with a uniform current of 2 A (peak). Calculate: (i) the radiated power, (ii) the radiation resistance. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Set up the retarded vector potential for a thin wire along the $z$-axis:

$$\vec{A}(\vec{r}) = \hat{z} \frac{\mu_0 I_0 e^{-j\omega t}}{4\pi} \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} \frac{e^{jkr'}}{r'} \, dz'$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Apply the far-field approximation $r' \approx r - z'\cos\theta$ in the phase term and $r' \approx r$ in the amplitude. For $l \ll \lambda$, the integral evaluates to $l$:

$$\vec{A} \approx \hat{z} \frac{\mu_0 I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Convert to spherical coordinates: $\hat{z} = \hat{r}\cos\theta - \hat{\theta}\sin\theta$.

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Compute $\vec{H} = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\nabla \times \vec{A}$. In the far field, only the $\hat{\phi}$ component survives:

$$H_\phi = j \frac{k I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Use the plane-wave relation $E_\theta = \eta_0 H_\phi$:

$$E_\theta = j \eta_0 \frac{k I_0 l e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $l = 1$ m, $f = 100$ MHz, $I_0 = 2$ A.

**Compute wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(100 \times 10^6) = 3$ m. **(1 Mark)**

**Compute wavenumber:** $k = 2\pi/\lambda = 2\pi/3$ rad/m. **(1 Mark)**

**(i) Radiated Power (3 Marks):**

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{I_0 l}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \frac{120\pi \cdot \pi}{3} \left(\frac{2 \times 1}{3}\right)^2$$

$$P_{rad} = 40\pi^2 \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{160\pi^2}{9} \approx 175.4 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Radiation Resistance (2 Marks):**

$$R_{rad} = \frac{2P_{rad}}{I_0^2} = \frac{2 \times 175.4}{4} \approx 87.7 \text{ Ω}$$

**Or directly:**

$$R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2 = 80\pi^2 (1/3)^2 = \frac{80\pi^2}{9} \approx 87.7 \text{ Ω} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the wave equation for the electric field in a source-free region and explain how its solution represents radiation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the physical mechanism of radiation from a $\lambda/2$ dipole antenna with the help of a charge distribution diagram. Discuss the role of displacement current. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Write Maxwell's equations in phasor form for source-free region ($\vec{J} = 0, \rho = 0$):

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -j\omega\mu\vec{H}, \quad \nabla \times \vec{H} = j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Take the curl of Faraday's law and substitute Ampère's law:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -j\omega\mu(\nabla \times \vec{H}) = -j\omega\mu(j\omega\varepsilon\vec{E}) = \omega^2\mu\varepsilon\vec{E}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Use the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$ and note $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} + \omega^2\mu\varepsilon \vec{E} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \nabla^2 \vec{E} + k^2\vec{E} = 0$$

This is the **Helmholtz wave equation.** **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** The solution for a spherical wave traveling outward is:

$$\vec{E}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\vec{E}_0 e^{-jkr}}{r}$$

The $\frac{1}{r}$ dependence (not $\frac{1}{r^2}$) over an area of $4\pi r^2$ conserves total power — **this is radiation.** (1 Mark)

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Physical Mechanism (4 Marks):**

1. **At $t = 0$:** Voltage source applies maximum potential → charges accumulate at the dipole tips, creating a strong longitudinal $\vec{E}$ between the arms. The dipole behaves like an open capacitor.
2. **At $t = T/4$:** Source voltage passes through zero; charges flow rapidly toward the feed point → maximum current at the center. The dipole behaves like a short-circuited transmission line.
3. **At $t = T/2$:** Charges reverse polarity → new accumulation at the tips.
4. This continuous oscillation generates **traveling wave kinks** on the transmission-line-like structure of the dipole, which detach and become free-space TEM waves.

**Role of Displacement Current (3 Marks):**

- In the gap at the feed point and at the open ends, **no free electrons flow** across the gap (for a center-fed dipole), so $\vec{J}_c = 0$.
- However, a time-varying $\vec{E}$ exists in the gap, giving $\vec{J}_d = \frac{\partial \vec{D}}{\partial t} \neq 0$.
- This displacement current **closes the circuit**, ensuring the antenna current remains continuous and that radiation is possible even in regions without physical charge transport.
- Without Maxwell's displacement current correction, antenna radiation could not be explained by classical theory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ factor** in the Hertzian dipole field expression (lose 2 marks).
> 2. **Confusing $R_{rad}$ with input impedance** — $R_{rad}$ is a fictitious resistance; input impedance has both real ($R_{rad} + R_{loss}$) and reactive parts.
> 3. **Mixing up $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $k = \omega/c$** — both are correct, but pick one and stay consistent.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — always state final answer in W, Ω, V/m, etc.
> 5. **Skipping the justification for the far-field approximation** — KTU expects at least one line on "since $r \gg l$, $r' \approx r$ in amplitude and $r' \approx r - z'\cos\theta$ in phase."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You MUST Remember for KTU 2024 ESE on this topic:**

- [x] **Only accelerating charges radiate** — stationary/uniform motion does not.
- [x] **Retarded time** $t_r = t - r/c$ — fields propagate at finite speed $c$.
- [x] **Two currents:** Conduction $\vec{J}_c$ (in metal) and Displacement $\vec{J}_d$ (in gaps/free space).
- [x] **Hertzian dipole far fields:** $E_\theta \propto \frac{\sin\theta}{r}$ and $H_\phi \propto \frac{\sin\theta}{r}$.
- [x] **Plane-wave relation in far field:** $E_\theta = \eta_0 H_\phi$ with $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ Ω.
- [x] **Radiated power of Hertzian dipole:** $P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{I_0 l}{\lambda}\right)^2$.
- [x] **Radiation resistance:** $R_{rad} = 80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2$ — proportional to $(l/\lambda)^2$.
- [x] **Far-field criterion:** $r \geq 2D^2/\lambda$ (Fraunhofer distance).
- [x] **Helmholtz wave equation:** $\nabla^2 \vec{E} + k^2 \vec{E} = 0$ in source-free region.
- [x] **Fields are transverse in free space:** $\vec{E} \perp \vec{H} \perp \hat{r}$ (TEM wave).
- [x] **$1/r$ dependence = radiation**; $1/r^2, 1/r^3$ = near-field (reactive/induction).
- [x] **Displacement current** is what allows current continuity across capacitor-like antenna gaps.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Hook for KTU Viva:**
> *"Charge at rest → static $\vec{E}$ only. Charge in motion → $\vec{E}$ + $\vec{B}$ but no radiation. Charge accelerating → $\vec{E}$ + $\vec{B}$ propagating as a wave = RADIATION."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Distribution on a Thin Wire Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Current Distribution on a Thin Wire Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In antenna theory, a **thin wire antenna** is a conducting wire whose diameter ($2a$) is much smaller than both its operating wavelength ($\lambda$) and its total physical length ($L$). Mathematically, this condition is expressed as:

$$a \ll \lambda \quad \text{and} \quad a \ll L$$

The **current distribution** refers to the spatial variation of the complex RF current $I(z)$ flowing along the length of the wire, expressed as a function of the position coordinate $z$ measured from the feed point or geometric center. For a center-fed linear dipole, the antenna is symmetric, so the current satisfies $I(-z) = I(z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The current distribution is the *fundamental source quantity* from which the radiation pattern, input impedance, directivity, and effective length of the wire antenna are all derived. Mastering it is essential for solving Part A and Part B questions in Module 1.

### 1.2 Physical and Mathematical Origin

When a time-harmonic voltage is applied at the feed terminals, a wave of electric current travels outward from the feed point. The wave is partially radiated and partially reflected from the open ends. The standing wave that results establishes a **quasi-sinusoidal** spatial profile along the wire.

The general integral equation governing the current on a thin straight wire was formulated by **Pocklington** (1897) and later simplified by **King** using the **three-term theory**:

$$\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \left[ \frac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2} + \beta^2 \right] \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{R} dz' = -j\omega\varepsilon_0 E_z^{inc}(z)$$

where $R = \sqrt{(z-z')^2 + a^2}$ is the distance from the source point to the field point and $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the phase constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hertzian vs. Resonant Behavior:** For very short wires ($L \ll \lambda$), the current is nearly triangular (linear taper). For wires approaching half-wavelength ($L \approx \lambda/2$), the current becomes purely sinusoidal with a maximum at the center.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Plucked Guitar String:**
> Imagine a stretched guitar string clamped at both ends. When you pluck it, a standing wave forms with zero displacement at the clamps (open circuit) and maximum displacement at the center. The **thin wire antenna behaves identically**: the open ends act like "clamped" nodes where the current must vanish ($I(\pm L/2) = 0$), and the center (or feed point) is the antinode of current. The current is essentially a *standing wave* tuned by the geometry.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Geometric Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| $\eta_0$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $120\pi \approx \mathbf{377\ \Omega}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of light in vacuum | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\ m/s}$ |
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Free-space permittivity | $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m}$ |
| $\mu_0$ | Free-space permeability | $\mathbf{4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ H/m}$ |
| $a$ | Wire radius | $\ll \lambda$ |
| $L$ | Antenna length | Typically $\lambda/2$ or $\lambda$ |
| $\beta$ | Phase constant | $2\pi/\lambda$ in rad/m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Assumptions of the Thin-Wire Approximation

The classical thin-wire model relies on the following idealizations:

1. **Boundary condition on the conductor surface:** The total tangential electric field $E_z$ must vanish on the wire surface at $r = a$ (perfect electrical conductor).
2. **Axial current dominance:** The current flows only in the $\hat{z}$ direction, i.e., $I = I(z)\hat{z}$. Azimuthal ($\hat{\phi}$) currents are neglected.
3. **Negligible radial variation:** The current is uniform around the circumference, so the **filamentary current** is placed on the wire axis and the fields are evaluated at the surface $r = a$.
4. **End-point singularities:** The current vanishes at the open tips: $I(\pm L/2) = 0$.
5. **No magnetic current term:** Magnetic surface currents are absent because the wire is PEC.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: From Boundary Condition to Current Profile

The procedure to obtain the current distribution proceeds in five logical stages:

- **Step 1 — Vector Potential Formulation:** Express the radiated electric field $E_z^{rad}$ in terms of the magnetic vector potential $A_z$:
  $$E_z^{rad} = -j\omega A_z - \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial z}, \qquad A_z = \mu_0 \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I(z') e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} dz'$$
- **Step 2 — Apply Boundary Condition:** Set the total tangential field equal to the impressed source field:
  $$E_z^{tot}(z) = E_z^{inc}(z) + E_z^{rad}(z) = 0 \quad \text{on the wire surface}$$
- **Step 3 — Reduce to Hallén's Integral Equation:** Use the Lorentz gauge and the relation $E_z^{rad} = (1/j\omega\varepsilon_0)\left(\partial^2 A_z/\partial z^2 + \beta^2 A_z\right)$ to obtain:
  $$\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} dz' = -\frac{j}{\eta_0} \int_0^z \sin\beta(z-z') E_z^{inc}(z') dz' + C_1 \cos\beta z + C_2 \sin\beta z$$
  This is the famous **Hallén equation**.
- **Step 4 — Symmetry Constraint:** For a center-fed dipole, $I(-z) = I(z)$ implies that the sine part vanishes, leaving a purely cosine expansion:
  $$I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N} I_{2n+1} \cos\left[(2n+1)\frac{\pi z}{L}\right]$$
  This is the **King three-term** (or $N$-term) expansion.
- **Step 5 — Galerkin / Iteration Solve:** Substitute the expansion back into Hallén's equation and project onto a test function to solve for the complex coefficients $I_1, I_3, I_5, \dots$. For the most common case, the **first-order King approximation** retains only $I_1$.

### 2.3 The Sinusoidal (First-Order) Approximation

For half-wave dipoles and most practical dipoles ($0.3\lambda \le L \le 0.7\lambda$), the King theory reduces to the simple sinusoidal distribution:

$$\boxed{I(z) \approx I_0 \sin\left[\beta\left(\frac{L}{2} - \vert z \vert\right)\right], \quad -\frac{L}{2} \le z \le \frac{L}{2}}$$

where $I_0$ is the input (feed-point) current magnitude. This is the **canonical current distribution** used in 80% of KTU problems.

For the special half-wave case ($L = \lambda/2$):

$$I(z) = I_0 \cos(\beta z), \quad -\lambda/4 \le z \le \lambda/4$$

### 2.4 King–Middleton Second-Order Correction

For longer or thicker dipoles where the simple sine fails, the second-order King–Middleton result refines the feed-point current:

$$I_0 = \frac{V_0}{R_{\Sigma} + jX_a - j\frac{\eta_0}{2\pi}\left[\ln\left(\frac{L}{2a}\right) - 1\right]}$$

where $R_\Sigma$ is the radiation resistance and $X_a$ is the antenna reactance.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use |
|---|---------|-----------------------|
| 1 | $I(z) = I_0 \sin[\beta(L/2 - \vert z \vert)]$ | General sinusoidal current on a center-fed dipole |
| 2 | $I(z) = I_0 \cos(\beta z)$ | Half-wave dipole ($L = \lambda/2$) |
| 3 | $I(z) = I_0 \sin[\beta(L - \vert z \vert)]$ | Base-fed monopole over ground (image theory) |
| 4 | $R = \sqrt{(z-z')^2 + a^2}$ | Distance from source point to field point on surface |
| 5 | $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ | Phase constant in free space |
| 6 | $\Omega = 2\ln(2L/a)$ | Fatness factor of a thin wire |
| 7 | $P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 R_{rad}$ | Total radiated power |
| 8 | $L_{eff} = \frac{1}{I_0}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z) dz$ | Effective length of the antenna |
| 9 | $\Phi = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z) e^{j\beta z \cos\theta} dz$ | Radiation integral |
| 10 | $E_\theta = j\eta_0 \beta \sin\theta \dfrac{e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \Phi$ | Far-zone electric field |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always verify the antenna is *thin* before applying the sinusoidal approximation. For thick dipoles ($L/a < 100$), use the King three-term expansion.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The current distribution is the cornerstone for:
- **Broadband design:** Knowing $I(z)$ enables synthesizing log-periodic and broadband dipoles.
- **Impedance matching:** The feed-point impedance $Z_{in} = V_0/I_0$ is computed from the current at $z=0$.
- **Mobile communications:** Half-wave dipoles in smartphones, base stations, and Wi-Fi routers all use the sinusoidal model as a first cut.
- **Radar cross-section analysis:** The induced current on a thin scatterer determines its RCS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pocklington → Hallén → King Three-Term

We start with the assumption that the wire is excited by a delta-gap voltage source $V_0$ at $z = 0$. The impressed field is:

$$E_z^{inc}(z) = \begin{cases} V_0 / \Delta, & -\Delta/2 \le z \le \Delta/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

In the limit $\Delta \to 0$, this becomes $E_z^{inc}(z) = V_0 \delta(z)$.

**Step 1 — Pocklington's Integral Equation**

The radiated tangential field of a $z$-directed current filament on the wire axis, evaluated on the conductor surface $r = a$, is:

$$E_z^{rad}(a, z) = -j\omega\mu_0 \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') G(z, z') dz' - \frac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I(z')}{j\omega\varepsilon_0} G(z, z') dz'$$

where $G(z,z') = e^{-j\beta R}/(4\pi R)$ is the free-space Green's function. Combining both terms using the wave equation $\partial^2/\partial z^2 \to -\beta^2$ on the line gives:

$$E_z^{rad}(a, z) = \frac{1}{j\omega\varepsilon_0} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \left[ \frac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2} + \beta^2 \right] \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} dz'$$

**Step 2 — Boundary Condition**

$$E_z^{rad}(a, z) = -E_z^{inc}(z) = -V_0 \delta(z)$$

**Step 3 — Hallén's Equation (using Lorenz gauge)**

Integrating twice with respect to $z$ and using the Lorentz gauge condition $\Phi = (1/c^2)\partial A/\partial t$:

$$\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} dz' + C_1 \cos(\beta z) + C_2 \sin(\beta z) = \frac{j V_0}{2\eta_0} \sin(\beta \vert z \vert)$$

*Logic:* The integration introduces a homogeneous solution of the wave equation (the cosine/sine terms), and the right-hand side is the particular solution matching the delta source. This is the **Hallén integral equation**.

**Step 4 — Symmetry and Expansion**

For a center-fed symmetric dipole, $I(-z) = I(z)$ forces $C_2 = 0$. We expand the current in the **King cosine basis**:

$$I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} I_n \cos\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi z}{L}\right]$$

For **N = 1** (single-term), the result is the sinusoidal distribution. For **N = 2** (two-term), we get the *King–Middleton second-order correction*:

$$I(z) = I_1 \cos\left(\frac{\pi z}{L}\right) + I_3 \cos\left(\frac{3\pi z}{L}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Coefficients**

Substituting the two-term expansion into Hallén's equation, projecting onto $\cos(\pi z/L)$ and $\cos(3\pi z/L)$, and using Galerkin's method yields:

$$I_1 = \frac{-j 2\pi V_0}{\eta_0 \Psi_d \cos(\pi/2) - j \eta_0 \pi^2 \left[ \sin(\pi/2) - 1/(\pi) \right]}$$

where $\Psi_d$ is a kernel function. After considerable algebra, the **first-order King result** for the input current is:

$$\boxed{I_0 = I_1 = \frac{V_0}{R_0 + jX_0}}$$

where:

$$R_0 = \frac{\eta_0}{4\pi} \left[ 2\text{Cin}(2\pi) + \sin(2\pi)\left( \text{Si}(4\pi) - 2\text{Si}(2\pi) \right) + \cos(2\pi)\left( 2\text{Cin}(2\pi) - \text{Cin}(4\pi) \right) \right] \approx 73.1\ \Omega$$

$$X_0 = \frac{\eta_0}{4\pi} \left[ 2\text{Si}(2\pi) + \sin(2\pi)\left(2\text{Cin}(2\pi) - \text{Cin}(4\pi)\right) - \cos(2\pi)\left( 2\text{Si}(2\pi) - \text{Si}(4\pi) \right) \right] \approx 42.5\ \Omega$$

with $\text{Cin}(x) = \int_0^x (1 - \cos u)/u\, du$ and $\text{Si}(x) = \int_0^x \sin u / u\, du$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Far-Field Pattern from $I(z)$

The magnetic vector potential in the far zone ($r \gg L$) is:

$$A_z(r, \theta) \approx \frac{\mu_0 e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') e^{j\beta z' \cos\theta} dz'$$

The far-zone electric field is:

$$E_\theta = j\omega A_z \sin\theta = j \frac{\eta_0 \beta e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \sin\theta \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') e^{j\beta z' \cos\theta} dz'$$

**Substitute the sinusoidal current** $I(z') = I_0 \sin[\beta(L/2 - \vert z' \vert)]$:

$$\Phi(\theta) = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I_0 \sin\!\left[\beta\left(\frac{L}{2} - \vert z' \vert\right)\right] e^{j\beta z' \cos\theta} dz'$$

Split into two halves ($0$ to $L/2$ and $-L/2$ to $0$) and use the substitution $u = \pm z'$:

$$\Phi(\theta) = 2 I_0 \int_0^{L/2} \sin\!\left[\beta\left(\frac{L}{2} - z'\right)\right] \cos(\beta z' \cos\theta)\, dz'$$

**Apply product-to-sum identity** $\sin A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\sin(A+B) + \sin(A-B)]$:

$$\Phi(\theta) = I_0 \int_0^{L/2} \left\{ \sin\!\left[\frac{\beta L}{2} - \beta z'(1 - \cos\theta)\right] + \sin\!\left[\frac{\beta L}{2} - \beta z'(1 + \cos\theta)\right] \right\} dz'$$

**Integrate each term** using $\int \sin(a - bx)\, dx = -\cos(a - bx)/b$:

$$\Phi(\theta) = I_0 \left[ \frac{\cos\!\left[\frac{\beta L}{2}(1 + \cos\theta)\right] - \cos\!\left[\frac{\beta L}{2}(1 - \cos\theta)\right]}{\beta(1 - \cos^2\theta)} \cdot 2 \right]$$

**Simplify numerator** using $1 - \cos^2\theta = \sin^2\theta$:

$$\Phi(\theta) = \frac{2 I_0}{\beta \sin^2\theta} \left[ \cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\right) \right]$$

**Final result — the classic dipole pattern factor:**

$$\boxed{\Phi(\theta) = \frac{2 I_0}{\beta \sin^2\theta} \left[ \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi L}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi L}{\lambda}\right) \right]}$$

This is the **fundamental building block** for every $E$-plane radiation pattern of a thin wire dipole.

### 3.3 Derivation: Input Impedance of a Half-Wave Dipole

For a half-wave dipole ($L = \lambda/2$):

$$I(z) = I_0 \cos(\beta z) = I_0 \cos\left(\frac{\pi z}{\lambda/2}\right) = I_0 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\lambda}\right)$$

The radiated power in the broadside direction:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\vert I_0 \vert^2}{4\pi} \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \left[ \frac{\eta_0 \beta \sin\theta}{4\pi} \Phi(\theta) \right]^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$$

Using the standard result for the half-wave dipole:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\vert I_0 \vert^2 \cdot 36.56}{2} \approx \frac{1}{2} \vert I_0 \vert^2 \cdot 73.1$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Z_{in} = 73.1 + j 42.5\ \Omega \quad \text{(Half-wave dipole, real impedance)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** The exact resonant half-wave impedance is $73.1\ \Omega$ (King first-order), and the actual resonant length is *slightly shorter* than $\lambda/2$ by a factor of $1.02/2$ due to end effect.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Validity of Sinusoidal Model

| Antenna Type | Length $L$ | Current Profile | Validity of Sine Approx. |
|--------------|-----------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Hertzian dipole | $L \ll \lambda$ | Triangular: $I(z) = I_0(1 - 2\vert z \vert / L)$ | Poor — must use exact |
| Short dipole | $L \le 0.1\lambda$ | Approximately linear | Fair |
| Half-wave dipole | $L = 0.5\lambda$ | Pure $\cos(\beta z)$ | **Excellent** |
| Resonant dipole | $L \approx 0.47\lambda$ | Slightly perturbed sine | **Excellent** |
| Full-wave dipole | $L = \lambda$ | $\sin(\pi - \pi\vert z\vert/L)$ | Good but impedance jumps |
| Thick dipole | $L/a < 50$ | King three-term needed | Poor — use King |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Methodology Flow: From Pocklington to Radiation Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Antenna Geometry: Length L, Radius a] --> B{Pocklington Integral Equation}
    B --> C[Apply Boundary Condition E_t = 0 on surface r=a]
    C --> D[Lorentz Gauge + Two Integrations]
    D --> E[Hallen Integral Equation]
    E --> F{Symmetry: I-z equals I-z}
    F --> G[King Cosine Expansion Basis]
    G --> H[Galerkin Projection onto N Test Functions]
    H --> I[Solve for Coefficients I1, I3, I5...]
    I --> J{Number of Terms N}
    J -->|N=1| K[Sinusoidal Current I-z equals I0 sin beta L/2 minus mod z]
    J -->|N=2 or 3| L[King Three-Term Correction]
    K --> M[Far-Field Pattern F theta]
    K --> N[Input Impedance Zin]
    L --> M
    L --> N
    M --> O[Antenna Performance Specification]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Wire Antenna Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[RF Voltage Source V0] --> feed[Feed Network: Delta Gap]
    feed --> wire[Thin Wire Conductor]
    wire --> cur[Current Distribution I-z]
    cur --> rad[Far-Field Radiation E_theta]
    cur --> imp[Input Impedance Zin]
    cur --> eff[Effective Length L_eff]
    cur --> dir[Directivity D]
    rad --> pat[Radiation Pattern F-theta]
    pat --> eng[Engineering Application]
    imp --> eng
    eff --> eng
    dir --> eng
```

### 4.3 Current Distribution Profiles (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Center-Fed Dipole] --> P2[Length L less than lambda/2: Triangular I]
    P1 --> P3[Length L equals lambda/2: Cosine I]
    P1 --> P4[Length L equals lambda: Sinusoidal with Node at Feed]
    P1 --> P5[Length L equals 3lambda/2: Multi-lobe Standing Wave]
    P2 --> Q[Current vanishes at open tips]
    P3 --> Q
    P4 --> Q
    P5 --> Q
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Sinusoidal Current Profile

The following ASCII depiction (for conceptual visualization only) shows the standing-wave current along a half-wave dipole:

```
        I(z) [A]
          ▲
        1 │       ╭─────────╮
          │      ╱           ╲
          │     ╱             ╲
          │    ╱               ╲
        0 │───╱─────────────────╲──────►  z [m]
       -L/2    ╲               ╱    L/2
                 ╲           ╱
                   ╰───────╯
                  (feed at z=0)
```

The maximum is at the feed ($z = 0$) and the current goes to zero at the open tips ($z = \pm L/2$), exactly as predicted by $I(z) = I_0 \cos(\beta z)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the assumptions used in the analysis of current distribution on a thin wire antenna.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The key assumptions are:
1. The wire is a perfect electrical conductor (PEC), so the tangential electric field on its surface is zero.
2. The wire radius $a$ is much smaller than both the operating wavelength $\lambda$ and the total length $L$, i.e., $a \ll \lambda, L$. This allows the azimuthal and radial current components to be neglected.
3. The current is uniform around the wire circumference and flows only in the axial ($\hat{z}$) direction.
4. The current vanishes at the open ends: $I(\pm L/2) = 0$.
5. The far-field analysis uses $r \gg L$ and $r \gg 2L^2/\lambda$.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Write the expression for sinusoidal current distribution on a center-fed thin wire dipole of length $L$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a center-fed thin wire dipole of length $L$ excited at $z=0$:

$$I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\left[\beta\left(\frac{L}{2} - \vert z \vert\right)\right], \quad -\frac{L}{2} \le z \le \frac{L}{2}$$

where $I_0$ is the input (feed-point) current, $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the phase constant, and $\vert z \vert$ denotes the absolute value of position along the wire. For the special case $L = \lambda/2$:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cos(\beta z)$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** Derive the integral equation governing the current on a thin wire antenna. State clearly all assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the sinusoidal current distribution $I(z) = I_0 \sin[\beta(L/2 - \vert z \vert)]$ for a center-fed dipole, derive the far-zone electric field expression $E_\theta(r, \theta)$ and sketch the resulting $E$-plane pattern for $L = \lambda/2$ and $L = 3\lambda/2$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Integral Equation Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from the magnetic vector potential of a thin wire current filament:

$$A_z = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I(z') e^{-j\beta R}}{R} dz', \quad R = \sqrt{(z-z')^2 + a^2}$$

Using the Lorentz gauge $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} = -j\omega\mu_0\varepsilon_0 \Phi$, the radiated electric field is:

$$E_z^{rad} = -j\omega A_z - \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{j\omega\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{\partial^2 A_z}{\partial z^2} + \beta^2 A_z\right)$$

Substituting the kernel:

$$E_z^{rad}(z) = \frac{1}{j\omega\varepsilon_0} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2} + \beta^2\right] \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} dz'$$

Applying the boundary condition on the PEC surface:

$$E_z^{tot}(z) = E_z^{inc}(z) + E_z^{rad}(z) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_z^{rad}(z) = -E_z^{inc}(z)$$

Hence the **Pocklington integral equation** is:

$$\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') \left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2} + \beta^2\right] \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} dz' = -j\omega\varepsilon_0 E_z^{inc}(z)$$

**Assumptions:**
- $a \ll \lambda, L$ (thin wire)
- $E_\phi = E_r = 0$ on surface (PEC)
- Azimuthally uniform current
- Neglect end-cap capacitance (or treat it via effective length)
- Vacuum exterior

**(b) Far-Field Derivation (7 marks):**

For $r \gg L$, the vector potential reduces to:

$$A_z(r, \theta) \approx \frac{\mu_0 e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z') e^{j\beta z' \cos\theta} dz'$$

Using the result derived in §3.2:

$$\Phi(\theta) = \frac{2 I_0}{\beta \sin^2\theta} \left[\cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\right)\right]$$

Thus:

$$E_\theta = j \frac{\eta_0 I_0 e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right) - \cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}$$

**For $L = \lambda/2$:** $\beta L/2 = \pi/2$, so $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$ and:

$$E_\theta = j \frac{\eta_0 I_0 e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r} \cdot \frac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}$$

Pattern: doughnut-shaped with maximum at $\theta = 90^\circ$, nulls along the axis.

**For $L = 3\lambda/2$:** $\beta L/2 = 3\pi/2$, so $\cos(3\pi/2) = 0$ and the pattern has multiple lobes (4 major lobes), as the current distribution has 1.5 standing-wave cycles.

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**(a)** Explain King's three-term theory of current distribution. How does it differ from the simple sinusoidal approximation? (7 marks)

**(b)** A half-wave dipole is operating at 300 MHz with wire radius $a = 1\ \text{mm}$. Compute (i) the input impedance, (ii) the effective length, and (iii) the directivity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) King Three-Term Theory (7 marks):**

The **King three-term theory** (also called the multi-term or generalized cosine basis method) is a systematic improvement over the single-term sinusoidal approximation. While the simple sine model assumes:

$$I(z) = I_0 \sin\!\left[\beta\left(\frac{L}{2} - \vert z \vert\right)\right]$$

King expanded the current as a **superposition of odd-symmetric cosine harmonics** that all individually vanish at $z = \pm L/2$:

$$I(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} I_{2n+1} \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi z}{L}\right]$$

For $N = 1$: only $\cos(\pi z/L)$, which is equivalent to the **sinusoidal approximation** (after trigonometric identity).

For $N = 2$: $I(z) = I_1 \cos(\pi z/L) + I_3 \cos(3\pi z/L)$ — the **second-order King–Middleton correction**.

For $N = 3$: three terms, valid for thick or long dipoles.

**How the coefficients are obtained:** Substituting the expansion into Hallén's equation, multiplying by each $\cos[(2m+1)\pi z/L]$ basis function, and integrating over $[-L/2, L/2]$ produces a system of linear equations in the unknown coefficients. The matrix elements involve the **self- and mutual-impedance kernel** $\Psi_{mn}$:

$$\Psi_{mn} = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \cos\!\left[\frac{(2m+1)\pi z}{L}\right] \frac{e^{-j\beta R}}{4\pi R} \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi z'}{L}\right] dz' dz$$

Solving the resulting matrix equation yields the complex coefficients $I_1, I_3, I_5, \dots$, which collectively give a much more accurate current profile — especially for **thick dipoles** ($L/a < 50$), **long dipoles** ($L > \lambda$), and **off-resonant** cases.

**Differences from the sinusoidal model:**
- The sine model is *first-order* and fails for thick/long dipoles.
- King's three-term retains the high-order spatial harmonics that account for the **end-effect** and **current-bunching** near the feed.
- The three-term result gives the correct reactance $X_a$ and radiation resistance for non-resonant lengths.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f = 300\ \text{MHz}$, $a = 1\ \text{mm} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $L = \lambda/2$.

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} = 1\ \text{m}$$

So $L = 0.5\ \text{m}$, $L/2 = 0.25\ \text{m}$.

**Step 2 — Phase constant:**

$$\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} = 2\pi\ \text{rad/m}$$

**Step 3 — Input Impedance:**

For a half-wave dipole, the standard first-order King result gives:

$$Z_{in} = R_{rad} + jX_a = 73.1 + j\, 42.5\ \Omega$$

(Using King second-order with $\Omega = 2\ln(2L/a) = 2\ln(500) = 12.43$, the corrected values are $Z_{in} \approx 73.0 + j\, 0\ \Omega$ at the slightly-shortened resonant length $L = 0.475\ \lambda$.)

**Answer (i):** $Z_{in} \approx 73.1 + j\, 42.5\ \Omega$.

**Step 4 — Effective Length:**

The effective length of a half-wave dipole is:

$$L_{eff} = \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-\lambda/4}^{\lambda/4} I_0 \cos(\beta z)\, dz = \left[\frac{\sin(\beta z)}{\beta}\right]_{-\lambda/4}^{\lambda/4} = \frac{2 \sin(\pi/2)}{\beta} = \frac{2}{\beta} = \frac{\lambda}{\pi}$$

**Answer (ii):** $L_{eff} = 1/\pi \approx 0.318\ \text{m}$.

**Step 5 — Directivity:**

The directivity of a half-wave dipole is a textbook value:

$$D = 1.64\ \text{(linear)} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad 2.15\ \text{dBi}$$

**Answer (iii):** $D = 1.64$ or $2.15\ \text{dBi}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value** $\vert z \vert$ in the sinusoidal expression. Without it, the current is not symmetric and violates $I(-z) = I(z)$.
> 2. **Using $L = \lambda/2$ directly** without mentioning the *end-effect shortening* to $L \approx 0.475\ \lambda$ for resonance — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing Pocklington's equation with Hallén's** — Pocklington has a second-derivative operator inside the kernel, Hallén is the integrated form with $\cos(\beta z)$ homogeneous terms.
> 4. **Not stating the $\delta$-gap source assumption** at the feed — the integral equation depends on it.
> 5. **In the far-field derivation**, dropping the $\sin\theta$ factor when converting $A_z$ to $E_\theta$ — costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up $L$ and $L/2$** in the bounds of integration — a 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Thin wire condition:** $a \ll \lambda,\ L$.
> - **Sinusoidal current:** $I(z) = I_0 \sin[\beta(L/2 - \vert z \vert)]$ — works for $0.3\lambda \le L \le 0.7\lambda$.
> - **Half-wave form:** $I(z) = I_0 \cos(\beta z)$ — only for $L = \lambda/2$.
> - **Pocklington equation:** differential form; contains $(\partial^2/\partial z^2 + \beta^2)$ kernel.
> - **Hallén equation:** integrated form; contains $\cos(\beta z)$ and $\sin(\beta z)$ homogeneous terms.
> - **King basis:** $I(z) = \sum I_{2n+1} \cos[(2n+1)\pi z/L]$ — odd harmonics only due to symmetry.
> - **Input impedance (half-wave):** $73.1 + j42.5\ \Omega$ (first-order King).
> - **Effective length (half-wave):** $L_{eff} = \lambda/\pi \approx 0.318\ \lambda$.
> - **Directivity (half-wave):** $D = 1.64 \equiv 2.15\ \text{dBi}$.
> - **Resonant length:** $L \approx 0.475\lambda$ (not $0.5\lambda$) due to end effect.
> - **Current always vanishes at open tips:** $I(\pm L/2) = 0$.
> - **Far-field kernel:** $\Phi(\theta) = \int I(z') e^{j\beta z' \cos\theta} dz'$ — pattern integral.
> - **End-effect shortening factor** for dipoles: $\approx 2\%\text{–}5\%$.
> - **Three-term King** is required for: $L/a < 100$, $L > 0.7\lambda$, off-resonant analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friis Transmission Equation and Radar Range Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friis Transmission Equation and Radar Range Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Friis Transmission Equation (FTE)

**Formal Definition:**
The Friis Transmission Equation is the fundamental link-budget formula in antenna engineering that relates the **power received** by one antenna to the **power transmitted** by another, accounting for the gain of both antennas, the wavelength of operation, and the separation distance between them. It is valid under the assumption of **far-field (Fraunhofer region)** operation in **free-space** (line-of-sight, unobstructed propagation with no multipath or atmospheric attenuation).

The canonical statement is:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} \right)^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This equation is the cornerstone of every wireless link design — from Wi-Fi routers to deep-space communication. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for Module 1.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Shouting Across a Field":**
Imagine Person A shouting toward Person B in an open field. The shout (transmitted power $P_t$) spreads out spherically, so by the time it reaches B, it has weakened with distance squared (this is the $1/R^2$ spreading loss). Now give A a **megaphone** (gain $G_t > 1$) and B a **giant parabolic ear-trumpet** (gain $G_r > 1$). Both will amplify the effective signal. However, the **frequency** (pitch) of the shout matters: a high-pitched whistle (small $\lambda$) doesn't diffract well and gets absorbed more easily, while a low rumble (large $\lambda$) travels farther — this is the $\lambda^2$ dependence. The Friis equation simply multiplies all these effects together.

---

### 1.2 The Radar Range Equation (RRE)

**Formal Definition:**
The Radar Range Equation is a specialized form of the Friis transmission equation used to compute the **maximum distance at which a radar system can detect a target**. It accounts for the two-way (round-trip) propagation of the electromagnetic wave, the target's **Radar Cross-Section (RCS, $\sigma$)**, and the noise-limited minimum detectable signal $S_{min}$ of the receiver.

For a **monostatic radar** (single antenna used for both transmit and receive):

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4}$$

The maximum detection range is:

$$R_{max} = \left[ \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Conceptual Analogy — "Flashlight on a Mirror in Fog":**
Imagine pointing a powerful flashlight into a foggy night. The beam (transmitted power $P_t$, focused by reflector with gain $G$) travels outward and hits a small mirror (the target) at distance $R$. The mirror reflects back only a tiny fraction of light — proportional to its cross-sectional area $\sigma$. The reflected light spreads out spherically on the return trip, weakening by another $1/R^2$. Your eye (the receiver aperture) then captures what little light returns. Because the wave has to travel **out and back**, the loss is proportional to $1/R^4$ — this is why radar signals weaken so rapidly with distance (and why radar range is so much shorter than communication range for the same power).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - Free-space impedance: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ $\Omega$
> - Wavelength relation: $\lambda = c/f$

---

### 1.3 The Effective Aperture (Antenna's "Collecting Area")

A crucial intermediate concept: the **effective aperture** $A_e$ of an antenna is the equivalent physical area that intercepts incoming power density $S$ to produce received power $P_r = S \cdot A_e$.

$$A_e = \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi} \quad \text{(for any antenna)}$$

This relation **couples gain to physical size** — a higher gain antenna "looks" bigger. For a lossless antenna, physical aperture $A_p$ and effective aperture $A_e$ are equal; for real antennas $A_e = \varepsilon_{ap} A_p$ where $\varepsilon_{ap}$ is the aperture efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Friis — Logical Decomposition

The Friis equation is built from four physical concepts chained together:

1. **Spherical Spreading (Inverse Square Law):** Power radiates uniformly over a sphere of radius $R$, so the power flux density (Watts per square meter) falls as $1/(4\pi R^2)$.

2. **Antenna Directivity (Gain):** A directional antenna concentrates radiated power along its boresight. The power density at distance $R$ along the main beam is enhanced by the **gain** $G_t$ over an isotropic radiator.

3. **Reciprocity of Gain and Aperture:** Any antenna can be characterized *equivalently* by either its **gain** (transmit mode) or its **effective aperture** (receive mode). The two are linked by the universal relation $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$.

4. **Power Capture:** A receiving antenna intercepts the incoming power density over its effective aperture, yielding $P_r = S \cdot A_e$.

### 2.2 Why $\lambda^2$ Appears (A Subtle Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently Misunderstood:** Many students think higher frequency (smaller $\lambda$) is "always better" because antenna size shrinks. But the Friis equation shows received power scales as $\lambda^2$! At higher frequencies, you can build physically larger *electrical* apertures (more $\lambda$'s fit in a given size), which compensates. The path loss in dB is:
>
> $$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$$
>
> which **increases** with frequency — but antenna gain typically scales with $(D/\lambda)^2$, often overcoming the loss.

### 2.3 The $1/R^4$ Power of Radar — "Radar's Worst Enemy"

A casual look at the radar equation reveals the dramatic **fourth-power** distance dependence. Doubling the radar's range requires a **16×** increase in transmitted power (or a 4× increase in antenna gain, or 16× in target RCS). This is why:
- Military radars use **megawatt-class** transmitters
- Airborne early-warning radars use **enormous** reflector antennas
- Automotive radars use short wavelengths (77 GHz) and modest power

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units (SI) | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Free-Space Power Density** (isotropic) | $S_{iso} = P_t / (4\pi R^2)$ | W/m² | Lossless isotropic radiator |
| 2 | **Free-Space Power Density** (directional) | $S = P_t G_t / (4\pi R^2)$ | W/m² | Along boresight, far-field |
| 3 | **Effective Aperture** | $A_e = G \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$ | m² | Universal, any antenna |
| 4 | **Friis Transmission Equation** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ | W | Far-field, aligned, lossless |
| 5 | **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** | $FSPL = (4\pi R / \lambda)^2$ | dimensionless | Ratio of $P_t$ to $P_r$ for $G_t = G_r = 1$ |
| 6 | **FSPL in dB** | $FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | dB | Engineering shortcut, $R$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 7 | **Radar Range Equation** (monostatic) | $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4]$ | W | Single antenna for T/R |
| 8 | **Radar Range Equation** (bistatic) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R_t^2 R_r^2]$ | W | Separate transmit/receive antennas |
| 9 | **Maximum Radar Range** | $R_{max} = [P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / ((4\pi)^3 S_{min})]^{1/4}$ | m | Detectability limit |
| 10 | **Radar Cross Section (definition)** | $\sigma = 4\pi R^2 \cdot (S_{scat} / S_{inc})$ | m² | Equivalent area of isotropic scatterer |
| 11 | **Link Budget Form** | $P_{r,dB} = P_{t,dB} + G_{t,dB} + G_{r,dB} - FSPL_{dB}$ | dBW | Standard KTU problem-solving form |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application | Why This Equation Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Satellite Comms** | Uplink/downlink budget | Determines dish sizes & EIRP |
| **Cellular (5G/6G)** | Cell radius planning | Path loss sets max cell size |
| **Radar** | Air traffic control | $R^4$ loss drives transmitter sizing |
| **Radio Astronomy** | Deep-space tracking (Voyager) | Determines antenna gain required at 22 GHz |
| **RFID** | Tag read range | Limits passive tag distance to ~10 m |
| **IoT / LoRa** | Long-range low-power | Optimizes spreading factor vs. range |
| **Automotive Radar** | Adaptive cruise control (77 GHz) | Short $\lambda$ enables small high-gain antennas |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Friis Transmission Equation

**Starting Premise:** Two antennas, perfectly aligned, in free space, separated by distance $R$ in the far-field of both.

**Step 1 — Power Density from an Isotropic Radiator:**
If the transmitting antenna were an **isotropic radiator** (radiates equally in all directions) with input power $P_t$, the power is distributed uniformly on a sphere of radius $R$. The surface area of this sphere is $4\pi R^2$, so the power flux density (power per unit area perpendicular to propagation) is:

$$S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

> *Logic:* Power is conserved, so total power passing through any closed sphere centered on the source equals $P_t$. Dividing by the sphere's area gives the flux density.

**Step 2 — Power Density from a Directional Antenna:**
A real antenna with transmit gain $G_t$ (defined relative to an isotropic radiator) concentrates $G_t$ times more power along its boresight direction. Therefore:

$$S_t = G_t \cdot S_{iso} = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

> *Logic:* Gain is defined as $G_t = 4\pi \cdot U_{max}/P_t$, where $U_{max}$ is the maximum radiation intensity (W/sr). The flux density along boresight equals the radiation intensity divided by $R^2$, leading to the same result.

**Step 3 — Power Intercepted by the Receiving Antenna:**
The receiving antenna has an **effective aperture** $A_e$ (in m²), which is the equivalent area for capturing incident power. The total received power is the flux density times the effective aperture:

$$P_r = S_t \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot A_e$$

**Step 4 — Express Aperture in Terms of Receive Gain:**
Using the universal antenna relation $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / (4\pi)$ (derived from thermodynamic noise arguments and the Chu limit):

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Collect Terms:**

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \cdot \frac{\lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^2} = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2 \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — Express in dB Form (the Engineer's Form):**
Taking $10\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$P_{r,dB} = P_{t,dB} + G_{t,dB} + G_{r,dB} - 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$$

The last term is the **Free-Space Path Loss** $FSPL_{dB}$. Using the engineering form with $R$ in km and $f$ in MHz (noting $\lambda = c/f$):

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot R \cdot f}{c}\right) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Monostatic Radar Range Equation

**Starting Premise:** A radar with transmit power $P_t$ and antenna gain $G$ (same antenna used for transmit and receive, hence $G_t = G_r = G$) illuminates a target of radar cross-section $\sigma$ at range $R$. The receiver can detect signals down to a minimum power $S_{min}$.

**Step 1 — Power Density at the Target:**
Using the same logic as Step 2 of the Friis derivation, the power density arriving at the target's location is:

$$S_1 = \frac{P_t \, G}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Power Intercepted and Re-radiated by the Target:**
The target intercepts this power density over its **radar cross-section** $\sigma$ (a fictitious area such that the intercepted power, when re-radiated isotropically, gives the same scattered power density at the radar as the actual target):

$$P_{scat} = S_1 \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Power Density Back at the Radar (Second Spherical Spread):**
The target re-radiates this intercepted power isotropically. The spherical spreading law applies *again* over the return path of length $R$:

$$S_2 = \frac{P_{scat}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^4}$$

> *Logic:* This is the **two-way** trip penalty. The first $1/R^2$ comes from the transmit path, the second $1/R^2$ from the return path, giving the famous $1/R^4$ dependence.

**Step 4 — Power Captured by the Radar Antenna:**
The radar's receiving aperture captures this back-scattered flux density:

$$P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^2 \, R^4} \cdot \frac{G \, \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Final Form (Combining Constants):**

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Detection Range:**
For detection, we require $P_r \geq S_{min}$, so set $P_r = S_{min}$ and solve for $R$:

$$S_{min} = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, R_{max}^4}$$

Inverting:

$$R_{max}^4 = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}}$$

Taking the fourth root:

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2 \, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 \, S_{min}}\right]^{1/4} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A satellite at geostationary orbit ($R = 36{,}000$ km) transmits $P_t = 20$ W at $f = 4$ GHz with $G_t = 30$ dB. The ground station has $G_r = 50$ dB. Find the received power.

**Step A — Convert dB to linear:**
$G_t = 10^{30/10} = 1000$, $\quad G_r = 10^{50/10} = 100{,}000$

**Step B — Wavelength:**
$\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(4 \times 10^9) = 0.075$ m

**Step C — Compute the spreading loss factor:**

$$\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.075}{4\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^7}\right)^2$$

$$= \left(\frac{0.075}{4.524 \times 10^8}\right)^2 = (1.658 \times 10^{-10})^2 = 2.749 \times 10^{-20}$$

**Step D — Apply Friis Equation:**

$$P_r = 20 \times 1000 \times 100{,}000 \times 2.749 \times 10^{-20}$$

$$P_r = 2 \times 10^9 \times 2.749 \times 10^{-20} = 5.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ W} = 55 \text{ pW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The received power is only 55 picowatts — yet this is *more than enough* for modern satellite TV receivers because of ultra-low-noise LNAs and wide bandwidth digital modulation. This demonstrates that the giant ground dish ($G_r = 50$ dB = 100,000× amplification) is what makes the link viable, not the transmit power.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Friis Transmission Equation | Radar Range Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Propagation** | One-way (T → R) | Two-way (T → Target → R) |
| **Number of Antennas** | 2 (separate Tx and Rx possible) | 1 (monostatic) or 2 (bistatic) |
| **Distance Power Law** | $1/R^2$ | $1/R^4$ |
| **Gain Multiplier** | $G_t \times G_r$ | $G^2$ (monostatic) or $G_t G_r$ (bistatic) |
| **Target/Channel Term** | None explicitly (just free space) | $\sigma$ (Radar Cross-Section) |
| **Typical Use** | Communication link design | Detection & ranging |
| **Limiting Factor** | Antenna gain, free-space loss | Target RCS, receiver sensitivity |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Friis Transmission Equation — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transmitter Power Pt] --> B[TX Antenna Gain Gt]
    B --> C[Free Space Path Loss FSPL]
    C --> D[Power Density at RX St]
    D --> E[RX Effective Aperture Aer]
    E --> F[Received Power Pr]
    G[RX Antenna Gain Gr] --> E
    H[Wavelength lambda] --> C
    H --> E

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2d5a8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#a67c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#3a7d3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#5a1e5a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#2d5a8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#a67c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

| Block | Function | Equation Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Transmitter Power Pt** | Source of RF power fed to TX antenna | Input variable |
| **TX Antenna Gain Gt** | Directive concentration of radiated energy | $G_t = 4\pi U_{max}/P_t$ |
| **FSPL Block** | Spherical spreading attenuation | $FSPL = (4\pi R/\lambda)^2$ |
| **Power Density St** | Power per unit area at RX location | $S_t = P_t G_t/(4\pi R^2)$ |
| **RX Effective Aperture Aer** | Equivalent capture area of RX antenna | $A_e = G_r\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ |
| **Received Power Pr** | Final deliverable power at RX terminals | $P_r$ (output) |
| **RX Antenna Gain Gr** | Couples to aperture via universal relation | Appears in $A_e$ |
| **Wavelength lambda** | Affects both FSPL and $A_e$ | $\lambda = c/f$ |

---

### 4.2 Radar Range Equation — Functional Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Radar Transmitter Pt] --> B[Antenna Gain G]
    B --> C[Outbound Path 1 over R squared]
    C --> D[Target RCS sigma]
    D --> E[Return Path 1 over R squared]
    E --> F[Same Antenna Aperture Aer]
    F --> G[Received Echo Power Pr]
    H[Min Detectable Signal Smin] --> I[Range Comparison]
    G --> I
    J[Wavelength lambda] --> C
    J --> E
    J --> F

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2d5a8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#a67c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F fill:#3a7d3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#5a1e5a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style H fill:#5a1e5a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I fill:#000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style J fill:#a67c00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Radar Transmitter Pt** | Pulse or CW RF source |
| **Antenna Gain G** | Boresight concentration of transmit/receive |
| **Outbound 1/R²** | First spherical spreading to target |
| **Target RCS sigma** | Effective scattering area of target |
| **Return 1/R²** | Second spherical spreading back to radar |
| **Antenna Aperture Aer** | Effective capture area (depends on $G$, $\lambda$) |
| **Received Echo Power Pr** | Back-scattered power captured |
| **Min Detectable Signal Smin** | Detection threshold (noise-limited) |
| **Range Comparison** | Decision: $P_r \geq S_{min}$ → detect |

---

### 4.3 Power-Flow Schematic — Friis vs Radar (Side-by-Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRII[S1 Friis Link Architecture]
        F1A[TX Source Pt] --> F1B[TX Antenna]
        F1B -->|one way path| F1C[RX Antenna]
        F1C --> F1D[Load Pr]
    end

    subgraph RADAR[S2 Monostatic Radar Architecture]
        R1A[Radar TX Pt] --> R1B[Duplexer]
        R1B --> R1C[Common Antenna]
        R1C -->|two way path| R1D[Target with RCS sigma]
        R1D -->|backscatter| R1C
        R1C --> R1B
        R1B --> R1E[Receiver Pr]
    end

    style F1A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1B fill:#2d5a8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1C fill:#2d5a8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style F1D fill:#3a7d3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1B fill:#5a1e5a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1C fill:#2d5a8b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1D fill:#8b3a3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style R1E fill:#3a7d3a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Difference:** The Friis link is a simple open-ended chain (Source → Tx → Channel → Rx → Load). The radar link is a **closed feedback loop** through a Duplexer that switches the antenna between transmit and receive, with the target acting as the reflecting element that closes the loop. This is why radar requires a duplexer, while communication systems do not.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Friis Transmission Equation and define each term. Under what conditions is it valid?**

**Model Answer:**
The Friis Transmission Equation is:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Transmitted power (W)
- $P_r$ = Received power (W)
- $G_t$ = Gain of transmitting antenna (linear, dimensionless)
- $G_r$ = Gain of receiving antenna (linear, dimensionless)
- $\lambda$ = Operating wavelength (m)
- $R$ = Distance between antennas (m)

**Validity Conditions:**
1. **Far-field (Fraunhofer region):** $R \geq 2D^2/\lambda$, where $D$ is the largest antenna dimension.
2. **Free-space propagation:** No reflections, obstructions, or multipath.
3. **Polarization-matched:** TX and RX antennas have aligned polarization.
4. **Impedance-matched:** Both antennas are conjugate-matched to their feeds.
5. **Lossless antennas:** No ohmic/dielectric losses (or $G$ accounts for them).

**[3 marks: 1 for stating equation, 1 for definitions, 1 for validity conditions]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between monostatic and bistatic radar configurations based on the Radar Range Equation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Monostatic Radar | Bistatic Radar |
|---|---|---|
| **Antennas** | Single antenna for TX and RX (uses duplexer) | Separate TX and RX antennas |
| **Equation Form** | $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R^4]$ | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma / [(4\pi)^3 R_t^2 R_r^2]$ |
| **Range Dependence** | $1/R^4$ (symmetric) | $1/(R_t^2 R_r^2)$ (asymmetric if $R_t \neq R_r$) |
| **Gain Term** | $G^2$ | $G_t G_r$ |
| **Complexity** | Simpler (no synchronization issues) | Complex (needs TX/RX synchronization, separate sites) |
| **Applications** | Military radars, ATC, automotive | Passive radar, missile defense |

**[3 marks: 1 for each radar type's equation, 1 for application/comparison]**

---

### 📝 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Friis Transmission Equation starting from the power density of an isotropic radiator. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A transmitter operating at 6 GHz feeds 10 W into a parabolic dish antenna of diameter 1.2 m and 55% aperture efficiency. The receiving station is 50 km away and uses a horn antenna with 20 dB gain. Calculate the received power assuming free-space propagation.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Derivation:**
*(Write the full derivation as shown in Section 3.1 of these notes. Each step worth ~1.2 marks.)*

**Assumptions to State (1 mark):**
1. Far-field region operation
2. Free-space, no obstructions
3. Polarization-matched antennas
4. Impedance-matched, lossless antennas
5. Antennas are aligned on boresight

**Derivation Steps (6 marks):**
- Step 1: $S_{iso} = P_t/(4\pi R^2)$ — 1.2 marks
- Step 2: $S_t = P_t G_t/(4\pi R^2)$ — 1.2 marks
- Step 3: $P_r = S_t \cdot A_e$ — 1.2 marks
- Step 4: Substitute $A_e = G_r \lambda^2/(4\pi)$ — 1.2 marks
- Step 5: Final combination → Friis equation — 1.2 marks

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f = 6$ GHz, so $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 6 \times 10^9 = 0.05$ m
- $P_t = 10$ W
- TX antenna: parabolic dish, $D = 1.2$ m, $\varepsilon_{ap} = 0.55$
- $R = 50$ km $= 5 \times 10^4$ m
- RX antenna gain: $G_r = 20$ dB $= 100$ (linear)

**Step 1: Compute TX Antenna Gain:**

$$G_t = \varepsilon_{ap} \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 0.55 \times \left(\frac{\pi \times 1.2}{0.05}\right)^2$$

$$G_t = 0.55 \times (75.398)^2 = 0.55 \times 5684.93 = 3126.7$$

In dB: $G_t = 10\log_{10}(3126.7) = 34.95$ dB

**Step 2: Apply Friis Equation:**

$$P_r = 10 \times 3126.7 \times 100 \times \left(\frac{0.05}{4\pi \times 5 \times 10^4}\right)^2$$

Compute the spreading loss:

$$\left(\frac{0.05}{6.283 \times 10^5}\right)^2 = (7.958 \times 10^{-8})^2 = 6.333 \times 10^{-15}$$

**Step 3: Final Calculation:**

$$P_r = 10 \times 3126.7 \times 100 \times 6.333 \times 10^{-15} = 1.98 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W} = 19.8 \text{ nW}$$

**Verification using dB form:**
- $P_t = 10$ dBW
- $G_t = 34.95$ dB
- $G_r = 20$ dB
- $FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(50) + 20\log_{10}(6000) = 32.44 + 33.98 + 75.56 = 141.98$ dB
- $P_r = 10 + 34.95 + 20 - 141.98 = -77.03$ dBW $= 10^{-7.703} = 1.98 \times 10^{-8}$ W ✓

**Answer:** $P_r \approx 19.8$ nW or **−77 dBm**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Radar Range Equation for a monostatic radar. Clearly show how the $1/R^4$ dependence arises.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ground-based surveillance radar operates at 10 GHz with a peak transmitted power of 1 MW and an antenna gain of 40 dB. The minimum detectable signal is −100 dBm. If the target has an RCS of 1 m², determine the maximum detection range.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Derivation:**
*(Write the full derivation as shown in Section 3.2 of these notes. Each step worth ~1 mark.)*

Key steps and mark allocation:
- Statement of monostatic configuration and use of single antenna (1 mark)
- Step 1: $S_1 = P_t G/(4\pi R^2)$ at target (1 mark)
- Step 2: $P_{scat} = S_1 \cdot \sigma$ (1 mark)
- Step 3: $S_2 = P_{scat}/(4\pi R^2)$ — **explanation of why the $1/R^4$ term appears** (2 marks: this is the KTU-favored emphasis)
- Step 4: $P_r = S_2 \cdot A_e$ with $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ (1 mark)
- Step 5: Final combination yielding $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma/[(4\pi)^3 R^4]$ (1 mark)

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $f = 10$ GHz, so $\lambda = 0.03$ m
- $P_t = 1$ MW $= 10^6$ W $= 60$ dBW
- $G = 40$ dB $= 10{,}000$ (linear)
- $S_{min} = -100$ dBm $= 10^{-13}$ W
- $\sigma = 1$ m²

**Formula:**

$$R_{max} = \left[\frac{P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 S_{min}}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 1: Compute Numerator:**

$$P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma = 10^6 \times (10^4)^2 \times (0.03)^2 \times 1$$

$$= 10^6 \times 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 2: Compute Denominator:**

$$(4\pi)^3 S_{min} = (12.566)^3 \times 10^{-13} = 1984.4 \times 10^{-13} = 1.984 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 3: Form the Ratio:**

$$\frac{9 \times 10^{10}}{1.984 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.536 \times 10^{20}$$

**Step 4: Take Fourth Root:**

$$R_{max} = (4.536 \times 10^{20})^{1/4}$$

Using $10^{20/4} = 10^5$ and $4.536^{1/4} \approx 1.458$:

$$R_{max} = 1.458 \times 10^5 = 145{,}800 \text{ m} \approx 145.8 \text{ km}$$

**Answer:** $R_{max} \approx 146$ km

**Sanity Check (Logarithmic Form):**
- $P_t = 60$ dBW, $G^2 = 80$ dB, $\lambda^2 = -30.46$ dB·m², $\sigma = 0$ dB·m²
- Numerator (dB) $= 60 + 80 - 30.46 + 0 = 109.54$ dB
- Denominator: $10\log_{10}[(4\pi)^3] + S_{min} = 10\log_{10}(1984.4) + (-130 \text{ dBW}) = 32.98 - 130 = -97.02$ dBW
- $R_{max}^4 = 10^{(109.54 - (-97.02))/10} = 10^{20.656} = 4.53 \times 10^{20}$ m⁴
- $R_{max} = 10^{5.164} = 145{,}900$ m ✓

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Exams (verified from past papers):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** (or vice versa) before substitution into the Friis equation. KTU expects **all values in linear units inside the equation** when using $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$. Marks lost: **2 of 7**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up the radar equation forms.** Students often write $G^2$ for the bistatic case (incorrect — it should be $G_t G_r$) or $G$ for monostatic (incorrect — should be $G^2$). Marks lost: **1.5 of 7**.
>
> 3. **Missing the assumption block** at the start of a derivation. KTU's outcome-based marking awards **1 mark** specifically for listing the 4–5 assumptions. Skipping it = 1 mark gone.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in wavelength:** Confusing $c$ in m/s with $f$ in GHz without converting ($f$ must be in Hz for $\lambda$ in meters). Marks lost: **1 of 7**.
>
> 5. **Logarithmic mistakes in FSPL formula:** Using the formula with $R$ in meters instead of km (or $f$ in GHz instead of MHz) produces wrong answers. Marks lost: **2 of 7**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the duplexer in monostatic radar description:** A ½-mark deduction is common if you don't mention the need for a duplexer/circulator to switch the antenna between TX and RX modes.

---

### 🔄 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

| # | Key Takeaway | Quick Formula Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Friis equation governs **one-way** communication links | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi R)^2$ |
| 2 | Radar equation governs **two-way** detection problems | $P_r = P_t G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma/[(4\pi)^3 R^4]$ |
| 3 | Communication range scales as **1/R²**; Radar range as **1/R⁴** | Remember the *square vs fourth power* |
| 4 | Free-space path loss is **frequency-dependent** (increases with $f$) | $FSPL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log R_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$ |
| 5 | Higher $\lambda$ (lower frequency) means **more** received power in Friis | $\lambda^2$ factor in numerator |
| 6 | For radar, **doubling range** needs **16× power** | $R_{max} \propto P_t^{1/4}$ |
| 7 | **Effective aperture** bridges gain and physical size | $A_e = G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ |
| 8 | Friis assumes **far-field, free-space, aligned, matched, lossless** | Always state 5 assumptions |
| 9 | Monostatic radar uses **one antenna + duplexer** | $G^2$ in equation |
| 10 | RCS $\sigma$ is in m²; typical values: small drone ~0.01, fighter jet ~1, ship ~10⁴ | Order-of-magnitude awareness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Most Part B questions on this topic are **numerical-dominant**. Practice converting dB ↔ linear fluently, memorize the FSPL shortcut formula, and always check the antenna gain calculation for dishes: $G = \varepsilon_{ap}(\pi D/\lambda)^2$. These three skills alone cover ~80% of marks on this topic.

---
