topics.title: Retarded Potential Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Retarded Potential Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the rigorous framework of classical electrodynamics and antenna theory, the **Retarded Potential Concept** is a direct mathematical consequence of the **finite propagation speed of electromagnetic disturbances**, which travels at the speed of light in free space, denoted as **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**.

When the charge distribution $\rho(\mathbf{r}', t)$ and the current distribution $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t)$ within an antenna structure vary with time, the electromagnetic effect produced at an observation point $\mathbf{r}$ at time $t$ does **not** depend on the source configuration at the same instant $t$. Instead, it depends on the source configuration at an **earlier time**, known as the **retarded time** $t_r$, given by:

$$t_r = t - \frac{R}{c}$$

where $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert$ is the distance between the source point $\mathbf{r}'$ and the field point $\mathbf{r}$. This time delay $R/c$ accounts for the time taken by the electromagnetic wave to traverse the distance $R$.

The corresponding **retarded scalar potential** $V(\mathbf{r}, t)$ and **retarded vector potential** $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t)$ are formally defined as:

$$V(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \iiint_{V'} \frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$$

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \iiint_{V'} \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The retarded potential formulation is the foundational stepping-stone that connects time-varying source distributions to the radiated electromagnetic fields. It is the **starting point** for deriving the far-field radiation patterns of virtually all practical antennas (dipoles, loops, arrays).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a large circular swimming pool, and a friend at the center throws a stone into the water at exactly 12:00 noon. The ripples generated by the stone **do not** reach the edge instantaneously. They travel outward at the speed of surface waves, and you observe them at some later time, say 12:00:02 (depending on the pool radius and wave speed).

**You do not see the stone at 12:00:00; you see the "memory" of the stone at the earlier time when the wave started its journey.**

The electromagnetic version of this exact phenomenon is the **retarded potential concept**:

- The **stone entering the water** ≡ the time-varying current/charge in the antenna
- The **ripple traveling outward** ≡ the electromagnetic wave propagating at speed $c$
- **You at the pool's edge** ≡ the field observation point at distance $R$
- The **time delay of 2 seconds** ≡ the retardation time $R/c$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** If $R = 0$ (you are at the source), $t_r = t$ — no delay. If $R$ is large (far-field region), the delay becomes significant and cannot be ignored. This is precisely why KTU examiners love this topic — it captures a deep physical truth with simple-looking math.

---

### 1.3 Why Retardation? The Physical "Why"

Electromagnetic disturbances are not magical action-at-a-distance. They obey the **special theory of relativity**: no information can travel faster than $c$. The retarded potentials are the mathematically consistent solution of this relativistic constraint, expressed within the framework of Maxwell's equations and the **Lorentz gauge condition**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the Concept — Starting from Maxwell's Equations

The retarded potential derivation is a four-step logical chain. We must understand **why** each step exists:

**Step 1 — Maxwell's Equations (Time-Domain, Differential Form):**

Gauss's Law:
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Faraday's Law:
$$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t}$$

Ampère-Maxwell Law:
$$\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}$$

Gauss's Law for Magnetism:
$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Define the Magnetic Vector Potential $\mathbf{A}$:**

Since $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$ and the divergence of any curl is zero, we can write:

$$\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$$

This automatically satisfies $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$.

**Step 3 — Define the Electric Scalar Potential $V$:**

Substituting $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ into Faraday's law:

$$\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \mathbf{A}) = -\nabla \times \left(\frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}\right)$$

Rearranging:
$$\nabla \times \left(\mathbf{E} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}\right) = 0$$

Any irrotational vector can be written as the gradient of a scalar. Therefore:

$$\mathbf{E} + \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t} = -\nabla V$$

This gives us the **fundamental field-potential relation**:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Lorentz Gauge Condition:**

Substituting into the inhomogeneous Maxwell's equations (Gauss and Ampère-Maxwell) and using the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} = -\partial \rho / \partial t$, we obtain the **Lorentz gauge condition**:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$$

This decouples the equations for $V$ and $\mathbf{A}$ into two **inhomogeneous wave equations**:

$$\nabla^2 V - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{A}}{\partial t^2} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Lorentz Gauge Matters:** Without it, the equations for $V$ and $\mathbf{A}$ are coupled and very difficult to solve. The Lorentz gauge makes them mathematically independent, allowing us to solve each one separately using **Green's function** methods.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize this table completely. Nearly every KTU Part B question on retarded potentials expects you to write the wave equation and the final integral solution.

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|--------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Retarded time | $t_r = t - R/c$ | Time at which the source must be evaluated |
| 2 | Retarded scalar potential | $V(\mathbf{r}, t) = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \iiint \dfrac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$ | Coulomb-like integral with time delay |
| 3 | Retarded vector potential | $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \iiint \dfrac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$ | Biot-Savart-like integral with time delay |
| 4 | Lorentz gauge | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ | Decouples $V$ and $\mathbf{A}$ |
| 5 | Inhomogeneous wave equation (scalar) | $\nabla^2 V - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = -\dfrac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$ | Wave equation for $V$ |
| 6 | Inhomogeneous wave equation (vector) | $\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial^2 \mathbf{A}}{\partial t^2} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$ | Wave equation for $\mathbf{A}$ |
| 7 | Electric field via potentials | $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}$ | Fundamental E-field relation |
| 8 | Magnetic field via potentials | $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ | Fundamental B-field relation |
| 9 | Free-space wave speed | $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Speed of light |
| 10 | Impedance of free space | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0 / \varepsilon_0} \approx 377 \, \Omega$ | Used in far-field E/H ratio |
| 11 | Green's function (retarded) | $G(\mathbf{r}, t; \mathbf{r}', t') = \dfrac{\delta(t' - t + R/c)}{4\pi R}$ | Solution kernel of the wave equation |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The retarded potential formulation is the **mathematical engine** behind:

- **Antenna radiation analysis** — computing $E_\theta, E_\phi$ far-field patterns of dipoles, loops, horns.
- **Radar cross-section (RCS) calculations** — finding scattered fields from complex targets.
- **Wireless channel modeling** — predicting signal arrival times in multipath environments.
- **Electromagnetic compatibility (EMC)** — analyzing unintentional radiated emissions from PCB traces and cables.
- **Bio-electromagnetics** — safety assessment of RF exposure from mobile phones and medical implants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Retarded Scalar Potential

We begin with the **inhomogeneous scalar wave equation** in free space:

$$\nabla^2 V - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}, t)}{\varepsilon_0}$$

We wish to find $V(\mathbf{r}, t)$ for an arbitrary source distribution $\rho(\mathbf{r}, t)$.

**Method:** Use the **Green's function** technique. We first solve for a **point source** (Dirac delta source) and then superpose.

#### Step 1 — The Green's Function Problem

Replace $\rho(\mathbf{r}, t)/\varepsilon_0$ with a unit point impulse $\delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}') \delta(t - t')$ located at $\mathbf{r}'$ acting at time $t'$. The resulting equation for the Green's function $G(\mathbf{r}, t; \mathbf{r}', t')$ is:

$$\nabla^2 G - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial t^2} = -\delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}') \delta(t - t')$$

The right-hand side is a **four-dimensional Dirac delta** — a point source in both space and time.

#### Step 2 — Symmetry and Shift of Origin

By spatial translation invariance, $G$ depends only on the relative distance $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert$. By time-translation invariance, $G$ depends only on $\tau = t - t'$. Therefore:

$$G(\mathbf{r}, t; \mathbf{r}', t') = G(R, \tau) = G(R, t - t')$$

#### Step 3 — Spherical Symmetry Reduction

Since $G$ depends only on $R$ (not on angles), the Laplacian simplifies to its radial form in spherical coordinates:

$$\nabla^2 G = \frac{1}{R^2} \frac{\partial}{\partial R} \left( R^2 \frac{\partial G}{\partial R} \right) = \frac{1}{R} \frac{\partial^2 (RG)}{\partial R^2}$$

Therefore the Green's function equation becomes (excluding $R = 0$, where the delta is singular):

$$\frac{1}{R} \frac{\partial^2 (RG)}{\partial R^2} - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial \tau^2} = 0$$

Let $u(R, \tau) = R \cdot G(R, \tau)$. Then:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial R^2} - \frac{1}{c^2} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial \tau^2} = 0$$

This is the classic **one-dimensional wave equation** in $R$ for each fixed $\tau$.

#### Step 4 — General Solution of the Wave Equation

The general solution of the 1-D wave equation is a superposition of forward- and backward-traveling waves:

$$u(R, \tau) = f\left(\tau - \frac{R}{c}\right) + g\left(\tau + \frac{R}{c}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$G(R, \tau) = \frac{f\left(\tau - R/c\right)}{R} + \frac{g\left(\tau + R/c\right)}{R}$$

#### Step 5 — Apply Causality (Killer of the Advanced Potential!)

The term $g(\tau + R/c) = g(t - t' + R/c)$ corresponds to a **signal arriving before it is emitted** — this violates **causality**. The principle of causality demands that no effect can precede its cause in time. Therefore:

$$g\left(\tau + \frac{R}{c}\right) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Causality Principle is the soul of the retarded potential.** Without it, the mathematics would also admit a physically impossible "advanced" solution. KTU examiners frequently award a 2-mark bonus for explicitly mentioning this rejection.

The remaining solution is:

$$G(R, \tau) = \frac{f(\tau - R/c)}{R}$$

#### Step 6 — Match the Delta-Function Source

To find $f$, integrate the Green's equation over a small sphere of radius $\varepsilon$ around $R = 0$ in the limit $\varepsilon \to 0$. Using the divergence theorem:

$$\iiint \nabla^2 G \, dv = \oiint \nabla G \cdot d\mathbf{S} = -\iiint \delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}') \delta(\tau) \, dv = -\delta(\tau)$$

On the surface of the small sphere $R = \varepsilon$, $\nabla G \approx \frac{\partial G}{\partial R} \hat{\mathbf{R}}$, and $d\mathbf{S} = \varepsilon^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, \hat{\mathbf{R}}$:

$$\oiint \frac{\partial G}{\partial R} \varepsilon^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = 4\pi \varepsilon^2 \frac{\partial G}{\partial R}\bigg\vert_{R=\varepsilon} = -\delta(\tau)$$

Since $G \approx f(\tau)/\varepsilon$ near the origin:

$$4\pi \varepsilon^2 \cdot \left(-\frac{f(\tau)}{\varepsilon^2}\right) = -4\pi f(\tau) = -\delta(\tau)$$

Therefore:

$$f(\tau) = \frac{\delta(\tau)}{4\pi}$$

#### Step 7 — Final Retarded Green's Function

$$\boxed{G(\mathbf{r}, t; \mathbf{r}', t') = \frac{\delta(t' - t + R/c)}{4\pi R}}$$

This is the **retarded Green's function** of the 3-D wave equation. The numerator is a Dirac delta enforcing the retarded time condition $t' = t - R/c$.

#### Step 8 — Construct the Retarded Scalar Potential

By linearity (the wave equation is linear), the full potential is a superposition of point-source contributions weighted by the source $\rho$:

$$\boxed{V(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \iiint_{V'} \frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t - R/c)}{R} \, dv'}$$

This is the **retarded scalar potential**. The argument $t - R/c$ inside $\rho$ is precisely the retarded time $t_r$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Retarded Vector Potential (By Analogy)

The wave equation for $\mathbf{A}$ is **identical in form** to the scalar equation, with $\rho/\varepsilon_0$ replaced by $\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$. Performing the identical derivation (or simply using the **principle of superposition of analogous results**):

$$\boxed{\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \iiint_{V'} \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t - R/c)}{R} \, dv'}$$

The vector current $\mathbf{J}$ has three Cartesian components, each of which satisfies the same scalar wave equation independently. Hence the vector derivation is a component-wise repetition of the scalar one.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Retrieval of $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ from Retarded Potentials

Once the retarded potentials are known, the fields follow directly from:

$$\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}, \qquad \mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}$$

The gradient and time derivative operations are taken at the **field point** $\mathbf{r}$ and **observation time** $t$, with the source quantities evaluated at the **retarded time** $t_r = t - R/c$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Cases

| Limiting Case | Condition | Effect on Retarded Potential | Physical Interpretation |
|---------------|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| Static (electrostatics) | $\partial / \partial t \to 0$, $t_r \to t$ | $V$ reduces to Coulomb's law | No propagation, instantaneous action |
| Quasi-static (near-field) | $R \ll \lambda$ | $t_r \approx t$, retardation negligible | Reactive fields dominate |
| Radiation (far-field) | $R \gg \lambda$ | $1/R$ decay, retardation essential, transverse $\mathbf{E} \perp \mathbf{B}$ | Propagating wave with $\vert E\vert/\vert H\vert = \eta_0$ |
| Free-space propagation | $R \to \infty$ | $R \approx r - \hat{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \mathbf{r}'$ (Fraunhofer approximation) | Plane-wave-like locally at observation point |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Chain: From Source Variation to Radiated Field

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete cause-effect chain that the retarded potential concept formalizes. It illustrates how a time-varying source at an earlier instant manifests as a radiated field at a later instant, mediated by the retardation delay.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Time varying current source J of r prime comma t] --> waveA[Electromagnetic disturbance generated at source point r prime]
    waveA --> propA[Wave propagates outward at speed c]
    propA --> distA[Travels distance R to reach field point r]
    distA --> delayA[Time delay t r equals t minus R over c]
    delayA --> fldA[Retarded potential V of r comma t computed using t r]
    fldA --> fldB[Retarded potential A of r comma t computed using t r]
    fldB --> gradA[Apply curl operator to obtain magnetic field B]
    fldA --> gradB[Apply gradient and time derivative to obtain electric field E]
    gradA --> radA[Far field radiation pattern E theta and E phi]
    gradB --> radA
    radA --> outA[Radiated electromagnetic wave observed at point r and time t]
    style srcA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style outA fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#663300,color:#000000
    style delayA fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Retarded Potential Solver Pipeline

For engineering deployment, the retarded potential concept is implemented as a numerical pipeline. The diagram below abstracts the computational flow used in commercial antenna simulation software (e.g., method-of-moments solvers like NEC, FEKO).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA[Input mesh of antenna geometry] --> nB[Define current distribution J of r prime comma t on mesh elements]
    nB --> nC[For each field point r compute distance R]
    nC --> nD[Compute retarded time t r equals t minus R over c for each mesh element]
    nD --> nE[Evaluate source at retarded time J of r prime comma t r]
    nE --> nF[Integrate J of r prime comma t r over R d v prime]
    nF --> nG[Multiply by mu0 over 4 pi to get vector potential A of r comma t]
    nG --> nH[Compute curl of A to get B field]
    nG --> nI[Compute time derivative of A to get electric field contribution]
    nH --> nJ[Combine E and B into Poynting vector S equals E cross H]
    nI --> nJ
    nJ --> nK[Output radiation intensity and directivity]
    style nA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
    style nK fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    style nD fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Comparison Topology — Retarded vs. Advanced Potential

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rootN[Green function solution of wave equation] --> br1[Two mathematically valid branches]
    br1 --> pA[Retarded branch uses delta t prime equals t minus R over c]
    br1 --> pB[Advanced branch uses delta t prime equals t plus R over c]
    pA --> cA[Physically valid effect arrives after cause]
    pB --> cB[Physically invalid effect would arrive before cause]
    cB --> cC[Rejected by causality principle]
    cA --> cD[Adopted in all antenna and field analysis]
    style pA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style pB fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    style cC fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000
    style cD fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the retarded scalar and vector potentials. Explain the physical significance of the retardation term $R/c$.**

**Model Answer:**

The retarded scalar potential at field point $\mathbf{r}$ and time $t$ is defined as:

$$V(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \iiint_{V'} \frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$$

The retarded vector potential is:

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \iiint_{V'} \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$$

where $t_r = t - R/c$ is the retarded time and $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert$.

**Physical Significance of $R/c$:** The term $R/c$ represents the **time taken by the electromagnetic disturbance to propagate** from the source point $\mathbf{r}'$ to the field point $\mathbf{r}$ at the finite speed of light $c$. It captures the principle of **causality** — fields observed at time $t$ depend on the source configuration at the earlier instant $t_r$, not at the present time. If the source current changes suddenly, the field at distance $R$ will respond only after a delay of $R/c$ seconds.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Lorentz gauge condition. Why is it necessary in deriving retarded potentials?**

**Model Answer:**

The Lorentz gauge condition is:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$$

**Explanation:** It is a constraint relating the divergence of the vector potential $\mathbf{A}$ to the time rate of change of the scalar potential $V$, weighted by $\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 = 1/c^2$.

**Necessity:** When the potentials $\mathbf{A}$ and $V$ are introduced via $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \partial \mathbf{A}/\partial t$, the potentials are **not unique** — a gauge transformation can be applied without changing the physical fields. Substituting these into Maxwell's equations yields coupled equations for $V$ and $\mathbf{A}$. The Lorentz gauge **decouples** them, transforming them into two independent inhomogeneous wave equations. This decoupled form has a clean retarded Green's function solution, which is essential for deriving the retarded potentials. Without this gauge, the problem becomes mathematically intractable.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the retarded time argument inside $\rho$ and $\mathbf{J}$** is the #1 reason students lose marks. Always write $\rho(\mathbf{r}', t_r)$ — never $\rho(\mathbf{r}', t)$.
> - **Failing to explicitly mention the causality rejection of the advanced solution** costs 2 marks. KTU evaluators specifically look for this.
> - **Skipping the derivation of the Lorentz gauge or starting directly from the wave equation** is a 3-mark penalty. Always show the logical path.
> - **Confusing $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert$ with the magnitude of $\mathbf{r}$ alone** is a common error in the far-field approximation step.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations, derive the inhomogeneous wave equations for the scalar potential $V$ and the vector potential $\mathbf{A}$. Clearly state the role of the Lorentz gauge. (7 marks)

**(b)** Using the retarded Green's function, derive the expressions for the retarded scalar and vector potentials. Explain why the advanced solution is rejected. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Starting from Maxwell's equations in free space:

$$\nabla \cdot \mathbf{E} = \frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}, \qquad \nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0 \mathbf{J} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \mathbf{E}}{\partial t}$$

Define $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ (ensures $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0$). Substituting into Faraday's law gives:

$$\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \frac{\partial \mathbf{A}}{\partial t}$$

Substituting into Gauss's law:

$$\nabla^2 V + \frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A}) = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Substituting into the Ampère-Maxwell law and taking the curl:

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{A}}{\partial t^2} - \nabla\left(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial V}{\partial t}\right) = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

The **Lorentz gauge** $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ eliminates the gradient term and decouples the equations, yielding:

$$\nabla^2 V - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial t^2} = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0}$$

$$\nabla^2 \mathbf{A} - \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \mathbf{A}}{\partial t^2} = -\mu_0 \mathbf{J}$$

**(b)** The retarded Green's function satisfies:

$$\nabla^2 G - \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 G}{\partial t^2} = -\delta(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}')\delta(t - t')$$

By spherical symmetry, $G(R, \tau) = f(\tau - R/c)/R + g(\tau + R/c)/R$. Causality forces $g = 0$. Matching the delta source yields $f(\tau) = \delta(\tau)/(4\pi)$. Therefore:

$$G(\mathbf{r}, t; \mathbf{r}', t') = \frac{\delta(t' - t + R/c)}{4\pi R}$$

Superposing point-source responses weighted by $\rho$ and $\mathbf{J}$:

$$V(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\iiint \frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t - R/c)}{R} \, dv'$$

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\iiint \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t - R/c)}{R} \, dv'$$

The advanced term $g(\tau + R/c)$ corresponds to a field arriving **before** the source emits — a violation of causality (information cannot travel backward in time). It is therefore physically rejected.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1 Choice]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the physical meaning of the retardation time $t_r$. Describe the time evolution of fields from an oscillating current element. (7 marks)

**(b)** A short dipole antenna of length $L = 0.1$ m carries a sinusoidal current $I(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ at frequency $f = 300$ MHz. Using the retarded vector potential, write the expression for $\mathbf{A}$ at a far-field point. Determine the wavelength and the ratio of retardation time to one period. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The retardation time is $t_r = t - R/c$. **Physical meaning:** When a current oscillates at the source, the field observed at distance $R$ at time $t$ corresponds to the source configuration at the **earlier** instant $t_r$, since the electromagnetic wave needs a finite time $R/c$ to reach the observer.

**Diagram description:** Draw a vertical dipole at the origin with sinusoidal current arrows pointing up and down. Draw concentric spherical wavefronts expanding outward. Label the observation point at distance $R$ with the arrow indicating wave arrival time $t$, and label the source emission time as $t_r = t - R/c$. Indicate the wave traveling at speed $c$ with directional arrows.

For a time-harmonic current $I_0 \cos(\omega t)$, the retarded current evaluated at $t_r$ is $I_0 \cos[\omega(t - R/c)] = I_0 \cos(\omega t - kR)$ where $k = \omega/c$. The phase lag $kR$ accumulates with distance, producing the radiation pattern.

**(b)** Given: $L = 0.1$ m, $f = 300$ MHz, so $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 3 \times 10^8$ rad/s.

Wavelength:

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3 \times 10^8} = 1 \text{ m}$$

Period:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^8} = 3.33 \text{ ns}$$

The retarded vector potential in the far field is:

$$\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r}, t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \frac{I_0 \cos(\omega t - kR)}{R} \, dz \, \hat{\mathbf{z}}$$

For a short dipole ($L \ll \lambda$) and far-field approximation $R \approx r$ in the denominator:

$$\mathbf{A} \approx \frac{\mu_0 I_0 L \cos(\omega t - kr)}{4\pi r} \hat{\mathbf{z}}$$

The retardation time at distance $r$ is $t_r = t - r/c$. The retardation time ratio:

$$\frac{t_r}{T} = \frac{r/c}{1/f} = \frac{r f}{c} = \frac{r}{\lambda}$$

For $r = 10$ m: $t_r/T = 10/1 = 10$. So the field is 10 periods behind the source.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- **Retarded time** $t_r = t - R/c$ is the cornerstone. Always use it inside the source terms.
- **Retarded scalar potential**: $V = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\iiint \dfrac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$ — same form as Coulomb's law but with retarded time.
- **Retarded vector potential**: $\mathbf{A} = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\iiint \dfrac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{R} \, dv'$ — same form as Biot-Savart with retarded time.
- **Lorentz gauge**: $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ is **not optional** — it is the bridge that decouples the wave equations.
- **Inhomogeneous wave equations** are the starting point of the entire derivation. Memorize both forms.
- **Field-potential relations**: $\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{E} = -\nabla V - \partial \mathbf{A}/\partial t$.
- **Green's function**: $G = \dfrac{\delta(t' - t + R/c)}{4\pi R}$ enforces the retarded time condition.
- **Causality** is the reason the **advanced solution is rejected** — not mathematical preference, physical necessity.
- **Time-harmonic case**: replace $t_r$ with $t - R/c$ and use the factor $e^{j(\omega t - kR)}$ in phasor form.
- **Speed of light**: $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0} = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. **Impedance of free space**: $\eta_0 = 377 \, \Omega$.
- **Far-field signature**: $1/R$ amplitude decay, transverse $\mathbf{E} \perp \mathbf{B} \perp \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, and ratio $\vert E \vert / \vert H \vert = \eta_0$.
- **Common mistake to avoid**: Don't drop the retardation term $R/c$ in the argument of $\rho$ and $\mathbf{J}$ — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
- **Always distinguish** $R = \vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert$ (exact) from $r - \hat{\mathbf{r}} \cdot \mathbf{r}'$ (far-field Fraunhofer approximation).
- The retarded potential is the **mathematical foundation** of radiation — every antenna pattern you compute in subsequent modules flows from this concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infinitesimal Dipole, Small Dipole, and Small Circular Loop Antenna
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Infinitesimal Dipole, Small Dipole, and Small Circular Loop Antenna

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Infinitesimal Dipole (Hertzian Dipole)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> An **Infinitesimal Dipole**, also known as a **Hertzian Dipole**, is a hypothetical, linearly polarized, straight wire antenna whose physical length $dz$ is vanishingly small compared to the operating wavelength ($\Delta z \ll \lambda$, typically $\Delta z < 0.01\lambda$). It is fed at its center by a uniform time-harmonic current $I_0 e^{j\omega t}$ of constant magnitude and phase along its entire length.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Think of the Hertzian dipole as a **single vibrating guitar string that is frozen in a single instant of vibration**. Because the string is infinitely short, every point on it moves up and down in perfect unison (uniform current). It cannot support a standing wave. While physically unrealizable, it is the **"atom" of all antenna theory** — every practical antenna can be mathematically built by stringing thousands of these infinitesimal dipoles end-to-end (this is the foundation of the *Method of Moments*). It is to electromagnetics what the point mass is to classical mechanics: an idealized building block.

The intrinsic impedance of free space is **$\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$**, and the free-space wavenumber is **$k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$**.

---

## 1.2 The Small Dipole

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Small Dipole** is a center-fed, straight, thin-wire antenna whose total physical length $l$ is electrically short (typically $l \le \lambda/10$ or, by some texts, $l \le \lambda/50$). Unlike the Hertzian dipole, the current distribution is **not uniform**; it tapers linearly from a maximum value $I_0$ at the central feed point to zero at the two open ends, approximated by $I(z) = I_0\left(1 - \dfrac{2\vert z\vert}{l}\right)$ for $-l/2 \le z \le l/2$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **thin metal rod being held at its center while the two ends are completely free**. Electrons (current) can be pushed in only at the center, so they pile up there and gradually thin out toward the tips where they have "nowhere to go." The current distribution is therefore **triangular** — maximum in the middle, zero at the ends. This triangular profile is the key distinction from the Hertzian dipole.

The far-field of a small dipole is identical in *shape* to that of a Hertzian dipole (both are $\sin\theta$ patterns), but the radiated amplitude is scaled by $l/2$ because the average current over the wire is $I_0/2$.

---

## 1.3 The Small Circular Loop Antenna

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Small Circular Loop Antenna** is a closed-loop, thin-wire conductor of radius $a$ carrying a spatially uniform (constant magnitude and phase), time-harmonic current $I_0 e^{j\omega t}$. It is classified as *electrically small* when its circumference $C = 2\pi a$ satisfies $C \le \lambda/10$ (some texts use $a \le \lambda/25$ or $C \le \lambda/3$). The loop may have any number of turns $N$, in which case the far-field amplitude scales by $N$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> Picture a **hula-hoop made of copper wire** through which a perfectly steady, uniform current circulates. Because the loop is much smaller than a wavelength, the current does not vary appreciably from one point on the loop to the next. The loop behaves as a **magnetic dipole** — it is the exact electromagnetic dual of the Hertzian electric dipole. Where the straight dipole creates an electric field aligned with it, the small loop creates a magnetic field aligned with its axis. You can think of the small loop as a tiny "ring magnet" broadcasting into space.

**Key Physical Constants Used Throughout:**
- $\eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega \approx 377\ \Omega$ — Intrinsic impedance of free space.
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ — Permeability of free space.
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ — Permittivity of free space.
- $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ — Speed of light.
- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$ — Free-space wavenumber.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Infinitesimal Dipole — Operational Logic

The Hertzian dipole is analyzed by:
1. **Assume uniform current** $I_0$ over length $dz$ (justified because $dz \ll \lambda$).
2. **Compute the magnetic vector potential** $A_z$ at a far-field point $(r, \theta, \phi)$ using the retarded integral.
3. **Derive $\vec{H}$** by taking the curl $\vec{H} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_0}\nabla \times \vec{A}$.
4. **Derive $\vec{E}$** from $\vec{E} = \dfrac{1}{j\omega\varepsilon_0}\nabla \times \vec{H}$.
5. **Integrate the Poynting vector** $\vec{S} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{Re}(\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^*)$ over a far-field sphere to find radiated power.
6. **Equate** radiated power to $\dfrac{1}{2}\vert I_0 \vert^2 R_r$ to extract radiation resistance.

> **Why this matters in engineering:** The Hertzian dipole is the *canonical reference antenna*. Its input impedance, radiation resistance, and pattern shape are the benchmarks against which every real antenna (Yagi-Uda, log-periodic, microstrip patch, etc.) is judged and matched.

---

## 2.2 The Small Dipole — Operational Logic

1. **Approximate current as triangular:** $I(z) = I_0\left(1 - \dfrac{2\vert z \vert}{l}\right)$ — physically enforced by the open ends.
2. **Decompose into $N$ Hertzian dipoles** of length $dz$, each with current $I(z)$.
3. **Integrate the far-field contributions** along the wire length.
4. **Obtain pattern identical to Hertzian dipole** but amplitude scaled by an *effective length* $l_e = l/2$.
5. **Compute average current** $\langle I \rangle = I_0/2$ to relate the result to a single equivalent Hertzian dipole.

> **Real-world utility:** Small dipoles appear in compact wireless devices, RFID tags, hearing-aid loops, and broadband noise measurement (the *Standard Gain Horn* is sometimes calibrated against a known short dipole).

---

## 2.3 The Small Circular Loop — Operational Logic

1. **Assume uniform current** $I_0$ around the loop (justified because $C \ll \lambda$).
2. **Compute magnetic vector potential** $A_\phi$ from the line integral of the current.
3. **Apply small-loop approximation** ($a \ll \lambda$) to retain only the lowest-order term in $kr$.
4. **Far fields:** Electric field is $\phi$-directed (azimuthal), magnetic field is $\theta$-directed — *orthogonal* to the dipole case.
5. **Recognize duality:** Loop fields mirror those of a magnetic dipole moment $m = I_0 \pi a^2$, whereas straight dipole fields mirror an electric dipole moment $p$.

> **Real-world utility:** Small loops are used as **receiving antennas in direction finding**, as **field probes in EMC/EMI test chambers**, and as **navigation aids (ADF) in aviation** because of their excellent null sharpness (the $\sin\theta$ pattern has a deep null on the loop axis).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the following table** — these are the *only* formulas you need to solve any KTU 2024 Module-1 problem on these three antenna types.

| # | Quantity | Infinitesimal Dipole (Hertzian) | Small Dipole (length $l \le \lambda/10$) | Small Circular Loop (radius $a \le \lambda/25$) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Current profile | Uniform $I_0$ | Triangular $I_0(1-2\vert z\vert/l)$ | Uniform $I_0$ |
| 2 | Far-field $\vec{E}$ (dominant) | $E_\theta = \dfrac{j\eta\, k I_0\, dz\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\sin\theta$ | $E_\theta = \dfrac{j\eta\, k I_0\, l\, e^{-jkr}}{8\pi r}\sin\theta$ | $E_\phi = \dfrac{j\eta\, k^{2} a^{2} I_0\, e^{-jkr}}{4 r}\sin\theta$ |
| 3 | Far-field $\vec{H}$ | $H_\phi = E_\theta / \eta$ | $H_\phi = E_\theta / \eta$ | $H_\theta = -E_\phi / \eta$ |
| 4 | Radiation Resistance $R_r$ | $80\pi^{2}\left(\dfrac{dz}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $20\pi^{2}\left(\dfrac{l}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$ | $31200\left(\dfrac{\pi a^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}\right)^{2} = 20\pi^{2}\left(\dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\right)^{4}$ |
| 5 | Directivity $D_0$ | $1.5$ (or $1.76$ dB) | $1.5$ (or $1.76$ dB) | $1.5$ (or $1.76$ dB) |
| 6 | Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW) | $90^\circ$ | $90^\circ$ | $90^\circ$ |
| 7 | Maximum Effective Aperture $A_{em}$ | $0.119\,\lambda^{2}$ | $0.119\,\lambda^{2}$ | $0.119\,\lambda^{2}$ |
| 8 | Total Radiated Power $P_{rad}$ | $I_0^{2} R_r / 2$ | $I_0^{2} R_r / 2$ | $I_0^{2} R_r / 2$ |
| 9 | Field Polarization (at $\theta = 90^\circ$) | Linear (along $\hat{\theta}$) | Linear (along $\hat{\theta}$) | Linear (along $\hat{\phi}$) |
| 10 | Equivalent / Effective Length | $dz$ | $l_e = l/2$ | N/A (effective area $A_e = \pi a^{2}$) |

### Important Boundary / Validity Conditions
- Hertzian: $\Delta z \le 0.01\,\lambda$ (or $0.05\lambda$ looser)
- Small dipole: $l \le \lambda/10$ (some authors: $l \le \lambda/50$)
- Small loop: $C = 2\pi a \le \lambda/3$ (standard) or $a \le \lambda/25$ (strict)
- **Far-field region:** $r \ge \dfrac{2D^{2}}{\lambda}$, and $r \gg \lambda$

### Why these three antennas have the *same* directivity (1.5)
The identical directivity is **not a coincidence** — it reflects the **Babinet–Booker principle** and the **electromagnetic duality** between the electric dipole (Hertzian / small dipole) and the magnetic dipole (small loop). All three radiate an *isotropic-equivalent* $r^{-2}$ power density modulated by a $\sin^{2}\theta$ angular factor, which integrates to the same total power regardless of polarization orientation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Philosophy (KTU Valuation):** The Board examiner awards full marks *only* when you (i) state the starting formula, (ii) show all intermediate steps, (iii) justify each approximation, and (iv) box the final answer. The derivations below are deliberately exhaustive — adapt the same style in your exam script.

---

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Far-Field of the Hertzian Dipole

**Step 1 — Set up the magnetic vector potential.**
A $z$-directed Hertzian dipole of length $dz$ carrying uniform current $I_0$ produces, in the far field ($r \gg \lambda$, $r \gg dz$), the $z$-component of the magnetic vector potential:

$$
A_z \approx \frac{\mu_0\, I_0\, dz}{4\pi r}\, e^{-jkr}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to spherical components.**
In spherical coordinates, $A_z = A_r\cos\theta - A_\theta\sin\theta$. In the *far field*, the radial component $A_r$ produces only a non-radiating $1/r^2$ field and can be neglected. We retain only the $\theta$-component:

$$
A_\theta = -\frac{\mu_0\, I_0\, dz\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnetic field.**
The far-field magnetic field has only a $\phi$-component:

$$
H_\phi = j\omega\varepsilon_0\, A_\theta \sin\theta
$$

Substitute and simplify using $\eta = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}$ and $k = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0}$:

$$
H_\phi = j\,\frac{k\, I_0\, dz\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the electric field from Faraday's law.**
In the far field, the $\theta$-component of the electric field is related to $H_\phi$ by the intrinsic impedance:

$$
E_\theta = \eta\, H_\phi
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta\, k\, I_0\, dz\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time-average Poynting vector.**

$$
\vec{S}_{av} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{Re}\!\left(\vec{E} \times \vec{H}^{*}\right) = \frac{\vert E_\theta\vert^{2}}{2\eta}\,\hat{r}
$$

Substituting $\vert E_\theta\vert = \dfrac{\eta\, k\, I_0\, dz\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}$:

$$
S_{av,\,r} = \frac{\eta}{2}\left(\frac{k\, I_0\, dz}{4\pi r}\right)^{2}\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}}{32\pi^{2}\, r^{2}}\,\sin^{2}\theta
$$

**Step 6 — Integrate over a far-field sphere to obtain $P_{rad}$.**

$$
P_{rad} = \oint_S \vec{S}_{av} \cdot d\vec{s} = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^{\pi} S_{av,\,r}\, r^{2}\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi
$$

The $\phi$-integral gives $2\pi$:

$$
P_{rad} = \frac{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}}{16\pi}\int_0^{\pi}\sin^{3}\theta\, d\theta
$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate $\int_0^{\pi}\sin^{3}\theta\, d\theta$.**
Use the identity $\sin^{3}\theta = \sin\theta(1 - \cos^{2}\theta)$ and the substitution $u = \cos\theta$:

$$
\int_0^{\pi}\sin^{3}\theta\, d\theta = \int_{-1}^{1}(1-u^{2})\, du = \left[u - \frac{u^{3}}{3}\right]_{-1}^{1} = \frac{4}{3}
$$

**Step 8 — Assemble the total radiated power.**

$$
P_{rad} = \frac{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}}{16\pi} \cdot \frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}}{12\pi}
$$

**Step 9 — Extract the radiation resistance.**
By definition, $P_{rad} = \dfrac{1}{2}\vert I_0\vert^{2}\, R_r$. Equating:

$$
\frac{1}{2}\vert I_0\vert^{2}\, R_r = \frac{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}}{12\pi}
$$

$$
R_r = \frac{\eta\, k^{2}\, dz^{2}}{6\pi}
$$

Now substitute $\eta = 120\pi$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$
R_r = \frac{120\pi \cdot (2\pi/\lambda)^{2}\, dz^{2}}{6\pi} = \frac{120\pi \cdot 4\pi^{2}\, dz^{2}}{6\pi\,\lambda^{2}} = 80\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{dz}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_r^{\text{Hertzian}} = 80\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{dz}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}\ \Omega\,}
$$

**Step 10 — Compute the directivity.**
Directivity is $D_0 = \dfrac{4\pi\, U_{\max}}{P_{rad}}$, where $U_{\max} = \dfrac{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}}{32\pi^{2}}$ (the radiation intensity at $\theta = 90^\circ$):

$$
D_0 = \frac{4\pi \cdot \eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}/(32\pi^{2})}{\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0^{2}\, dz^{2}/(12\pi)} = \frac{4\pi}{32\pi^{2}} \cdot 12\pi = \frac{3}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_0^{\text{Hertzian}} = 1.5 \quad\text{or}\quad 10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.76\ \text{dB}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Small Dipole (Triangular Current)

**Step 1 — Express the triangular current.**
For a center-fed short dipole, the current is maximum $I_0$ at $z = 0$ and linearly falls to zero at $z = \pm l/2$:

$$
I(z) = I_0\!\left(1 - \frac{2\vert z \vert}{l}\right) \quad\text{for}\quad -\frac{l}{2} \le z \le \frac{l}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into an integral of Hertzian dipoles.**
Each infinitesimal slice $dz$ acts as a Hertzian dipole carrying current $I(z)$. The far-field contribution of slice $dz$ is:

$$
dE_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta\, k\, I(z)\, dz\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the full length.**
Because $r \gg l$ in the far field, $r$ does not vary appreciably across the dipole and is treated as constant:

$$
E_\theta = j\,\frac{\eta\, k\, e^{-jkr}\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\int_{-l/2}^{l/2} I(z)\, dz
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**
By symmetry, integrate from $0$ to $l/2$ and double the result:

$$
\int_{-l/2}^{l/2} I_0\!\left(1 - \frac{2\vert z \vert}{l}\right)dz = 2\, I_0 \int_0^{l/2}\!\left(1 - \frac{2z}{l}\right)dz
$$

$$
= 2\, I_0 \left[z - \frac{z^{2}}{l}\right]_0^{l/2} = 2\, I_0 \left[\frac{l}{2} - \frac{l}{4}\right] = 2\, I_0 \cdot \frac{l}{4} = \frac{I_0\, l}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the final far-field expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,E_\theta^{\text{small dipole}} = j\,\frac{\eta\, k\, I_0\, l\, e^{-jkr}}{8\pi r}\,\sin\theta\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Derive the radiation resistance.**
Following the same procedure as §3.1 (Poynting integration) but with the factor $l/2$ replacing $dz$:

$$
R_r = 80\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{l/2}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2} = 20\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_r^{\text{small dipole}} = 20\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^{\!2}\ \Omega\,}
$$

> **Effective Length Insight:** $l_e = l/2$ — the small dipole radiates only *half* as strongly as a Hertzian dipole of length $l$, because the average current is $I_0/2$.

---

## 3.3 Full Derivation: Small Circular Loop

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
A circular loop of radius $a$ lies in the $xy$-plane. Current $I_0$ flows uniformly. The point of observation is at $(r, \theta, \phi)$ in spherical coordinates with $r \gg \lambda$ and $a \ll \lambda$.

**Step 2 — Vector potential from the line integral.**
A point source on the loop is located at $(a\cos\phi', a\sin\phi', 0)$. The distance from this source to the field point is, to first order in $a/r$ (far-field binomial expansion):

$$
R \approx r - a\sin\theta\cos(\phi - \phi')
$$

The magnetic vector potential has only a $\phi$-component (by symmetry):

$$
A_\phi = \frac{\mu_0\, I_0}{4\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} \frac{e^{-jkR}}{R}\, a\, d\phi'
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the far-field approximation $R \approx r$ in the denominator and $R \approx r - a\sin\theta\cos(\phi - \phi')$ in the phase:**

$$
A_\phi \approx \frac{\mu_0\, I_0\, a\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\int_0^{2\pi} e^{jka\sin\theta\cos(\phi - \phi')}\, d\phi'
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Bessel identity.**
Using the Bessel-function identity $\int_0^{2\pi} e^{j\alpha\cos\beta}\, d\beta = 2\pi J_0(\alpha)$:

$$
A_\phi \approx \frac{\mu_0\, I_0\, a\, e^{-jkr}}{2r}\, J_0(ka\sin\theta)
$$

**Step 5 — Small-loop approximation $ka\sin\theta \ll 1$.**
For a small loop, $ka \ll 1$. Use $J_0(x) \approx 1$ for $x \to 0$:

$$
A_\phi \approx \frac{\mu_0\, I_0\, a\, e^{-jkr}}{2r}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial observation:** The small-loop vector potential is *independent of $\theta$*! This is a profound difference from the straight dipole, where $A_z \propto \sin\theta$.

**Step 6 — Compute the magnetic field.**
In the far field, only the $\theta$-component of $\vec{H}$ survives (from $\nabla \times \vec{A}$):

$$
H_\theta = -\frac{1}{\mu_0 r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r A_\phi\right) = \frac{jk\, I_0\, a\, e^{-jkr}}{2 r}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the electric field.**

$$
E_\phi = \eta\, H_\theta = \frac{j\eta\, k\, I_0\, a\, e^{-jkr}}{2 r}
$$

We prefer to write this in terms of loop area $A = \pi a^{2}$, using $a = A/(\pi a)$:

$$
\boxed{\,E_\phi^{\text{small loop}} = \frac{j\eta\, k^{2}\, I_0\, A\, e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}\,\sin\theta\,}
$$

> **Why the extra $\sin\theta$ appears in the final KTU form:** Balanis's standard formula includes this factor because the loop has a *direction-dependent projection* into the far field (the loop's dipole moment is normal to the loop plane). The factor $\sin\theta$ comes from projecting the $\hat{z}$-directed magnetic moment onto $\hat{\theta}$.

**Step 8 — Derive the radiation resistance.**
Compute $|\vec{S}_{av}| = \dfrac{|E_\phi|^2}{2\eta}$, integrate over a sphere, and equate to $\dfrac{1}{2}I_0^2 R_r$:

$$
P_{rad} = \frac{\eta\, k^{4}\, I_0^{2}\, a^{4}}{12\pi} = \frac{I_0^{2}\, R_r}{2}
$$

Solving:

$$
R_r = \frac{\eta\, k^{4}\, a^{4}}{6\pi}
$$

Substitute $\eta = 120\pi$ and $k = 2\pi/\lambda$:

$$
R_r = \frac{120\pi \cdot (2\pi/\lambda)^{4}\, a^{4}}{6\pi} = \frac{120\pi \cdot 16\pi^{4}\, a^{4}}{6\pi\,\lambda^{4}} = 320\pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{a}{\lambda}\right)^{\!4}
$$

Using circumference $C = 2\pi a$:

$$
R_r = 320\pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{a}{\lambda}\right)^{\!4} = 320\pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{C}{2\pi\lambda}\right)^{\!4} = \frac{320\pi^{4}}{16\pi^{4}}\!\left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^{\!4} = 20\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^{\!4}
$$

Equivalently, in terms of area $A = \pi a^{2}$:

$$
R_r = 31200\left(\frac{A}{\lambda^{2}}\right)^{2} = 31200\left(\frac{\pi a^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}\right)^{2}\ \Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_r^{\text{small loop}} = 31200\!\left(\frac{\pi a^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}\right)^{\!2} = 20\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{C}{\lambda}\right)^{\!4}\ \Omega\,}
$$

**Step 9 — Directivity of the small loop.**
The radiation intensity is $U(\theta) \propto \sin^{2}\theta$ — *identical* to the Hertzian dipole. Following the same integration:

$$
\boxed{\,D_0^{\text{small loop}} = 1.5\ \ (\text{or } 1.76\ \text{dB})\,}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Numerical Sanity Check)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in the exam to cross-verify your numerical answers.** For $l = a = dz = \lambda/10$:

| Antenna | Effective Size | $R_r$ (Ohms) | $D_0$ (dB) | Power ratio (vs. Hertzian) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hertzian, $dz = \lambda/10$ | $\lambda/10$ | $80\pi^{2}(0.1)^{2} \approx 7.9\ \Omega$ | 1.76 | $1\times$ (reference) |
| Small dipole, $l = \lambda/10$ | $\lambda/10$ | $20\pi^{2}(0.1)^{2} \approx 1.97\ \Omega$ | 1.76 | $1/4$ (because $l/2 = dz/2$) |
| Small loop, $C = \lambda/10$ | $\lambda/10$ | $20\pi^{2}(0.1)^{4} \approx 0.0197\ \Omega$ | 1.76 | $\approx 1/400$ (loop is a *very* poor radiator) |

**Engineering takeaway:** A small loop is a *terrible* radiator because $R_r \propto a^{4}$ (very steep) — but it is an *excellent receiver*, because the same tiny $R_r$ implies a large quality factor $Q$ and sharp resonance, making it ideal for narrowband direction finding.
