# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Iterated Function System (IFS) — The Engine of Fractals

Every fractal antenna is generated by an **Iterated Function System (IFS)**, which is a finite set of **contractive affine transformations** $w_i: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^n$ of the form:

$$
w_i(\vec{x}) = A_i \vec{x} + \vec{b}_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N
$$

where:
- $A_i$ is a linear scaling/rotation matrix (with $\|A_i\| < 1$ for contraction)
- $\vec{b}_i$ is a translation vector
- $N$ is the number of generator functions

> [!NOTE]
> **Why IFS matters in antenna design:** Each iteration $N$ produces a new set of $N_w$ wire segments, increasing the **effective electrical length** without increasing the **bounding box (physical footprint)**. The current has to traverse a longer path, lowering the resonant frequency — this is the *miniaturization mechanism*.

## 2.2 Self-Similarity and Fractal Dimension — The Core Mathematics

A set $S$ is **self-similar** if it can be decomposed into $N$ copies of itself, each scaled by a factor $r$ where $0 < r < 1$. The **similarity (Hausdorff) dimension** $D_F$ is then defined by:

$$
D_F = \frac{\ln(N)}{\ln(1/r)}
$$

This is the single most important equation in fractal antenna design. Derivation and worked examples follow in Section 3.

## 2.3 The Two Engineering Drivers in Detail

### A. Miniaturization via Space-Filling
As $N \to \infty$, the fractal curve's length grows geometrically:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^N
$$

but the **bounding box dimension** stays constant. The resonant frequency therefore drops:

$$
f_0^{(N)} = \frac{c}{2 L_{\text{total}}} = \frac{c}{2 L_0} \cdot r^N
$$

> **Engineering utility:** A Koch fractal monopole of order 4 with $r = 1/3$ achieves the same resonance as a straight monopole at **$\approx 41\%$ of the original length** — proven in Cohen (2002).

### B. Multi-Band Operation via Self-Similarity
Because each scaled sub-copy resonates at a multiple of the base frequency, the input impedance $Z_{in}(f)$ exhibits log-periodic repetition. The $k$-th resonance occurs at:

$$
f_k = f_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^k, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots
$$

For Sierpinski gasket with $r = 1/2$: $f_k = 2^k f_0$ — a perfect **harmonic log-periodic** response.

## 2.4 Common Fractal Antenna Geometries

| Fractal Type | Generator $N$ copies | Scale $r$ | $D_F$ | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sierpinski Gasket** | 3 | 1/2 | 1.5850 | Multi-band log-periodic |
| **Koch Snowflake/Monopole** | 4 | 1/3 | 1.2619 | Maximum space-filling, miniaturization |
| **Minkowski Island** | 4 (ringed) | 1/4 | 1.5000 | Reduced ground plane size |
| **Hilbert Curve** | 4 | 1/2 | 2.0000 | Strong mutual coupling, RFID |
| **Cantor Strip** | 2 | 1/3 | 0.6309 | Edge-feeding, CPW-fed multiband |
| **Peano Curve** | 9 | 1/3 | 2.0000 | Maximum 2D space-filling |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers 90% of KTU numerical questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_F = \ln(N)/\ln(1/r)$ | Similarity dimension | Identifying fractal order |
| 2 | $L_{\text{total}} = L_0 (1/r)^N$ | Total length after $N$ iterations | Miniaturization calc |
| 3 | $f_0^{(N)} = f_0^{(0)} \cdot r^N$ | Resonant frequency scaling | Miniaturization calc |
| 4 | $f_k = f_0 \cdot (1/r)^k$ | $k$-th resonance frequency | Multi-band design |
| 5 | $\lambda_{\text{eff}} = 2 L_{\text{total}}$ | Effective electrical wavelength | Resonant length |
| 6 | $\text{SR} = f^{(N)}/f^{(0)} = r^N$ | Size reduction ratio | Koch monopole sizing |
| 7 | $\text{BW} \approx f_H - f_L$ | Bandwidth at $-10$ dB return loss | Practical figure-of-merit |
| 8 | $D_T \le D_F \le D_E$ | Dimensionality bound | Validity check |
| 9 | $A_{\text{frac}} = A_0 \cdot (1/r)^{2N}$ | Area scaling (2D fractals) | Sierpinski patch |
| 10 | $R_{\text{rad}} = 80\pi^2 (L_{\text{eff}}/\lambda)^2$ | Radiation resistance | Small antenna limit |

**Units reminder:** All lengths in meters, $f$ in Hz, $D_F$ dimensionless, $R_{\text{rad}}$ in $\Omega$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fractal antennas are deployed commercially in:
- **Mobile handsets** (e.g., fractal PIFA in iPhone internal antennas)
- **RFID tags** at UHF (860–960 MHz) using Hilbert/Meander curves
- **GPS / GNSS** multi-band ceramic patch antennas (Sierpinski)
- **Wearable / body-area networks** — flexible Koch dipoles on textile substrates
- **5G mmWave arrays** — Sierpinski fractals for ultra-wideband response

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Similarity (Hausdorff) Dimension $D_F$

**Starting premise:** A self-similar set $S$ is the union of $N$ non-overlapping copies of itself, each scaled by factor $r$ (with $0 < r < 1$).

**Step 1 — Apply the self-similar covering rule at scale $r$:**
$$
\text{Number of N-sized boxes covering } S = N
$$
$$
\text{Side length of each box} = r \cdot \epsilon \quad (\text{where } \epsilon \text{ is the original box side})
$$

**Step 2 — Generalize to the box-counting definition:**
To cover $S$ with boxes of side $r^n$ (after $n$ zoom steps), we need $N^n$ boxes. The box-counting dimension is:

$$
D_F = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\ln(N^n)}{\ln(1/r^n)} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \ln(N)}{n \ln(1/r)}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (since $n$ cancels):**

$$
\boxed{D_F = \frac{\ln(N)}{\ln(1/r)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical sanity check:**
- For $N=1$, any $r$ gives $D_F = 0$ (point set). ✓
- For $r \to 1$ (no scaling), $D_F \to \infty$. ✓
- For $N \to \infty$, $r \to 0$, $D_F \to \infty$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of $N$-th Iteration Koch Monopole

**Given:** Base straight monopole of height $h_0 = \lambda_0/4$ (quarter-wave).

**Step 1 — Koch generator rule:**
Each straight segment of length $L$ is replaced by 4 segments, each of length $L/3$. So per iteration: $L_{\text{new}} = (4/3) L_{\text{old}}$.

**Step 2 — Total length after $N$ iterations:**

$$
L_N = h_0 \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^N
$$

**Step 3 — Effective wavelength (quarter-wave resonance):**

$$
L_N = \frac{\lambda_N}{4} \implies \lambda_N = 4 h_0 \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^N
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to resonant frequency using $\lambda_N = c/f_N$:**

$$
f_N = \frac{c}{4 h_0} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^N
$$

**Step 5 — Size Reduction Ratio (SR):**

$$
\boxed{\text{SR} = \frac{f_N}{f_0} = \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^N}
$$

**Numerical verification for $N=4$:**
$$
\text{SR} = (0.75)^4 = 0.3164 \approx 32\%
$$
This matches the published result that a **4th-order Koch monopole** resonates at $\sim 32\%$ of the height of a straight quarter-wave monopole. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Koch Monopole Height Calculation (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** Design a Koch monopole antenna to resonate at **900 MHz** (GSM band). The base straight monopole height is $h_0 = \lambda_0/4$. Use **3 iterations**.

**Step 1 — Free-space wavelength:**

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{900 \times 10^6} = 0.3333 \text{ m} = 33.33 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Base height:**

$$
h_0 = \frac{\lambda_0}{4} = \frac{0.3333}{4} = 0.0833 \text{ m} = 8.33 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Length after $N=3$ iterations:**

$$
L_3 = h_0 \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^3 = 0.0833 \times \frac{64}{27} = 0.0833 \times 2.370 = 0.1975 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical height (vertical projection) of Koch monopole:**

Since the Koch generator has 3 of 4 segments vertical and 1 horizontal "bump," the projected height is:

$$
H_3 = L_3 \cdot \frac{3}{4} = 0.1975 \times 0.75 = 0.1481 \text{ m} \approx 14.8 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 5 — Reduction ratio vs. straight monopole:**

$$
\text{Reduction} = 1 - \frac{H_3}{h_0} = 1 - \frac{14.8}{8.33} \times \text{(factor)} \rightarrow \text{Electrical length grew } 137\%
$$

The straight monopole would need $8.33$ cm of height for the *same* resonance; the Koch *electrical* length is 137% longer while its *vertical footprint* is comparable — meaning the antenna has been "folded" into a tighter space.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Sierpinski Gasket Resonances (Multi-Band)

> **Problem:** A 2nd-iteration Sierpinski gasket patch resonates at $f_0 = 1$ GHz. Find the next **two** higher resonances.

**Step 1 — Identify $N$ and $r$:**
Sierpinski gasket: $N = 3$ sub-copies, scale $r = 1/2$.

**Step 2 — Apply multi-band frequency formula:**

$$
f_k = f_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^k = 1 \text{ GHz} \cdot (2)^k
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $k=1$ and $k=2$:**

$$
f_1 = 1 \times 2^1 = 2 \text{ GHz} \quad \text{(DCS-1800 / 3G)}
$$
$$
f_2 = 1 \times 2^2 = 4 \text{ GHz} \quad \text{(C-band / WiFi)}
$$

**Step 4 — Log-periodic verification:**
$\log_2(f_k)$: $\{0, 1, 2\}$ — a perfect arithmetic progression. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Sierpinski gasket is *mathematically* the cleanest log-periodic fractal antenna, which is why it dominates the research literature on multi-band patch design.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Identifying Fractal Dimension (Box-Counting)

> **Problem:** A fractal patch is composed of 8 self-similar copies, each scaled by $r = 1/4$. Find $D_F$ and verify it lies in the valid range for a 2D planar antenna.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
D_F = \frac{\ln(8)}{\ln(1/1/4)} = \frac{\ln(8)}{\ln(4)} = \frac{3 \ln 2}{2 \ln 2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5
$$

**Step 2 — Validity check:**
For 2D embedding with $D_T = 1$ (curve): $1 < 1.5 < 2$ ✓

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation:** A dimension of **1.5** indicates the structure fills approximately 50% more "electrical area" than a 1D curve but is not a true 2D surface — a balanced trade-off between bandwidth and surface-wave losses.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Guide

| Design Goal | Recommended Fractal | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum size reduction | **Koch monopole** | $(4/3)^N$ length growth per iter |
| Cleanest multi-band | **Sierpinski gasket** | Exact log-periodic: $f_k = 2^k f_0$ |
| Circular polarization + multi-band | **Sierpinski patch** with truncated tip | Degenerate modes |
| Compact UHF RFID | **Hilbert curve meander** | Best 2D space-filling |
| Ultra-wideband (UWB) | **Koch snowflake** | Smooth impedance variation |
| Wearable / textile | **Fractal meander (Minkowski)** | Flexible, low profile |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Total: 6 marks | Time: ~6 min | KTU University Exam Pattern**

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a fractal antenna. List its two key properties used in antenna engineering.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):**
> A **fractal antenna** is a radiating element whose geometry is generated by recursively applying a mathematical procedure (Iterated Function System — IFS) to a base shape, resulting in a structure with a **non-integer Hausdorff dimension** $D_F$ where $D_T < D_F < D_E$.

> **Two Key Properties (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Self-Similarity:** The structure looks identical at multiple scale levels, enabling **multi-band operation** with log-periodic resonance at $f_k = f_0 (1/r)^k$.
> 2. **Space-Filling Property:** The curve can be made to densely fill a 2D area as the iteration number $N \to \infty$, enabling **miniaturization** — same electrical length packed into a smaller physical footprint.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] A fractal curve is composed of 6 self-similar copies, each scaled by a factor of $r = 1/3$. Find its Hausdorff dimension and state whether it is a valid 1D curve embedded in 2D space.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply the dimension formula (2 marks):**

$$
D_F = \frac{\ln(N)}{\ln(1/r)} = \frac{\ln(6)}{\ln(1/(1/3))} = \frac{\ln(6)}{\ln(3)} = \frac{1.7918}{1.0986} \approx 1.6310
$$

**Step 2 — Validity check (1 mark):**
For a curve ($D_T = 1$) embedded in 2D ($D_E = 2$):
$$
D_T = 1 < D_F = 1.6310 < D_E = 2 \quad \checkmark
$$
Hence, it is a **valid 1D fractal curve** with significant 2D area-filling tendency.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Total: 14 marks | Time: ~25 min | KTU ESE Module Pattern**
> *Note: KTU ESE Module pattern provides internal choice between two questions on the same module. Below are two alternatives.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Iterated Function System (IFS)** approach to generate fractal antenna geometries. Derive the **similarity dimension** formula and compute $D_F$ for a **Koch curve** (4 self-similar copies, scale factor $r = 1/3$). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Koch snowflake monopole antenna** is to be designed for **2.4 GHz** WLAN operation. The base (0-th iteration) straight monopole has height $h_0 = \lambda_0/4$. Calculate:
   (i) The base height $h_0$ in cm.  
   (ii) The total wire length after **3 iterations**.  
   (iii) The resonant frequency ratio $f_3 / f_0$ and the **size reduction** achieved.  
   (iv) The second resonant frequency in the multi-band log-periodic response. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — IFS and Koch Dimension (7 marks)

**Block diagram of IFS-based fractal generation (3 marks):**

The IFS procedure consists of the following steps:

1. **Choose base initiator geometry** (e.g., a straight line segment $S_0$).
2. **Define $N$ contractive affine transformations** $w_i(\vec{x}) = A_i \vec{x} + \vec{b}_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$, each with scaling factor $r_i = \|A_i\| < 1$.
3. **Apply all $N$ transformations to the initiator** to obtain the first iteration $S_1 = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} w_i(S_0)$.
4. **Recursively apply** the same $N$ transformations to each sub-segment of $S_1$ to get $S_2, S_3, \dots, S_N$.
5. **Verify self-similarity:** $S_{k+1} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} w_i(S_k)$ for all $k \ge 0$.

The **Koch curve generator** is the canonical example:
- $N = 4$ transformations, each with $r = 1/3$
- Three of the four sub-segments are collinear with the original, and one forms the "bump" at 60°.

**Derivation of the similarity dimension (3 marks):**

A self-similar set $S$ is covered by $N$ sub-copies of itself, each scaled by $r$. After $n$ recursive steps, the number of sub-copies is $N^n$ and each has linear scale $r^n$. The box-counting dimension is:

$$
D_F = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\ln(N^n)}{\ln(1/r^n)} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \ln N}{n \ln(1/r)} = \frac{\ln N}{\ln(1/r)}
$$

**Computation for Koch curve (1 mark):**

$$
D_F^{\text{Koch}} = \frac{\ln(4)}{\ln(1/(1/3))} = \frac{\ln 4}{\ln 3} = \frac{1.3863}{1.0986} \approx 1.2619
$$

Since $1 < 1.2619 < 2$, it is a valid 1D fractal curve. ✓

---

#### Part (b) — Koch Snowflake Monopole Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Base height in cm (1.5 marks):**

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ cm}
$$

$$
h_0 = \frac{\lambda_0}{4} = \frac{12.5}{4} = 3.125 \text{ cm}
$$

**(ii) Total wire length after 3 iterations (2 marks):**

The Koch generator multiplies the segment length by $4/3$ per iteration. Therefore:

$$
L_3 = h_0 \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^3 = 3.125 \times \frac{64}{27} = 3.125 \times 2.3704 = 7.41 \text{ cm}
$$

**(iii) Resonant frequency ratio and size reduction (2.5 marks):**

For a fixed wire length, $f \propto 1/L$. Hence:

$$
\frac{f_3}{f_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_3} = \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^3 = \frac{27}{64} = 0.4219
$$

Equivalently, $f_3 = 0.4219 \times 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 1.0125$ GHz.

**Size reduction (interpretation):** The same physical height $h_0 = 3.125$ cm, when fractally iterated to order 3, would now resonate at 1.0125 GHz instead of 2.4 GHz — equivalent to replacing a $\lambda/4 = 7.8$ cm straight monopole at 1 GHz with a Koch monopole of only 3.125 cm height (a **~60% size reduction**).

**(iv) Second resonant frequency in the log-periodic response (1 mark):**

For the Koch monopole, the second resonance is approximately at:

$$
f_2 \approx f_0 \cdot \left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^1 = 2.4 \times 1.333 = 3.2 \text{ GHz} \quad (\text{approx.})
$$

(More precisely, due to the self-similar sub-copies being scaled by $r = 1/3$, the next sub-resonance occurs at $\sim 3.6$ GHz based on empirical simulations.)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **self-similarity** and **space-filling** properties of fractal antennas. Discuss how each property is exploited in **miniaturization** and **multi-band antenna design** with relevant equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Sierpinski gasket patch antenna** has its first resonance at **1.8 GHz** (GSM-1800). Use the **similarity dimension** formula and the **multi-band frequency scaling** to find:
   (i) The Hausdorff dimension $D_F$ of the Sierpinski gasket.  
   (ii) The 2nd, 3rd, and 4th resonant frequencies in the log-periodic response.  
   (iii) The total number of sub-triangles and the cumulative wire length after **3 iterations**, given the initial equilateral triangle side $a_0 = 5$ cm. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Self-Similarity and Space-Filling (7 marks)

**Self-Similarity (3.5 marks):**
A fractal set $S$ is **self-similar** if it can be expressed as a union of $N$ scaled copies of itself:

$$
S = \bigcup_{i=1}^{N} w_i(S), \quad \text{where} \quad w_i(\vec{x}) = r \vec{x} + \vec{t}_i
$$

In antenna engineering, self-similarity is exploited in **multi-band design**: since the entire structure contains scaled replicas of itself, the input impedance $Z_{in}(f)$ exhibits log-periodic repetition. The $k$-th resonance occurs at:

$$
f_k = f_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{r}\right)^k
$$

For Sierpinski ($r = 1/2$): $f_k = 2^k f_0$ — harmonics at $\{1, 2, 4, 8, \dots\} f_0$.

**Space-Filling (3.5 marks):**
A space-filling curve's total length grows geometrically with iteration count $N$:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_0 \cdot (1/r)^N
$$

The **bounding box (footprint)** remains constant, so the resonant frequency drops:

$$
f_0^{(N)} = \frac{c}{2 L_{\text{total}}} = f_0^{(0)} \cdot r^N
$$

This is the **miniaturization mechanism**: same physical size, lower resonant frequency (or equivalently, same resonance in a smaller size). A 4th-order Koch monopole achieves a size reduction of $\sim 68\%$ over its straight counterpart.

---

#### Part (b) — Sierpinski Gasket Patch Numericals (7 marks)

**(i) Hausdorff dimension of Sierpinski gasket (2 marks):**

For Sierpinski: $N = 3$ sub-triangles, scale $r = 1/2$.

$$
D_F = \frac{\ln(3)}{\ln(1/(1/2))} = \frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.0986}{0.6931} \approx 1.5850
$$

**(ii) Resonant frequencies (2.5 marks):**

$$
f_k = f_0 \cdot 2^k, \quad f_0 = 1.8 \text{ GHz}
$$

$$
f_1 = 1.8 \times 2 = 3.6 \text{ GHz} \quad \text{(WiFi / 5G sub-6)}
$$
$$
f_2 = 1.8 \times 4 = 7.2 \text{ GHz} \quad \text{(X-band)}
$$
$$
f_3 = 1.8 \times 8 = 14.4 \text{ GHz} \quad \text{(Ku-band)}
$$

**(iii) Total sub-triangles and wire length after 3 iterations (2.5 marks):**

Number of smallest triangles at iteration $k$: $N_k = 3^k$.

$$
N_3 = 3^3 = 27 \text{ sub-triangles}
$$

Side length of each smallest triangle: $a_3 = a_0 \cdot (1/2)^3 = 5 \times 0.125 = 0.625$ cm.

**Total perimeter (= cumulative wire length in 2D patch context):**

$$
P_3 = N_3 \cdot 3 \cdot a_3 = 27 \times 3 \times 0.625 = 50.625 \text{ cm}
$$

(Compare to the original perimeter: $P_0 = 3 a_0 = 15$ cm. The cumulative boundary grows by factor $(3/2)^3 = 3.375$.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls That Cost 2–3 Marks Per Question**
>
> 1. **Confusing $D_F$ with $D_E$:** Students often write $D_F$ as an integer (1 or 2). Always show the **logarithmic computation** explicitly and quote $D_F$ to 4 decimal places.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the scale factor in $L_{\text{total}}$:** A common error is $L_N = N \cdot L_0$ instead of $L_N = L_0 (1/r)^N$. The growth is **geometric**, not linear.
>
> 3. **Wrong projection of Koch height:** When a vertical monopole is Koch-iterated, only **3 of 4 segments** lie in the original vertical direction. The vertical projection is $H_N = L_N \cdot (3/4)$, not $L_N$.
>
> 4. **Mixing up multi-band formula:** For Sierpinski, $f_k = 2^k f_0$ (since $1/r = 2$). For Koch, the self-similar sub-copies are at $1/r = 3$, but practical Koch antennas do not produce clean log-periodic multi-bands due to current discontinuity at corners. **Always state the geometry when using the formula.**
>
> 5. **No units in numerical answers:** KTU strictly deducts 0.5 mark for missing units (cm, MHz, etc.) in numerical sub-parts.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Fractal** = non-integer Hausdorff dimension, self-similar, space-filling
- ✅ **Master formula:** $D_F = \ln(N) / \ln(1/r)$ — must be memorized
- ✅ **Length growth:** $L_N = L_0 (1/r)^N$ (geometric, not linear)
- ✅ **Frequency scaling:** $f_N = f_0 \cdot r^N$ (miniaturization) and $f_k = f_0 (1/r)^k$ (multi-band)
- ✅ **Sierpinski gasket:** $N=3$, $r=1/2$, $D_F = 1.5850$ — best for clean multi-band
- ✅ **Koch curve:** $N=4$, $r=1/3$, $D_F = 1.2619$ — best for miniaturization
- ✅ **Minkowski:** $N=4$, $r=1/4$, $D_F = 1.5$ — best for compact ground planes
- ✅ **Hilbert:** $D_F = 2$ — maximum 2D space-filling, used in RFID
- ✅ **Benoit Mandelbrot** coined the term (1975); **Nathan Cohen** built the first fractal antenna (1988)
- ✅ **IFS** = Iterated Function System — generator of all deterministic fractals
- ✅ **Self-similarity** → multi-band; **Space-filling** → miniaturization
- ✅ **Bounding box** stays constant; only electrical length grows with iterations
- ✅ **Validity range:** $D_T < D_F < D_E$ (always check after computing $D_F$)
- ✅ **Free-space constants to memorize:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\eta_0 = 377 \; \Omega$
- ✅ **Empirical result to remember:** 4th-order Koch monopole ≈ 32% size of straight monopole
- ✅ **Practical iteration limit:** $N = 0$ to $5$ (beyond $N=5$, gain drops and fabrication errors dominate)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Array Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Array Antennas ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > An **Antenna Array** is a spatially distributed assembly of two or more individual radiating elements (antennas), geometrically configured and electrically excited with specific relative amplitudes and progressive phase shifts, such that the fields radiated by the individual elements superpose in the far-field region to produce a desired radiation pattern with enhanced directivity, gain, and shaped beam characteristics. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a group of five people standing in a straight line, each shouting the same message in the exact same direction. If they all shout at the same instant with equal volume, the sound waves will **add constructively** directly in front of the line, producing a louder, more directional sound — almost like a "spotlight of voice." If you delay the voice of each person slightly so the wavefronts align perfectly outward, you can aim the sound precisely forward (broadside) or along the line (end-fire). An antenna array does precisely this — but with **electromagnetic (EM) waves** instead of sound. By controlling: - The **geometry** (linear, planar, circular placement) - The **excitation amplitude** ($A_n$) of each element - The **progressive phase** ($\beta$) between elements …we can shape, steer, and sharpen the radiated beam electronically — without physically rotating the antenna. This is the foundation of **smart antennas, radar, 5G MIMO, and satellite communication**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Syllabus Highlights:** > - The radiation pattern of an array is governed by the **Pattern Multiplication Theorem**. > - **Total Field = Element Pattern × Array Factor** > - Only **identical** and **equally oriented** elements allow pattern multiplication. > - Two major classical array types: **Broadside** and **End-fire**. ### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - Free-space impedance: $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **$\Omega$** - Wavelength relation: $\lambda = c/f$ - Element spacing usually expressed as a fraction of $\lambda$, e.g., $d = 0.5\lambda$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The N-Element Linear Array — Conceptual Framework Consider **N identical isotropic radiators** placed along the z-axis with uniform spacing $d$. Each element is fed with a current of amplitude $A_n$ and progressive phase shift $\beta$. **Step-by-step logic for the array factor derivation:** 1. **Far-field assumption:** The observer is at distance $r \gg d$, so the path difference between adjacent elements is geometrically $d\cos\theta$. 2. **Phase difference** introduced by this path delay is $\psi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} d \cos\theta$. 3. **Progressive phase excitation** adds an additional controlled phase $\beta$ between successive elements. 4. **Net phase difference** between adjacent elements is therefore: $\xi = \psi - \beta$. 5. **Superposition:** The total field is the phasor sum of contributions from all $N$ elements. ### 2.2 Array Factor (AF) — The Core Equation $$AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_n \, e^{j n \xi}$$ When all elements are uniformly excited ($A_n = 1$): $$AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \xi} = \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \, e^{j(N-1)\xi/2}$$ > [!NOTE] > The magnitude of the Array Factor is the **normalized radiation intensity pattern** of the array. $$ \vert AF \vert = \frac{1}{N} \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \right\vert $$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Notes** | |---|---|---| | Phase from path | $\psi = \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta$ | Geometric phase difference | | Net phase per element | $\xi = \psi - \beta$ | Includes progressive phase $\beta$ | | Array Factor (uniform) | $AF = \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} e^{j(N-1)\xi/2}$ | Geometric series closed form | | Normalized AF magnitude | $AF_n = \frac{1}{N}\left\vert \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \right\vert$ | Range: 0 → 1 | | Maxima condition | $\xi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \dots$ | Principal maximum | | Broadside condition | $\beta = 0$, max at $\theta = 90°$ | $\psi = 0$ at broadside | | End-fire condition | $\beta = \pm kd$ | Max along axis $\theta = 0°$ or $180°$ | | HPBW (approx) | $HPBW \approx \frac{0.886 \lambda}{Nd \cos\theta_0}$ (rad) | In radians, broadside | | First-null beamwidth | $FNBW = \frac{2\lambda}{Nd}$ (rad) | Between first nulls | | Directivity (broadside) | $D_0 \approx \frac{2N d}{\lambda}$ | For uniform broadside | | Directivity (end-fire) | $D_0 \approx \frac{4N d}{\lambda}$ | Twice broadside for same N | | Binomial coefficients | $A_n = \binom{N-1}{n}$ | For binomial array | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipes (`|`) are used inside table cells**; absolute values use $\vert ... \vert$ to preserve markdown structure. ### 2.4 Why Arrays? — Engineering Utility - **Radar Systems:** Electronic beam steering in phased-array radars (no mechanical motion). - **5G/6G Base Stations:** Massive MIMO arrays for spatial multiplexing and beamforming. - **Satellite Communications:** Shaped beams to concentrate energy on a specific geographic region. - **Radio Astronomy:** Aperture synthesis by combining distant antennas. - **Direction Finding:** Estimating angle-of-arrival (AoA) of incoming signals. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Array Factor of a Uniform N-Element Linear Array **Given:** N isotropic radiators along the z-axis, uniform spacing $d$, uniform amplitude $A_n = 1$, progressive phase $\beta$. **Step 1 — Field contribution of the n-th element** For an observer at far-field point $P$ making angle $\theta$ with the array axis, the distance from the n-th element is approximately $r_n = r - n d \cos\theta$. **Step 2 — Phase introduced by path delay** $$ \text{Phase from } n\text{-th element} = e^{-j k r_n} = e^{-j k r} \cdot e^{+j k n d \cos\theta} $$ **Step 3 — Adding the excitation phase** Each element carries progressive phase $e^{+j n \beta}$ (conjugate convention). Combining: $$ \text{Field from } n\text{-th element} \propto e^{+j n (k d \cos\theta + \beta)} $$ **Step 4 — Define the net phase increment** $$ \xi = k d \cos\theta + \beta = \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta + \beta $$ **Step 5 — Total Array Factor (sum of geometric series)** $$ AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{j n \xi} = 1 + e^{j\xi} + e^{j 2\xi} + \dots + e^{j(N-1)\xi} $$ **Step 6 — Multiply by $e^{j\xi}$ to use the geometric series trick** $$ AF(1 - e^{j N \xi}) = 1 - e^{j N \xi} $$ (After multiplying by $(1 - e^{j\xi})$). The sum collapses to: $$ AF = \frac{1 - e^{j N \xi}}{1 - e^{j \xi}} $$ **Step 7 — Apply Euler's identity** Using $1 - e^{j\alpha} = 2 \sin(\alpha/2) e^{j(\alpha - \pi)/2}$: $$ AF = \frac{2 \sin(N\xi/2) \, e^{j(N\xi - \pi)/2}}{2 \sin(\xi/2) \, e^{j(\xi - \pi)/2}} $$ **Step 8 — Final compact form** $$ \boxed{\, AF = \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \, e^{j(N-1)\xi/2} \,} $$ **Step 9 — Magnitude (the observable radiation pattern)** $$ \vert AF \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \right\vert $$ The phase factor $e^{j(N-1)\xi/2}$ represents a constant phase reference and does not affect the magnitude. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Conditions for Principal Maximum, Nulls, and Secondary Maxima **Principal maximum condition:** The denominator $\sin(\xi/2)$ must be zero while the numerator remains finite, i.e., $$ \xi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \dots, \pm 2 m \pi $$ At these angles, all N phasors add in phase. The array factor magnitude equals $N$ (its maximum). **First null condition:** Numerator must vanish while denominator is non-zero: $$ \frac{N\xi}{2} = \pm \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \xi = \pm \frac{2\pi}{N} $$ **First null beamwidth (FNBW):** Setting $\xi = \pm 2\pi/N$ for a broadside array ($\beta = 0$): $$ \frac{2\pi d}{\lambda}\cos\theta_1 = \pm \frac{2\pi}{N} $$ $$ \cos\theta_1 = \pm \frac{\lambda}{N d} $$ $$ \boxed{\, FNBW = 2\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta_1\right) = 2\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{Nd}\right) \,} $$ For $Nd \gg \lambda$, the small-angle approximation gives: $$ FNBW \approx \frac{2\lambda}{Nd} \text{ (radians)} $$ **Secondary maxima (grating lobes):** Occur at $$ \frac{N\xi}{2} = \pm \frac{3\pi}{2}, \pm \frac{5\pi}{2}, \dots $$ giving maxima of magnitude $1/N$ (about $-13.5$ dB below principal max for very large N). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pattern Multiplication Theorem If the elements of an array are not isotropic but have their own element pattern $EP(\theta, \phi)$, and the array is symmetric with identical, equally-oriented elements, then: **The total field of the array is the product of the element pattern and the array factor.** $$ \boxed{\, E_{\text{total}}(\theta, \phi) = EP(\theta, \phi) \times AF(\theta, \phi) \,} $$ **Why it works:** Consider element pattern expressed as a polynomial in $\cos\theta$ — for a linear array, the total field is the product of a geometric series (the array factor) and the element's spatial response. When all elements share the same orientation and feed, the geometry of superposition preserves the multiplicative structure. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Directivity of a Broadside Uniform Linear Array The directivity in the direction of the principal maximum ($\theta_0 = 90°$ for broadside) is: $$ D_0 = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ For a broadside uniform array with $Nd \gg \lambda$ and narrow beam, the solid angle of the main beam can be approximated as: $$ \Omega_A \approx \frac{\lambda^2}{N d} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \text{(rectangular beam approximation)} $$ After detailed integration of the normalized array factor (omitted here for brevity, but standard in Balanis): $$ \boxed{\, D_0 \approx \frac{2 N d}{\lambda} \quad \text{(Broadside)} \,} $$ For an end-fire array with the Hansen-Woodyard condition ($\beta = -kd - \pi/N$): $$ \boxed{\, D_0 \approx \frac{4 N d}{\lambda} \quad \text{(End-fire, H-W)} \,} $$ End-fire yields **twice** the broadside directivity for the same $N$ and $d$, because the beam is unidirectional in only one hemisphere. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Geometry of an N-Element Linear Array ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source 1] --> B[Element 1
z = 0] A1[Source 2 phase beta] --> C[Element 2
z = d] A2[Source 3 phase 2beta] --> D[Element 3
z = 2d] A3[Source N phase Nminus1 beta] --> E[Element N
z = N minus 1 times d] B --> F[Far Field Point P
angle theta] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[Superposed Field
E total = EP times AF] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Array Antenna System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FEED[Feed Network] S[Signal Source] --> PS[Phase Shifter Bank] PS --> A1[Amp 1] PS --> A2[Amp 2] PS --> A3[Amp N] end subgraph ARRAY[Antenna Array] A1 --> AE1[Element 1] A2 --> AE2[Element 2] A3 --> AEN[Element N] end subgraph FAR[Far Field Region] AE1 --> FF[Superposed EM Field] AE2 --> FF AEN --> FF FF --> PAT[Shaped Radiation Pattern] end ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree: Broadside vs End-Fire vs Phased ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Where is the
principal max desired?} Q1 -- Perpendicular to axis theta equals 90 --> BS[Broadside Array
beta equals 0
psi equals 0] Q1 -- Along the axis theta equals 0 or 180 --> EF[End-fire Array
beta equals plus or minus kd] Q1 -- At arbitrary angle theta0 --> PH[Phased Array
beta equals minus k d cos theta0] BS --> R1[D approximately 2 N d over lambda] EF --> R2[D approximately 4 N d over lambda
Hansen Woodyard] PH --> R3[Electronic beam steering
theta0 sweep] ``` ### 4.4 Pattern Multiplication Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR EP[Element Pattern
sin theta] --> MUL[Multiplication] AF[Array Factor
sin N xi over 2 over sin xi over 2] --> MUL MUL --> TOT[Total Array Pattern
E total] TOT --> OUT[Main Lobe, Sidelobes, Nulls] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define an antenna array. What is the significance of progressive phase shift? **Model Answer (3 marks):** An antenna array is an assembly of two or more radiating elements arranged spatially and excited with controlled relative amplitudes and phases to achieve a desired radiation pattern. The progressive phase shift $\beta$ determines the direction of the main beam; by varying $\beta$, the beam can be electronically steered without physically moving the antenna. This enables **phased array operation**, which is fundamental in radar and 5G. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse **progressive phase $\beta$** (controlled excitation) with **spatial phase $\psi$** (geometry-induced). Always clearly distinguish the two; their difference $\xi$ is the key to array factor analysis. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** State the **Pattern Multiplication Theorem** and mention one condition under which it is valid. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Pattern Multiplication Theorem states that the total radiation pattern of an array is the product of the individual element pattern and the array factor: $$ E_{\text{total}}(\theta, \phi) = EP(\theta, \phi) \cdot AF(\theta, \phi) $$ **Condition:** The array must consist of *identical* and *equally oriented* radiating elements. If elements are dissimilar, the theorem does not hold in its simple multiplicative form. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with internal choice (QPS format). Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Broadside Array Analysis** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Derive the array factor of a uniform N-element linear array with spacing $d$ and progressive phase $\beta$. State the condition for the principal maximum. (7 marks) **(b)** A 4-element uniform broadside linear array has element spacing $d = \lambda/2$. Sketch the radiation pattern in polar form and determine the first-null beamwidth. Compute the directivity using the standard broadside formula. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Place N isotropic radiators along the z-axis. The n-th element is at position $z_n = nd$. **Step 2:** The far-field distance to a point at angle $\theta$ from the array axis is: $$ r_n \approx r - n d \cos\theta $$ **Step 3:** Field contribution from the n-th element (with excitation $A_n e^{j n \beta}$): $$ E_n \propto \frac{e^{-j k r_n}}{r_n} A_n e^{j n \beta} $$ **Step 4:** Factor out the common term and sum: $$ E_{\text{total}} \propto \frac{e^{-j k r}}{r} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_n e^{j n (k d \cos\theta + \beta)} $$ **Step 5:** Define the net inter-element phase: $$ \xi = k d \cos\theta + \beta $$ **Step 6:** The Array Factor is therefore: $$ AF = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} A_n e^{j n \xi} $$ **Step 7:** For uniform excitation $A_n = 1$, the geometric series yields: $$ AF = \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \, e^{j(N-1)\xi/2} $$ **Step 8:** Principal maximum occurs when: $$ \xi = 0, \pm 2\pi, \pm 4\pi, \dots, \pm 2m\pi $$ (All N phasors are perfectly in phase.) **(7 marks complete)** --- **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, broadside ($\beta = 0$), uniform excitation. **Step 1:** Net phase: $$ \xi = k d \cos\theta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \cos\theta = \pi \cos\theta $$ **Step 2:** Principal max at $\xi = 0 \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = 90°$ (broadside ✓). **Step 3:** First null condition: $$ \frac{N\xi}{2} = \pm \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \xi = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} $$ $$ \pi \cos\theta_1 = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta_1 = \pm \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 60°, 120° $$ **Step 4:** First-null beamwidth: $$ FNBW = 120° - 60° = 60° $$ **Step 5:** Directivity (broadside): $$ D_0 \approx \frac{2 N d}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 4 \times (\lambda/2)}{\lambda} = 4 $$ $$ D_0 \text{ (in dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(4) \approx 6.02 \text{ dB} $$ **Step 6:** Pattern sketch — 4 main lobes at $\theta = 90°$ with two minor sidelobes; nulls at $60°$ and $120°$; secondary maxima between principal and nulls at much lower magnitude ($1/N = 0.25$). **(7 marks complete)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — End-Fire Array & Pattern Multiplication** **(a)** Explain the principle of an end-fire array. Derive the condition for the progressive phase shift $\beta$ for an end-fire array, including the **Hansen–Woodyard condition** for enhanced directivity. (7 marks) **(b)** A 4-element linear array has element pattern $EP(\theta) = \sin\theta$. Element spacing is $\lambda/2$ and progressive phase $\beta = -\pi/2$. Using the pattern multiplication theorem, determine the directions of the main lobe and the first nulls of the total array pattern. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** An **end-fire array** has its principal maximum directed **along the axis of the array** ($\theta = 0°$ or $\theta = 180°$), as opposed to broadside ($\theta = 90°$). **Step 2:** For principal max at $\theta = 0°$, we need $\xi = 0$ at $\theta = 0°$: $$ \xi = k d \cos 0 + \beta = k d + \beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \beta = -k d $$ **Step 3:** For max at $\theta = 180°$: $\cos 180° = -1 \Rightarrow \beta = +k d$. **Step 4:** Standard end-fire condition: $\beta = \pm kd$ (or equivalently $\beta = \pm 2\pi d/\lambda$). **Step 5:** **Hansen–Woodyard enhanced end-fire condition** shifts the maximum slightly off-axis to optimize directivity: $$ \beta = -\left(k d + \frac{\pi}{N}\right) \quad \text{(for max at } \theta = 0°) $$ This gives a directivity of $D_0 \approx 4Nd/\lambda$, which is **twice** the broadside value. **Step 6:** Trade-off: H-W condition increases directivity but **raises the first sidelobe level** (often to about $-3$ dB), whereas standard end-fire has lower sidelobes (around $-13$ dB). **(7 marks complete)** --- **Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, $\beta = -\pi/2$, $EP(\theta) = \sin\theta$. **Step 1:** Compute the net phase: $$ \xi = k d \cos\theta + \beta = \pi \cos\theta - \frac{\pi}{2} $$ **Step 2:** Principal max condition: $\xi = 0$: $$ \pi \cos\theta - \frac{\pi}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 60° $$ **Step 3:** Array factor magnitude (un-normalized): $$ \vert AF \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin(2\xi)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \right\vert $$ **Step 4:** First null of AF: $N\xi/2 = \pm\pi \Rightarrow \xi = \pm\pi/2$: $$ \pi \cos\theta - \frac{\pi}{2} = \pm\frac{\pi}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = 1 \text{ or } 0 $$ $$ \Rightarrow \theta = 0° \text{ or } 90° $$ **Step 5:** Apply pattern multiplication: $$ E_{\text{total}}(\theta) = \sin\theta \times \left\vert \frac{\sin(2\xi)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \right\vert $$ **Step 6:** The element pattern $\sin\theta$ has a **null at $\theta = 0°$** and **maximum at $\theta = 90°$**. **Step 7:** Therefore, the **total pattern's principal maximum is NOT at $\theta = 60°$** because $EP(60°) = \sin 60° = 0.866$, but the *true* max of $E_{\text{total}}$ is found where the product is largest. The nulls of the total pattern occur at: - $\theta = 0°$ (from element pattern, since $\sin 0 = 0$) - $\theta = 90°$ (from AF first null) The main beam is now **shifted and shaped** between $0°$ and $90°$, with the peak somewhere around $\theta = 60°$ to $75°$ depending on the product's exact maximum. **(7 marks complete)** --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Evaluations:** > 1. **Mixing up $\beta$ and $\psi$:** Many students write $\xi = \beta - \psi$ (sign error). Correct is $\xi = \psi - \beta$ in the convention where the field has $e^{+jk r_n}$. > 2. **Forgetting the phase factor** $e^{j(N-1)\xi/2}$ in AF. It doesn't affect magnitude but examiners expect awareness. > 3. **Confusing FNBW with HPBW:** First-Null Beamwidth spans the full lobe; Half-Power Beamwidth is narrower. > 4. **Directivity formula misuse:** Use $D = 2Nd/\lambda$ for broadside, $D = 4Nd/\lambda$ for end-fire (H-W) — *not* the other way around. > 5. **Pattern multiplication condition:** Do not apply it if elements are not identical or not equally oriented. > 6. **No units in final answers:** Always state FNBW in degrees/radians and directivity in dB or numeric. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Antenna Array:** Group of N elements with controlled amplitude and phase excitation. - **Array Factor (AF):** Phasor sum of N terms, $AF = \sum A_n e^{j n \xi}$. - **Uniform Linear Array (ULA) AF magnitude:** $\frac{1}{N} \left\vert \frac{\sin(N\xi/2)}{\sin(\xi/2)} \right\vert$. - **Net inter-element phase:** $\xi = (2\pi d/\lambda)\cos\theta + \beta$. - **Pattern Multiplication:** $E_{\text{total}} = EP \times AF$ (identical, equally-oriented elements). - **Broadside array:** $\beta = 0$, max at $\theta = 90°$, $D_0 \approx 2Nd/\lambda$. - **End-fire array:** $\beta = \pm kd$, max along axis, $D_0 \approx 4Nd/\lambda$ with H-W condition. - **First-Null Beamwidth (broadside):** $FNBW \approx 2\lambda/(Nd)$ radians. - **Hansen–Woodyard condition:** $\beta = -kd - \pi/N$; increases $D_0$ by 2× at the cost of higher SLL. - **Phased array:** Beam steered to arbitrary $\theta_0$ by setting $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$. - **Grating lobes:** Appear when $d > \lambda$, causing multiple principal maxima. - **Sidelobe Level (SLL):** For uniform array, first sidelobe is at $-13.5$ dB; for binomial array, no sidelobes; for Dolph–Tchebyscheff, SLL is a design parameter. - **Spacing rule of thumb:** Keep $d \leq \lambda$ to avoid grating lobes; $d = \lambda/2$ is the KTU-classical choice. - **Directivity increase:** Doubling $N$ or doubling $d$ both double the directivity (broadside). - **Practical arrays:** Used in radar (phased arrays), 5G (MIMO), satellite (shaped-beam), radio astronomy. - **KTU 2024 Exam weightage:** Module 1 typically carries 20–25% of total marks; array problems are 14-mark favorites. - **Remember the Balanis convention** for AF sign; KTU textbooks (Balanis, Kraus) follow $\xi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$. > [!TIP] > **Quick revision formula card:** For any uniform N-element array, the principal maximum occurs at $\xi = 0$, first nulls at $\xi = \pm 2\pi/N$, and the array factor is fully characterized by the single variable $\xi$. Memorize this — it solves 80% of KTU array problems.

topics.title: Binomial Array, Dolph Chebyshev Array, Electronic Beam Steering Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Binomial Array, Dolph Chebyshev Array, Electronic Beam Steering Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Binomial Array

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Binomial Array** is a linear broadside antenna array whose element excitation amplitudes follow the binomial coefficients (i.e., $(1, n, \frac{n(n-1)}{2}, \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)}{6}, \dots)$ ), arranged symmetrically about the array centre. The defining feature is that **all minor (side) lobes are completely eliminated**, leaving only the single main lobe whose HPBW is the widest possible for the given array length.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Volume Knob" Picture:**
Imagine a row of 5 speakers. If you wire them in such a way that the centre one is loudest and the edge ones are barely whispering (ratio $1:4:6:4:1$), you stop "leaking" sound sideways. The energy is forced to roll forward only. That is the essence of the Binomial Array — it pushes the side-lobe energy to **mathematical zero**.

- **Physical Insight:** The amplitudes mimic the coefficients of $(1+x)^n$, hence the name.
- **Side Lobe Level (SLL):** $-\infty$ dB (theoretically no side lobes).
- **Major Drawback:** For large $N$, the ratio between the central and edge currents becomes enormous (e.g., for $N=11$, ratio is $1:252$), making practical feeding networks nearly impossible.

---

### 1.2 The Dolph–Chebyshev Array

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Dolph–Chebyshev Array** (proposed by C. L. Dolph, 1946) is an optimally designed non-uniform linear array in which the element amplitudes are synthesised so that **all side lobes have an equal specified level** while the **beamwidth is the narrowest achievable** for that side-lobe level — or equivalently, the side-lobe level is minimised for a given beamwidth.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Sprinkler" Picture:**
Picture a garden sprinkler laid flat. A binomial array is like a sprinkler where water vanishes entirely sideways (wasted edges, huge centre jet). A Chebyshev sprinkler is *engineered*: it sprinkles an **even, controlled mist** on the sides while still firing a strong central jet. You choose how much mist is acceptable, and Chebyshev gives you the strongest central jet for that choice.

- **Optimality:** It is the *only* array that simultaneously minimises beamwidth for a fixed SLL — a Pareto-optimal design.
- **Trade-off Parameter:** The design SLL (in dB), usually chosen between **$-20$ dB** and **$-60$ dB** in practice.
- **Mathematical Tool:** Chebyshev polynomials of the first kind: $T_m(x) = \cos(m \cos^{-1} x)$.

---

### 1.3 Electronic Beam Steering Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** **Electronic Beam Steering** is the technique of redirecting the main lobe (maximum radiation direction) of an antenna array by varying the **progressive phase shift $\beta$** between successive elements, **without any mechanical rotation** of the physical antenna structure.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Mexican Wave" Picture:**
Imagine 100 people doing a Mexican wave in a stadium. By adjusting the *time delay* between adjacent people, the wave appears to travel at any angle you choose — even if the people are sitting still in a straight line. The radiated wavefront from an array behaves identically: by adding a small phase lag to each next element, the wavefront tilts, and the beam points in a new direction.

- **Steering Equation:** $\theta_0 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\dfrac{\beta}{kd}\right)$
- **No Inertia:** Switching speeds of microseconds (vs. seconds for mechanical gimbals).
- **Core Application:** Phased Array Radars (AESA), 5G MIMO base stations, satellite tracking.

---

### 1.4 Why These Three Topics Are Clustered Together

| Concept | Design Knob Used | Trade-off Solved |
|---|---|---|
| Binomial Array | Amplitude weighting | Eliminate side lobes completely |
| Dolph–Chebyshev Array | Amplitude weighting | Optimise beamwidth vs SLL |
| Electronic Beam Steering | Phase weighting | Point the optimised beam anywhere |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Array — Foundation Recap

For an $N$-element linear array along the $z$-axis with uniform spacing $d$, the **array factor** is:

$$AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} a_n \, e^{j n \psi}$$

where the **generalised angle variable** is:

$$\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$$

- $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ is the free-space wave number.
- $\beta$ is the **progressive phase shift** between adjacent elements.
- $a_n$ is the amplitude excitation of the $n$-th element.

### 2.2 Binomial Array — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Choose the polynomial.**
Use the binomial expansion $(1+x)^n$.

**Step 2 — Take the coefficients as excitation amplitudes.**
The amplitude of the $n$-th element (counting from edge) equals $\binom{N-1}{n}$.

**Step 3 — Verify the array factor.**
For a 4-element binomial array:

$$AF(\theta) = 1 + 3e^{j\psi} + 3e^{j2\psi} + e^{j3\psi} = (1+e^{j\psi})^3$$

**Step 4 — Identify the polynomial behaviour.**
$(1+e^{j\psi})^3$ has **no real-axis roots** other than the principal maxima — hence, **no minor lobes**.

**Step 5 — Calculate directivity.**
For a binomial array of length $L$, $D \approx 2\,\dfrac{L}{\lambda}$ (in linear scale, an extra factor of $\approx 1.5$ over uniform).

### 2.3 Dolph–Chebyshev Array — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the desired SLL ratio $R_0$.**
$$R_0 = 10^{\,\text{SLL}/20} \quad \text{(SLL is a negative dB value)}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Chebyshev polynomial scaling.**
Map the polynomial argument so that the array factor equals $T_{N-1}(z_0 \cos(\psi/2))$, where:
$$z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\frac{1}{N-1}\cosh^{-1}(R_0)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Expand and equate coefficients.**
Match the polynomial coefficients to $a_n e^{jn\psi}$ to obtain the excitation amplitudes.

**Step 4 — Realise the array factor.**

$$AF(\theta) = T_{N-1}\!\left(z_0 \cos\frac{\psi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Properties.**
- All minor lobes lie on a horizontal line at level $1/R_0$ in the polynomial space.
- Main lobe level = $T_{N-1}(z_0) = R_0$.
- HPBW is the minimum possible for the chosen SLL.

### 2.4 Electronic Beam Steering — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define desired scan angle $\theta_0$.**

**Step 2 — Solve for progressive phase $\beta$.**

$$\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$$

**Step 3 — Apply phase shifters.**
Each element receives a phase $n\beta$, implemented via digital phase shifters (e.g., 6-bit, 5.625° resolution).

**Step 4 — Validate scanning range.**
To avoid grating lobes, $d \le \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ for $\pm 90°$ scan; $d \le \dfrac{\lambda}{\sqrt{3}}$ for $\pm 60°$ scan.

**Step 5 — Account for beam squint (frequency-dependent).**

$$\Delta\theta = -\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}\tan\theta_0$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$ | Generalised angle variable | rad |
| 2 | $AF(\theta) = \sum a_n e^{jn\psi}$ | Array factor (complex) | — |
| 3 | $a_n = \binom{N-1}{n}$ | Binomial amplitude weights | A or V |
| 4 | $(1+e^{j\psi})^{N-1}$ | Binomial AF (closed form) | — |
| 5 | $T_m(x) = \cos(m\cos^{-1}x)$ | Chebyshev polynomial | — |
| 6 | $z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\frac{\cosh^{-1}R_0}{N-1}\right]$ | Chebyshev scaling factor | — |
| 7 | $R_0 = 10^{\,\text{SLL}/20}$ | Voltage SLL ratio | — |
| 8 | $AF = T_{N-1}(z_0\cos(\psi/2))$ | Chebyshev AF | — |
| 9 | $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$ | Steering phase | rad |
| 10 | $\theta_0 = \cos^{-1}(-\beta/kd)$ | Scan angle | rad or ° |
| 11 | $d \le \lambda/(1+\vert\sin\theta_m\vert)$ | Grating-lobe-free spacing | m |
| 12 | $HPBW_{\text{unif}} \approx 0.886\,\dfrac{\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_0}$ | HPBW after steering | rad |
| 13 | $D \approx \dfrac{2\,\text{SLL}_{lin}+1}{\text{SLL}_{lin}+1}\,N$ approx | Array directivity | linear |
| 14 | $G(\theta) = \dfrac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}$ | Gain via beam solid angle | dBi |
| 15 | $\Delta\theta = -\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}\tan\theta_0$ | Beam squint | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always convert SLL between dB and linear **voltage** ratio using $R_0 = 10^{\text{SLL}/20}$ (NOT $10^{\text{SLL}/10}$ — that is power ratio). A $20$ dB SLL corresponds to $R_0 = 0.1$.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Binomial Arrays** — Used in very low-interference radio astronomy front-ends and in precision direction-finding where the **complete absence** of grating lobes is non-negotiable.
- **Dolph–Chebyshev Arrays** — Industry standard for **side-lobe-controlled** radars (AWACS, FPS-132), satellite communication earth-station feeds, and modern 5G/6G base-station arrays.
- **Electronic Beam Steering** — The backbone of **AESA (Active Electronically Scanned Array) radars** (e.g., AN/APG-81 on F-35), 5G/6G beamforming MIMO, automotive mmWave radars (77 GHz), and low-earth-orbit satellite tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Array Factor of an $N$-Element Binomial Array

**Given:** $N$ isotropic elements with amplitudes $a_n = \binom{N-1}{n}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$ along the $z$-axis with spacing $d$.

**To Derive:** Show that the AF simplifies to $(1+e^{j\psi})^{N-1}$ and that all side lobes vanish.

**Step 1 — Write the array factor as a finite sum.**

$$AF(\theta) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \binom{N-1}{n} \, e^{jn\psi}$$

**Step 2 — Recognise the binomial expansion identity.**

$$(1+x)^{N-1} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \binom{N-1}{n} \, x^n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $x = e^{j\psi}$.**

$$(1+e^{j\psi})^{N-1} = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \binom{N-1}{n} \, e^{jn\psi}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the array factor.**

$$\boxed{\,AF(\theta) = (1+e^{j\psi})^{N-1}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Rewrite in trigonometric form to extract the pattern.**
Factor out $e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}$:

$$AF(\theta) = e^{j(N-1)\psi/2}\left[2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\psi}{2}\right)\right]^{N-1}$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude (since the pattern is real and non-negative for $\cos(\psi/2) \ge 0$):**

$$\vert AF(\theta)\vert = \left[2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\psi}{2}\right)\right]^{N-1}$$

**Step 7 — Identify zeros.**
$\vert AF(\theta)\vert = 0$ requires $\cos(\psi/2) = 0$, i.e., $\psi/2 = \pm \pi/2, \pm 3\pi/2, \dots$, giving $\psi = \pm\pi, \pm 3\pi, \dots$. But these correspond to points **opposite** to the broadside direction and are thus **suppressed** by the $(N-1)$-th power — no intermediate minor lobes exist. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Chebyshev Polynomial Recurrence and Side-Lobe Equalisation

**Step 1 — Define the Chebyshev polynomial of degree $m$.**

$$T_m(x) = \cos(m\cos^{-1}x), \quad \vert x\vert \le 1$$

$$T_m(x) = \cosh(m\cosh^{-1}x), \quad \vert x\vert > 1$$

**Step 2 — Recurrence relation.**

$$T_m(x) = 2x\,T_{m-1}(x) - T_{m-2}(x), \quad T_0 = 1, \; T_1 = x$$

**Step 3 — Compute the first few polynomials.**

$$T_2(x) = 2x^2 - 1$$

$$T_3(x) = 4x^3 - 3x$$

$$T_4(x) = 8x^4 - 8x^2 + 1$$

**Step 4 — Show that $\vert T_m(x)\vert \le 1$ for $\vert x\vert \le 1$ (oscillation property).**

Because $T_m(x) = \cos(m\phi)$ where $\phi = \cos^{-1}x \in [0,\pi]$, the cosine function is bounded by unity. Hence, in the interval $[-1, 1]$, the polynomial oscillates between $-1$ and $+1$ with exactly $m$ roots.

**Step 5 — Map the cosine variable to the array generalised angle.**
Let $x = z_0\cos(\psi/2)$ with $z_0 > 1$. Then:

- When $z_0\cos(\psi/2) \in [-1, 1]$, i.e., $-1/z_0 \le \cos(\psi/2) \le 1/z_0$, the polynomial oscillates — these are the **side-lobe region**.
- When $z_0\cos(\psi/2) > 1$, i.e., $\cos(\psi/2) > 1/z_0$, the polynomial grows monotonically — this is the **main-lobe region**.

**Step 6 — Set the side-lobe envelope level.**
All oscillations are bounded by $\pm 1$, so the side-lobe peak relative to main-lobe peak is $1/T_{N-1}(z_0)$. Thus:

$$R_0 = T_{N-1}(z_0)$$

**Step 7 — Invert to find $z_0$ for a desired SLL.**

$$\boxed{\,z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\frac{1}{N-1}\cosh^{-1}(R_0)\right]\,} \quad \text{where} \quad R_0 = 10^{\text{SLL}/20}$$

$\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Element Amplitudes for a 4-Element Chebyshev Array (SLL = $-20$ dB)

**Step 1 — Convert SLL to voltage ratio.**
$R_0 = 10^{-20/20} = 0.1$

**Step 2 — Compute $z_0$.**

$$z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\frac{\cosh^{-1}(0.1)}{3}\right] = \cosh\!\left[\frac{2.9922}{3}\right] = \cosh(0.9974) \approx 1.5089$$

**Step 3 — Form the array factor.**
For $N = 4$, $N-1 = 3$. The target polynomial is $T_3(x) = 4x^3 - 3x$. Substitute $x = z_0\cos(\psi/2)$:

$$AF(\psi) = 4z_0^3\cos^3(\psi/2) - 3z_0\cos(\psi/2)$$

**Step 4 — Expand in terms of multiple-angle cosines.**
Using the identity $\cos^3\alpha = \dfrac{1}{4}\cos 3\alpha + \dfrac{3}{4}\cos\alpha$:

$$AF(\psi) = z_0^3\cos(3\psi/2) + 3(z_0^3 - z_0)\cos(\psi/2)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation with $z_0 = 1.5089$.**

$$z_0^3 \approx 3.435$$

$$z_0^3 - z_0 \approx 1.926$$

**Step 6 — The pattern contains only $e^{j3\psi/2}$, $e^{j\psi/2}$, $e^{-j\psi/2}$, $e^{-j3\psi/2}$ terms — i.e., four terms corresponding to four elements. The amplitudes are symmetric:**

$$a_0 = a_3, \qquad a_1 = a_2$$

**Step 7 — Compute normalised amplitudes.**
From the coefficient matching, $a_{\text{centre}} : a_{\text{edge}} = (z_0^3 + 3z_0^3 - 3z_0) : z_0^3 = \text{derived from symmetric placement}$.

For a 4-element Chebyshev array with $-20$ dB SLL, the standard table gives:

$$a_1 : a_2 : a_3 : a_4 = 1.000 : 1.930 : 1.930 : 1.000$$

**Step 8 — Verify SLL.**
The side-lobe level is, by construction, $-20$ dB exactly. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Electronic Beam Steering Angle $\theta_0$

**Step 1 — Maximum of the array factor.**
The array factor peaks when all the $e^{jn\psi}$ terms add constructively, i.e., when $\psi = 0$:

$$kd\cos\theta_0 + \beta = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\theta_0$.**

$$\boxed{\,\cos\theta_0 = -\frac{\beta}{kd}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Recover the steering equation.**

$$\theta_0 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(-\frac{\beta}{kd}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Special cases.**
- $\beta = 0 \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 90°$ (broadside).
- $\beta = -kd \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 0°$ (end-fire, in the +$z$ direction).
- $\beta = +kd \Rightarrow \theta_0 = 180°$ (end-fire, in the $-z$ direction).

**Step 5 — Maximum unambiguous scan range.**
For no grating lobes, $d \le \lambda/2$ gives unambiguous scan over $\theta_0 \in [0°, 180°]$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Uniform Array | Binomial Array | Dolph–Chebyshev Array |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amplitude Ratio | $1:1:\dots:1$ | $\binom{N-1}{0}:\binom{N-1}{1}:\dots$ | Computed from $z_0$ and $T_{N-1}$ |
| Side Lobes | $\approx -13.2$ dB | $-\infty$ dB | Equal-level, designer-chosen |
| Beamwidth (for fixed $L$) | Narrowest | Widest | Narrowest *for chosen SLL* |
| Efficiency | Highest | Lowest | Intermediate |
| Practical $N$ | Any | $N \le 10$ typical | Any |
| Typical Application | Reference | Astronomy | Radar, Satcom |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Comparison of Amplitude Weighting Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Array Design Start] --> B{Design Goal?}
    B -->|Zero side lobes| C[Binomial Array]
    B -->|Controlled side lobes| D[Dolph-Chebyshev Array]
    B -->|Equal cost / simple| E[Uniform Array]

    C --> C1[Pick N elements]
    C1 --> C2[Amplitudes = C(N-1, n)]
    C2 --> C3[AF = 1 + e^(j psi) sup N-1]
    C3 --> C4[Side lobes = 0]

    D --> D1[Choose desired SLL in dB]
    D1 --> D2[R0 = 10 sup SLL/20]
    D2 --> D3[Compute z0 via cosh inverse]
    D3 --> D4[Expand T sub N-1 of z0 cos psi/2]
    D4 --> D5[Match coefficients to a sub n]
    D5 --> D6[All side lobes equal to SLL]

    E --> E1[All a sub n equal]
    E1 --> E2[SLL approx -13.2 dB]

    C4 --> F[Apply Phase Steering]
    D6 --> F
    E2 --> F

    F --> F1[Set target angle theta0]
    F1 --> F2[Compute beta = -kd cos theta0]
    F2 --> F3[Feed phase shifters]
    F3 --> F4[Beam points to theta0]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style F4 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#fff3b0,color:#000000
    style D fill:#cce5ff,color:#000000
    style E fill:#e0e0e0,color:#000000
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Electronic Beam Steering Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIG[Signal Source]
        TX[Transmitter / Receiver]
        LO[Local Oscillator]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Beam Steering Controller]
        CPU[Microcontroller / DSP]
        ANG[Target Angle Selector]
        PHASE[Phase Computation Block beta = -kd cos theta0]
    end

    subgraph ARRAY[Antenna Array]
        E1[Element 1 Phase Shifter 0 deg]
        E2[Element 2 Phase Shifter beta]
        E3[Element 3 Phase Shifter 2 beta]
        EN[Element N Phase Shifter N-1 times beta]
    end

    TX --> E1
    TX --> E2
    TX --> E3
    TX --> EN
    LO --> E1
    LO --> E2
    LO --> E3
    LO --> EN

    ANG --> CPU
    CPU --> PHASE
    PHASE --> E1
    PHASE --> E2
    PHASE --> E3
    PHASE --> EN

    E1 --> RAD[Radiated Wavefront Tilted by theta0]
    E2 --> RAD
    E3 --> RAD
    EN --> RAD

    style RAD fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style CPU fill:#4a148c,color:#ffffff
    style PHASE fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Chebyshev Polynomial Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Desired SLL dB and N] --> R0[R0 equals 10 sup SLL over 20]
    R0 --> AC[Compute cosh inverse R0]
    AC --> Z[Compute z0 equals cosh of AC over N-1]
    Z --> POL[Form polynomial T sub N-1 of z0 cos psi over 2]
    POL --> EXP[Expand using trig identities]
    EXP --> COEF[Match coefficients to a sub n e sup j n psi]
    COEF --> OUT[Final Excitation Vector a sub 1 ... a sub N]

    style S fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style OUT fill:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff
    style Z fill:#f57f17,color:#000000
```

---

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Beam Steering via Progressive Phase

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Array Broadside beam at 90 deg] --> B[Apply progressive phase beta]
    B --> C{Is beta zero?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Beam stays at 90 deg]
    C -->|No, beta negative| E[Beam tilts forward toward 0 deg]
    C -->|No, beta positive| F[Beam tilts backward toward 180 deg]

    E --> G{Spacing check}
    G -->|d less than lambda over 2| H[Grating lobe free scan]
    G -->|d greater than lambda over 2| I[Grating lobes appear warning]

    F --> G

    style A fill:#1a237e,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#ff6f00,color:#000000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the binomial array excitation coefficients for a 5-element linear array and explain why its side lobe level is theoretically $-\infty$ dB.*

**Model Answer:**
For $N = 5$ elements, the amplitudes are the binomial coefficients of $(1+x)^4 = 1 + 4x + 6x^2 + 4x^3 + x^4$.

$$\mathbf{a} = [1, 4, 6, 4, 1]$$

The array factor becomes $AF(\theta) = (1+e^{j\psi})^4$. Its magnitude is $\vert 2\cos(\psi/2)\vert^4$, which is non-negative everywhere and only touches zero when $\cos(\psi/2) = 0$. The zeros lie **only** at $\psi = \pm \pi$ (the back direction) and are raised to the 4th power, eliminating all intermediate minor lobes. Hence the SLL is theoretically $-\infty$ dB. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Write the array factor expression for a Dolph–Chebyshev array and define the role of the parameter $z_0$.*

**Model Answer:**
For an $N$-element Chebyshev array with progressive phase $\psi = kd\cos\theta + \beta$,

$$AF(\theta) = T_{N-1}\!\left(z_0 \cos\frac{\psi}{2}\right)$$

The parameter $z_0$ is the **scaling factor** that controls the trade-off between main-lobe width and side-lobe level. It is computed from the desired SLL:

$$z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\frac{\cosh^{-1}(R_0)}{N-1}\right], \qquad R_0 = 10^{\text{SLL}/20}$$

Larger $z_0$ (deeper SLL) yields a wider main lobe and vice versa. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Binomial Array Design & Beam Steering

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Design a 6-element binomial array with element spacing $d = \lambda/2$. Determine the normalised excitation amplitudes, plot the array factor (qualitative sketch), and compute its directivity.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the same 6-element array, calculate the progressive phase shift $\beta$ required to steer the beam to $\theta_0 = 60°$. State the constraint on $d$ to avoid grating lobes for full hemispherical scan.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Binomial coefficients for $N=6$:**
$(1+x)^5 = 1 + 5x + 10x^2 + 10x^3 + 5x^4 + x^5$

$$\mathbf{a} = [1, 5, 10, 10, 5, 1]$$

**Step 2 — Normalise to peak amplitude 1:**
Divide by 10:

$$\mathbf{a}_{norm} = [0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.0, 0.5, 0.1]$$

**Step 3 — Array factor:**
$$AF(\theta) = \left[2\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\psi}{2}\right)\right]^{5}, \qquad \psi = \pi\cos\theta$$

**Step 4 — Main lobe at $\theta = 90°$**, $\psi = 0$, $AF_{max} = 2^5 = 32$.

**Step 5 — HPBW:** Find where $AF = 0.707 \times 32 = 22.6$:
$$2\cos(\psi/2) = 22.6^{1/5} = 1.766 \Rightarrow \cos(\psi/2) = 0.883 \Rightarrow \psi/2 = 28°$$
$$\psi = 56° \Rightarrow \cos\theta = 56°/180° = 0.312 \Rightarrow \theta \approx 71.8°$$

$$HPBW = 180° - 2(71.8°) \approx 36.4°$$

**Step 6 — Directivity** (Balanis approximation for binomial):
$$D \approx \frac{2(N-1) + 1}{1} = 11 \quad \text{or} \quad 10.4 \text{ dBi}$$

*(More accurately, $D \approx 1.5 \times \dfrac{2L}{\lambda} = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 = 8.75$ dBi.)* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Steering equation:**

$$\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0 = -\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\cdot\frac{\lambda}{2}\cos(60°) = -\pi \cdot 0.5 = -\frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad} = -90°$$

**Step 2 — Progressive phase per element:** $\Delta\phi = \beta = -90°$.

**Step 3 — Required element phases (left to right):**
$$\phi_n = n\beta = 0°, -90°, -180°, -270°, -360°, -450°$$

**Step 4 — Grating-lobe-free condition:**
For a scan range $\pm 90°$ (full hemisphere), $d \le \lambda/2$. General condition:

$$d \le \frac{\lambda}{1 + \vert\sin\theta_m\vert}$$

For $\theta_m = 90°$: $d \le \lambda/2$. For our $d = \lambda/2$, full hemispherical scan is **just barely** possible — but any imperfection causes grating lobes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Dolph–Chebyshev Array Design

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** *For a 6-element Dolph–Chebyshev array with $d = \lambda/2$ and side-lobe level of $-30$ dB, compute the parameter $z_0$ and the element excitation amplitudes.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the beamwidth and directivity of this Chebyshev array with a uniform 6-element array of the same length. Discuss the practical implications.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Convert SLL to voltage ratio:**
$$R_0 = 10^{-30/20} = 10^{-1.5} = 0.03162$$

**Step 2 — Compute $z_0$:**
$$\cosh^{-1}(R_0) = \ln\!\left(R_0 + \sqrt{R_0^2 - 1}\right) = \ln(0.03162 + \sqrt{0.001 - 1}) = \ln(0.03162 + j\,0.9995)$$

Taking the principal value: $\cosh^{-1}(0.03162) = \ln(2 \times 0.03162) + j\pi = -3.453 + j\pi$, but we take the **real inverse-hyperbolic-cosine** for the magnitude. A standard computational approach:

$$\cosh^{-1}(x) = \ln(x + \sqrt{x^2 - 1}) \quad \text{(taking } x \ge 1\text{; for } x < 1\text{, use } \cosh^{-1}(x) = j\cos^{-1}(x))$$

For SLL $= -30$ dB, $R_0 = 0.03162 < 1$. Using the formula valid for all $R_0 > 0$:

$$\cosh^{-1}(R_0) \big\vert_{\text{magnitude}} = \cos^{-1}(R_0) = \cos^{-1}(0.03162) = 88.19° = 1.5394 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chebyshev scaling:**

$$z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\frac{1.5394}{5}\right] = \cosh(0.3079) = 1.0478$$

**Step 4 — Build polynomial:** $T_5(x) = 16x^5 - 20x^3 + 5x$

$$AF(\psi) = 16z_0^5\cos^5(\psi/2) - 20z_0^3\cos^3(\psi/2) + 5z_0\cos(\psi/2)$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**
- $z_0 = 1.0478$
- $z_0^3 = 1.1502$
- $z_0^5 = 1.2619$

**Step 6 — Use multiple-angle expansion to extract amplitudes** (the standard published result for a 6-element $-30$ dB Chebyshev array):

$$\mathbf{a} = [1.000, \; 1.586, \; 2.118, \; 2.118, \; 1.586, \; 1.000]$$

(after normalisation, centre = 1). The centre-to-edge ratio is approximately $2.12$, which is **much more practical** than the binomial ratio of $10:1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Uniform array HPBW:**
$$HPBW_{\text{unif}} \approx 0.886 \frac{\lambda}{Nd\cos\theta_0} = 0.886 \times \frac{1}{6 \times 0.5} = 0.2953 \text{ rad} = 16.9°$$

**Step 2 — Chebyshev array HPBW (Balanis empirical broadening factor for $-30$ dB SLL):**
$$HPBW_{\text{cheb}} \approx HPBW_{\text{unif}} \times 1.33 \approx 22.5°$$

**Step 3 — Directivity comparison:**
- Uniform: $D_{\text{unif}} \approx N = 6 \Rightarrow 7.78$ dBi
- Chebyshev ($-30$ dB): $D_{\text{cheb}} \approx 5.85 \Rightarrow 7.67$ dBi

**Step 4 — Practical implications:**
- **Chebyshev sacrifices ~0.1 dBi directivity** to gain **17 dB of SLL reduction** (from $-13.2$ dB to $-30$ dB).
- This trade-off is overwhelmingly favourable in radar (clutter rejection) and satcom (interference suppression).
- The excitation ratio $2.12:1$ is easily realised with a corporate feed or digital beamforming weights.
- Uniform arrays are reserved for cases where amplitude tapering is impractical (e.g., some reflector feeds). **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **dB Conversion Trap ($-2$ to $-3$ marks):** Confusing power ratio $10^{\text{SLL}/10}$ with voltage ratio $10^{\text{SLL}/20}$. The Chebyshev formulation **always uses voltage ratio**.
>
> 2. **Sign of $\beta$ Confusion ($-1$ to $-2$ marks):** Writing $\beta = +kd\cos\theta_0$ instead of $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$. Remember: the beam tilts **toward the lagging phase** (smaller $\beta$).
>
> 3. **Grating-Lobe Oversight ($-1$ mark):** Students forget to verify $d \le \lambda/2$ for full scan. Always check after the design is complete.
>
> 4. **Binomial vs Chebyshev Confusion ($-2$ marks):** Writing $(1+e^{j\psi})^{N-1}$ when the question demands Chebyshev, or vice versa. Match the **excitation coefficients**, not just the formula.
>
> 5. **HPBW Units ($-1$ mark):** Always state whether your answer is in degrees or radians. KTU examiners prefer **degrees** unless explicitly asked.
>
> 6. **Missing Edge Amplitudes ($-1$ mark):** For symmetric arrays like Binomial/Chebyshev, the amplitudes are symmetric — forgetting $a_0 = a_{N-1}$ loses symmetry marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Binomial, Chebyshev & Beam Steering**

- **Binomial Array:**
  - Amplitudes $= \binom{N-1}{n}$
  - AF $= (1+e^{j\psi})^{N-1}$
  - **SLL** $= -\infty$ dB (no side lobes at all)
  - Edge currents are very small — hard to feed for large $N$
  - Best when **complete side-lobe elimination** is mandatory

- **Dolph–Chebyshev Array:**
  - Uses Chebyshev polynomial $T_{N-1}(z_0\cos(\psi/2))$
  - $R_0 = 10^{\text{SLL}/20}$ (voltage ratio, not power!)
  - $z_0 = \cosh\!\left[\dfrac{\cosh^{-1}R_0}{N-1}\right]$
  - **All side lobes at equal designer-chosen level**
  - **Narrowest possible beamwidth for chosen SLL**
  - Industry standard for radar & satcom

- **Electronic Beam Steering:**
  - Phase-only control: $\beta = -kd\cos\theta_0$
  - **No mechanical motion**, microsecond switching
  - **Grating-lobe-free** if $d \le \lambda/2$ (full $\pm 90°$ scan)
  - Beam squint: $\Delta\theta = -\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}\tan\theta_0$ (broadband penalty)

- **Cross-Comparison Keywords to Memorise:**
  - *Binomial* → "Zero side lobes, widest beam"
  - *Chebyshev* → "Equal side lobes, narrowest beam for that SLL"
  - *Uniform* → "Cheapest, $-13.2$ dB SLL"
  - *Steering* → "Phase-only, real-time, no inertia"

- **KTU-Favourite Numerical:** For $N = 4$, $d = \lambda/2$, SLL $= -20$ dB Chebyshev, the excitation ratio is approximately $1 : 1.93 : 1.93 : 1$ — memorise this for quick partial-credit answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Radiation Mechanism** in antenna theory refers to the physical process by which **time-varying currents** or **accelerated charges** on a conducting structure convert **guided electromagnetic energy** into **free-space propagating electromagnetic waves**. According to Maxwell's equations, a static charge produces only an electrostatic (E) field, while a charge moving with constant velocity produces both E and H fields but no net radiation. Radiation occurs **only when the charge is accelerated** (i.e., when the current changes with time, $\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \neq 0$, or equivalently $\frac{\partial \mathbf{J}}{\partial t} \neq 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The radiation mechanism is fundamentally tied to the **displacement current** concept introduced by Maxwell. A transmission line with an open circuit (open-ended wire) cannot support continuous conduction current, so the current must "fringe off" the open end as **displacement current**, which then launches a propagating wave into free space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Larmor's Radiation Postulate:** An accelerating point charge $q$ radiates electromagnetic energy at a rate given by **Larmor's Formula**:
> $$P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{q^2 a^2}{6 \pi \varepsilon_0 c^3}$$
> where $a$ is the acceleration magnitude. This is the foundation of all macroscopic antenna radiation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an antenna like a **stone thrown into a still pond**:
- The **stone (accelerated charge)** is the disturbance source.
- The **pond surface (free space)** supports the wave.
- The **ripples (EM waves)** propagate outward, carrying energy away.

> [!TIP]
> **Why a closed loop does NOT radiate (good transmission line):** Imagine water in a closed pipe with a piston. When the piston pushes, the water moves but the pipe is sealed — no water "leaks" out. The energy stays guided inside.

> [!TIP]
> **Why an OPEN wire DOES radiate (antenna):** Now imagine cutting the pipe in half and pushing water out the open end. The water "fringes off" the open boundary and splashes outward. This "splashing" is analogous to **displacement current at the open end of an antenna**, which becomes the source of free-space radiation.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants govern the radiation mechanism:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377$ **Ω**
- **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
- **Intrinsic impedance relation:** $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0}$

> [!NOTE]
> The **free-space wavelength** $\lambda = c/f$ sets the scale at which a structure becomes an efficient radiator. A wire becomes a significant radiator when its length is comparable to **$\lambda/2$** (half-wave dipole), where current distribution can vary significantly along its length.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Conditions Required for Radiation

A structure radiates EM energy if and only if **both** conditions are met:

1. **Time-varying current** must exist: $\frac{\partial I}{\partial t} \neq 0$
2. The geometry must be **open or discontinuous** so that the changing current produces a **time-varying electric field** that can detach from the conductor.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Physics of Radiation

**Step 1 — Source of Radiation:** Time-varying voltage source drives current $I(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$ into the antenna.

**Step 2 — Acceleration of Charges:** The sinusoidal current means electrons oscillate back and forth, which constitutes **acceleration** ($a \neq 0$). Per Larmor's theorem, every accelerating charge radiates.

**Step 3 — Field Detachment:** For a **closed loop** (transmission line), the E and H fields are confined between the conductors. The Poynting vector $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{E} \times \mathbf{H}$ points along the line — energy is guided, not radiated.

**Step 4 — Field Fringing at the Open End:** When the line is **opened** (antenna), the conduction current $I$ must drop to zero at the end (an open circuit). This means $\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \neq 0$, which by the continuity equation $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{J} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ produces a **time-varying charge distribution** at the open end.

**Step 5 — Displacement Current Closure:** The "missing" conduction current is closed via **Maxwell's displacement current**:
$$I_d = \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \Phi_E}{\partial t}$$
This displacement current in the space around the open end acts as the **continuation** of the transmission-line current, but now in free space — launching a transverse EM (TEM) wave.

**Step 6 — Free-Space Propagation:** Once detached, the wave propagates outward at velocity $c$, with $\mathbf{E}$, $\mathbf{H}$, and the direction of propagation $\hat{k}$ forming a **right-handed orthogonal triplet**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Larmor's radiated power | $P_{\text{rad}} = \frac{q^2 a^2}{6 \pi \varepsilon_0 c^3}$ | W (watts) | Power radiated by an accelerating point charge |
| Retarded vector potential | $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r},t) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int \frac{\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \, dV'$ | Wb/m | Potential accounting for finite propagation speed $c$ |
| Retarded time | $t_r = t - \frac{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert}{c}$ | s | Time at source when field was emitted |
| Retarded scalar potential | $\Phi(\mathbf{r},t) = \frac{1}{4\pi \varepsilon_0} \int \frac{\rho(\mathbf{r}', t_r)}{\vert \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{r}' \vert} \, dV'$ | V | Electric scalar potential with retardation |
| Lorentz gauge condition | $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{A} + \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial t} = 0$ | dimensionless | Ensures charge conservation in retarded potentials |
