# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual Short-Answer
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> Define **Circular Polarisation**. State the two necessary conditions on the orthogonal field components for a wave to be circularly polarised.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Circular Polarisation (CP)** is the special case of elliptical polarisation in which the tip of the electric field vector **$\vec{E}$** traces a **perfect circle** in the plane perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation, completing one full revolution per RF cycle.
>
> The two necessary conditions for a wave $\vec{E}(z,t)=E_{x}\cos(\omega t-kz)\hat{x}+E_{y}\cos(\omega t-kz+\delta)\hat{y}$ to be circularly polarised are:
>
> 1. **Equal amplitudes:** $E_{x}=E_{y}=E_{0}$
>
> 2. **Quadrature phase:** $\delta = \pm 90^{\circ}\;(\pm\pi/2\;\text{rad})$
>
> The choice of sign determines the **sense of rotation**: $\delta=-90^{\circ}$ gives **RHCP** and $\delta=+90^{\circ}$ gives **LHCP** (per IEEE convention).

---

### Question A2 — Definition / Recall
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> What is **Axial Ratio (AR)**? What is its value for an *ideal* circularly polarised wave, expressed both as a linear ratio and in decibels?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Axial Ratio (AR)** is the ratio of the **major axis** to the **minor axis** of the polarisation ellipse. It is the standard figure-of-merit for quantifying how "circular" a polarisation state actually is.
>
> $$\mathrm{AR} = \frac{A_{\text{major}}}{A_{\text{minor}}}$$
>
> For an **ideal** circularly polarised wave, the major and minor axes are equal (the ellipse is a circle), hence:
>
> $$\boxed{\;\mathrm{AR}_{\text{ideal}} = 1 \;=\; 0\;\text{dB}\;}$$
>
> In practice, an AR of $\le 3$ dB is considered good CP, and $\le 1$ dB is excellent (typical of well-designed GPS patch antennas).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive CP Theory

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question — Module 2 Internal Choice Option A]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive, starting from the general expression for a transverse electromagnetic wave, the conditions under which the wave becomes **circularly polarised**. Clearly distinguish between **RHCP** and **LHCP** using the IEEE convention, and state the Poynting vector and wave-impedance implications.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A satellite downlink at **12 GHz** uses **RHCP**. The ground station's receive helical antenna has diameter $D = 1.0$ cm, pitch $S = 0.5$ cm, and 8 turns. Compute (i) the helix circumference, (ii) whether the helix is operating in the axial (CP) mode, and (iii) the **Axial Ratio in dB** if the measured major and minor field axes are $E_{\text{maj}}=1.20$ V/m and $E_{\text{min}}=1.05$ V/m.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

> **Solution Outline:** We start from the general field and apply the two CP conditions. The detailed derivation is given in **§3.1 above**. The examiner expects the following key steps:

**Step 1 — Write the general E-field:**

$$\vec{E}(z,t) = E_{x}\cos(\omega t - kz)\,\hat{x} + E_{y}\cos(\omega t - kz + \delta)\,\hat{y}$$

**Step 2 — Apply CP conditions** $E_x = E_y = E_0$ and $\delta = \pm\pi/2$.

For $\delta = -\pi/2$ (RHCP per IEEE):

$$\vec{E}(z,t) = E_{0}\cos(\omega t - kz)\,\hat{x} + E_{0}\sin(\omega t - kz)\,\hat{y}$$

**Step 3 — Show the locus is a circle:**

$$\frac{E_{x}^{2}}{E_{0}^{2}} + \frac{E_{y}^{2}}{E_{0}^{2}} = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{circle of radius }E_{0}$$

**Step 4 — Sense of rotation (IEEE):** Tracking the field tip at $z=0$ over a quarter cycle:
- $t=0$: $(E_x, E_y) = (E_0, 0)$ on $+\hat{x}$
- $t=T/4$: $(E_x, E_y) = (0, E_0)$ on $+\hat{y}$

This is a **counter-clockwise rotation** when viewed looking *into* the wave (i.e., from receiver towards source, against $+\hat{z}$). By IEEE convention, this is **RHCP** for $\delta=-\pi/2$. Conversely $\delta=+\pi/2$ yields **LHCP**.

**Step 5 — Poynting vector & impedance:** The instantaneous Poynting vector is:

$$\vec{S}(t) = \frac{\vert\vec{E}\vert^{2}}{\eta_{0}}\,\hat{z} = \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{\eta_{0}}\,\hat{z}$$

which is **constant in time and direction** — a defining property of CP. The wave impedance is:

$$Z_{w} = \frac{\vert\vec{E}\vert}{\vert\vec{H}\vert} = \eta_{0} = 120\pi\;\Omega \approx 377\;\Omega$$

[7 marks: 1 mark for each of the 5 key points, 2 marks for clarity/rigour]

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $f = 12$ GHz, hence $\lambda = c/f = 3\times10^{8}/12\times10^{9} = 0.025$ m $= 2.5$ cm
- Helix: $D = 1.0$ cm $\Rightarrow C = \pi D = \pi \times 1.0 = 3.1416$ cm
- Pitch $S = 0.5$ cm
- $E_{\text{maj}} = 1.20$ V/m, $E_{\text{min}} = 1.05$ V/m

**Step (i) — Helix circumference:**

$$C = \pi D = \pi \times 1.0\;\text{cm} = 3.1416\;\text{cm} \approx 3.14\;\text{cm}$$

**Step (ii) — Check axial-mode (CP) condition $C \approx \lambda$:**

$$\lambda = 2.5\;\text{cm}, \quad C = 3.14\;\text{cm}$$

Ratio: $C/\lambda = 3.14/2.5 = 1.257$. For the axial mode, we require $0.75 \le C/\lambda \le 1.33$ (Kraus's empirical condition for broadband CP). Since $1.257$ is in this range, **the helix is operating in the axial (CP) mode** ✓.

**Step (iii) — Axial Ratio in dB:**

$$\mathrm{AR} = \frac{E_{\text{maj}}}{E_{\text{min}}} = \frac{1.20}{1.05} = 1.1429$$

Converting to dB:

$$\mathrm{AR}_{[\mathrm{dB}]} = 20\log_{10}(1.1429) = 20 \times 0.05799 = 1.16\;\text{dB}$$

> **Result:** $\mathrm{AR} \approx 1.16$ dB — this is an **excellent** axial ratio, indicating high-quality circular polarisation suitable for satellite reception.

[7 marks: 2 marks each for parts (i) and (iii), 3 marks for part (ii)]

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Applications-Focused)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question — Module 2 Internal Choice Option B]**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with the aid of a clear sketch, the working principle of the **axial-mode helical antenna** as a circularly polarised radiator. Derive the condition for **circular polarisation** in terms of helix dimensions. Why is this antenna preferred for satellite communication?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A transmit antenna radiates **RHCP** with EIRP of **+30 dBW** at **4 GHz** towards a receive antenna located **36,000 km** away (geostationary orbit). The receive antenna is also RHCP with gain **20 dBi**. Compute the **received power** using the Friis transmission equation, accounting for the polarisation mismatch if the receive antenna were (i) RHCP and (ii) LHCP. State any KTU-relevant engineering inferences.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:**

The axial-mode helical antenna consists of a helix wound from a conducting wire, fed by a coaxial line at one end with a ground plane. When the helix circumference $C = \pi D$ is approximately equal to one wavelength ($\lambda$), the antenna operates in the **axial mode (T₀ mode)**, where maximum radiation occurs along the helix axis.

**Field Decomposition:**

The current on the helix can be decomposed into two orthogonal components in the far-field:
1. A **horizontal (tangential) component** $E_\phi$ — from the curved portion of each turn
2. A **vertical (axial) component** $E_\theta$ — from the wire advancing forward by pitch $S$ per turn

**CP Condition Derivation:**

When $C = \pi D \approx \lambda$, the two components are in **time-phase quadrature** (90° apart) and have **nearly equal amplitudes** — satisfying the CP conditions automatically. The axial-mode condition can be derived rigorously by requiring that the phase progression per turn equals $2\pi$ for constructive interference along the axis:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\bigl[S\cos\alpha - (\pi D)\sin\alpha\bigr] = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; S = \pi D \tan\alpha$$

For typical CP operation, the pitch angle $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(S/\pi D)$ is in the range **12° to 15°**.

**Why Preferred for SATCOM:**
- Inherently CP — no external hybrid coupler needed
- Wide bandwidth (AR < 3 dB over ~50% bandwidth)
- High gain (proportional to number of turns $N$)
- Sense of rotation is fixed by helix handedness — no ambiguity

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- EIRP = +30 dBW $= 10^{3}$ W $= 1000$ W
- $f = 4$ GHz $\Rightarrow \lambda = 3\times10^{8}/4\times10^{9} = 0.075$ m $= 7.5$ cm
- $R = 36{,}000$ km $= 3.6 \times 10^{7}$ m
- $G_r$ (dBi) = 20 dBi $\Rightarrow G_r = 100$ (linear)

**Step 1 — Compute path loss term $\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} = \frac{0.075}{4\pi \times 3.6 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{0.075}{4.524 \times 10^{8}} = 1.658 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2} = 2.749 \times 10^{-20}$$

Converting to dB:

$$10\log_{10}(2.749 \times 10^{-20}) = 10(-19.561) = -195.61\;\text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute received power in dB (case i, RHCP–RHCP, PLF = 1):**

$$P_r\,[\text{dBW}] = \mathrm{EIRP} + G_r - L_{\text{path}} + 0 \quad(\text{PLF} = 1)$$

$$P_r = 30 + 20 - 195.61 + 0 = -145.61\;\text{dBW}$$

In linear: $P_r = 10^{-14.561} = 2.74 \times 10^{-15}$ W $= 2.74$ fW.

**Step 3 — Compute received power (case ii, RHCP–LHCP, PLF = 0):**

$$P_r\,[\text{dBW}] = 30 + 20 - 195.61 - \infty = -\infty\;\text{dBW}$$

i.e., **zero received power** — total rejection by the orthogonal-sense antenna.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert EIRP from dBW to W, or vice versa, is the single biggest source of numerical error. Always state units explicitly in *every* step.
> 2. **Confusing RHCP/LHCP convention:** Some textbooks use the *physics* convention (looking *along* propagation). KTU uses the *IEEE* convention (looking *into* propagation). Mismatch here is worth 2–3 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **Path loss sign:** $L_{\text{path}}$ is a *positive* number (in dB). When written as $-(4\pi R/\lambda)^2$ in the Friis equation, the minus sign is already included. Students often double-subtract and get wrong answers.
> 4. **EIRP vs $P_t G_t$:** EIRP already *includes* the transmit antenna gain. Do not multiply by $G_t$ again.
> 5. **PLF for CP:** Many students forget that PLF for matched CP is **1 (0 dB)** and for opposite-sense is **0 (-∞ dB)**. Missing this distinction costs the full 7 marks of part (b).
> 6. **Not stating the IEEE convention explicitly:** Examiners deduct 1 mark if a student does not explicitly write "per IEEE Std 145-1983" when defining the sense of rotation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and revise from it the night before the exam.**

- [x] **CP definition** = ellipse degenerates to a circle (special case of EP)
- [x] **Two conditions:** $E_x = E_y$ (equal amplitudes) **AND** $\delta = \pm 90°$ (quadrature phase)
- [x] **IEEE sense convention:** $\delta = -90°$ → RHCP, $\delta = +90°$ → LHCP (observer looks *into* the wave)
- [x] **Axial Ratio (AR):** = 1 (0 dB) for perfect CP, $\le 3$ dB for good CP
- [x] **Poynting vector of CP wave is constant in time** — defines its time-averaged power stability
- [x] **Helix axial-mode condition:** $C = \pi D \approx \lambda$, pitch angle $12°$–$15°$
- [x] **Helix handedness fixes CP sense:** RH-wound helix → RHCP, LH-wound → LHCP
- [x] **PLF for CP-CP link:** = 1 (matched), = 0 (opposite sense, total rejection)
- [x] **GPS, SATCOM, radar, RFID** are canonical CP applications — memorise at least three with reasons
- [x] **Friis equation for CP-CP:** $P_r = \mathrm{EIRP}\cdot G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ (with PLF = 1)
- [x] **Poincaré sphere:** every polarisation state = one point on the unit sphere; CP = poles
- [x] **Microstrip CP variants:** truncated-corner (single feed), nearly-square (dual feed), sequential-rotation array
- [x] **Faraday rotation** in ionosphere rotates LP but **not CP** — crucial KTU fact
- [x] **Time-domain check:** For $\delta = +\pi/2$, $\vec{E}(t) = E_0[\cos(\omega t)\hat{x} - \sin(\omega t)\hat{y}]$ → LHCP (CCW looking into wave)
- [x] **Frequency-domain identity:** $\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}(e^{j\theta}+e^{-j\theta})$ — the CP wave has equal +ve and −ve frequency components (no carrier suppression)

> **End of Module 2 — Circular Polarisation Notes. All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation! 🎓**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substrates for Microstrip Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Substrates for Microstrip Antennas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Microstrip Antenna (MSA)** is a low-profile, resonant-type printed antenna fabricated using **printed circuit board (PCB)** technology. It consists of three fundamental layers stacked together:

1. A thin, flat **conducting patch** (usually copper, gold, or silver) on the top side.
2. A **dielectric substrate** sandwiched in the middle.
3. A continuous **ground plane** conductor on the bottom side.

The **dielectric substrate** is the heart of a microstrip antenna. It is the **electrically insulating, non-conductive** layer that mechanically supports the radiating patch, isolates it from the ground plane, and critically determines the **electromagnetic behavior** of the antenna — including its resonant frequency, bandwidth, radiation efficiency, gain, and size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 2**
> The substrate (often also called the *dielectric substrate* or *dielectric medium*) is characterized by four key parameters:
> - **Dielectric Constant / Relative Permittivity (εᵣ)**
> - **Loss Tangent (tan δ)**
> - **Substrate Thickness (h)**
> - **Thermal, mechanical and machining properties**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Notation Used in KTU Textbooks**
> *c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s* (speed of light in free space), *fᵣ* (resonant frequency in Hz), *λ₀* (free-space wavelength), *Q* (Quality factor). The thickness *h* is typically expressed in **millimeters (mm)** for practical design, while εᵣ and tan δ are **dimensionless** ratios.

### 1.2 The Microstrip Antenna Cross-Section — A Visual

```
       ┌───────────────────────┐
       │   Conducting Patch    │   ← Top conductor (length L, width W)
       ├───────────────────────┤
       │                       │
       │   Dielectric          │   ← SUBSTRATE (the focus of this topic)
       │   Substrate (h, εᵣ)   │     - thickness = h
       │                       │     - permittivity = εᵣ
       │                       │     - loss tangent = tan δ
       ├───────────────────────┤
       │   Ground Plane        │   ← Bottom conductor (continuous)
       └───────────────────────┘
```

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floor Mat" Intuition

Think of a microstrip antenna like a **person standing on a foam mattress above a metal floor**:

- The **person's footprint** is the conducting patch — wherever the electromagnetic field "concentrates" and radiates.
- The **foam mattress** is the dielectric substrate — its softness (loss tangent) and density (dielectric constant) control how the field distributes, how much leaks out, and how much gets trapped.
- The **metal floor** is the ground plane — a near-perfect reflector that bounces radiation upward.

If the mattress is **very dense (high εᵣ ≈ 10)**: the field is tightly confined, the antenna becomes **physically small**, but only a small amount of energy escapes — efficiency suffers. The person "sinks" into the foam and the wave gets trapped.

If the mattress is **very soft / light (low εᵣ ≈ 2.2)**: the field extends far outside the patch, the antenna is **larger**, but a great deal of energy radiates outward — efficiency is high.

If the mattress is **too thick**: parts of the energy start to **slide sideways along the mattress** instead of escaping upward — these are called **surface waves**, and they are usually undesirable because they waste energy and couple between elements.

This is the **fundamental trade-off** in substrate selection.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is the substrate so important?**
> It is the *only* engineering variable that simultaneously controls the antenna's **electrical size, bandwidth, efficiency, and spurious surface wave excitation**. The patch dimensions can be computed only after εᵣ and h are fixed.

### 1.4 Why Substrates Are the "Hidden Hero" of Microstrip Antennas

| Desired Property | How Substrate Controls It |
|---|---|
| Miniaturization | Higher εᵣ reduces patch dimensions |
| Bandwidth | Higher *h* and lower εᵣ increase bandwidth |
| Efficiency | Lower tan δ and lower εᵣ reduce losses |
| Surface wave suppression | Lower *h* (h/λ₀ < 0.05) avoids TM₀ mode |
| Polarization purity | Substrate isotropy and uniformity |
| Fabrication ease | Dimensional stability, machining properties |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Master Parameters of a Substrate

#### 2.1.1 Relative Dielectric Constant (εᵣ)

- Also called *relative permittivity* or *dielectric constant*.
- Ratio of permittivity of the substrate to that of free space: **εᵣ = ε / ε₀**, where **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**.
- Indicates how much the substrate **slows down** an electromagnetic wave.
- Effective wavelength inside the substrate: **λg = λ₀ / √εᵣ**.
- Patch electrical length scales as **1/√εᵣ**.

#### 2.1.2 Loss Tangent (tan δ)

- Measures the **dissipative (ohmic + dielectric) loss** inside the substrate.
- tan δ = σ / (ωε) = ε″ / ε′.
- Directly reduces **radiation efficiency (η) and gain (G)**.
- Typical values: Duroid ≈ 0.0009; FR4 ≈ 0.02; air ≈ 0 (lossless).

#### 2.1.3 Substrate Thickness (h)

- Electrically measured as **h/λ₀**.
- Thicker substrate → wider bandwidth and more efficient radiation.
- Beyond a critical thickness, **surface waves** are excited.

#### 2.1.4 Substrate Homogeneity & Isotropy

- For accurate modeling, the substrate must be **homogeneous, isotropic, and non-magnetic (μᵣ ≈ 1)**.
- Most practical substrates satisfy this assumption.

### 2.2 The Fundamental Design Trade-Off (KTU's "Golden Triangle")

The substrate choices form a tight **inter-locked triangle** that every microstrip designer must navigate:

- **Smaller antenna (high εᵣ) ↔ Lower bandwidth ↔ Lower efficiency (sometimes).**
- **Wider bandwidth (high h, low εᵣ) ↔ Larger size ↔ Surface-wave risk.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept**
> *"Why can't we just use the lowest εᵣ substrate available to get the best efficiency?"*
> Answer: Extremely low εᵣ makes the antenna impractically large for most modern wireless devices (mobile phones, GPS, RFID). There is a **practical engineering compromise** between size, bandwidth, and cost.

### 2.3 Surface Waves — The Invisible Enemy

When the substrate is **electrically thick**, part of the trapped energy propagates as **bound surface waves** along the substrate–air interface, rather than radiating into free space. The dominant lowest-order mode is **TM₀ (Transverse Magnetic)**.

The condition for the onset of the dominant TM₀ surface wave mode is:

$$h_{c} = \frac{\lambda_0}{4\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}$$

If **h > h_c**, surface waves are excited. For this reason, practical KTU designs always enforce:

$$h \;\le\; \frac{0.05\,\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic: What Happens When You Change Substrate?

If you **double εᵣ** (e.g., from 2.2 to 4.4):
- Patch length scales as **1/√εᵣ**, so it shrinks by **factor ≈ 1.414**.
- Bandwidth typically **decreases** because higher εᵣ → tighter field confinement.
- Surface-wave cutoff becomes *more restrictive* on h.
- **Spurious feed radiation** becomes more pronounced.

If you **double h**:
- Bandwidth and radiation efficiency **increase**.
- Surface-wave excitation risk **increases** (h may exceed h_c).
- Probe-feed inductance rises, requiring compensation.

If you **halve tan δ**:
- Loss decreases, **efficiency and gain increase**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are direct, board-exam relevant. Memorize the table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective dielectric constant (W/h > 1) | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Effective dielectric constant (W/h < 1) | $\varepsilon_{eff} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}\right)^{-1/2} + 0.02\!\left(1-\dfrac{W}{h}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_r-1}{4}\right)^2$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Fringing field extension | $\Delta L = 0.412h\,\dfrac{(\varepsilon_{eff}+0.3)\left(\dfrac{W}{h}+0.264\right)}{(\varepsilon_{eff}-0.258)\left(\dfrac{W}{h}+0.8\right)}$ | metres |
| 4 | Effective electrical length | $L_{eff} = L + 2\Delta L$ | metres |
| 5 | Resonant frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2L_{eff}\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Practical patch length | $L = \dfrac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2\Delta L$ | metres |
| 7 | Wavelength in substrate | $\lambda_g = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | metres |
| 8 | Surface-wave onset thickness | $h_c = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{4\sqrt{\varepsilon_r-1}}$ | metres |
| 9 | Maximum safe thickness | $h_{max} \le \dfrac{0.05\,\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | metres |
| 10 | Quality factor (approx., conductor + dielectric + surface) | $\dfrac{1}{Q_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{Q_c} + \dfrac{1}{Q_d} + \dfrac{1}{Q_{sw}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | VSWR Bandwidth (approx.) | $BW = \dfrac{f_r}{Q_{tot}}\cdot \dfrac{(VSWR-1)}{\sqrt{VSWR}} \times 100\,\%$ | percent |
| 12 | Radiation efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{tot}}{Q_{rad}} = \dfrac{1}{1+Q_{rad}/Q_{loss}}$ | Fraction (0–1) |
| 13 | Loss-tangent power loss | $P_{loss} = \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r \tan\delta \int\!\vert E \vert^2 dV$ | Watts |
| 14 | Resonance-extending rule of thumb | $L \approx 0.49\,\lambda_g$ for $L > W$ | metres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Table Substitution Note**: All absolute-value bars inside the table are written as `\vert` (not the keyboard `|` symbol) to preserve valid markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Substrate Class | Why Engineers Use It |
|---|---|
| **Low εᵣ (≈ 2.2) Duroid** | High-performance aerospace, phased-array radars needing high efficiency |
| **Mid εᵣ (≈ 4.4) FR4** | Low-cost commercial Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, IoT |
| **High εᵣ (≈ 10) Alumina** | Microwave integrated circuits, MMIC compatibility |
| **Very high εᵣ (≈ 12.9) GaAs** | Active integrated antennas, monolithic microwave designs |
| **Air / foam (εᵣ ≈ 1.0–1.2)** | Wideband, UWB, high-power applications |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Dielectric Constant (ε_eff) from Fringing Field Analysis

The microstrip line is a quasi-TEM structure. A fraction of the electric field lines travel through the air (ε₀) above the patch, while the rest travel through the substrate (εᵣ). Since the phase velocity varies with position, an **effective** dielectric constant is defined such that the **total phase delay** equals that of a uniform medium.

**Step 1 — Identify the two extreme cases**

When **W/h → ∞** (very wide line), almost all field is in the substrate, so:
$$\varepsilon_{eff} \rightarrow \varepsilon_r \quad \text{as} \quad \frac{W}{h} \to \infty$$

When **W/h → 0** (very narrow line), most field escapes into air, so:
$$\varepsilon_{eff} \rightarrow \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} \quad \text{as} \quad \frac{W}{h} \to 0$$

**Step 2 — Empirical interpolation (Hammerstad / Schneider closed form)**

The most widely used expression, **valid for W/h > 1**, is the Schneider approximation:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Why this form?**

The term $\left(1 + \dfrac{12h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}$ acts as a **smooth weighting factor**:
- When *W/h* is large, the term → 1 and ε_eff → ε_r (case from Step 1).
- When *W/h* is small, the term → 0 and ε_eff → (ε_r + 1)/2 (case from Step 1).

This satisfies both asymptotic limits — a hallmark of a physically consistent empirical model.

**Step 4 — Correction for narrow strips (W/h ≤ 1)**

A second-order correction is added:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12h}{W}\right)^{-1/2} + 0.02\left(1 - \frac{W}{h}\right)\left(\frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{4}\right)^{2}
$$

This term vanishes as W/h → 1 and becomes important only for W/h < 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip**: For typical rectangular patches, W/h > 1 is almost always satisfied. Students are usually allowed to use the simpler expression (Step 3 only) in numerical problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resonant Frequency and Patch Length (L)

**Step 1 — Boundary condition for resonance**

A rectangular patch resonates when the electrical length (along the L-direction) is **half a wavelength in the effective medium**:

$$L_{eff} = \frac{\lambda_{eff}}{2} = \frac{\lambda_0}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}$$

**Step 2 — Account for fringing**

The patch is **electrically longer** than its physical length by 2ΔL, because the fringing fields at the radiating edges extend the effective cavity:

$$L_{eff} = L + 2\Delta L$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the resonant frequency**

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{2L_{eff}\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} = \frac{c}{2(L + 2\Delta L)\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the required physical length**

$$
L = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2\Delta L
$$

This is the **most important equation** in microstrip antenna design — it links the substrate's εᵣ to the patch's physical dimension.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU ESE Model):**
A rectangular microstrip patch antenna is to be designed for a resonant frequency of **2.4 GHz** (typical Wi-Fi band). The substrate used is **RT-Duroid 5880** with εᵣ = 2.2 and thickness h = 0.787 mm. For a probe-fed design, the patch width is chosen as W = 4.0 cm. Determine the effective dielectric constant, the fringing extension, and the required physical patch length.

**Step 1 — Check W/h ratio**

$$
\frac{W}{h} = \frac{40\,\text{mm}}{0.787\,\text{mm}} = 50.8 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; W/h \gg 1
$$

So we use the **simple form** of the effective dielectric constant formula.

**Step 2 — Compute ε_eff**

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{2.2 + 1}{2} + \frac{2.2 - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12 \times 0.787}{40}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.6 + 0.6 \left(1 + 0.2361\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.6 + 0.6 \times (1.2361)^{-0.5}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.6 + 0.6 \times 0.8995
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.6 + 0.5397 = 2.1397
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ΔL (fringing extension)**

$$
\Delta L = 0.412 \times 0.787 \times \frac{(\varepsilon_{eff}+0.3)\left(\dfrac{W}{h}+0.264\right)}{(\varepsilon_{eff}-0.258)\left(\dfrac{W}{h}+0.8\right)}
$$

Plug values: ε_eff = 2.1397, W/h = 50.8

$$
\Delta L = 0.3242 \times \frac{(2.1397+0.3)(50.8+0.264)}{(2.1397-0.258)(50.8+0.8)}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.3242 \times \frac{2.4397 \times 51.064}{1.8817 \times 51.6}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.3242 \times \frac{124.571}{97.096}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.3242 \times 1.2831
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.4160\;\text{mm} = 0.000416\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute physical length L**

$$
L = \frac{c}{2f_r\sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2\Delta L
$$

$$
L = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 2.4 \times 10^{9} \times \sqrt{2.1397}} - 2 \times 0.000416
$$

$$
L = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4.8 \times 10^{9} \times 1.4628} - 0.000832
$$

$$
L = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{7.0214 \times 10^{9}} - 0.000832
$$

$$
L = 0.04273 - 0.000832 = 0.04190\;\text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{L \approx 41.9\;\text{mm}, \quad W = 40.0\;\text{mm}, \quad \varepsilon_{eff} \approx 2.14}
$$

This is the **canonical 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi patch** — values seen in nearly every KTU lab manual.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Surface-Wave Onset Thickness for a Typical Substrate

**Given**: εᵣ = 2.2, f_r = 2.4 GHz
**Find**: h_c

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f_r} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.125\;\text{m}
$$

$$
h_c = \frac{\lambda_0}{4\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}} = \frac{0.125}{4 \sqrt{1.2}} = \frac{0.125}{4 \times 1.0954}
$$

$$
h_c = \frac{0.125}{4.3818} = 0.02853\;\text{m} = 28.53\;\text{mm}
$$

Since **h = 0.787 mm ≪ h_c = 28.53 mm**, **no surface wave** is excited — design is safe. ✓

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define dielectric constant of a substrate. Why is a low dielectric constant substrate preferred for microstrip patch antennas?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The relative dielectric constant (εᵣ) of a substrate is the ratio of the permittivity of the substrate material to that of free space: εᵣ = ε/ε₀. It is a dimensionless quantity that indicates how much the substrate concentrates electric flux and slows the propagation of electromagnetic waves. Typical values range from 1.0 (air) to about 12.9 (Gallium Arsenide).

**Why low εᵣ is preferred (1.5 marks):**
A low εᵣ substrate is preferred for the following reasons:
- **Stronger fringing fields** at the patch edges → better radiation efficiency.
- **Wider operational bandwidth**, because bandwidth is inversely proportional to √εᵣ.
- **Reduced surface-wave excitation**, because the critical thickness h_c is larger.
- **Lower conductor loss** in many cases, since the field is less tightly bound.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution Insight**: 1 mark for crisp definition, 1 mark for any two reasons, 1 mark for completeness. Avoid vague "it improves performance" answers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is loss tangent? State its significance in microstrip antenna design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
The loss tangent (tan δ) is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the intrinsic dissipative loss of a dielectric material. It is defined as the ratio of the imaginary part to the real part of the complex permittivity: tan δ = ε″/ε′ = σ/(ωε). It represents the energy lost as heat per cycle relative to the energy stored in the material.

**Significance in MSA design (1.5 marks):**
- Directly affects the **dielectric loss** component of the total antenna loss.
- A low tan δ (e.g., 0.0009 for Duroid) yields **high radiation efficiency and gain**, while a high tan δ (e.g., 0.02 for FR4) significantly degrades performance, especially above 3 GHz.
- It is one of the four key parameters used in the Quality Factor (Q) calculation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **structure of a microstrip patch antenna**. Define the four important substrate parameters that govern the design of a microstrip antenna. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (2 marks):**
A neat cross-sectional sketch of a microstrip antenna showing:
- Top: conducting patch (length L, width W, thickness t)
- Middle: dielectric substrate (thickness h, εᵣ, tan δ)
- Bottom: continuous ground plane

**Explanation of structure (1 mark):**
The patch and ground plane together form a **dielectric-filled cavity** bounded by the patch and ground. The fringing fields at the two open ends of the patch (along the L-direction) are responsible for radiation.

**Four substrate parameters (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dielectric constant | εᵣ | Ratio of substrate permittivity to free-space permittivity | 2.2 to 12.9 |
| Loss tangent | tan δ | Ratio of dielectric loss to stored energy per cycle | 0.0009 to 0.02 |
| Thickness | h | Physical separation between patch and ground plane | 0.254 mm to 3.2 mm |
| Homogeneity / Isotropy | — | Material uniformity in all directions | Required for accurate modeling |

---

**(b)** A rectangular microstrip patch antenna uses a substrate with εᵣ = 2.2, h = 0.787 mm, and is designed to operate at 2.45 GHz. For a patch width of 38 mm, compute the **effective dielectric constant, the fringing length extension ΔL, and the physical patch length L**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute W/h ratio**
W/h = 38/0.787 = 48.29 ≫ 1, so the simple form of ε_eff is used.

**Step 2 — Compute effective dielectric constant**

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = \frac{2.2 + 1}{2} + \frac{2.2 - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12 \times 0.787}{38}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} = 1.6 + 0.6 \left(1 + 0.2485\right)^{-0.5} = 1.6 + 0.6 \times 0.8957
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_{eff} = 2.137}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute fringing extension ΔL**

$$
\Delta L = 0.412 \times 0.787 \times \frac{(2.137 + 0.3)(48.29 + 0.264)}{(2.137 - 0.258)(48.29 + 0.8)}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.3242 \times \frac{2.437 \times 48.554}{1.879 \times 49.09} = 0.3242 \times \frac{118.327}{92.241}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta L = 0.4158\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute physical length L**

$$
L = \frac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2 \Delta L
$$

$$
L = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 2.45 \times 10^{9} \times \sqrt{2.137}} - 2 \times 0.0004158
$$

$$
L = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{4.9 \times 10^{9} \times 1.4619} - 0.000832
$$

$$
L = 0.04188 - 0.000832
$$

$$
\boxed{L = 41.05\;\text{mm}}
$$

**Final Design:** W = 38 mm, L ≈ 41.05 mm, on Duroid substrate.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the **effect of substrate parameters on the performance of a microstrip patch antenna**. Discuss the surface-wave phenomenon and the conditions to avoid it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Effect of substrate parameters (4 marks):**
- **εᵣ (dielectric constant):** A higher εᵣ reduces the patch size (L scales as 1/√εᵣ) and concentrates fields inside the substrate, but it narrows the bandwidth and can excite surface waves more easily. A lower εᵣ gives wider bandwidth and better radiation.
- **tan δ (loss tangent):** Directly reduces radiation efficiency. A small tan δ (e.g., 0.0009) is mandatory for high-frequency and high-efficiency designs.
- **h (thickness):** A thicker substrate increases bandwidth and efficiency, but beyond a critical thickness h_c, surface waves are excited.
- **Anisotropy / non-uniformity:** Causes cross-polarization and unpredictable radiation patterns.

**Surface-wave phenomenon (3 marks):**
- A surface wave is a **bound-mode propagation** that travels along the substrate–air interface, parallel to the patch, instead of radiating into free space.
- The dominant mode is the **TM₀ mode**, which is excited above a critical substrate thickness.
- **Onset condition:**

$$
h_c = \frac{\lambda_0}{4\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}}
$$

- **To avoid surface waves:**

$$
h \le \frac{0.05\,\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}
$$

- Surface waves waste power, cause **mutual coupling** in arrays, and corrupt **polarization purity**. They can be suppressed by **electromagnetic bandgap (EBG) structures, defected ground structures (DGS), or simply using thinner substrates**.

---

**(b)** A microstrip antenna uses a substrate with εᵣ = 4.4, h = 1.6 mm, and is to be designed for 1.9 GHz. Determine whether **surface waves will be excited**. If yes, suggest two methods to suppress them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute free-space wavelength**

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.9 \times 10^{9}} = 0.1579\;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute critical thickness**

$$
h_c = \frac{\lambda_0}{4\sqrt{\varepsilon_r - 1}} = \frac{0.1579}{4\sqrt{3.4}} = \frac{0.1579}{4 \times 1.8439}
$$

$$
h_c = \frac{0.1579}{7.3756} = 0.02141\;\text{m} = 21.41\;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare**
Given **h = 1.6 mm ≪ h_c = 21.41 mm**, so **no surface waves will be excited** in this design. ✓

**Step 4 — Check h_max rule of thumb**

$$
h_{max} = \frac{0.05\,\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.1579}{\sqrt{4.4}} = \frac{0.007895}{2.0976} = 0.003764\;\text{m} = 3.76\;\text{mm}
$$

**Since h = 1.6 mm < 3.76 mm**, design is well within the safe regime. ✓

**Suppression methods (if h were too large):**
1. **Use Electromagnetic Band-Gap (EBG) structures** patterned into the ground plane.
2. **Use Defected Ground Structure (DGS)** slots to disrupt surface wave paths.
3. **Reduce substrate thickness** to below h_c.
4. **Stack a low-permittivity superstrate** to alter the propagation constant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to use `W/h` in the correct form, mixing mm with m. Always convert h and W to the **same unit** before substituting.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In surface-wave problems, students compute h_c but **forget to compare with the actual h**. The question always demands an explicit yes/no conclusion, not just a number.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** They write `tan δ` as `dielectric constant` due to similar terminology. **Dielectric constant is εᵣ; loss tangent is tan δ.** Examiners specifically deduct one mark if these are confused.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In (a)/(b) split questions, the 7-mark sub-part requires **both** numerical and analytical depth. Writing only formulae without substituting values yields at most 4 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Substrates for Microstrip Antennas**

- The **substrate** is the dielectric layer sandwiched between the patch and the ground plane.
- The **four key parameters** are: εᵣ, tan δ, h, and material homogeneity.
- The **effective dielectric constant ε_eff** lies between (εᵣ + 1)/2 and εᵣ.
- **Resonant frequency** of a patch: fᵣ = c / (2 L_eff √ε_eff).
- **Fringing extension** ΔL depends on ε_eff and W/h.
- **Bandwidth** increases with **h** and decreases with **εᵣ**.
- **Surface waves** appear when h > λ₀ / (4√(εᵣ − 1)) — they are **parasitic** and must be avoided.
- **Standard safe design rule:** h ≤ 0.05 λ₀ / √εᵣ.
- **Low εᵣ substrates** → larger antenna, higher efficiency, wider bandwidth. (e.g., Duroid 2.2)
- **High εᵣ substrates** → smaller antenna, lower efficiency, narrower bandwidth. (e.g., GaAs 12.9, Alumina 9.8)
- **Low tan δ substrates** → higher efficiency and gain. (e.g., Duroid 0.0009 vs FR4 0.02)
- **FR4** is the **lowest cost** but is **not recommended above ~3 GHz** due to its high loss tangent.
- **Patch width** for efficient radiation: W ≈ c / (2 fᵣ) × √(2 / (εᵣ + 1)).
- **Practical patch length** is always **slightly less** than λ_g/2 due to fringing (≈ 0.49 λ_g).
- For **MMIC integration**, high-εᵣ substrates like GaAs or Alumina are mandatory.
- For **wideband or UWB applications**, low-εᵣ foam or air substrates are preferred.
- **μᵣ = 1** is assumed for almost all practical microstrip substrates (non-magnetic).
- **The substrate controls FOUR key outcomes: size, bandwidth, efficiency, surface-wave behavior.**
- **Numericals in KTU exams** typically test the computation of ε_eff, ΔL, L, and fᵣ from given h, εᵣ, and W.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feeding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feeding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In antenna engineering, **Feeding Methods** (also termed *excitation techniques* or *feed networks*) refer to the structured electromagnetic techniques employed to deliver Radio Frequency (RF) power from a transmission line or waveguide source to the radiating element (or array of elements) of an antenna. The primary objective of any feed network is to achieve **efficient power transfer** (minimizing reflection), provide the correct **amplitude distribution** across radiating elements (in array antennas), and establish the required **phase progression** to steer or shape the radiated beam.

For a microstrip patch antenna (the most commonly tested context in KTU Module 2), feeding methods are broadly classified into two families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Classification of Feeding Techniques**
> 1. **Contacting (Direct) Methods** — Microstrip Line Feed, Coaxial Probe Feed
> 2. **Non-Contacting (Indirect / Electromagnetic) Methods** — Aperture Coupled Feed, Proximity Coupled Feed
> 3. **Array Feed Networks** — Corporate (Parallel) Feed, Series (Sequential) Feed

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are watering a long rectangular garden bed using a hose. There are multiple ways to do this:

- **Direct Pipe (Coaxial Feed)**: You punch a hole directly into the pipe and let water jet in. Simple, but the jet disturbs the flow — analogous to a *coaxial probe feed* poking through the ground plane.
- **Side-Channel (Microstrip Line Feed)**: You attach a smaller side pipe that gradually merges into the main pipe. Water flows in smoothly, but the side pipe itself is now part of the structure — like a *microstrip line feed*.
- **Coupled Waterfall (Aperture / Proximity Feed)**: The hose runs *underneath* the garden bed, and water rises through a slot/capillary gap into the bed above, without any physical contact. This is the *electromagnetically coupled* family (aperture coupled and proximity coupled feeds).

The "smoothness" of this water transfer is what engineers call **impedance matching** (typically targeting a **standard 50 Ω** input).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Free-space impedance**: $Z_0 = \eta_0 = 376.73 \text{ Ω} \approx 377 \text{ Ω}$ (intrinsic impedance of free space)
- **Standard feed line characteristic impedance**: $\mathbf{50 \; \Omega}$ (most RF systems)
- **Standard reference for VSWR**: VSWR $\leq 2$ (acceptable match bandwidth)
- **Dielectric constant for common substrates**: $\varepsilon_r$ (FR-4 ≈ **4.4**, Rogers RO4003C ≈ **3.38**, Duroid 5880 ≈ **2.2**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio)**
> VSWR is the ratio of maximum to minimum voltage on a lossless transmission line, given by $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$, where $\Gamma$ is the reflection coefficient at the feed point. A perfectly matched feed has $\Gamma = 0$ and VSWR = 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Microstrip Line Feed (Edge Feed)

**Construction**: A conducting strip of width $W_f$ is etched on the same substrate as the patch, with the patch typically of width $W$ and length $L$. The feed line is connected directly at the **radiating edge** of the patch.

**Operational Concept**:
- The feed line itself is part of the radiating structure.
- Impedance matching is achieved by **inset feeding** — recessing the feed point into the patch by a distance $y_0$ from the edge.
- The input impedance at the inset feed point is given by Bahl & Bhartia (1976):

$$Z_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(y_0) + jX_{\text{in}}(y_0)$$

Where:

$$R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = \dfrac{R_{\text{in}}(0)}{\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L} y_0\right)} \quad \text{and} \quad X_{\text{in}}(y_0) = \dfrac{X_{\text{in}}(0)}{\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{L} y_0\right)}$$

- $R_{\text{in}}(0) \approx 100$–$300\;\Omega$ (resonant resistance at the edge for a half-wave patch)

**Why it works**: As the feed point moves inward, the coupling to the resonant mode reduces, lowering the input resistance from hundreds of ohms down to a manageable 50 Ω. The $\cos^2$ law describes the standing-wave current distribution along the patch.

**Limitations**:
- Spurious feed-line radiation → raises cross-polarization and side-lobe levels (critical KTU pitfall).
- Narrow bandwidth (typically 2–5%).

### 2.2 Coaxial Probe Feed (Direct/Indirect)

**Construction**: The outer conductor of a coaxial cable is bonded to the ground plane; the inner conductor extends through the substrate and is soldered to the patch.

**Operational Concept**:
- The probe can be positioned anywhere inside the patch — at $(x_p, y_p)$ — to control input impedance.
- The probe introduces a **series inductance** $X_L$ that becomes significant at high frequencies (mmWave), causing reactance mismatch.
- Input impedance (rigorous model from Carver & Mink, 1981) is a sum of resonant resistance and a probe reactance term:

$$Z_{\text{in}} = R_{\text{in}} + jX_{\text{in}}$$

Where $X_{\text{in}}$ has a component $\approx -j\dfrac{\eta_0 k_0 h}{2\pi}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2}{\beta d_p}\right) - 0.5772\right]$, with $d_p$ as probe diameter and $h$ as substrate height.

**Advantages**: Easy fabrication, low spurious radiation, robust impedance control.

**Disadvantages**: Narrow bandwidth, drilling required (poor for arrays), probe inductance limits high-frequency use.

### 2.3 Aperture Coupled Feed

**Construction (two substrates)**: The feed line is on the **bottom substrate** (above the ground plane). A small **slot (aperture)** is etched in the ground plane. The patch sits on the **top substrate** above the slot. Power couples electromagnetically from the feed line, through the slot, to the patch.

**Operational Concept**:
- The slot is typically centered under the patch, oriented **perpendicular** to the feed line for maximum coupling.
- Slot length $L_s \approx 0.1\lambda_0$ to $0.2\lambda_0$ controls coupling strength.
- The aperture is modeled as a **magnetic current** $M_s$ in the ground plane (Huygens' equivalence principle).
- The slot is terminated $\lambda_s/4$ beyond the patch center (open-circuited stub) to direct all energy upward.
- Spurious radiation is suppressed because the feed line is **below** the ground plane.

**Advantages**:
- Wide bandwidth (often **> 30%**, e.g., 21% for a typical design)
- Independent optimization of feed and patch substrates
- Low cross-polarization, ideal for arrays

**Disadvantages**:
- Multilayer alignment difficulty
- Back-radiation through the slot must be absorbed

### 2.4 Proximity Coupled (Electromagnetically Coupled) Feed

**Construction (two substrates)**: The microstrip feed line is on the **lower substrate**; the patch is on the **upper substrate**, with no physical contact. Electromagnetic coupling occurs across the dielectric stack.

**Operational Concept**:
- Coupling strength is controlled by the **length of the open-circuited stub** of the feed line under the patch, and the substrate thicknesses.
- Wider bandwidth than probe/microstrip feed (typically **13% or more**).
- No probe inductance, no slot — purely capacitive/dielectric coupling.

**Advantages**: Highest bandwidth among the four, low spurious radiation.

**Disadvantages**: Hardest to fabricate (multilayer lamination), sensitive to substrate thickness.

### 2.5 Array Feed Networks — Corporate & Series

**Corporate (Parallel) Feed**:
- Uses a **binary tree of power dividers** (T-junctions, Wilkinson dividers) to split power equally/unequally to each element.
- All path lengths from input to each element are **identical** → in-phase feeding → broadside beam.
- Beam steering achieved by **adjusting path lengths** with phase shifters.
- **Advantage**: Independent amplitude/phase control per element (used in phased arrays).
- **Disadvantage**: Complex, lossy for large arrays ($N$ elements need $\log_2 N$ levels of dividers).

**Series (Sequential) Feed**:
- Elements are connected one after another along a single transmission line.
- Each element is fed from a **section of the line**, and the power flows to the next element.
- Path lengths differ progressively → introduces a **progressive phase shift** $\Delta\phi$ between elements, steering the beam.
- **Advantage**: Simple, low loss, compact (no power dividers).
- **Disadvantage**: Bandwidth-limited (beam squints with frequency), narrow-beam steering.

The progressive phase for series feed to steer at angle $\theta_0$ is:

$$\Delta \phi = -k_0 d \sin\theta_0$$

where $d$ is the inter-element spacing.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for KTU Module 2 Feeding Methods. Use the $\vert$ notation (rendered as `\vert`) for absolute values — **never use the pipe `|` inside table cells**, as it breaks Markdown rendering.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Reflection coefficient at feed | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_{\text{in}} - Z_0}{Z_{\text{in}} + Z_0}$ | $Z_0 = 50\;\Omega$ (standard) |
| 2 | VSWR | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}$ | $\geq 1$; $\text{VSWR} = 1$ is perfect match |
| 3 | Return loss (dB) | $R_L = -20 \log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$ | $R_L = \infty$ for perfect match |
| 4 | Inset feed resistance | $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = \dfrac{R_{\text{in}}(0)}{\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)}$ | Bahl & Bhartia model |
| 5 | Patch resonant length (effective) | $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 2\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = 0.412h\,\dfrac{(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} + 0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} - 0.258)(W/h + 0.813)}$ |
| 6 | Resonant frequency | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2 L_{\text{eff}} \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 7 | Effective dielectric constant | $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\!\left[1 + 12\dfrac{h}{W}\right]^{-1/2}$ | Schneider's formula |
| 8 | Probe reactance (Carver-Mink) | $X_{\text{in}} \approx -\dfrac{\eta_0 k_0 h}{2\pi}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2}{k_0 d_p}\right) - 0.5772\right]$ | For coax probe feed |
| 9 | Series feed progressive phase | $\Delta \phi = -k_0 d \sin\theta_0$ | $k_0 = 2\pi/\lambda_0$ |
| 10 | Beam squint (bandwidth limit) | $\dfrac{\Delta\theta}{\theta_0} \approx \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}\tan\theta_0$ | Critical for series-fed arrays |
| 11 | Power division in corporate feed | $P_n = P_{\text{in}} \cdot G_n$ | $G_n$ = power divider gain |
| 12 | Aperture slot length (typical) | $L_s \approx 0.1\lambda_0$ to $0.2\lambda_0$ | Aperture-coupled feed |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G mmWave Arrays (28/39 GHz)**: Use **corporate feed** with beamforming ICs (Anokiwave, Renesas) for electronically steered arrays.
- **GPS Patch Antennas (1.575 GHz)**: Use **probe feed** with high-$\varepsilon_r$ ceramic substrate for compact designs.
- **Satellite Phased Arrays**: Use **series feed** for low-profile, low-loss beam steering (e.g., direct-broadcast satellite reception).
- **Radar & Missile Seekers**: Use **aperture-coupled** patches for wideband, low-cross-pol performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inset Feed Point Location for $50\;\Omega$ Match

**Problem (KTU-style)**: A rectangular microstrip patch antenna has resonant resistance at the radiating edge $R_{\text{in}}(0) = 180\;\Omega$. The patch has length $L = 30$ mm, operating on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$ (Duroid 5880). Find the inset feed distance $y_0$ such that the input resistance is $50\;\Omega$.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation** (Bahl & Bhartia, edge-fed patch with inset):

$$R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = \dfrac{R_{\text{in}}(0)}{\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values**:

$$50 = \dfrac{180}{\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{30\;\text{mm}}\right)}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the cosine term**:

$$\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{30}\right) = \dfrac{180}{50} = 3.6$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cosine value**:

$$\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{30}\right) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{3.6}} = \sqrt{0.2778} = 0.5270$$

**Step 5 — Apply the inverse cosine**:

$$\dfrac{\pi y_0}{30} = \cos^{-1}(0.5270) = 58.19^\circ = 1.0156\;\text{rad}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $y_0$**:

$$y_0 = \dfrac{1.0156 \times 30}{\pi} = \dfrac{30.468}{3.1416} = 9.70\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Verify by substitution**:

$$\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \times 9.70}{30}\right) = \cos^2(1.0156\;\text{rad}) = (0.5270)^2 = 0.2778$$

$$R_{\text{in}}(9.70) = \dfrac{180}{0.2778} = 648.0\;\Omega \quad (\text{apparent mismatch!})$$

**Wait — recheck the algebra.** The original equation is $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0)\cos^{-2}(\cdot)$ — meaning the resistance **increases** as we move inward? No, this is the standard Bahl-Bhartia form for an inset-fed patch, but typically the resistance **decreases** from the edge. Let us re-derive carefully.

**Corrected Standard Form** (per Bahl, Bhartia & Gupta, *Microstrip Antenna Design Handbook*, 1981):

The voltage across the slot at the edge of the patch is maximum, and the input resistance of an inset-fed patch is:

$$R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0)\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)$$

where $R_{\text{in}}(0) \approx 200\text{–}300\;\Omega$ at the edge for a half-wave patch with $W \gg h$.

**Re-solving with corrected formula**:

$$50 = 180 \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)$$

$$\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right) = \dfrac{50}{180} = 0.2778$$

$$\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right) = 0.5270$$

$$\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L} = \cos^{-1}(0.5270) = 58.19^\circ = 1.0156\;\text{rad}$$

$$y_0 = \dfrac{1.0156 \times 30}{\pi} = 9.70\;\text{mm}$$

**Final answer**: $\boxed{y_0 \approx 9.70\;\text{mm}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Formulas Are NOT the Same for Every Feed Type**. Many students blindly memorize the $\cos^{-2}$ form from a different textbook. **Always state the governing equation before substituting** to avoid losing 4–6 marks in Part B.

### 3.2 Derivation: Aperture-Coupled Feed — Coupling Coefficient

For an aperture-coupled patch, the **coupling coefficient** $\kappa$ between the feed line and the patch is related to the slot length $L_s$ and the open-circuited stub length $L_{\text{ap}}$ (Pozar, 1985):

$$S_{11} \approx \dfrac{1 - \kappa^2}{1 + \kappa^2} \quad \text{(at resonance, neglecting losses)}$$

**Design Rule (from Pozar)**: For a **perfect match** ($S_{11} = 0$), we need $\kappa = 1$, which is achieved when:

$$L_s \approx 0.17\,\lambda_0 \quad \text{and} \quad L_{\text{ap}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{slot}}}{4}$$

where $\lambda_{\text{slot}}$ is the guided wavelength in the slot-bearing substrate.

### 3.3 Derivation: Series Feed Beam Squint

Consider an $N$-element linear series-fed array. The progressive phase between elements is $\Delta\phi$. The beam points at:

$$\sin\theta_0 = -\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{k_0 d}$$

If frequency changes by $\Delta f$ (where $\Delta f / f_0 = \epsilon \ll 1$), the guided wavelength changes, so:

$$\Delta\phi' = \Delta\phi \cdot (1 + \epsilon)$$

The new beam direction is:

$$\theta_0' = \sin^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\Delta\phi'}{k_0' d}\right] = \sin^{-1}\!\left[(1+\epsilon)\sin\theta_0\right]$$

For small $\epsilon$ and using Taylor expansion:

$$\theta_0' \approx \theta_0 + \epsilon\,\theta_0 \tan\theta_0$$

So the **beam squint** is:

$$\Delta\theta_{\text{squint}} = \epsilon\,\theta_0\tan\theta_0 = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0}\,\theta_0\tan\theta_0$$

This explains why series-fed arrays are **bandwidth-limited** for off-broadside beams.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Performance of the Four Microstrip Feed Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table** for KTU 2-mark and 14-mark questions. Memorize it.

| Criterion | Microstrip Line | Coaxial Probe | Aperture Coupled | Proximity Coupled |
|-----------|:--------------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Spurious Radiation** | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Bandwidth** | 2–5% | 2–5% | 5–21% | 10–13% |
| **Fabrication** | Easy | Easy (drilling) | Difficult (multilayer) | Very Difficult |
| **Impedance Match Control** | Good (via inset) | Good (via probe position) | Excellent (slot + stub) | Excellent (stub length) |
| **Cross-Polarization** | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Suitability for Arrays** | Moderate | Poor (probe per element) | Excellent | Good |
| **Polarization Purity** | Poor | Moderate | High | High |
| **Best Application** | Low-cost single patches | GPS, narrowband | Wideband / phased arrays | Broadband designs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Feeding Methods

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Aperture-Coupled Feed Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR

    B --> C["Slot Aperture in Ground Plane"]:::slot

    D --> E["Free Space Radiation"]:::rad

    F -.->|Tune for max coupling| C

    classDef src fill:#c00000,stroke:#c00000,color:#ffffff

```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Series vs Corporate Feed Architecture for $N = 4$ Array

```mermaid

    B1 --> C1["T Junction Divider Level 2a"]:::div

    C1 --> D2["Element 2"]:::elem

    D2b --> B4["Series Feed Line Segment 3"]:::ser

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams**:
> - The **top architecture** (binary tree) is the **Corporate Feed** — equal path lengths → broadside beam.
> - The **bottom architecture** (chain) is the **Series Feed** — progressive phase shift → steered beam, terminated with a matched load to absorb residual power.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the difference between contacting and non-contacting feed methods in microstrip antennas? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer**:

Contacting (direct) feed methods involve a **physical metallic connection** between the feed line and the radiating patch. Examples: *microstrip line feed* and *coaxial probe feed*.

Non-contacting (indirect) feed methods use **electromagnetic coupling** to transfer power from the feed line to the patch, with no DC path. Examples: *aperture coupled feed* and *proximity coupled feed*.

Non-contacting methods offer **higher bandwidth, lower spurious radiation, and better polarization purity**, at the cost of multilayer fabrication complexity.

**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for examples, 1 for tradeoff discussion)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Why is a quarter-wave open stub used in aperture-coupled microstrip antennas?*

**Model Answer**:

The quarter-wave ($\lambda_s/4$) open-circuited stub extends beyond the slot center and presents an **effective short circuit** at the slot location (since a quarter-wave line transforms an open circuit into a short circuit). This ensures that:

1. Maximum magnetic current is excited in the slot.
2. The slot radiates efficiently into the upper substrate (where the patch sits).
3. Minimum back-radiation into the lower substrate (feed side).

Thus the stub acts as an **impedance transformer** that maximizes the forward coupling to the patch.

**(3 marks — 1 for the open-to-short transformation, 1 for slot excitation, 1 for back-radiation suppression)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice**: Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Inset Feed & Matching Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

(a) Explain the **microstrip line inset feed** technique for a rectangular patch antenna. Derive the expression for the input resistance as a function of inset depth $y_0$. **(7 marks)**

(b) A rectangular microstrip patch antenna operating at 2.4 GHz has a resonant edge resistance $R_{\text{in}}(0) = 200\;\Omega$ and a length $L = \dfrac{\lambda_g}{2} = 30.9$ mm (using $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$). Calculate the **inset feed depth** $y_0$ required to match the antenna to a 50 $\Omega$ feed line. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

**Inset feed technique** (also called "recessed feed"): A microstrip line of width $W_f$ (chosen to give 50 $\Omega$ characteristic impedance) is etched on the same substrate as the patch. Instead of connecting the line at the radiating edge (where the resistance is very high, $\sim 200$–$300\;\Omega$), the line is **recessed** into the patch by a distance $y_0$ from the edge.

**Why it works**: The patch can be modeled as a slot (magnetic-wall resonator) of length $L$ supporting a standing wave. The voltage across the slot is maximum at the edges and zero at the center. The input impedance tapped at position $y_0$ is therefore related to the **voltage standing wave** along the slot.

**Derivation** (sketch):

Let $V_0$ be the voltage at the radiating edge, and $V(y_0)$ the voltage at the inset point. The standing wave gives:

$$V(y_0) = V_0 \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)$$

If the edge resistance (at $y_0 = 0$) is $R_{\text{in}}(0)$, then the corresponding power at resonance is:

$$P_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{\vert V_0\vert^2}{2 R_{\text{in}}(0)} = \dfrac{\vert V(y_0)\vert^2}{2 R_{\text{in}}(y_0)}$$

Equating:

$$\dfrac{V_0^2}{R_{\text{in}}(0)} = \dfrac{V_0^2 \cos^2(\pi y_0/L)}{R_{\text{in}}(y_0)}$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0)\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)}$$

This is the **Bahl–Bhartia formula**, valid for $-L/2 \leq y_0 \leq L/2$.

The reactance $X_{\text{in}}(y_0) \approx 0$ at the resonant frequency, and a small imaginary correction is added for off-resonance operation.

**(7 marks — 2 for concept, 3 for derivation, 2 for final formula and validity range)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Given**:
- $R_{\text{in}}(0) = 200\;\Omega$
- $L = 30.9$ mm
- Target: $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = 50\;\Omega$

**Step 1** — Use the inset-feed equation:

$$R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0)\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)$$

**Step 2** — Substitute:

$$50 = 200\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right)$$

**Step 3** — Isolate the $\cos^2$ term:

$$\cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right) = \dfrac{50}{200} = 0.25$$

**Step 4** — Take the square root:

$$\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L}\right) = 0.5$$

**Step 5** — Apply inverse cosine:

$$\dfrac{\pi y_0}{L} = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{3}\;\text{rad}$$

**Step 6** — Solve for $y_0$:

$$y_0 = \dfrac{L}{3} = \dfrac{30.9}{3} = 10.3\;\text{mm}$$

**Final answer**: $\boxed{y_0 = 10.3\;\text{mm} \approx L/3}$

**Verification**:
- $\cos(\pi \times 10.3 / 30.9) = \cos(60^\circ) = 0.5$
- $\cos^2(60^\circ) = 0.25$
- $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = 200 \times 0.25 = 50\;\Omega$ ✓

**(7 marks — 1 for setup, 2 for substitution, 2 for trig manipulation, 1 for solution, 1 for verification)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> 1. Do **not** confuse $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0) \cos^{-2}(\cdot)$ with the **correct** $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0) \cos^2(\cdot)$. The wrong form gives $y_0 = 16.7$ mm (cosine-inverse of 0.25 is $60^\circ$, then divided by 3 gives 1/6 of L — wait, actually both forms give *different* answers: cosine squared gives $y_0 = L/3 \approx 10.3$ mm, while cosine inverse squared gives $y_0 = L/6 \approx 5.15$ mm). Always write the formula with correct sign convention.
> 2. **Units consistency**: If $L$ is in mm, $y_0$ comes out in mm. No unit conversion errors.
> 3. Show all trig steps. Skipping $\cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60^\circ$ loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Array Feed Networks*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**

(a) Compare **corporate** and **series** feed networks for antenna arrays. Discuss their relative merits in terms of beam steering, bandwidth, and design complexity. **(7 marks)**

(b) A 4-element linear array operating at 10 GHz uses a **series feed** with inter-element spacing $d = \lambda_0/2 = 15$ mm. Find the required **progressive phase shift** $\Delta\phi$ to steer the beam at $\theta_0 = 30^\circ$ from broadside. If the operating frequency drifts to 10.2 GHz, calculate the **beam squint** $\Delta\theta$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

**Corporate (Parallel) Feed**:
- Structure: A **binary tree of power dividers** (T-junctions, Wilkinson dividers).
- All elements receive equal power (or weighted amplitude).
- Path length from input port to **every** element is **identical** → in-phase excitation → broadside beam (or steered with phase shifters).
- **Beam steering**: Achieved by inserting **phase shifters** in each branch (active phased array).
- **Bandwidth**: Excellent — does not depend on frequency, since the network is matched at every node.
- **Complexity**: Increases as $O(\log_2 N)$ for $N$ elements; many components, more loss.

**Series (Sequential) Feed**:
- Structure: Elements tapped off a **single transmission line** in sequence.
- Each element couples a portion of the wave; the rest continues to the next element.
- A **progressive phase shift** $\Delta\phi$ is built into the inter-element path length: $\Delta\phi = -\beta_0 \cdot d_{\text{line}}$, where $d_{\text{line}}$ is the line segment length.
- **Beam steering**: Built-in via the path-length difference; no phase shifters needed for fixed-beam designs.
- **Bandwidth**: Poor — the phase shift is **frequency-dependent**; beam direction drifts with frequency (beam squint).
- **Complexity**: Simple, compact, low loss; suited for fixed-beam applications.

**Comparison Table** (must appear in answer):

| Criterion | Corporate Feed | Series Feed |
|-----------|:--------------:|:-----------:|
| Beam steering | Phase shifters (active) | Path length (passive) |
| Bandwidth | Wide | Narrow |
| Design complexity | High | Low |
| Loss for $N$ elements | $\sim \log_2 N$ stages | Single line |
| Beam squint | None | Significant |

**(7 marks — 2 each for explanation of corporate & series, 2 for comparison table, 1 for summary)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Given**:
- $f_0 = 10$ GHz, $d = 15$ mm, $\theta_0 = 30^\circ$, $N = 4$ elements, $f' = 10.2$ GHz

**Step 1** — Compute the free-space wavelength:

$$\lambda_0 = \dfrac{c}{f_0} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 0.03\;\text{m} = 30\;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2** — Verify inter-element spacing:

$$d = \lambda_0/2 = 15\;\text{mm} \quad\checkmark$$

**Step 3** — Compute wavenumber:

$$k_0 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda_0} = \dfrac{2\pi}{0.03} = 209.44\;\text{rad/m}$$

**Step 4** — Apply the progressive phase formula for series feed:

$$\Delta\phi = -k_0 d \sin\theta_0$$

$$\Delta\phi = -209.44 \times 0.015 \times \sin(30^\circ)$$

$$\Delta\phi = -209.44 \times 0.015 \times 0.5 = -1.5708\;\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\phi = -\dfrac{\pi}{2} = -90^\circ}$$

**Step 5** — Compute beam squint at 10.2 GHz:

The fractional frequency drift:

$$\epsilon = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = \dfrac{10.2 - 10}{10} = 0.02$$

The new beam direction:

$$\sin\theta_0' = (1 + \epsilon)\sin\theta_0 = 1.02 \times 0.5 = 0.51$$

$$\theta_0' = \sin^{-1}(0.51) = 30.66^\circ$$

**Step 6** — Compute the squint:

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_0' - \theta_0 = 30.66^\circ - 30^\circ = 0.66^\circ$$

**Alternative (using squint formula)**:

$$\Delta\theta \approx \epsilon\,\theta_0\tan\theta_0 = 0.02 \times 30^\circ \times \tan(30^\circ) = 0.02 \times 30 \times 0.5774 = 0.346^\circ$$

(Note: The two methods give slightly different answers because the small-angle Taylor expansion used in the squint formula is approximate.)

**Final answer**: $\boxed{\Delta\phi = -\pi/2\;\text{rad} = -90^\circ, \quad \Delta\theta \approx 0.66^\circ}$

**(7 marks — 2 for $k_0$ and $d$ verification, 2 for $\Delta\phi$ calculation, 2 for squint computation, 1 for final answer)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**:
> 1. The sign of $\Delta\phi$ matters. A **negative** $\Delta\phi$ (phase lag) corresponds to beam steering toward $+\theta$ (right of broadside). Many students forget the sign.
> 2. $\Delta\theta$ in the squint formula is in **radians**; the conversion to degrees must be explicit if you use the Taylor approximation.
> 3. For high squint values (e.g., $\epsilon > 5\%$), the linear approximation $\Delta\theta \approx \epsilon\theta_0\tan\theta_0$ breaks down. Use the exact $\sin^{-1}$ formula.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feeding Methods (Module 2)**
> - **Four primary microstrip feeds**: Microstrip line, Coaxial probe, Aperture coupled, Proximity coupled.
> - **Inset feed** uses the Bahl–Bhartia formula: $R_{\text{in}}(y_0) = R_{\text{in}}(0)\cos^2(\pi y_0/L)$. For $50\;\Omega$ match from $R_{\text{in}}(0) = 200\;\Omega$, inset is $L/3$.
> - **Coaxial probe** introduces series inductance; problematic above ~6 GHz.
> - **Aperture coupled** uses two substrates + slot; bandwidth can exceed 20%.
> - **Proximity coupled** has the **highest bandwidth** among the four.
> - **Corporate feed**: parallel power dividers; equal path lengths; broadside beam; supports phase-shifter-based beam steering.
> - **Series feed**: chain of elements; built-in progressive phase; subject to **beam squint**.
> - **Beam squint formula**: $\Delta\theta \approx (\Delta f/f_0)\,\theta_0\tan\theta_0$.
> - **VSWR** = 1 is ideal; VSWR $\leq 2$ is the standard bandwidth criterion.
> - **Standard reference impedance** = 50 $\Omega$ (some systems use 75 $\Omega$).
> - **Slot length** in aperture feed: $L_s \approx 0.17\lambda_0$.
> - **Quarter-wave open stub** transforms open circuit → short circuit at slot, maximizing coupling.
> - **Beam steering angle** in series feed: $\sin\theta_0 = -\Delta\phi/(k_0 d)$.
> - **Trade-off to remember**: bandwidth ↑ → fabrication complexity ↑.
> - **For KTU exams**: Always draw a labeled diagram, state the governing equation, show all intermediate trig steps, and use proper SI units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Line Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Transmission Line Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Transmission Line Model (TLM) for Antennas** is a classical analytical framework that treats a linear radiating antenna (such as a dipole) as a **two-wire transmission line whose open-circuited ends have been progressively flared apart into a straight radiating structure.** According to Balanis (the standard reference adopted by KTU for PEECT865), the transmission line model is the **first and most intuitive method** for analyzing wire-type antennas. It assumes that the current on a thin, center-fed dipole follows the same **sinusoidal standing-wave distribution** that exists on an open-circuited two-wire transmission line of equivalent length. Formally: $$I(z) \;=\; I_0 \,\frac{\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}-|z|\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\,\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}, \qquad -\tfrac{L}{2}\le z\le \tfrac{L}{2}$$ where: - $L$ = total physical length of the dipole - $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ = phase constant - $I_0$ = input (feed-point) current - $z$ = position along the dipole axis, measured from the feed-point ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **The Garden Hose Analogy** — Imagine a long garden hose lying on the ground. Water (current) flows in, pressure builds up, and because the far end is sealed, water sloshes back and forth — creating a *standing wave* with maximum amplitude at the closed end and a node at the input if the length is just right. Now **pull the two conductors of a transmission line apart** and flatten them into two straight metal rods pointing in opposite directions. The radio-frequency current, instead of staying confined inside the wires, now radiates into free space because the geometry has been "opened up." The *pattern of current* along the rods is still sinusoidal (just like in the hose), and that current distribution is what produces the antenna's radiation pattern. This is, in essence, the **Transmission Line Model**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standards - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** - **Free-space impedance:** $\eta_0 \approx 376.73 \;\Omega$ **(≈ 120π Ω)** - **Wavelength-frequency relation:** $\lambda = c/f$ - **Phase constant (free space):** $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ **(rad/m)** > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Transmission Line Model forms the foundation for deriving the **far-field pattern of a thin linear dipole** and is a **direct, frequently-asked 7-to-14-mark topic** in Module 2. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Logic — Step by Step The Transmission Line Model proceeds in four conceptual stages: 1. **Begin with a two-wire transmission line** carrying a TEM wave. Voltage $V$ and current $I$ are related by the characteristic impedance $Z_0$. 2. **Progressively open (flare) the two conductors apart**, maintaining the *same spacing-related capacitance and inductance per unit length* in a localized sense. 3. **At each incremental opening angle**, a small fraction of the guided wave *leaks* into space — the line is no longer a perfect waveguide. The remaining current redistributes itself. 4. **In the limit** where the conductors form two collinear rods of length $L/2$ on either side of the feed (i.e., a dipole), the current *must vanish* at the open ends ($z = \pm L/2$) by the boundary condition that no current can flow off the end of a thin wire. This forces a sinusoidal standing wave. ### 2.1.1 Why Sinusoidal? > [!IMPORTANT] > Because the dipole ends are **open circuits**, the current at $z = \pm L/2$ must be **zero**. A standing wave on a lossless line automatically satisfies $\sin(\beta s) = 0$ at $s = \pm L/2$, where $s = L/2 - |z|$ is the distance from the nearest end. Hence the current must be sinusoidal. ### 2.1.2 How Each Step Links to Radiation - The sinusoidal current $I(z)$ acts as a **line source** of radiation. - Each infinitesimal element $dz$ contributes a spherical wave whose far-field amplitude is proportional to $I(z)\,dz$. - Integrating these contributions along the length gives the **total far-field pattern** $E_\theta$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations — Memorize these for the 14-mark questions.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Current on dipole | $I(z) = I_0 \dfrac{\sin\!\left[\beta\left(\frac{L}{2}-\vert z\vert\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\frac{L}{2}\right)}$ | A; $-\tfrac{L}{2}\le z\le\tfrac{L}{2}$ | | 2 | Far-zone E-field | $E_\theta = j\,\dfrac{\eta_0\, I_0\, e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r}\left[\dfrac{\cos\!\left(\beta\frac{L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\beta\frac{L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]$ | V/m | | 3 | Normalized pattern | $F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Half-wave dipole ($L=\lambda/2$) | $F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}$ | classic 8-figure | | 5 | Input impedance (half-wave) | $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j\,42.5$ | $\Omega$ (Balanis) | | 6 | Resonant length | $L_{res} \approx 0.48\,\lambda$ to $0.5\,\lambda$ | shortens due to end-effect | | 7 | Half-power beamwidth (HPBW) | $\approx 78^\circ$ | for $\lambda/2$ dipole | | 8 | Directivity (half-wave) | $D_0 = 1.643 \;(= 2.16\text{ dBi})$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Maximum effective aperture | $A_{em} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}\,D_0 \approx 0.130\,\lambda^2$ | m² | | 10 | Phase constant | $\beta = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\omega}{c}$ | rad/m | > [!WARNING] > When writing $\vert z \vert$ in any formula, use `\vert z \vert` in LaTeX so the table markdown does **not** break. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HF/VHF/UHF dipole design:** Every commercial whip antenna, Yagi-Uda driven element, and folded dipole is first analyzed using the transmission line model. - **RFID and IoT tag antennas:** Compact dipole tags rely on current-distribution intuition from the TLM. - **Broadcasting towers:** AM broadcast dipoles are designed using TLM-predicted sinusoidal currents. - **EMC/EMI testing:** Broadband dipole probes use TLM-derived patterns to characterize electromagnetic compatibility. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Current Distribution on a Linear Dipole ### Starting Point Consider a center-fed dipole of total length $L$, oriented along the $z$-axis, with the feed-point at $z = 0$. ### Step 1 — Boundary Condition Because the ends of the dipole are open (no conductor beyond), the **current must vanish at $z = \pm L/2$**: $$I\!\left(z = \pm \tfrac{L}{2}\right) = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Standing-Wave Assumption The transmission line model postulates that the current is a **standing sinusoidal wave** with wavenumber $\beta$: $$I(z) = A\,\sin\!\bigl[\beta(L/2 - \vert z \vert)\bigr] + B\,\cos\!\bigl[\beta(L/2 - \vert z \vert)\bigr]$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Boundary Condition Substitute $z = \pm L/2$ into the sine/cosine argument. The argument becomes $\sin(0) = 0$ and $\cos(0) = 1$. To force $I(\pm L/2) = 0$: $$I(\pm L/2) = A\cdot 0 + B\cdot 1 = B = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; B = 0$$ So only the sine term survives. ### Step 4 — Normalize Using Feed-Point Current At the feed-point ($z = 0$), the argument is $\sin(\beta L/2)$. Set this equal to $I_0$: $$I(0) = A\,\sin\!\left(\beta\,\tfrac{L}{2}\right) = I_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = \dfrac{I_0}{\sin(\beta L/2)}$$ ### Step 5 — Final Closed Form Substituting $A$ back into the surviving sine-only expression: $$\boxed{\,I(z) = I_0\,\dfrac{\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}-\vert z\vert\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\,\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}\,}$$ **Interpretation:** - The numerator is **zero** at the ends $\Rightarrow$ boundary condition satisfied. - The denominator is **maximum (positive)** when $L = \lambda/2$ ($\beta L/2 = \pi/2$) $\Rightarrow$ peak input current for a half-wave dipole. - For $L = \lambda$, $\sin(\beta L/2) = \sin(\pi) = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ **no current** can flow $\Rightarrow$ full-wave dipole cannot be center-fed by this simple model (requires offset feed). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Far-Field Radiation Pattern of the Dipole ### Step 1 — Vector Potential from a Current Element For an infinitesimal current element $I(z')\,dz'$ located at position $z'$ on the $z$-axis, the magnetic vector potential in the far-zone is: $$dA_z = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\,\dfrac{I(z')\,e^{-j\beta R}}{R}\,dz'$$ where $R$ is the distance from the source point to the observation point. ### Step 2 — Far-Zone Approximation In the far-zone ($r \gg L$), the distance $R$ is approximated as: $$R \approx r - z'\cos\theta$$ Substituting this into the exponential phase term only (the $1/R$ amplitude term uses $R \approx r$): $$\dfrac{e^{-j\beta R}}{R} \approx \dfrac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r}\,e^{+j\beta z'\cos\theta}$$ ### Step 3 — Integrate Along the Dipole Length $$A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0\,e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} I(z')\,e^{+j\beta z'\cos\theta}\,dz'$$ Insert the sinusoidal current expression from Derivation 1. ### Step 4 — Compute the Integral $$\int_{-L/2}^{L/2} \sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\tfrac{L}{2}-\vert z'\vert\right)\right] e^{+j\beta z'\cos\theta}\,dz'$$ Split the integral at $z'=0$ and use the even-symmetry of $\sin[\beta(L/2-\vert z'\vert)]$: $$= 2\int_{0}^{L/2}\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\tfrac{L}{2}-z'\right)\right]\cos(\beta z'\cos\theta)\,dz'$$ Substitute $u = \beta(L/2 - z')$, so $z' = L/2 - u/\beta$ and $dz' = -du/\beta$.
