The limits $z'=0\Rightarrow u=\beta L/2$, and $z'=L/2\Rightarrow u=0$: $$= \dfrac{2}{\beta}\int_{0}^{\beta L/2}\sin(u)\,\cos\!\left[\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}-u\right)\cos\theta\right]\,du$$ Expanding the cosine using $\cos(A-B) = \cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B$, with $A = (\beta L/2)\cos\theta$ and $B = u\cos\theta$: $$= \dfrac{2}{\beta}\!\left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)\!\int_0^{\beta L/2}\!\!\sin u\,\cos(u\cos\theta)\,du + \sin\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)\!\int_0^{\beta L/2}\!\!\sin u\,\sin(u\cos\theta)\,du\right]$$ Both integrals evaluate (using product-to-sum identities) to: $$= \dfrac{2}{\beta\,\sin\theta}\!\left[\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\right)\right]$$ ### Step 5 — Compute $E_\theta$ from $A_z$ Using $E_\theta = j\omega\mu_0\,A_z\sin\theta / (4\pi) \cdot (\text{full factor})$, and after absorbing constants into $I_0$: $$\boxed{\,E_\theta(\theta) = j\,\dfrac{\eta_0\,I_0\,e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r}\!\left[\dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]\,}$$ ### Step 6 — Normalized Pattern Function Dividing out all constants (including the phase and $1/r$ factors), we obtain the **radiation pattern**: $$\boxed{\,F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}\,}$$ ### Step 7 — Special Case: Half-Wave Dipole ($L = \lambda/2$) Setting $\beta L/2 = \pi/2$, so $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$: $$F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}$$ This is the **classical "figure-8" doughnut pattern** in the E-plane. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Input Impedance of a Half-Wave Dipole Using the induced-EMF method (Balanis, Ch. 8), the input impedance of a thin center-fed dipole of length $L$ is: $$Z_{in} = R_{in} + jX_{in}$$ For the **half-wave dipole ($L = \lambda/2$)**, the numerical result is: $$Z_{in} \approx 73 + j\,42.5\;\;\Omega$$ To make the dipole **resonant** (purely real impedance), the length is *slightly shortened* to about $0.475\lambda$ to $0.48\lambda$, eliminating the reactive part: $$L_{res} \approx 0.48\,\lambda \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; Z_{in}\approx 70\;\Omega\;\;\text{(purely real)}$$ > [!NOTE] > The reactive component $j42.5\;\Omega$ can also be cancelled with a small lumped reactive element at the feed if the physical length must remain exactly $\lambda/2$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Dipole Lengths | Length $L$ | $\beta L/2$ | Pattern Shape | Input $Z_{in}$ | Notes | |------------|--------------|----------------|-----------------|-------| | $L \ll \lambda$ | $\to 0$ | Nearly isotropic ($\approx \sin\theta$) | $Z_{in}\to 0$ | Hertzian (infinitesimal) dipole limit | | $L = 0.5\lambda$ | $\pi/2$ | Figure-8 (doughnut) | $73 + j42.5\;\Omega$ | Most common practical dipole | | $L = 0.48\lambda$ | $0.48\pi$ | Nearly figure-8 | $\approx 70\;\Omega$ (resonant) | Resonant half-wave | | $L = \lambda$ | $\pi$ | $\sin(\pi\cos\theta)/\sin\theta$ | Very high | **Cannot** be center-fed (TLM) | | $L = 1.5\lambda$ | $3\pi/2$ | Multi-lobed | Complex | Higher-order mode | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow of the Transmission Line Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Two-wire TEM transmission line carrying I and V"] --> B["Progressively flare the two conductors apart"] B --> C["At each angle, a fraction of energy leaks into free space as radiation"] C --> D["In the limit: two collinear rods forming a linear dipole of length L"] D --> E["Open-circuit end condition forces sinusoidal current I of z"] E --> F["Current distribution I of z acts as a continuous line source"] F --> G["Integrate dz over entire length to compute vector potential A sub z"] G --> H["Far-zone approximation R approx r minus z cos theta"] H --> I["Solve integral using product to sum identities"] I --> J["Derive normalized radiation pattern F of theta"] J --> K["Apply special case L equals lambda by 2 for figure 8 doughnut pattern"] K --> L["Compute input impedance Zin via induced EMF method"] L --> M["Output: complete antenna characterization for design"] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Physical Geometry of the Dipole ```mermaid flowchart LR Feed["Feed point at z = 0
impedance Zin"] --> UpperRod["Upper arm
length L by 2
z from 0 to plus L by 2"] Feed --> LowerRod["Lower arm
length L by 2
z from 0 to minus L by 2"] UpperRod --> UpperEnd["Open end
I equals 0 at z equals plus L by 2"] LowerRod --> LowerEnd["Open end
I equals 0 at z equals minus L by 2"] UpperRod -.->|"Sinusoidal current"| Pattern["Far field pattern
F of theta
figure 8 shape"] LowerRod -.->|"Sinusoidal current"| Pattern ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Source["Source Stage"] S1["RF Source
voltage Vs
impedance Zs"] end subgraph Feed["Feed Network"] F1["Feed line
characteristic impedance Z0
typically 50 ohms"] F2["Balun
converts balanced to unbalanced"] end subgraph Antenna["Antenna Stage - TLM"] A1["Input terminals
z equals 0
current I0"] A2["Upper radiating arm
sinusoidal current distribution"] A3["Lower radiating arm
mirror image distribution"] A4["Open ends
I equals 0 boundary"] end subgraph Field["Far-Field Stage"] FF1["Radiated E theta
function of theta"] FF2["Pattern function F of theta"] FF3["Directivity D0
HPBW"] end S1 --> F1 --> F2 --> A1 A1 --> A2 A1 --> A3 A2 --> A4 A3 --> A4 A2 --> FF1 A3 --> FF1 FF1 --> FF2 --> FF3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]` **State the basic assumptions of the transmission line model used to analyze a linear dipole antenna.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The transmission line model assumes that: 1. The current on a thin, center-fed dipole follows a **sinusoidal standing-wave distribution** identical to that on an open-circuited two-wire transmission line. 2. The **current vanishes at the open ends** of the dipole ($z = \pm L/2$) because no current can flow beyond the conductor tip. 3. The current is **maximum at the feed-point** (for $L \le \lambda$) and is symmetric about the feed. 4. The **far-field is derived by treating each current element** $I(z')dz'$ as a Hertzian dipole and integrating over the entire length. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Write the expression for the current distribution on a center-fed thin linear dipole of length $L$ and explain why it is sinusoidal.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The current distribution is: $$I(z) = I_0\,\dfrac{\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}-\vert z\vert\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\,\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}$$ The distribution is sinusoidal because (i) the dipole ends are open circuits, forcing $I = 0$ at $z = \pm L/2$, (ii) the antenna behaves like a transmission-line resonator supporting standing waves, and (iii) the phase constant $\beta$ along the wire equals that of the free-space propagation, leading to a $\sin(\beta s)$ dependence on the distance $s$ from each end. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]` **(a)** Starting from the current distribution on a center-fed thin linear dipole, **derive the far-field radiation pattern** $F(\theta)$ for a dipole of length $L$. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** Hence **obtain the normalized pattern** of a half-wave dipole, sketch its E-plane pattern, and state the **HPBW and directivity**. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Solution to A(a) — Derivation of Far-Field Pattern **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the current distribution: $$I(z) = I_0\,\dfrac{\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}-\vert z\vert\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\,\dfrac{L}{2}\right)}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Express the vector potential from a current element: $$A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\int_{-L/2}^{L/2}\dfrac{I(z')\,e^{-j\beta R}}{R}\,dz'$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply far-zone approximation: $$R \approx r - z'\cos\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{e^{-j\beta R}}{R} \approx \dfrac{e^{-j\beta r}}{r}\,e^{+j\beta z'\cos\theta}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Set up and split the integral at $z'=0$ (using even symmetry of $\sin[\beta(L/2-\vert z'\vert)]$): $$A_z = \dfrac{\mu_0\,I_0\,e^{-j\beta r}}{4\pi r\,\sin(\beta L/2)}\cdot 2\int_{0}^{L/2}\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\tfrac{L}{2}-z'\right)\right]\!\cos(\beta z'\cos\theta)\,dz'$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute $u = \beta(L/2 - z')$ and evaluate using product-to-sum identities: $$\int_0^{\beta L/2}\sin u\,\cos(u\cos\theta)\,du = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\beta\,\sin^2\theta}\cdot \beta\,\sin\theta$$ giving the final compact result. **Final Answer (after substituting $A_z$ into $E_\theta = j\omega\mu_0 A_z\sin\theta$):** $$\boxed{\,E_\theta = j\,\dfrac{\eta_0 I_0 e^{-j\beta r}}{2\pi r}\!\left[\dfrac{\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}\right]\,}$$ (7 marks awarded for the complete derivation including all constant factors.) --- #### Solution to A(b) — Half-Wave Specialization **Step 1 (2 marks):** Substitute $L = \lambda/2$, so $\beta L/2 = \pi/2$. Since $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$: $$F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Confirm key values: - At $\theta = 90^\circ$: $F = \cos(0)/\sin(90^\circ) = 1/1 = 1$ (maximum). - At $\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$: $\cos(\pi/2)/\sin 0 = 0/0$ — apply L'Hôpital, gives $F = 0$ (null along the dipole axis). **Step 3 (2 marks):** Sketch — a **figure-8 (doughnut)** in the E-plane with two major lobes perpendicular to the dipole axis and **nulls at $\theta = 0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$**. **Step 4 (1 mark):** HPBW: $\approx 78^\circ$ (between the two half-power points). **Step 5 (1 mark):** Directivity: $D_0 = 1.643 \approx 2.16\text{ dBi}$. --- ### Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice `[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **transmission line model** of a dipole antenna. How is the current distribution obtained from this model? **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A **half-wave dipole** is operating at **100 MHz** in free space. Compute (i) its physical length, (ii) its input impedance, (iii) its HPBW, and (iv) its maximum effective aperture. **\[7 marks\]** --- #### Solution to B(a) — Transmission Line Model Explanation **Step 1 (2 marks):** Begin by drawing the geometry: - Start with a parallel-plate / two-wire transmission line carrying a TEM wave. - Show arrows indicating how the conductors are progressively *flared apart*. - End with a sketch of the dipole as two collinear rods of length $L/2$ on either side of the feed-point. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Explain the physical reasoning: - At each stage of flaring, a small portion of the guided energy leaks into free space as radiation. - In the limit of full opening, the structure becomes a dipole of length $L$. - The *current pattern* on the dipole is determined by the standing wave that must exist on an open-circuited transmission line of equivalent length. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply boundary conditions: - **Open ends** ($z = \pm L/2$) $\Rightarrow$ $I = 0$. - **Standing-wave assumption** $\Rightarrow$ $I(z) \propto \sin[\beta(L/2 - \vert z \vert)]$. - **Feed-point normalization** $\Rightarrow$ the constant is fixed by $I(0) = I_0$. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Write final expression: $$I(z) = I_0\,\dfrac{\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\tfrac{L}{2}-\vert z\vert\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\,\tfrac{L}{2}\right)}$$ --- #### Solution to B(b) — Numerical Computation **Given:** $f = 100$ MHz, free space. **(i) Physical length (2 marks):** $$\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3\times 10^8}{100\times 10^6} = 3\;\text{m}$$ $$L = \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = 1.5\;\text{m}$$ **(ii) Input impedance (2 marks):** For a thin half-wave dipole (from induced-EMF method): $$Z_{in} \approx 73 + j\,42.5\;\Omega$$ (Magnitude $|Z_{in}| \approx 84.4\;\Omega$; commonly matched to $75\;\Omega$ feeder.) **(iii) HPBW (1 mark):** $$\text{HPBW} \approx 78^\circ \quad \text{(for a half-wave dipole)}$$ **(iv) Maximum effective aperture (2 marks):** $$A_{em} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{4\pi}\,D_0 = \dfrac{(3)^2}{4\pi}\times 1.643$$ $$A_{em} = \dfrac{9 \times 1.643}{12.566} = \dfrac{14.787}{12.566} \approx 1.177\;\text{m}^2$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Points:** > 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** $I = 0$ at the dipole ends while writing the current expression → loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing the far-zone phase approximation** $R \approx r - z'\cos\theta$ with the amplitude approximation $R \approx r$ → loses 1 mark per occurrence. > 3. **Skipping constants** in $E_\theta$ (the $j\eta_0 e^{-j\beta r}/(2\pi r)$ prefactor is *not* the pattern — only the bracket is) → loses up to 2 marks. > 4. **Using $\lambda = c\,f$ instead of $c/f$** in numerical problems → 1 mark off. > 5. **Forgetting to specify the half-power reference** ($-3$ dB) when stating HPBW → loses 1 mark. > 6. **Writing $\vert z \vert$ with a plain ASCII pipe `|` inside LaTeX tables** → markdown will break; always use `\vert` or `\mid`. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Essential Takeaways for Last-Minute KTU Revision:** - **Transmission Line Model** = open out a two-wire line into a dipole; current stays sinusoidal. - **Current formula (must memorize):** $$I(z) = I_0\,\dfrac{\sin\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{L}{2}-\vert z\vert\right)\right]}{\sin\!\left(\beta\frac{L}{2}\right)}$$ - **Boundary conditions:** $I(\pm L/2) = 0$ (open ends), $I(0) = I_0$ (feed-point). - **Far-zone phase approximation:** $R \approx r - z'\cos\theta$ (in exponent only). - **Vector potential → $E_\theta$ integration** gives the classical pattern factor: $$F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\cos\theta\right)-\cos\!\left(\frac{\beta L}{2}\right)}{\sin\theta}$$ - **Half-wave dipole** ($L = \lambda/2$) pattern: $F(\theta) = \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta\right)}{\sin\theta}$ — **figure-8**. - **HPBW (half-wave):** $\approx 78^\circ$. - **Directivity (half-wave):** $D_0 = 1.643 \approx 2.16$ dBi. - **Input impedance (half-wave):** $Z_{in} \approx 73 + j\,42.5\;\Omega$. - **Resonant length:** $\approx 0.48\lambda$ (slightly shorter than $\lambda/2$). - **Maximum effective aperture:** $A_{em} = \dfrac{\lambda^2 D_0}{4\pi}$. - **Full-wave dipole** $L = \lambda$ → TLM predicts zero current at center feed (limitation of model). - **Free-space constants:** $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s, $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 376.73\;\Omega$, $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$. - **Why TLM fails:** for thicker dipoles, end-effects, and non-sinusoidal distributions, the **Method of Moments (MoM)** in Module 3 must be used.

topics.title: Broadbanding of Microstrip Antenna Using Stacked Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Broadbanding of Microstrip Antenna Using Stacked Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stacked Microstrip Antenna (SMA):** A multilayer antenna configuration in which two or more microstrip patches are physically separated along the broadside direction (i.e., stacked vertically above a common ground plane) and are electromagnetically coupled (rather than directly galvanically connected). One patch acts as the **driven (fed) element**, while the other(s) act as **parasitic patches**. By intentionally offsetting their resonant frequencies, the individual resonances merge into a single, broader impedance bandwidth — thereby *broadbanding* the inherently narrowband microstrip radiator.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this technique is classified under **Bandwidth Enhancement Techniques for Microstrip Antennas**, specifically as a **resonant (multi-resonator) loading method**, distinct from non-resonant methods such as impedance matching or substrate modification.

## 1.2 Why is Broadbanding Needed?

A conventional rectangular microstrip patch antenna has a fractional bandwidth of only **2–5 %** because:

- It behaves as a **high-Q resonant cavity** bounded above and below by electric walls.
- The input impedance varies very rapidly around resonance.
- $\text{VSWR} \leq 2$ is sustained only over a narrow frequency interval.

For modern wireless standards (Wi-Fi, WiMAX, UWB, 5G sub-6 GHz, GNSS), bandwidths of **10 % – 30 %** are mandatory. The stacked-element technique is one of the most elegant solutions.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Tuning Forks in a Music Box"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine three tuning forks of slightly different pitch struck simultaneously. None individually rings loudly, but their combined envelope of sound is *richer and longer-lasting* than any one fork. In a stacked patch antenna, each patch is a "tuning fork" resonating at a slightly different frequency. Electromagnetic coupling between them is the *air between the forks*. When their individual resonances are properly staggered, the **collective resonance envelope becomes broad and flat**, giving wideband performance.

## 1.4 Physical Configuration

The classical geometry (see Section 4) consists of:

| Layer # | Element | Function |
|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 (bottom) | **Ground plane** | PEC reflector |
| 2 | **Lower substrate (h₁)** | Hosts the driven patch |
| 3 | **Driven patch (L₁ × W₁)** | Fed by coaxial probe / microstrip line / aperture |
| 4 | **Air gap or foam (h₂)** | Low-ε_r spacer for coupling control |
| 5 | **Parasitic patch (L₂ × W₂)** | EM-coupled, no direct feed |
| 6 (optional) | **Upper substrate (h₃)** | Protection / radome |

The air/foam layer is critical — it has **low permittivity (ε_r ≈ 1)** which enhances fringing fields and broadens bandwidth.

## 1.5 Salient Features (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Bandwidth enhancement factor: **2× to 5×** compared to a single patch.
> - Typical achievable fractional bandwidth: **10 % – 25 %**.
> - Gain is usually comparable to or slightly higher than a single patch.
> - Cross-polarization can increase; design care is required.
> - Multiple stacked patches can yield even broader bandwidth (multi-resonance).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Stacking Broadens Bandwidth

The microstrip patch can be modelled as a **parallel RLC resonator** (cavity model):

$$
Z_{in}(f) = \frac{R}{1 + jQ\left(\dfrac{f}{f_r} - \dfrac{f_r}{f}\right)}
$$

where $R$ is the resonant resistance, $Q$ is the quality factor, and $f_r$ is the resonant frequency.

For the **single patch**, the −10 dB return-loss (VSWR = 2) bandwidth is:

$$
BW = \frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{Q\sqrt{\text{VSWR}}} = \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2}} \quad (\text{for VSWR} = 2)
$$

When a **second patch** is brought into proximity, the input impedance becomes the **sum of two coupled resonators** (loosely-coupled transformer model):

$$
Z_{in}(f) = Z_{11}(f) - \frac{Z_{12}^{2}(f)}{Z_{22}(f)}
$$

where $Z_{11}$, $Z_{22}$ are the self-impedances of patches 1 and 2, and $Z_{12}$ is the mutual (coupling) impedance.

> [!NOTE]
> The numerator term $\dfrac{Z_{12}^{2}}{Z_{22}}$ introduces an **additional pole** in the input impedance response. Two closely-spaced poles, when slightly offset in frequency, produce a **flattened reflection-coefficient curve** over a wider band — this is the mathematical essence of broadbanding via stacking.

## 2.2 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Driven patch** is designed to resonate at frequency $f_{r1}$ slightly below the desired band centre.
2. **Parasitic patch** is designed to resonate at frequency $f_{r2}$ slightly above the desired band centre.
3. The **air gap (h₂)** controls the coupling coefficient $k$:
   $$
   k \approx \frac{\varepsilon_{r2} \cdot h_2}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}\varepsilon_{r2}}} \cdot f(k_0 W, k_0 L)
   $$
4. The two resonances merge into a **single broadband resonance** when $f_{r2} - f_{r1} \approx k \cdot f_0$.
5. Above the resonance, the parasitic patch radiates; below it, the driven patch radiates — together they cover a wider band.
6. The feed location (typically probe-position $x_f, y_f$ on the driven patch) is iteratively optimized to maintain $|\Gamma| < -10\,\text{dB}$ across the combined band.

## 2.3 Design Equations

### 2.3.1 Resonant Frequency of Rectangular Patch

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{2 L_{\text{eff}} \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

### 2.3.2 Effective Dielectric Constant

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12 h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

### 2.3.3 Fringing Length Extension

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{h} = \frac{0.412 \left(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} + 0.3\right)\left(\dfrac{W}{h} + 0.264\right)}{\left(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} - 0.258\right)\left(\dfrac{W}{h} + 0.8\right)}
$$

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = L + 2 \Delta L
$$

### 2.3.4 Bandwidth Formulas

- Fractional bandwidth:
$$
\text{BW} (\%) = \frac{f_H - f_L}{f_c} \times 100
$$
- Bandwidth in terms of $Q$:
$$
\text{BW} = \frac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\text{VSWR}}}
$$
- Reflection coefficient to VSWR:
$$
\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}
$$

### 2.3.5 Coupling Factor for Stacked Patches

$$
k = \frac{\int\!\!\int \vec{E}_1 \cdot \vec{E}_2 \, dS}{\sqrt{\int\!\!\int \vert \vec{E}_1 \vert^2 dS \cdot \int\!\!\int \vert \vec{E}_2 \vert^2 dS}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical design rule:** Optimum broadbanding occurs when $f_{r2} - f_{r1} \approx k f_0$. If $k$ is too small, two distinct dips appear (narrow dual-band). If $k$ is too large, only a single sharp resonance remains (no improvement).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are essential for KTU ESE/ISA problems on broadbanding.

| # | Formula | Symbol / Quantity | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | $f_r = \dfrac{c}{2 L_{\text{eff}}\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Resonant frequency of rectangular patch | Hz |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1+\dfrac{12h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}$ | Effective permittivity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{h} = \dfrac{0.412(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}+0.3)(W/h+0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}-0.258)(W/h+0.8)}$ | Fringing-field extension | dimensionless |
| 4 | $L_{\text{eff}} = L + 2\Delta L$ | Effective patch length | m |
| 5 | $\text{BW}(\%) = \dfrac{f_H - f_L}{f_c}\times 100$ | Fractional bandwidth | % |
| 6 | $\text{VSWR} = \dfrac{1+\vert \Gamma\vert}{1-\vert\Gamma\vert}$ | Voltage standing wave ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_r}{h\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}\cdot \dfrac{W}{c}$ | Quality factor (approx) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{BW} = \dfrac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\dfrac{\text{VSWR}-1}{\text{VSWR}}}$ | Bandwidth from $Q$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $k \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r2} h_2}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r1}\varepsilon_{r2}}}\cdot g(k_0 L, k_0 W)$ | Coupling factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Z_{in}(f) = Z_{11}(f) - \dfrac{Z_{12}^{2}(f)}{Z_{22}(f)}$ | Coupled-resonator input impedance | Ω |

**Standard physical constants used:**

- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$ (speed of light)
- $\eta_0 = 377\,\Omega$ (free-space impedance)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite communications** (e.g., DBS receivers at Ku-band): stacked patches give 12–18 % bandwidth.
- **Radomes and aerospace**: stacked design permits conformal integration while meeting bandwidth specs.
- **Multi-standard wireless terminals** (GPS + GLONASS + Galileo): single stacked antenna covers multiple GNSS bands.
- **UWB applications**: extreme broadband versions (e.g., 3.1–10.6 GHz) achieved with multi-layer stacked designs.
- **Radar and EW systems**: broadband radiators reduce matched-filter distortion of chirped pulses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bandwidth from VSWR Specification

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting data (KTU-style):** A stacked microstrip antenna has been measured to have $Q = 28$ at its centre frequency. Compute the fractional bandwidth (in %) for which VSWR $\leq 2$.

### Step 1 — Write the bandwidth formula in terms of $Q$ and VSWR

$$
\text{BW} = \frac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\text{VSWR}}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute VSWR = 2

$$
\text{BW} = \frac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\frac{2 - 1}{2}} = \frac{1}{Q}\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $Q = 28$

$$
\text{BW} = \frac{1}{28 \cdot \sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{28 \times 1.4142} = \frac{1}{39.598}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical evaluation

$$
\text{BW} = 0.02525 \;\;\text{(fractional)}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to percentage

$$
\text{BW} (\%) = 0.02525 \times 100 = 2.525\,\%
$$

### Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This represents the bandwidth of **a single patch**. By adding a parasitic patch with carefully chosen offset, the effective $Q$ reduces to roughly $Q/2$ to $Q/3$, increasing BW to **5 % – 10 %**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resonant Frequency of a Stacked Rectangular Patch (Driven Element)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting data:** A driven patch has physical dimensions $L = 30\,\text{mm}$, $W = 40\,\text{mm}$, substrate $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, thickness $h = 1.6\,\text{mm}$. Compute the resonant frequency $f_r$.

### Step 1 — Compute the width-to-height ratio

$$
\frac{W}{h} = \frac{40}{1.6} = 25
$$

### Step 2 — Compute effective dielectric constant

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{2.2 + 1}{2} + \frac{2.2 - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12 \times 1.6}{40}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 1.6 + 0.6 \left(1 + 0.48\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 1.6 + 0.6 \left(1.48\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 1.6 + 0.6 \times 0.8220 = 1.6 + 0.4932 = 2.0932
$$

### Step 3 — Compute $\Delta L / h$

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{h} = \frac{0.412(2.0932 + 0.3)(25 + 0.264)}{(2.0932 - 0.258)(25 + 0.8)}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.412 \times 2.3932 \times 25.264}{1.8352 \times 25.8}
$$

$$
= \frac{24.9106}{47.348} = 0.5261
$$

### Step 4 — Compute $\Delta L$

$$
\Delta L = 0.5261 \times 1.6 = 0.8418\,\text{mm}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute effective length

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = L + 2\Delta L = 30 + 2 \times 0.8418 = 31.6836\,\text{mm}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute resonant frequency

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{2 L_{\text{eff}} \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 31.6836 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2.0932}}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{63.3672 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.4468}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{91.684 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{0.091684}
$$

$$
f_r = 3.272\,\text{GHz}
$$

### Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The driven patch resonates at $3.27\,\text{GHz}$. The parasitic patch, designed to resonate at $f_{r2} \approx 3.45\,\text{GHz}$, is stacked above with $h_2 \approx 5\,\text{mm}$ foam to achieve optimum broadbanding around the $3.35\,\text{GHz}$ centre.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Coupled-Resonator Input Impedance (Two-Patch Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting model:** Two lossless patches with self-impedances
> $Z_{11}(f) = \dfrac{R_1}{1+jQ_1 x}$ and $Z_{22}(f) = \dfrac{R_2}{1+jQ_2 x}$
> where $x = \dfrac{f}{f_0} - \dfrac{f_0}{f}$ is the normalized frequency detuning.
> Coupling impedance $Z_{12}(f) = j X_{12}(f)$, purely reactive for probe-fed stacks.

### Step 1 — Write the coupled input impedance

$$
Z_{in}(f) = Z_{11}(f) - \frac{Z_{12}^{2}(f)}{Z_{22}(f)}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the forms

$$
Z_{in}(f) = \frac{R_1}{1+jQ_1 x} - \frac{(jX_{12})^{2}}{\dfrac{R_2}{1+jQ_2 x}}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the second term

$$
= \frac{R_1}{1+jQ_1 x} + \frac{X_{12}^{2}(1+jQ_2 x)}{R_2}
$$

### Step 4 — Rationalize the first term

$$
= \frac{R_1(1-jQ_1 x)}{1+Q_1^{2}x^{2}} + \frac{X_{12}^{2}}{R_2} + j\frac{X_{12}^{2} Q_2 x}{R_2}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the resistive and reactive parts

$$
\text{Re}\{Z_{in}\} = \frac{R_1}{1+Q_1^{2}x^{2}} + \frac{X_{12}^{2}}{R_2}
$$

$$
\text{Im}\{Z_{in}\} = -\frac{R_1 Q_1 x}{1+Q_1^{2}x^{2}} + \frac{X_{12}^{2} Q_2 x}{R_2}
$$

### Step 6 — Set Im$\{Z_{in}\}$ = 0 to find resonance conditions

$$
\frac{R_1 Q_1 x}{1+Q_1^{2}x^{2}} = \frac{X_{12}^{2} Q_2 x}{R_2}
$$

This yields **two real solutions** for $x$ (and hence two resonant frequencies) provided $X_{12}^{2}$ is appropriately chosen. These are the two peaks of the broadband response.

### Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The coupled-resonator model explains why stacking **creates an additional resonance** that merges with the original, yielding a wider single-passband. This is the analytical backbone of the broadbanding technique expected in KTU derivations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Single Patch | Two-Element Stack | Improvement |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Fractional BW | 2 – 5 % | 10 – 20 % | 3× – 5× |
| Gain | 6 – 8 dBi | 7 – 9 dBi | ~1 dB |
| No. of feed lines | 1 | 1 (parasitic un-fed) | Same |
| Design complexity | Low | Moderate to high | – |
| Cross-pol level | −20 dB | −15 dB | Slightly degraded |
| $Q$ factor | 20 – 50 | 8 – 15 | Reduced |

---

## 3.5 Design Checklist for a Two-Element Stack

1. Choose the centre frequency $f_0$ (e.g., 3.5 GHz for WiMAX).
2. Pick lower substrate: $\varepsilon_{r1} \approx 2.2$, $h_1 \approx 1.6\,\text{mm}$.
3. Compute driven patch size using Section 3.2 formulas.
4. Choose air/foam gap: $h_2 \approx (0.05 - 0.15)\,\lambda_0$.
5. Design parasitic patch slightly smaller or larger to shift $f_{r2}$ by $5 - 10\,\%$.
6. Simulate in HFSS / CST / IE3D and tune for $\vert \Gamma\vert \leq -10\,\text{dB}$.
7. Verify gain, radiation pattern, and cross-polarization.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Side-View Geometry of a Two-Element Stacked Patch

The diagram below is rendered as a **Mermaid block-level architecture** since physical layered geometry is best understood via a labelled stack-up chart.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["LAYER 6 - Radome / Protective Cover - Optional"]
    B["LAYER 5 - Upper Substrate - epsilon_r3 low loss"]
    C["LAYER 4 - Parasitic Patch - L2 by W2 - EM Coupled"]
    D["LAYER 3 - Air Gap or Foam - epsilon_r approx 1 - Height h2"]
    E["LAYER 2 - Lower Substrate - epsilon_r1 around 2.2 - Height h1"]
    F["LAYER 1 - Driven Patch - L1 by W1 - Fed by Probe"]
    G["LAYER 0 - Ground Plane - PEC Reflector"]
    H["Coaxial Feed - 50 Ohm - Connects to Driven Patch"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    H -.feeds.-> F

    classDef parasitic fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef driven fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dielectric fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#4b0082,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#a9a9a9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef feed fill:#98fb98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class C parasitic
    class F driven
    class A,B,D,E dielectric
    class G ground
    class H feed
```

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the diagram from **top to bottom**: protective cover → upper dielectric → parasitic patch → low-ε coupling gap → lower dielectric → driven patch → ground plane. The coaxial feed pierces the lower dielectric to reach the driven patch. Electromagnetic energy leaks into the parasitic patch through the air gap, creating the second resonance.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Signal Flow in a Stacked Patch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Input Port - 50 Ohm Coax"]:::io
    DRV["Driven Patch - Resonator 1 - Frequency fr1"]:::resonator
    CPL["Coupling Network - Air Gap h2 - Coupling k"]:::coupler
    PAR["Parasitic Patch - Resonator 2 - Frequency fr2"]:::resonator
    RAD["Radiation to Free Space"]:::output
    RL["Return Loss S11 Monitor"]:::metric

    INP --> DRV
    DRV -- "fr1 near f0" --> CPL
    CPL -- "k controls BW" --> PAR
    PAR -- "fr2 near f0" --> RAD
    DRV -- "fr1 near f0" --> RAD
    INP -.reflected.-> RL

    classDef io fill:#fffacd,stroke:#8b7500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef resonator fill:#add8e6,stroke:#00008b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef coupler fill:#ffdab9,stroke:#a0522d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metric fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class INP io
    class DRV,PAR resonator
    class CPL coupler
    class RAD output
    class RL metric
```

**Interpretation of the signal-flow diagram:**

- The **input port** delivers RF power to the driven patch.
- The driven patch acts as **Resonator 1** at $f_{r1}$ and radiates directly.
- A portion of energy is **coupled** through the air gap (Coupling block) into the parasitic patch.
- The parasitic patch acts as **Resonator 2** at $f_{r2}$ and also radiates.
- The **return-loss monitor** senses the reflected wave at the input port — its frequency response exhibits the broadband behaviour.
- The combined radiation from both patches gives the wideband pattern.

---

## 4.3 Frequency-Response Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Single Patch - Sharp Peak"]:::narrow
    B["Parasitic Patch - Sharp Peak"]:::narrow
    C["Combined Stack - Broad Flat Top"]:::broad
    A --> C
    B --> C

    classDef narrow fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef broad fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class A,B narrow
    class C broad
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The narrow individual responses of patches 1 and 2 (red) merge into a broad composite response (green) when the coupling $k$ and frequency offset are properly chosen — the **defining principle of broadbanding by stacked elements**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is broadbanding of a microstrip antenna using stacked elements? Mention any two advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Broadbanding of a microstrip antenna using stacked elements is a technique in which two or more microstrip patches are placed vertically above a common ground plane and electromagnetically coupled. One patch (driven) is fed directly while the other(s) act as parasitic elements. By offsetting their resonant frequencies, the individual resonances merge to produce a wider impedance bandwidth.

**Two advantages:**

1. The fractional bandwidth is increased from the typical **2–5 %** (single patch) to **10–25 %** (stacked design).
2. Gain is comparable to or better than a single patch, and no additional feed network is required.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Why is the air gap (or low-ε_r foam) used between the driven and parasitic patches in a stacked microstrip antenna?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The air/foam gap serves three critical purposes:
> 1. It provides a **low-permittivity medium** that enhances fringing fields, lowering the effective $Q$ and broadening bandwidth.
> 2. Its **height $h_2$** acts as the primary design parameter controlling the **coupling coefficient $k$** between the driven and parasitic patches.
> 3. The mechanical spacing allows the resonant frequency of the parasitic patch to be tuned independently of the driven patch.

If the gap were filled with a high-ε_r material, the coupling would become too strong and the two resonances would no longer merge — defeating the broadbanding purpose.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 2 Choice A]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the resonant frequency of a rectangular microstrip patch antenna, clearly stating the significance of the fringing-length extension $\Delta L$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the technique of broadbanding a microstrip antenna using stacked elements, with a neat sketch. Discuss how the coupling coefficient and frequency offset influence the resulting bandwidth. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Cavity model assumption**

Treat the patch plus ground plane plus dielectric slab as a **cavity bounded by electric walls** on top and bottom and by **magnetic walls** on the sides.

**Step 2 — Resonant-mode equation**

For the dominant TM₁₀ mode, the resonant length must satisfy the half-wave condition:

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\lambda_g}{2} = \frac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $f_r$**

$$
f_r = \frac{c}{2 L_{\text{eff}} \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Definition of $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_r - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12 h}{W}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

**Step 5 — Significance of $\Delta L$**

The fringing fields at the radiating edges make the patch electrically longer than its physical dimension. The effective length is:

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = L + 2 \Delta L
$$

where $\Delta L$ is computed from the **Hammerstad formula**:

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{h} = \frac{0.412 (\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}+0.3)(W/h + 0.264)}{(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}-0.258)(W/h + 0.8)}
$$

If $\Delta L$ is ignored, $f_r$ is overestimated by 3–6 %.

---

### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — Sketch description**

A driven patch is printed on a lower substrate of thickness $h_1$ and $\varepsilon_{r1}$, fed by a coaxial probe. A parasitic patch sits above it, separated by an air/foam gap $h_2$ of $\varepsilon_{r2} \approx 1$, with an optional upper radome $h_3$.

**Step 2 — Resonance condition**

The driven patch resonates at $f_{r1}$ and the parasitic patch at $f_{r2}$, with $f_{r2} - f_{r1} \approx 5\text{–}10\,\%$.

**Step 3 — Coupled-resonator input impedance**

$$
Z_{in}(f) = Z_{11}(f) - \frac{Z_{12}^{2}(f)}{Z_{22}(f)}
$$

The mutual coupling introduces an **extra pole**, broadening the impedance match.

**Step 4 — Influence of $k$ and frequency offset**

- If $k$ is small (large $h_2$) and offset large → **dual-band response**, not broadband.
- If $k$ is large (small $h_2$) → single sharp resonance, no improvement.
- **Optimum broadbanding:** $k$ moderate ($h_2 \approx 0.05\lambda_0 - 0.15\lambda_0$) and $f_{r2} - f_{r1} \approx k f_0$.

**Step 5 — Achieved bandwidth**

Typically **10–20 %** fractional bandwidth (compared to 2–5 % for a single patch), with gain in the range 7–9 dBi.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Module 2 Choice B]`

**(a)** A rectangular microstrip patch antenna is designed on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 2.55$, $h = 1.6\,\text{mm}$, $W = 40\,\text{mm}$. Compute the resonant frequency for $L = 30\,\text{mm}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A two-element stacked microstrip antenna is to be designed for a centre frequency of 5.8 GHz. Choose appropriate patch dimensions for both driven and parasitic patches. Discuss how the air-gap height $h_2$ affects the bandwidth. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Step 1 — Compute $W/h$**

$$
\frac{W}{h} = \frac{40}{1.6} = 25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{2.55 + 1}{2} + \frac{2.55 - 1}{2}\left(1 + \frac{12 \times 1.6}{40}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

$$
= 1.775 + 0.775 \times (1.48)^{-0.5}
$$

$$
= 1.775 + 0.775 \times 0.8220 = 1.775 + 0.637 = 2.412
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta L / h$**

$$
\frac{\Delta L}{h} = \frac{0.412 (2.412 + 0.3)(25 + 0.264)}{(2.412 - 0.258)(25 + 0.8)}
$$

$$
= \frac{0.412 \times 2.712 \times 25.264}{2.154 \times 25.8}
$$

$$
= \frac{28.244}{55.573} = 0.5083
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta L$ and $L_{\text{eff}}$**

$$
\Delta L = 0.5083 \times 1.6 = 0.8133\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = 30 + 2(0.8133) = 31.627\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $f_r$**

$$
f_r = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 31.627 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{2.412}}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{63.254 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5531}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{98.243 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.054\,\text{GHz}
$$

**Final answer: $f_r \approx 3.05\,\text{GHz}$**

---

### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Step 1 — Target specification**

- Centre frequency: $f_0 = 5.8\,\text{GHz}$
- Substrate: $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $h_1 = 1.6\,\text{mm}$
- Air gap: $\varepsilon_{r2} \approx 1$, $h_2 = ?$

**Step 2 — Wavelength in free space**

$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{5.8 \times 10^{9}} = 51.72\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Driven patch design (resonates slightly below $f_0$, say 5.65 GHz)**

Choosing $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \approx 2.05$ at $W/h \approx 25$:

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{c}{2 f_{r1} \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 5.65 \times 10^{9} \times 1.432} = 18.53\,\text{mm}
$$

Subtracting $2\Delta L \approx 1.6\,\text{mm}$:

$$
L_1 \approx 17\,\text{mm}, \quad W_1 \approx 22\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Parasitic patch design (resonates slightly above $f_0$, say 5.95 GHz)**

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 5.95 \times 10^{9} \times 1.432} = 17.61\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
L_2 \approx 16\,\text{mm}, \quad W_2 \approx 21\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Effect of $h_2$ on bandwidth**

- **Small $h_2$ (≈ 1 mm):** tight coupling → single sharp resonance → BW ≈ 3 %.
- **Medium $h_2$ (≈ 3–5 mm, ≈ 0.05–0.10 λ₀):** moderate coupling → merged resonance → **BW ≈ 12–18 %** (optimum).
- **Large $h_2$ (≈ 8–10 mm):** weak coupling → two distinct dips → dual-band response.

**Final design summary:**

- $L_1 = 17\,\text{mm}$, $W_1 = 22\,\text{mm}$
- $L_2 = 16\,\text{mm}$, $W_2 = 21\,\text{mm}$
- $h_2 = 4\,\text{mm}$ (foam, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.05$)
- Expected bandwidth: **≈ 15 %** (≈ 870 MHz around 5.8 GHz)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\Delta L$** — many students compute $f_r = c / (2L\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ instead of using $L_{\text{eff}}$. This gives a 3–6 % error. **Marks deducted: 1–2**.
> 2. **Using $\varepsilon_r$ instead of $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$** — the resonant frequency must use $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$. **Marks deducted: 1**.
> 3. **Drawing the stack without labels** — sketches in stacked-element questions must clearly label $h_1$, $h_2$, $L_1$, $L_2$, and the feed. **Marks deducted: 1**.
> 4. **Confusing broadbanding with multi-band** — broadbanding = single broad passband; multi-band = two distinct passbands. Examiners look for this distinction explicitly. **Marks deducted: 2**.
> 5. **Not mentioning coupling factor $k$** — any broadbanding answer missing the coupling mechanism is incomplete. **Marks deducted: 1–2**.
> 6. **Units inconsistency** — keep $L$ in metres when computing $f_r$. Mixing mm and m is a frequent error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist:**

- [ ] **Definition:** A stacked microstrip antenna uses ≥ 2 patches above a common ground; one driven, others parasitic, all EM-coupled.
- [ ] **Why broadband?** Single patches are narrow-band (BW = 2–5 %) because of high $Q$.
- [ ] **Mechanism:** Multiple resonances merge into one broad passband when $f_{r2} - f_{r1} \approx k f_0$.
- [ ] **Key formula:** $f_r = c / (2 L_{\text{eff}} \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}})$.
- [ ] **Always use $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$, not $\varepsilon_r$.**
- [ ] **Hammerstad's $\Delta L$ formula must be applied** before computing $f_r$.
- [ ] **Coupling factor $k$** is controlled primarily by $h_2$ (the air/foam gap height).
- [ ] **Optimum gap:** $h_2 \approx 0.05\,\lambda_0$ to $0.15\,\lambda_0$.
- [ ] **Bandwidth enhancement:** 3×–5× over a single patch (i.e., 10–25 % fractional BW).
- [ ] **Driven patch** resonates slightly **below** $f_0$.
- [ ] **Parasitic patch** resonates slightly **above** $f_0$.
- [ ] **No additional feed** to the parasitic patch — energy transfers by EM coupling.
- [ ] **VSWR = 2 (or $\vert \Gamma\vert \leq -10\,\text{dB}$)** is the standard reference for bandwidth definition.
- [ ] **BW = 1 / (Q√2)** for VSWR = 2.
- [ ] **Coupled-resonator equation:** $Z_{in}(f) = Z_{11}(f) - Z_{12}^{2}(f) / Z_{22}(f)$ — extra pole → broadband.
- [ ] **Applications:** WiMAX, GNSS, DBS, UWB, radar, EW.
- [ ] **Cross-polarization** is slightly higher in stacked designs — design care required.
- [ ] **Three or more patches** can extend BW further at the cost of thickness and complexity.
- [ ] **Feed position $x_f, y_f$** on the driven patch must be tuned for the new resonance.
- [ ] **Numerical sanity check:** at 3 GHz on $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, a patch of $L \approx 30\,\text{mm}$ is typical.
- [ ] **Don't confuse broadbanding with aperture-coupled patches** — both enhance BW but aperture coupling uses a slot-fed geometry, not a stacked-patch geometry.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compact Circularly Polarised Antennas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Compact Circularly Polarised Antennas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Compact Circularly Polarised (CP) Antenna** is a radiating element whose overall physical footprint (ground-plane dimensions, substrate area, or volume) is significantly smaller than the conventional half-wavelength ($\lambda_0/2$) reference, while still radiating an electromagnetic wave whose **electric-field vector tip traces a circle in the plane orthogonal to the direction of propagation** at the radio frequency (RF) carrier.

Mathematically, for a wave travelling in the $+z$ direction, the radiated electric field takes the general orthogonal-component form:

$$\vec{E}(z,t) = \hat{a}_x E_x \cos(\omega t - \beta z) + \hat{a}_y E_y \cos(\omega t - \beta z + \delta)$$

The condition for **perfect circular polarisation** is the simultaneous satisfaction of two equations:

$$\boxed{\vert E_x \vert = \vert E_y \vert \quad \text{and} \quad \delta = \pm \pi/2 \;(90^\circ)}$$

The "+" sign yields **Left-Hand Circular Polarisation (LHCP)** and the "−" sign yields **Right-Hand Circular Polarisation (RHCP)** by the IEEE convention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, "compact" specifically refers to size-reduction techniques applied to inherently CP-radiating geometries such as **single-feed truncated-corner microstrip patches**, **nearly-square patches**, **slot antennas**, **crossed-dipoles**, and **Dielectric Resonator Antennas (DRA)**. Pure axial-mode helical antennas, while CP, are typically NOT considered "compact" because of their $\lambda_0/3$ axial length.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Clock-Hand" Picture

Imagine you are standing in front of a wall clock. The minute hand rotates around the clock-face centre. If you trace the tip of the minute hand with a pencil on a transparent sheet held in front of the clock, the pencil draws a **perfect circle**. Now suppose two hands exist — a "minute hand" pointing along the x-direction and a "hour hand" pointing along the y-direction — and they have **exactly equal lengths** (equal amplitude) and the hour hand is **always 90° ahead** of the minute hand. The composite tip still draws a perfect circle, but in a *specific* rotational sense.

This is **circular polarisation**:
- The x-axis electric field $\rightarrow$ the "minute hand".
- The y-axis electric field $\rightarrow$ the "hour hand".
- Equal amplitudes $\rightarrow$ equal lengths.
- 90° phase shift $\rightarrow$ quarter-cycle time lag.

**Why "compact"?** A standard rectangular patch needs a length of $\approx \lambda_0/2$ inside the dielectric. To *shrink* this, designers bend the current path (meandering), introduce slots, raise the dielectric constant ($ε_r$), use shorting posts, or stack high-permittivity superstrates — all without sacrificing the CP condition.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Compact CP antennas are the backbone of **GNSS (GPS L1/L2/L5, IRNSS, Galileo)**, **IoT sensor nodes**, **UHF RFID handhelds**, **drone telemetry links**, and **satellite mobile phones**, where platform area is precious and Faraday rotation through the ionosphere requires CP to avoid signal loss.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants & Metrics)

| Metric | Standard Threshold | Compact-Antenna Target |
|---|---|---|
| Axial Ratio (AR) | AR $\leq$ **3 dB** for CP | AR $\leq$ **3 dB** |
| 3-dB AR Bandwidth | Typically 1–2% of $f_0$ | Goal: ≥ 4% |
| Voltage Standing Wave Ratio (VSWR) | $\leq$ **2:1** | $\leq$ **2:1** |
| Gain | 5–8 dBic for patch | 2–6 dBic (reduced by miniaturisation) |
| Size reduction | 0.5$\lambda_0 \times$ 0.5$\lambda_0$ | $<$ **0.3$\lambda_0 \times$ 0.3$\lambda_0$** |

where **dBic** means *decibels with respect to an isotropic circularly polarised radiator*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental CP Design Strategies

Modern compact CP antennas fall into **two architectural camps**:

**Strategy A — Single-Feed Structures (Compact, simple feed network):**
- Truncated-corner square patch
- Nearly-square patch (slightly perturbed square)
- Slotted / slotted-corner patches
- Cross-slot aperture-coupled patches
- Stacked-patch with offset feeds

**Strategy B — Dual/Quad-Feed Structures (Wider AR bandwidth, larger footprint):**
- Square patch fed at two orthogonal edges (90° hybrid)
- Crossed-dipole with 90° delay line
- Four-feed sequential-rotation patch array

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 2 emphasises **single-feed compact CP antennas** because they integrate directly with MMIC/RFIC transceivers without external 90° hybrids, saving both cost and PCB real estate.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Why a Square Patch With Truncated Corners Generates CP

The classical **Shen–Lau / Sharma–Gupta square patch with truncated corners** works because of the following physical sequence:

1. The dominant mode of a square microstrip patch is **TM₁₀ / TM₀₁** (degenerate modes with **identical resonant frequencies** because the patch is square).
2. Perturbing one corner by an area $\Delta s$ shifts the resonant frequency of TM₀₁ **downward** by $\Delta f_1$ and shifts TM₁₀ **upward** by $\Delta f_2$.
3. At a specific frequency between these two perturbed resonances, the two orthogonal modes have **equal amplitudes** and a **90° phase difference** — satisfying the CP condition.
4. The result is CP radiation in the broadside direction ($+\hat{z}$).

The **size of truncation $\Delta s$** controls the **3-dB AR bandwidth** but does *not* control the resonant frequency $f_0$ to first order.

### 2.3 The "Nearly Square" Patch CP Mechanism

A nearly-square patch is a rectangle of dimensions $a \times b$ where $a \neq b$ but both are near $\lambda_g/2$. The patch is fed along the diagonal. The TM₀₁ and TM₁₀ modes have a small intrinsic frequency split:

$$f_1 = \frac{c}{2 a \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad ; \quad f_2 = \frac{c}{2 b \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

CP is obtained at the geometric mean frequency:

$$f_{CP} = \sqrt{f_1 \, f_2} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{ab}}$$

The **CP bandwidth** is proportional to the **aspect-ratio perturbation** $\Delta = \frac{a-b}{a+b}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vert E_x \vert = \vert E_y \vert$ | Equal-amplitude requirement for CP | V/m |
| 2 | $\delta = \pm \pi/2$ | 90° phase difference (LHCP/RHCP) | radians |
| 3 | $\text{AR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{E_{\max}}{E_{\min}} \right)$ | Axial Ratio definition | dB |
| 4 | $\text{AR} \leq 3$ dB | CP definition (KTU standard) | dB |
| 5 | $L_p \approx \dfrac{c}{2 f_r \sqrt{\varepsilon_{eff}}} - 2 \Delta L$ | Patch length (resonant) | m |
| 6 | $\Delta s / S \approx \dfrac{1}{Q_0}$ | Normalised corner truncation | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_{CP} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{ab}}$ | Nearly-square CP frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $\text{BW}_{AR} \approx \dfrac{\Delta}{Q_0}$ | AR fractional bandwidth | — |
| 9 | $k a < 1$ | Electrically small antenna (ESA) bound | — |
| 10 | $Q_{min} = \dfrac{1}{ka} + \dfrac{1}{(ka)^3}$ | Chu-Harrington fundamental limit | — |
| 11 | $\text{Size Reduction Factor (SRF)} = 1 - \dfrac{A_{compact}}{A_{\lambda/2}}$ | Compactness metric | 0–1 |
| 12 | $r_E = \dfrac{\lambda_0}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{1.5}}$ | Equivalent sphere radius | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE:** In KTU answer sheets, **never write `\|x\|`** for absolute value inside the table — use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in your LaTeX-typed notes to keep markdown table rows intact.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Compact CP antennas are deployed in:
- **GNSS multi-band receivers** (L1/L2/L5) — CP rejects ionospheric Faraday rotation losses.
- **UHF RFID handheld readers** — compact footprint fits in a gun-shaped casing.
- **Drone-to-drone swarming links** — orientation-agnostic reception.
- **Satellite-mobile handheld phones** (Thuraya, Iridium) — CP survives body-tilting.
- **Wearable biomedical telemetry patches** — wearable CP patches conform to curved skin and maintain link.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Axial Ratio from the Orthogonal Field Components

We begin with the time-domain radiated field of a CP antenna in the boresight direction ($\theta = 0$):

$$\vec{E}(t) = \hat{a}_x \, E_1 \cos(\omega t) + \hat{a}_y \, E_2 \cos(\omega t + \delta)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the y-component using the cosine addition formula.**

$$\cos(\omega t + \delta) = \cos(\omega t)\cos(\delta) - \sin(\omega t)\sin(\delta)$$

Substituting:

$$\vec{E}(t) = \hat{a}_x \, E_1 \cos(\omega t) + \hat{a}_y \, E_2 [\cos(\omega t)\cos(\delta) - \sin(\omega t)\sin(\delta)]$$

**Step 2 — Group the in-phase and quadrature terms.**

$$\vec{E}(t) = \cos(\omega t) [\hat{a}_x E_1 + \hat{a}_y E_2 \cos(\delta)] - \sin(\omega t) [\hat{a}_y E_2 \sin(\delta)]$$

**Step 3 — The instantaneous squared magnitude is:**

$$E^2(t) = E_1^2 \cos^2(\omega t) + E_2^2 \cos^2(\omega t + \delta)$$

$$E^2(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[E_1^2 + E_2^2] + \tfrac{1}{2}[E_1^2 - E_2^2]\cos(2\omega t) - E_1 E_2 \sin(\delta)\sin(2\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Identify maxima and minima.**

The maximum of $E^2(t)$ is:

$$E_{\max}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}[E_1^2 + E_2^2] + \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(E_1^2 - E_2^2)^2 + 4E_1^2 E_2^2 \sin^2(\delta)}$$

The minimum is:

$$E_{\min}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2}[E_1^2 + E_2^2] - \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(E_1^2 - E_2^2)^2 + 4E_1^2 E_2^2 \sin^2(\delta)}$$

**Step 5 — The Axial Ratio is the ratio of the semi-major to semi-minor axis of the polarisation ellipse.**

$$\text{AR} = \frac{E_{\max}}{E_{\min}} = \frac{\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}(E_1^2 + E_2^2) + \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(E_1^2 - E_2^2)^2 + 4 E_1^2 E_2^2 \sin^2(\delta)}}}{\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2}(E_1^2 + E_2^2) - \tfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{(E_1^2 - E_2^2)^2 + 4 E_1^2 E_2^2 \sin^2(\delta)}}}$$

**Step 6 — Test the perfect-CP limit ($E_1 = E_2 = E_0$, $\delta = \pi/2$).**

$$\text{AR} = \frac{\sqrt{E_0^2 + \sqrt{0 + 4 E_0^4}}}{\sqrt{E_0^2 - \sqrt{0 + 4 E_0^2 \cdot E_0^2 \cdot 1}}} = \frac{\sqrt{E_0^2 + E_0^2}}{\sqrt{E_0^2 - E_0^2}} = \frac{E_0\sqrt{2}}{0} \rightarrow \infty$$

Wait — this indicates the polarisation ellipse collapses to a line, i.e., pure **linear polarisation**. Let us re-examine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** A perfect AR = **1 (0 dB)** corresponds to a *circle*, and AR $\rightarrow \infty$ corresponds to a *line*. Using the **electric-field amplitude ratio** definition (not the squared magnitudes), the proper definition is:

$$\boxed{\text{AR (linear)} = \frac{\text{semi-major axis}}{\text{semi-minor axis}} = \frac{E_1^2 + E_2^2 + \sqrt{E_1^4 + E_2^4 + 2 E_1^2 E_2^2 \cos(2\delta)}}{E_1^2 + E_2^2 - \sqrt{E_1^4 + E_2^4 + 2 E_1^2 E_2^2 \cos(2\delta)}}}$$

Substituting $E_1 = E_2 = E_0$, $\delta = \pi/2$:

$$\cos(2\delta) = \cos(\pi) = -1$$

$$\text{AR} = \frac{2E_0^2 + \sqrt{2E_0^4 - 2E_0^4}}{2E_0^2 - \sqrt{2E_0^4 - 2E_0^4}} = \frac{2E_0^2 + 0}{2E_0^2 - 0} = 1$$

$$\text{AR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(1) = 0 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Test the linear-polarisation limit ($E_2 = 0$, arbitrary $\delta$).**

$$\text{AR} = \frac{E_1^2 + \sqrt{E_1^4 + 0}}{E_1^2 - \sqrt{E_1^4 + 0}} = \frac{2E_1^2}{0} \rightarrow \infty$$

$$\text{AR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(\infty) = \infty \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark \text{ (pure LP)}$$

This validates the formulation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CP Resonant Frequency of a Nearly-Square Patch

Consider a rectangular microstrip patch of length $a$ (along x-axis) and width $b$ (along y-axis) on a substrate of thickness $h$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$.

**Step 1 — Dominant TM₁₀₀ and TM₀₁₀ modes have resonant frequencies:**

$$f_{10} = \frac{c}{2 a \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad ; \quad f_{01} = \frac{c}{2 b \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

assuming the substrate is thin ($h \ll \lambda_0$) so that $\varepsilon_{eff} \approx \varepsilon_r$.

**Step 2 — For a square patch ($a = b$), the two modes are degenerate.**

$$f_{10} = f_{01} = f_0$$

This degenerate mode alone gives linear polarisation, NOT circular, because there is no mechanism to enforce the 90° phase difference.

**Step 3 — Introduce a small asymmetry.** Set $a = b + \Delta$, $\Delta \ll b$.

$$f_{10} = \frac{c}{2 (b+\Delta)\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \approx \frac{c}{2 b\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \left(1 - \frac{\Delta}{b}\right) = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{\Delta}{b}\right)$$

$$f_{01} = \frac{c}{2 b \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = f_0$$

**Step 4 — Geometric-mean frequency (where the two modes are equal amplitude and 90° out of phase):**

$$f_{CP} = \sqrt{f_{10} \cdot f_{01}} = f_0 \sqrt{1 - \frac{\Delta}{b}}$$

For small $\Delta$:

$$f_{CP} \approx f_0 \left(1 - \frac{\Delta}{2b}\right)$$

**Step 5 — The 3-dB AR fractional bandwidth is:**

$$\text{BW}_{AR} \approx \frac{\Delta f}{f_{CP}} = \frac{f_{01} - f_{10}}{f_{CP}} = \frac{f_0 \frac{\Delta}{b}}{f_0\left(1 - \frac{\Delta}{2b}\right)} \approx \frac{\Delta}{b}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BW}_{AR} \approx \frac{a-b}{b} = \frac{\Delta}{b}}$$

This confirms that AR bandwidth scales linearly with the geometric asymmetry.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A compact microstrip patch antenna operates at 2.45 GHz on an FR-4 substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, $h = 1.6$ mm). The patch is made "nearly square" with $a = 30.0$ mm and $b = 29.0$ mm, fed at the diagonal. Determine the CP frequency and the 3-dB AR bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Compute $f_0$ for a square patch.**

$$f_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.030 \times \sqrt{4.4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.060 \times 2.0976} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.12586} = 2.3835 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the two perturbed resonances.**

$$f_{10} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.030 \times \sqrt{4.4}} = 2.3835 \text{ GHz}$$

$$f_{01} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.029 \times \sqrt{4.4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.029 \times 2.0976} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.1216} = 2.4665 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the CP frequency.**

$$f_{CP} = \sqrt{2.3835 \times 2.4665} = \sqrt{5.8808} = 2.4251 \text{ GHz}$$

This is close to the desired 2.45 GHz — the design needs slight re-iteration.

**Step 4 — AR bandwidth.**

$$\text{BW}_{AR} = \frac{f_{01} - f_{10}}{f_{CP}} = \frac{2.4665 - 2.3835}{2.4251} = \frac{0.0830}{2.4251} = 0.0342 = 3.42\%$$

$$\text{BW}_{AR \text{ (MHz)}} = 3.42\% \times 2425 \text{ MHz} = 82.9 \text{ MHz}$$

This comfortably covers the entire **2.4–2.4835 GHz WiFi / Bluetooth ISM band** with a single nearly-square patch.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Compact CP Antenna System

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the high-level functional flow from RF source through feed network to radiator and into the propagating CP wave.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RFIn["RF Input Port 50 ohm"] --> FeedNet["Feed Network Branch"]
    FeedNet --> Hybrid["90 Degree Branch Line Hybrid or Sequential Phase Feed"]
    Hybrid --> ArmA["Feed Arm A along x axis"]
    Hybrid --> ArmB["Feed Arm B along y axis with 90 deg phase lag"]
    ArmA --> PatchA["Patch Edge A TM10 mode"]
    ArmB --> PatchB["Patch Edge B TM01 mode"]
    PatchA --> Radiator["Square or Nearly Square Patch with Corner Truncation"]
    PatchB --> Radiator
    Radiator --> EField["Orthogonal E field components EX and EY"]
    EField --> CPWave["Circularly Polarised Wave propagating in plus z direction"]
    CPWave --> ARCheck["Axial Ratio Measurement"]
    ARCheck --> ARResult{"AR is less than 3 dB?"}
    ARResult -- Yes --> CPValid["Valid CP Radiation LHCP or RHCP"]
    ARResult -- No --> Tune["Adjust corner truncation Delta S or aspect ratio"]
    Tune --> Radiator
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Compact CP Antenna Families

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPFamily["Compact Circularly Polarised Antennas"]
    CPFamily --> SF["Single Feed Designs"]
    CPFamily --> MF["Multi Feed Designs"]
    SF --> TC["Truncated Corner Square Patch"]
    SF --> NS["Nearly Square Patch"]
    SF --> SL["Slotted Patch L Shaped or U shaped slot"]
    SF --> CS["Circular Slot with stub"]
    SF --> DRA["Dielectric Resonator Antenna with crossed slot"]
    MF --> DP["Dual Feed with 90 deg Hybrid"]
    MF --> XP["Crossed Dipole with delay line"]
    MF --> SR["Sequential Rotation Array"]
    TC --> App1["GNSS L1 receivers"]
    NS --> App2["WiFi 2.4 GHz ISM"]
    SL --> App3["UHF RFID tags"]
    DRA --> App4["Millimetre wave 28 GHz 5G"]
    DP --> App5["Satellite mobile handsets"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Miniaturisation Strategy Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Base["Baseline Half Lambda Patch"] --> M1["Strategy 1 High Er substrate"]
    Base --> M2["Strategy 2 Shorting posts between patch and ground"]
    Base --> M3["Strategy 3 Meander line current path"]
    Base --> M4["Strategy 4 Slot loading on patch"]
    Base --> M5["Strategy 5 Stacked patch with parasitic element"]
    M1 --> Out["Compact CP radiator"]
    M2 --> Out
    M3 --> Out
    M4 --> Out
    M5 --> Out
    Out --> Metric["Size Reduction Factor SRF > 0.4 with AR less than 3 dB"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define *circular polarisation* in terms of the orthogonal components of the radiated electric field. State the IEEE handedness convention.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Circular polarisation is obtained when the radiated electric-field vector in the far-zone has two orthogonal components of **equal amplitude** and a **phase difference of exactly 90°**. The time-domain field is:

$$\vec{E}(t) = \hat{a}_x E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz) \pm \hat{a}_y E_0 \cos(\omega t - kz \mp \pi/2)$$

The upper sign yields **Left-Hand CP (LHCP)** and the lower sign yields **Right-Hand CP (RHCP)** by the IEEE definition. The axial ratio (AR) of the polarisation ellipse equals **1 (0 dB)** for perfect CP and is required to be $\leq$ **3 dB** in practical CP antenna specifications.

*(Mark split: 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark IEEE convention.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List any **three** miniaturisation techniques used to realise *compact* circularly polarised microstrip patch antennas.

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark each):**

1. **Truncated-corner perturbation** of a square patch — exploits mode splitting between TM₁₀ and TM₀₁ to generate CP while keeping the patch length near $\lambda_g/2$.
2. **High-permittivity substrate loading** — increasing $\varepsilon_r$ from 2.2 to 10.2 reduces the guided wavelength $\lambda_g$, shrinking the patch footprint by up to 70%.
3. **Slot-loading** — etching L-shaped, U-shaped, or cross-slots on the patch forces the surface current to meander, increasing the electrical length without increasing the physical size.
4. *(Optional fourth)* **Shorting-pin / shorting-wall technique** — pins near the feed create an artificial quarter-wave resonator, halving the patch length.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS — INTERNAL CHOICE (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A — Single-Feed Truncated-Corner CP Patch Design** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 marks] With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **single-feed truncated-corner square microstrip patch antenna** for circular polarisation. Derive the relationship between the corner truncation area $\Delta s$ and the 3-dB AR bandwidth.

**(b)** [7 marks] A 5.8 GHz square patch on a substrate with $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $h = 0.787$ mm has a side length of $L = 16.45$ mm. The corners are truncated by $\Delta s = 1.5$ mm × 1.5 mm each. Determine (i) the AR centre frequency and (ii) the AR bandwidth if the total Q-factor of the patch is $Q_0 = 45$.

**MODEL ANSWER**

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Derivation:**

A square microstrip patch has two orthogonal dominant modes, **TM₁₀** (along x) and **TM₀₁** (along y), which are degenerate (same resonant frequency $f_0$). When one corner of the square is truncated by a small area $\Delta s$, the **effective electrical length** along one diagonal increases while the other diagonal remains unchanged. This perturbs the boundary condition such that:

- The TM₀₁ mode resonance **decreases** by an amount $\Delta f_1$.
- The TM₁₀ mode resonance **increases** by an amount $\Delta f_2$.
- At a frequency $f_p$ between the two perturbed modes, the two modes have **equal amplitude** and a **phase difference of 90°**, generating CP.

The fractional AR bandwidth of a perturbed-patch CP radiator was shown by Sharma & Gupta to be:

$$\text{BW}_{AR} = \frac{\Delta s / S}{Q_0}$$

where $S$ is the total patch area and $Q_0$ is the unloaded quality factor of the patch. Hence **larger $\Delta s$ and lower $Q_0$** yield broader AR bandwidth.

The handedness (LHCP or RHCP) is determined by which corner is truncated: truncating the **+x, +y corner** yields **LHCP** and truncating the **−x, +y corner** yields **RHCP** in the standard configuration.

*(Mark split: 3 marks sketch + physical explanation, 2 marks derivation of AR vs $\Delta s$, 2 marks handedness rule.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the resonant wavelength and the patch quality factor.**

$$f_0 = 5.8 \text{ GHz} \Rightarrow \lambda_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9} = 51.72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the guided wavelength and patch area.**

$$\lambda_g = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{51.72}{\sqrt{2.2}} = \frac{51.72}{1.4832} = 34.87 \text{ mm}$$

$$S = L^2 = (16.45)^2 = 270.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the truncated area.**

$$\Delta s = 1.5 \times 1.5 = 2.25 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — AR bandwidth (Sharma-Gupta formula):**

$$\text{BW}_{AR} = \frac{\Delta s / S}{Q_0} = \frac{2.25 / 270.6}{45} = \frac{0.00832}{45} = 1.85 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\text{BW}_{AR (\text{MHz})} = 1.85 \times 10^{-4} \times 5800 = 1.07 \text{ MHz}$$

This is a very narrow bandwidth — typical of high-$Q$ low-$ε_r$ patches. Practical designs increase $\Delta s$ or use a thicker substrate (which lowers $Q_0$) to widen the AR band.

**Step 5 — AR centre frequency.** The AR centre is offset from $f_0$ by approximately:

$$f_p \approx f_0 + \frac{\Delta f_2 - \Delta f_1}{2} \approx f_0 \text{ (to first order)}$$

$$\boxed{f_p \approx 5.8 \text{ GHz}, \quad \text{BW}_{AR} \approx 1.07 \text{ MHz}}$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks numerical setup, 2 marks for the BW equation, 2 marks for final values, 1 mark for conclusion/handedness discussion.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — Nearly-Square Patch CP Antenna Design** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the *nearly-square* microstrip patch geometry for circular polarisation. Compare it with the truncated-corner patch in terms of AR bandwidth, design complexity, and fabrication tolerance.

**(b)** [7 marks] A nearly-square patch on a substrate $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$ has dimensions $a = 32$ mm and $b = 30$ mm. Find (i) the two perturbed resonant frequencies and (ii) the CP centre frequency and AR bandwidth when the patch is fed at the diagonal.

**MODEL ANSWER**

**Part (a) — Explanation & Comparison:**

A **nearly-square patch** is a rectangular microstrip patch whose length $a$ and width $b$ differ by a small amount $\Delta = a - b$, typically $1$–$5\%$ of $\lambda_g/2$. The patch is fed along the **diagonal**, which excites both TM₁₀ and TM₀₁ modes simultaneously.

The CP condition is naturally satisfied at the **geometric-mean frequency** of the two perturbed modes, where the modal amplitudes are equal and the phase offset is 90°. Unlike the truncated-corner patch, the nearly-square design does NOT use the boundary-perturbation technique but rather a **direct aspect-ratio asymmetry**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Truncated-Corner Patch | Nearly-Square Patch |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Square + diagonal cut | Plain rectangle |
| Feed point | Edge or inset | Diagonal |
| AR bandwidth | $1$–$2\%$ | $2$–$4\%$ |
| Design complexity | Moderate (need $\Delta s$) | Low (only $a, b$) |
| Fabrication tolerance | Sensitive to $\Delta s$ | Less sensitive |
| Application | GPS, narrow-band | WiFi, broadband |

*(Mark split: 3 marks explanation, 4 marks comparison table and discussion.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the two perturbed frequencies.**

$$f_{10} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.032 \times \sqrt{4.4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.064 \times 2.0976} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.1342} = 2.235 \text{ GHz}$$

$$f_{01} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.030 \times \sqrt{4.4}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.1216} = 2.467 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 2 — CP centre frequency.**

$$f_{CP} = \sqrt{f_{10} \times f_{01}} = \sqrt{2.235 \times 2.467} = \sqrt{5.514} = 2.348 \text{ GHz}$$

**Step 3 — AR fractional bandwidth.**

$$\text{BW}_{AR} = \frac{f_{01} - f_{10}}{f_{CP}} = \frac{2.467 - 2.235}{2.348} = \frac{0.232}{2.348} = 0.0988 = 9.88\%$$

$$\text{BW}_{AR (\text{MHz})} = 0.0988 \times 2348 \text{ MHz} = 232.0 \text{ MHz}$$

This 9.88% AR bandwidth easily covers the 2.4–2.4835 GHz ISM band.

$$\boxed{f_{10} = 2.235 \text{ GHz}, \; f_{01} = 2.467 \text{ GHz}, \; f_{CP} = 2.348 \text{ GHz}, \; \text{BW}_{AR} \approx 232 \text{ MHz (9.88\%)}}$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks for the formula setup, 2 marks for the two perturbed frequencies, 2 marks for $f_{CP}$, 1 mark for the bandwidth result.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Compact CP Antenna Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the IEEE handedness convention.** Always specify LHCP vs RHCP — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $f_0$ directly as the CP frequency** for nearly-square patches. The CP centre is at the **geometric mean** $\sqrt{f_1 f_2}$, NOT the arithmetic mean $(f_1+f_2)/2$. Examiners deduct 2 marks if you use arithmetic mean.
> 3. **Mixing up $\lambda_0$ vs $\lambda_g$.** Substrate permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ must be applied when computing physical patch size, but $\lambda_0$ is used for free-space path loss and far-field distances.
> 4. **Omitting units in the Sharma-Gupta formula.** The BW is dimensionless (fractional) — write both `%` and `MHz`.
> 5. **Drawing the truncated corner on the WRONG diagonal.** LHCP and RHCP depend on which corner is truncated. A wrong corner costs 1 mark in a sketch question.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the 90° phase requirement** when describing CP. Examiners expect BOTH conditions: equal amplitude AND $\pm \pi/2$ phase.

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **CP condition**: equal amplitudes, 90° phase shift between orthogonal $E_x$ and $E_y$.
- **AR $\leq$ 3 dB** is the universal CP acceptance threshold.
- **Single-feed CP antennas** are the most compact and use either corner truncation or aspect-ratio asymmetry.
- **Nearly-square CP frequency**: $f_{CP} = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$.
- **Truncated-corner AR BW**: $\text{BW}_{AR} = (\Delta s / S) / Q_0$.
- **Truncation direction controls handedness** — IEEE convention must be stated explicitly.
- **High-$\varepsilon_r$ substrate** shrinks physical size but increases $Q_0$, narrowing AR bandwidth.
- **Chu-Harrington limit** defines the fundamental trade-off between electrical size $ka$ and fractional bandwidth.
- **GNSS, WiFi, RFID, satellite-mobile, drones, wearables** are the top commercial applications.
- **Dual-feed CP designs** give wider bandwidth but lose compactness — often used in satellite handsets.
- **DRA-based CP antennas** are emerging at mmWave 5G (28/38 GHz) due to zero conductor loss.
- **Slot-loaded CP patches** can achieve $>40\%$ size reduction while preserving AR $<3$ dB.
- **Sequential rotation arrays** can synthesise CP from linearly polarised sub-arrays with $>15\%$ AR bandwidth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Microstrip Line (Using Software)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Microstrip Line (Using Software)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Microstrip Line:** A microstrip transmission line is a planar transmission-line geometry fabricated using printed-circuit-board (PCB) techniques. It consists of a **conducting strip of width $W$ and thickness $t$** separated from a **ground plane** by a **dielectric substrate** of thickness $h$ and relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{r}$.

In the context of KTU Module 2 (Antenna Theory and Design), the microstrip line is the foundational building element used to feed microstrip patch antennas, construct corporate-feed networks, and design microwave integrated circuits (MICs). Computer-Aided Design (CAD) using full-wave electromagnetic (EM) simulators is the industry-standard methodology for verifying analytical designs before fabrication.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a highway for high-frequency electrical signals (microwaves, typically **1 GHz to 100 GHz**). A normal copper wire is like a narrow, twisty country road — signals bounce around, get distorted, and leak energy everywhere. 

A **microstrip line** is like a wide, straight, multi-lane highway built on a flat, well-engineered ground:
- The **copper trace on top** is one lane (signal lane).
- The **dielectric board** is the road surface that controls how fast the signal travels.
- The **ground plane** at the bottom is the return lane — it gives the signal a clean, predictable path back to the source.

When you design this "highway" using software (HFSS, ADS, CST), you tell the computer: "Give me a road that lets a 2.4 GHz signal travel with **50 ohms of impedance**, losses below 0.5 dB/cm, and minimum reflections." The software simulates Maxwell's equations across the structure and reports back exactly how it will perform — saving weeks of trial-and-error fabrication.

### 1.3 Why Use Software for Microstrip Design?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closed-form analytical equations** (Hammerstad, Wheeler, etc.) provide first-pass values for strip width and effective permittivity, but they break down for:
> - Thick conductors ($t/h > 0.1$)
> - High dielectric constants ($\varepsilon_{r} > 12$)
> - Discontinuities (bends, T-junctions, gaps)
> - Substrate anisotropy
> - Finite ground plane and radiation losses

**Full-wave EM simulation** is therefore mandatory for accurate design, especially in the **microwave and millimeter-wave bands** where the wavelength $\lambda$ becomes comparable to the physical dimensions of the structure.

### 1.4 Key Design Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Role |
|-----------|--------|---------------|------|
| Characteristic Impedance | $Z_0$ | **20 – 120 Ω** | Determines W/h ratio |
| Substrate Height | $h$ | 0.2 – 3.2 mm | Controls line width and loss |
| Dielectric Constant | $\varepsilon_{r}$ | 2.2 – 10.2 | Sets effective permittivity |
| Loss Tangent | $\tan \delta$ | 0.0005 – 0.02 | Dielectric loss |
| Operating Frequency | $f_0$ | 1 – 60 GHz | Sets electrical length |
| Conductor Thickness | $t$ | 17 – 70 μm (1/2 – 2 oz Cu) | Affects loss and dispersion |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Why the Microstrip Works

A microstrip line supports a **quasi-TEM (Transverse Electro-Magnetic)** mode. The electric and magnetic fields are not entirely confined to the dielectric — part of the field exists in the air region above the strip. This is called the **inhomogeneous dielectric effect**, and it is what makes microstrip dispersion-prone at high frequencies.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning):**

1. **Signal Injection:** An RF signal is applied between the top strip and the ground plane.
2. **Field Configuration:** The electric field lines start perpendicular at the strip, curve through the dielectric, and terminate on the ground plane. The magnetic field loops around the strip.
3. **Wave Propagation:** Because the fields see **two media** (air above, substrate below) with different permittivities, the wave travels at an **effective velocity** $v_{p} = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{eff}}}$.
4. **Characteristic Impedance:** Determined by the ratio $W/h$ and the permittivity. Wider strips → lower impedance; thinner strips → higher impedance.
5. **Losses:** Two main loss mechanisms:
   - **Dielectric loss** $\alpha_{d}$ (from $\tan \delta$ of substrate)
   - **Conductor loss** $\alpha_{c}$ (from finite conductivity of copper, skin effect)

### 2.2 The Design Problem

Given the **target specifications**:
- Operating frequency $f_0$
- Characteristic impedance $Z_0$ (typically **50 Ω**)
- Substrate $\varepsilon_{r}$, $h$, $\tan \delta$, copper thickness $t$

**Find:** Strip width $W$, effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$, physical length $L$ for a given electrical length (e.g., quarter-wave), and predict losses $\alpha_{c}$, $\alpha_{d}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **closed-form synthesis equations** used as the *first step* in any software-based microstrip design. Software packages implement refined versions (Hammerstad-Jensen, Wadell, or full-wave solvers).

#### A. Effective Permittivity

For $W/h \leq 1$:
$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{r} + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_{r} - 1}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12\,h/W}} + 0.04\,(1 - W/h)^2 \right]$$

For $W/h \geq 1$:
$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{r} + 1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_{r} - 1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12\,h/W}}$$

#### B. Synthesis: Find $W$ for given $Z_0$ and $\varepsilon_{r}$

Define $A = \dfrac{Z_0}{60} \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} + 1}{2}} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} - 1}{\varepsilon_{r} + 1} \left( 0.23 + \dfrac{0.11}{\varepsilon_{r}} \right)$.

For $A \leq 1.393$:
$$\frac{W}{h} = 8\,e^{A} \big/ (e^{2A} - 2)$$

For $A \geq 1.393$:
$$\frac{W}{h} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \left[ B - 1 - \ln(2B - 1) + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{r} - 1}{2\varepsilon_{r}} \left\{ \ln(B - 1) + 0.39 - \dfrac{0.61}{\varepsilon_{r}} \right\} \right]$$

where $B = \dfrac{377\,\pi}{2\,Z_0\,\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r}}}$.
