#### C. Characteristic Impedance (Analysis, given $W/h$)

For $W/h \leq 1$:
$$Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} \ln\!\left( \frac{8h}{W} + \frac{W}{4h} \right)$$

For $W/h \geq 1$:
$$Z_0 = \frac{120\,\pi / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}{W/h + 1.393 + 0.667\,\ln(W/h + 1.444)}$$

#### D. Guided Wavelength and Physical Length

$$\lambda_{g} = \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{c}{f_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}$$

For a quarter-wave transformer at $f_0$:
$$L = \frac{\lambda_{g}}{4} = \frac{c}{4\,f_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}$$

#### E. Loss Coefficients (dB/length)

**Dielectric loss:**
$$\alpha_{d} = \frac{27.3\,\varepsilon_{r}\,(\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} - 1)\,\tan\delta}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}\,(\varepsilon_{r} - 1)\, \lambda_0} \quad [\text{dB/unit length}]$$

**Conductor loss (wide strip, $W/h \gg 1$):**
$$\alpha_{c} = \frac{8.68\,R_{s}}{Z_0\,W} \quad [\text{dB/unit length}]$$

where skin-effect surface resistance $R_{s} = \sqrt{\pi f_0 \mu_0 / \sigma}$ and $\sigma_{\text{Cu}} = 5.8 \times 10^{7}$ **S/m**.

#### F. Phase Velocity and Propagation Constant

$$v_{p} = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} \qquad \beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda_{g}} = \frac{2\pi f_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}{c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The formulas above feed directly into the schematic/layout environment of Keysight ADS, AWR Microwave Office, or into the parameter sweep of HFSS/CST. The closed-form values are used as **initial seeds** for the EM simulator's optimizer, which then refines them by solving the integral form of Maxwell's equations numerically (Method of Moments — MoM, or Finite Element Method — FEM).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Design Example (The KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem Statement (typical 14-mark KTU problem):**
> Design a **50 Ω** microstrip line on a substrate with $\varepsilon_{r} = 4.4$ (FR-4), $h = 1.6$ mm, $\tan \delta = 0.02$, and copper thickness $t = 35\,\mu$m (1 oz). The line must be **quarter-wave long at $f_0 = 2.4$ GHz**. Use software-equivalent closed-form synthesis. Also compute the total insertion loss per cm and verify whether the line is dispersive.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Closed-Form Synthesis

**Step 1 — Compute the parameter $A$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Z_0}{60}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{r}+1}{2}} + \frac{\varepsilon_{r}-1}{\varepsilon_{r}+1}\!\left(0.23 + \frac{0.11}{\varepsilon_{r}}\right) \\
  &= \frac{50}{60}\sqrt{\frac{4.4+1}{2}} + \frac{4.4-1}{4.4+1}\!\left(0.23 + \frac{0.11}{4.4}\right) \\
  &= 0.8333 \times \sqrt{2.7} + 0.6296 \times (0.23 + 0.025) \\
  &= 0.8333 \times 1.6432 + 0.6296 \times 0.255 \\
  &= 1.3693 + 0.1605 \\
  &= 1.5298
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $A = 1.5298 > 1.393$, we use the **wide-strip formula**.

**Step 2 — Compute parameter $B$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= \frac{377\,\pi}{2\,Z_0\,\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r}}} = \frac{377 \times 3.1416}{2 \times 50 \times \sqrt{4.4}} \\
  &= \frac{1184.4}{100 \times 2.0976} = \frac{1184.4}{209.76} \\
  &= 5.647
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the $W/h$ ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{W}{h} &= \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[ B - 1 - \ln(2B - 1) + \frac{\varepsilon_{r}-1}{2\varepsilon_{r}}\!\left\{\ln(B-1) + 0.39 - \frac{0.61}{\varepsilon_{r}}\right\} \right] \\
&= \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[ 5.647 - 1 - \ln(10.294) + \frac{3.4}{8.8}\!\left\{\ln(4.647) + 0.39 - \frac{0.61}{4.4}\right\} \right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times \left[ 4.647 - 2.331 + 0.3864 \times \{1.5366 + 0.39 - 0.1386\} \right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times \left[ 2.316 + 0.3864 \times 1.788 \right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times \left[ 2.316 + 0.691 \right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times 3.007 \\
&= 1.914
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore:**
$$W = 1.914 \times h = 1.914 \times 1.6 \text{ mm} = \mathbf{3.06 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute effective permittivity (since $W/h = 1.914 > 1$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{\varepsilon_{r}+1}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_{r}-1}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+12h/W}} \\
&= \frac{4.4+1}{2} + \frac{4.4-1}{2}\cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12/1.914}} \\
&= 2.7 + 1.7 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 6.270}} \\
&= 2.7 + 1.7 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{7.270}} \\
&= 2.7 + 1.7 \times \frac{1}{2.696} \\
&= 2.7 + 0.6304 \\
&= \mathbf{3.33}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Guided wavelength and physical length at 2.4 GHz:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_0 &= \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 125 \text{ mm} \\
\lambda_{g} &= \frac{\lambda_0}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{125}{\sqrt{3.33}} = \frac{125}{1.8249} = 68.5 \text{ mm} \\
L &= \frac{\lambda_g}{4} = \frac{68.5}{4} = \mathbf{17.12 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Surface resistance of copper at 2.4 GHz:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_s &= \sqrt{\frac{\pi f_0 \mu_0}{\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \times 2.4 \times 10^{9} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{5.8 \times 10^{7}}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\frac{(9.48) \times (1.257 \times 10^{-6})}{5.8 \times 10^{7}}} \\
    &= \sqrt{\frac{1.192 \times 10^{-5}}{5.8 \times 10^{7}}} \\
    &= \sqrt{2.055 \times 10^{-13}} \\
    &= 1.434 \times 10^{-6.5} \approx \mathbf{1.43 \times 10^{-2}\ \Omega/\square} \quad (0.0143\ \Omega)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Conductor loss (dB/cm):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{c} &= \frac{8.68\,R_s}{Z_0\,W} = \frac{8.68 \times 0.0143}{50 \times 0.306} \\
          &= \frac{0.1241}{15.3} \\
          &= 8.11 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{dB/cm} \\
          &= \mathbf{0.0811\ dB/cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Dielectric loss (dB/cm):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{d} &= \frac{27.3 \times \varepsilon_{r} \times (\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} - 1) \times \tan\delta}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}\,(\varepsilon_{r}-1)\,\lambda_0} \\
          &= \frac{27.3 \times 4.4 \times 2.33 \times 0.02}{1.8249 \times 3.4 \times 125\ \text{mm}} \\
          &= \frac{5.597}{775.6} \\
          &= 7.22 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{dB/mm} \\
          &= \mathbf{0.722\ dB/cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Total loss per cm:**
$$\alpha_{\text{total}} = \alpha_c + \alpha_d = 0.0811 + 0.722 = \mathbf{0.803\ dB/cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The dielectric loss **dominates** by nearly 10× because FR-4 has a high $\tan\delta = 0.02$. For a low-loss 2.4 GHz design, an engineer would **immediately switch to a substrate like Rogers RO4003C** ($\tan\delta = 0.0021$). This kind of decision is informed by the closed-form calculation *before* entering the simulator.

**Step 10 — Dispersion check (Kirschning–Tenten approximation for $f = 2.4$ GHz):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{ref}} &= \frac{Z_0}{8\pi h}\ \text{[GHz, with $h$ in mm]} = \frac{50}{8\pi \times 1.6} = 1.243\ \text{GHz} \\
\text{Since } f_0 = 2.4\ \text{GHz} < 1.5 f_{\text{ref}} = 1.86\ \text{GHz} &\Rightarrow \text{Borderline dispersive. Re-simulate in HFSS.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Transition to Software-Based Refinement

The closed-form values above are **the initial guess** that an engineer enters into the EM simulator:

| Software | Typical Workflow |
|----------|------------------|
| **ANSYS HFSS** | Draw substrate box → ground → strip of $W=3.06$ mm, $L=17.12$ mm → assign lumped 50 Ω wave port → set $f_0=2.4$ GHz, sweep 1–4 GHz → solve → read $Z_0$ from $S_{11}$ Smith chart → parametric sweep $W$ until $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$. |
| **Keysight ADS (Momentum)** | Use `MLIN` schematic component with computed $W$, $L$, $h$, $\varepsilon_{r}$ → run EM simulation → fine-tune. |
| **CST Studio Suite** | Use the *Microstrip Line* template, set substrate parameters, draw geometry, set waveguide ports, run *Time Domain Solver*. |
| **Sonnet Lite (free)** | Define substrate stack-up, draw strip, set box resonance, run MoM analysis — *excellent for student-level verification*. |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** The university's student version of Sonnet or the educational HFSS Student license can be used to perform exactly this design flow. Always export the **Smith chart**, **$S_{11}$ in dB**, and **$S_{21}$ in dB/cm** — these are the deliverables expected in the lab record.

    D --> D1[Z0 = 50 ohm]

    H --> I
    I --> J[Parametric Sweep on W]

```

### 4.2 Microstrip Line Functional Block Architecture

The diagram below shows the *signal-flow perspective* of a microstrip line as the simulator sees it — useful for understanding S-parameter extraction.

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three advantages of using full-wave EM simulation software (e.g., HFSS) over closed-form equations for microstrip line design.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Accuracy for complex geometries:** EM simulators solve Maxwell's equations numerically, accounting for finite ground plane, conductor thickness, and discontinuities — closed-form equations assume infinite ground and zero-thickness conductors.

2. **Captures higher-order effects:** Dispersion, surface-wave excitation, radiation loss at discontinuities, and substrate anisotropy are all modeled automatically, which closed-form equations ignore.

3. **Parametric optimization:** Built-in optimizers (HFSS Optimetrics, ADS Tuning) automatically sweep variables like $W$, $L$, or $h$ to meet target $Z_0$, $S_{11}$, or $S_{21}$ — saving manual iteration time.

4. **Visualization of fields:** Engineers can visualize E-field, H-field, surface currents, and far-field radiation, which is impossible analytically.

5. **Generates fabrication-ready files:** Outputs include DXF/Gerber files for PCB manufacturing.

*(Any 3 well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define "effective permittivity" of a microstrip line. Why is it always less than the substrate permittivity $\varepsilon_r$?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$ is the equivalent permittivity of a homogeneous dielectric that would give the same phase velocity as the actual inhomogeneous microstrip structure (with air above the substrate and dielectric below).

**Why $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} < \varepsilon_r$:** Because the electric field lines of the quasi-TEM mode exist partly in air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$) and partly in the substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 4.4$ for FR-4). Since the line integral of the field spends a fraction in air, the effective permittivity is a weighted average that is always **less than $\varepsilon_r$ but greater than 1**:

$$1 < \varepsilon_{\text{eff}} < \varepsilon_r$$

For example, for $W/h = 1.914$ and $\varepsilon_r = 4.4$, we computed $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = 3.33$, which lies between 1 and 4.4 as expected.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Students frequently write $W/h$ as a number without units, or forget to convert $h = 1.6$ mm when computing $R_s$ in SI units. Always carry units until the final step.
> 2. **Choosing the wrong formula branch:** The $W/h \leq 1$ and $W/h \geq 1$ cases use **different** equations for $Z_0$ and $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$. Forgetting to check this gives 0 marks for the synthesis step.
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda_0$ with $\lambda_g$:** Free-space wavelength $\lambda_0 = c/f_0$ is **different** from the guided wavelength $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}$. The physical length $L$ uses $\lambda_g$, not $\lambda_0$.
> 4. **Ignoring dispersion:** At $f > 5$ GHz on FR-4, $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$ increases with frequency. A correct 14-mark answer mentions Kirschning-Tenten correction or simply flags the design for software re-verification.
> 5. **No justification for software choice:** Just naming "HFSS" is not enough — state **why** (e.g., FEM, anisotropic support, frequency-domain solver for narrowband microstrip).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Derive the closed-form expression for the characteristic impedance of a **wide microstrip line** ($W/h \geq 1$) on a substrate of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using a suitable EM simulation software (HFSS / ADS / Sonnet), describe step-by-step the design of a **50 Ω, quarter-wave microstrip line at 3.5 GHz** on a Rogers RO4003C substrate ($\varepsilon_r = 3.55$, $h = 0.813$ mm, $\tan \delta = 0.0021$, copper thickness $t = 35\,\mu$m). Compute $W$, $L$, and total attenuation per cm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation of $Z_0$ for $W/h \geq 1$:**

> *Assumptions:*
> 1. Quasi-TEM mode of propagation.
> 2. Strip and ground plane are perfect conductors of zero thickness.
> 3. Substrate is homogeneous, isotropic, and lossless (loss added later as perturbation).
> 4. Ground plane is infinite in extent.
> 5. Air-dielectric interface produces an inhomogeneous medium → effective permittivity $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$.

**Conformal mapping derivation (sketch):**
A microstrip line can be transformed into a parallel-plate capacitor using the **Schwarz-Christoffel transformation**. The strip of width $W$ and the ground plane separated by effective height $h_{\text{eff}} = h \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} / \varepsilon_r}$ is mapped to a parallel-plate region. The capacitance per unit length is $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{\text{eff}} W / h_{\text{eff}}$, giving:

$$Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}} = \frac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}{c\,C} = \frac{120\,\pi / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}}{W/h + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln(W/h + 1.444)}$$

The constants **1.393** and **0.667** arise from curve-fitting the conformal-mapping result to numerical solutions of the Laplace equation. **(Full 7 marks require the integral form, the conformal mapping concept, and the final fit equation.)**

---

**(b) Numerical design at 3.5 GHz on RO4003C:**

**Step 1: Parameter $A$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{50}{60}\sqrt{\frac{3.55+1}{2}} + \frac{3.55-1}{3.55+1}\!\left(0.23+\frac{0.11}{3.55}\right) \\
  &= 0.8333 \times 1.506 + 0.5606 \times 0.261 \\
  &= 1.255 + 0.146 = 1.401
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $A = 1.401 > 1.393$, **wide-strip formula** applies.

**Step 2: Parameter $B$:**
$$B = \frac{377\pi}{2 \times 50 \times \sqrt{3.55}} = \frac{1184.4}{100 \times 1.884} = 6.288$$

**Step 3: $W/h$ ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{W}{h} &= \frac{2}{\pi}\!\left[6.288 - 1 - \ln(11.576) + \frac{2.55}{7.1}\!\left\{\ln(5.288) + 0.39 - \frac{0.61}{3.55}\right\}\right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times \left[5.288 - 2.449 + 0.359 \times \{1.665 + 0.39 - 0.172\}\right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times \left[2.839 + 0.359 \times 1.883\right] \\
&= 0.6366 \times 3.515 = 2.238
\end{aligned}
$$

$$W = 2.238 \times 0.813 = \mathbf{1.82\ mm}$$

**Step 4: $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$ (wide strip):**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{3.55+1}{2} + \frac{3.55-1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 12/2.238}} = 2.275 + 1.275 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{6.361}} = 2.275 + 0.505 = \mathbf{2.78}
$$

**Step 5: Length:**
$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3.5 \times 10^9} = 85.71\ \text{mm}, \quad L = \frac{\lambda_0}{4\sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}} = \frac{85.71}{4 \times 1.667} = \mathbf{12.86\ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Conductor loss:**
$$
R_s = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \times 3.5 \times 10^9 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{5.8 \times 10^7}} = 0.0175\ \Omega,\quad \alpha_c = \frac{8.68 \times 0.0175}{50 \times 0.182} = \mathbf{0.0167\ dB/cm}
$$

**Step 7: Dielectric loss:**
$$
\alpha_d = \frac{27.3 \times 3.55 \times 1.78 \times 0.0021}{1.667 \times 2.55 \times 85.71} = \mathbf{0.028\ dB/cm}
$$

**Total: $\alpha_{\text{total}} = 0.0167 + 0.028 = \mathbf{0.0447\ dB/cm}$**

**Software workflow (in HFSS / ADS):**

1. **New project → Insert HFSS design.**
2. **Create substrate box** with dimensions: width = $6W$ (= 10.9 mm), length = $3L$ (= 38.6 mm), height = $h$ (= 0.813 mm). Assign material Rogers RO4003C ($\varepsilon_r = 3.55$, $\tan\delta = 0.0021$).
3. **Draw ground plane** on bottom face of the box as a perfect-E boundary.
4. **Draw microstrip strip** of $W = 1.82$ mm, $L = 12.86$ mm on top face, assign copper.
5. **Create wave port** on the two end faces — assign 50 Ω lumped port.
6. **Solution setup:** Driven Modal, frequency sweep 1–6 GHz, max delta S = 0.01.
7. **Solve.**
8. **Post-process:** Plot $|S_{11}|$ in dB, $|S_{21}|$ in dB, Smith chart of $S_{11}$.
9. **Parametric sweep:** Add variable $W$, sweep 1.5 mm to 2.2 mm. For each value, re-solve. Plot $Z_0$ vs $W$. The value giving $Z_0 = 50\ \Omega$ is the optimized width.
10. **Final report:** Submit HFSS screenshots, Smith chart, $S$-parameters, optimized $W$, $L$, $\alpha_{\text{total}}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step workflow** for designing a microstrip line in **ANSYS HFSS**. List the four boundary conditions used and the role of the **lumped port vs wave port**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microstrip line has $W = 2$ mm, $h = 0.5$ mm, $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$ (Rogers RT/duroid 5880), $\tan \delta = 0.0009$, $t = 17\,\mu$m, $f_0 = 10$ GHz. Compute: (i) $Z_0$, (ii) $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$, (iii) guided wavelength, (iv) conductor loss per cm, (v) dielectric loss per cm, and (vi) total loss. State whether the line is dispersion-affected. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) HFSS Workflow + Boundary Conditions:**

**Workflow (5 steps):**
1. **Project & Design Setup:** Open HFSS → Project → Insert HFSS Design → choose *Driven Modal* solution type.
2. **3D Geometry:** Draw dielectric substrate box using *Box* primitive. Draw ground plane and strip using *Rectangle*. Use *Subtract* or *Assign Boundary* for the metal faces.
3. **Material Assignment:** Substrate → Rogers RT/duroid (or custom $\varepsilon_r$, $\tan\delta$). Metal → copper or *perfect conductor* (for initial design).
4. **Excitation:** Draw lumped port rectangles on the two open end faces, 4–5 times wider than $W$, 4–5 times $h$ deep. Assign *Lumped Port* with 50 Ω integration line.
5. **Analysis Setup & Solve:** Solution Frequency = $f_0$. Add Frequency Sweep (Fast or Interpolating). Set convergence criterion (Max Delta S = 0.02). Click *Analyze All*.

**Four Boundary Conditions:**
1. **Perfect E (Finite Conductivity):** Assigns an electric wall — used for ground plane and strip (zero tangential E). The *Finite Conductivity* variant includes conductor loss.
2. **Radiation Boundary:** Absorbs outgoing waves — used on the air box faces surrounding the substrate to mimic open space.
3. **Symmetry Plane (Perfect H or Perfect E):** Halves the simulation domain by exploiting symmetry — reduces compute time.
4. **Lumped / Wave Port:** Excitation boundary. **Lumped port** = a defined-impedance source at a single cross-section, good for lumped excitation. **Wave port** = a de-embeddable, mode-based excitation, more accurate for transmission lines, automatically computes the characteristic impedance from the propagating mode.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $W = 2$ mm, $h = 0.5$ mm, $\varepsilon_r = 2.2$, $\tan\delta = 0.0009$, $t = 17\,\mu$m, $f_0 = 10$ GHz.

**Step (i): $W/h = 2/0.5 = 4 \geq 1$ → wide-strip formula.**

**Step (ii): $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$:**
$$
\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{2.2+1}{2} + \frac{2.2-1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+12/4}} = 1.6 + 0.6 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{4}} = 1.6 + 0.3 = \mathbf{1.90}
$$

**Step (iii): $Z_0$:**
$$
Z_0 = \frac{120\pi/\sqrt{1.90}}{4 + 1.393 + 0.667 \ln(4+1.444)} = \frac{272.5}{4 + 1.393 + 0.667 \times 1.792} = \frac{272.5}{6.589} = \mathbf{41.4\ \Omega}
$$

**Step (iv): Guided wavelength:**
$$
\lambda_0 = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10 \times 10^9} = 30\ \text{mm}, \quad \lambda_g = \frac{30}{\sqrt{1.90}} = \frac{30}{1.378} = \mathbf{21.77\ mm}
$$

**Step (v): Conductor loss:**
$$
R_s = \sqrt{\frac{\pi \times 10^{10} \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7}}{5.8 \times 10^7}} = 0.0326\ \Omega, \quad \alpha_c = \frac{8.68 \times 0.0326}{41.4 \times 0.2} = \mathbf{0.0342\ dB/cm}
$$

**Step (vi): Dielectric loss:**
$$
\alpha_d = \frac{27.3 \times 2.2 \times 0.90 \times 0.0009}{1.378 \times 1.2 \times 30} = \frac{0.0487}{49.6} = \mathbf{9.82 \times 10^{-4}\ dB/cm} \approx 0.001\ \text{dB/cm}
$$

**Total loss: $\alpha_{\text{total}} = 0.0342 + 0.001 \approx \mathbf{0.035\ dB/cm}$**

**Dispersion check:**
$$f_{\text{ref}} = \frac{Z_0}{8\pi h} = \frac{41.4}{8\pi \times 0.5} = 3.29\ \text{GHz}, \quad 1.5 f_{\text{ref}} = 4.94\ \text{GHz}$$
Since $f_0 = 10$ GHz $\gg 4.94$ GHz, **the line is strongly dispersive**. The Kirschning–Tenten correction must be applied, and the design should be re-verified in HFSS.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- A **microstrip line** consists of a top strip ($W$, $t$), a substrate ($h$, $\varepsilon_r$, $\tan\delta$), and a ground plane. It supports a **quasi-TEM** mode.
- The **effective permittivity** $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$ satisfies $1 < \varepsilon_{\text{eff}} < \varepsilon_r$ and is computed using **two different formulas** depending on whether $W/h \leq 1$ or $W/h \geq 1$.
- The **synthesis equations** (given $Z_0$ and $\varepsilon_r$, find $W$) require the **intermediate parameter $A$** to decide which branch of the formula to use; $A = 1.393$ is the threshold.
- The **guided wavelength** $\lambda_g = \lambda_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}}$ — **never** confuse with $\lambda_0$ when finding the physical length of resonators, patches, or feed lines.
- The **two loss mechanisms** are **conductor loss** (depends on $R_s$, $W$, $Z_0$) and **dielectric loss** (depends on $\tan\delta$ and $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$). For FR-4, dielectric loss dominates; for Rogers, conductor loss dominates.
- **Software tools** for microstrip design: **HFSS** (3D FEM, most accurate), **ADS Momentum** (2.5D MoM, fast for planar circuits), **CST** (time-domain, broadband), **Sonnet** (planar MoM, student-friendly).
- The **software design flow** is: (1) closed-form seed values, (2) geometry creation, (3) boundary + port assignment, (4) frequency sweep, (5) post-process S-parameters, (6) **parametric optimization**, (7) fabrication export.
- **Dispersion** becomes significant when $f_0 > 1.5 \cdot f_{\text{ref}}$ where $f_{\text{ref}} = Z_0 / (8\pi h)$. At mm-wave frequencies, always re-simulate with the Kirschning–Tenten correction or a full-wave solver.
- **For the KTU exam**, the most common marks are lost by (a) using the wrong branch of the $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}}$ formula, (b) confusing $\lambda_0$ and $\lambda_g$, and (c) not justifying the choice of EM simulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway:** A microstrip line is analytically tractable to **first order**, but accurate design at microwave and mm-wave frequencies **mandates** full-wave EM simulation. The engineer's role is to use closed-form equations for the **initial seed** and then let the software's optimizer refine the geometry to meet the target $Z_0$, $S_{11}$, and $S_{21}$ specifications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
