topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Antenna Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Antenna Measurements: Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Antenna Measurements** refer to the systematic, quantitative experimental determination of the electrical and radiation performance parameters of an antenna under test (AUT). It is the practical bridge between theoretical antenna design (gain, directivity, pattern, polarization, impedance) and real-world deployment. The primary parameters measured are:

1. **Radiation Pattern** (amplitude and phase vs. angle θ, φ)
2. **Gain (G)** and **Directivity (D)**
3. **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW)** and **First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW)**
4. **Front-to-Back Ratio (FBR)** and **Sidelobe Level (SLL)**
5. **Polarization** (Linear, Circular, Elliptical, Axial Ratio, Sense of Rotation)
6. **Impedance / VSWR / Return Loss (S₁₁)**
7. **Bandwidth** and **Efficiency**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 2):** Students must be able to *describe* antenna ranges, *explain* radiation pattern, gain, directivity and polarization measurement techniques, and *design* the basic layout of an indoor/outdoor test range. Numerical problems on **Fraunhofer distance, Gain by two/three antenna method, and Beam solid angle** are frequently asked.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The Car Test-Drive Analogy"

Imagine you have just designed a brand-new sports car on a computer. The CAD model says it should go **300 km/h**, deliver **15 km/l**, and brake in **30 m**. None of this matters until the car is taken to a **proving ground** — a flat, controlled, instrumented test track with calibrated measuring equipment.

An **antenna measurement range** is the *"proving ground"* of an antenna. The anechoic chamber acts as the *empty test track* (free of reflections), the **positioner** rotates the *car (antenna)*, the **transmitting source antenna** plays the role of the *wind tunnel generator*, and the **Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) / Spectrum Analyzer / Power Meter** is the *speedometer/odometer* recording the readings.

**Real-world engineering utility:**
- **Telecom:** Verifying base-station panel antennas before tower-climb installation.
- **Defence & Radar:** Qualifying phased-array and reflector antennas for combat aircraft and naval vessels.
- **Satellite (ISRO/ESA):** Pre-launch testing of spaceborne payloads in compact ranges.
- **EMI/EMC Compliance:** Radiated emissions and susceptibility testing in fully-anechoic rooms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric to Remember:** The single most important geometric constraint in antenna measurement is the **Fraunhofer (far-field) distance** given by $R \ge \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$, where **D** is the largest dimension of the AUT and **λ** is the operating wavelength. Failure to meet this distance introduces **phase taper** error and pattern distortion.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology and Reference Quantities

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Standard Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| Operating Frequency | $f$ | **Hz / GHz** | Microwave bands (1 – 40 GHz) common |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **m** | $\lambda = c/f$ |
| Largest Antenna Dimension | $D$ | **m** | Aperture diameter / length |
| Far-Field (Fraunhofer) Distance | $R$ | **m** | $R \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ |
| Gain | $G$ | **dBi / dBd** | 0 dBd = 2.15 dBi |
| Directivity | $D_0$ | **dimensionless / dB** | $D_0 = 4\pi/\Omega_A$ |
| Return Loss | RL | **dB** | Acceptable ≥ **10 dB** |
| Voltage Standing Wave Ratio | VSWR | **ratio** | Acceptable ≤ **2:1** |
| Axial Ratio (Circular Polarization) | AR | **dB** | Excellent ≤ **3 dB** |
| Cross-Polar Discrimination | XPD | **dB** | Excellent ≥ **25 dB** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2 — Antenna Measurements: Section 2

## 2.1 The Generic Antenna Measurement System — A Functional Decomposition

A modern antenna measurement system, regardless of frequency, comprises the following functional blocks:

1. **RF Signal Source** — synthesised sweeper or CW generator producing a stable, known power $P_t$ at the operating frequency.
2. **Source (Transmitting) Antenna** — a calibrated, well-characterised broadband horn, log-periodic, or standard gain horn.
3. **Antenna Under Test (AUT)** — mounted on a precision **positioner** (azimuth–elevation, roll–azimuth, or spherical near-field scanner).
4. **Receiving Chain** — low-loss RF cable, **VNA / Spectrum Analyser / Power Meter**, and a low-noise amplifier (LNA) for weak-signal cases.
5. **Control & Data Processing Computer** — automates rotation, records amplitude/phase, normalises the pattern, and computes derived parameters.
6. **Test Environment** — outdoor open-area test site (OATS), anechoic chamber, or compact range.

## 2.2 Radiation Pattern Measurement — Theory of Operation

The AUT is rotated about its phase centre while a fixed source antenna illuminates it. The received power $P_r(\theta,\phi)$ (or field $E(\theta,\phi)$) is recorded as a function of angle. The **normalised power pattern** is defined as:

$$P_n(\theta,\phi) = \frac{S(\theta,\phi)}{S_{\max}}$$

where $S(\theta,\phi)$ is the power density (Poynting vector magnitude). For a linearly polarised source, only the **co-polar component** (e.g. $E_\theta$ for vertical polarisation) is measured; the orthogonal **cross-polar component** is captured by rotating the source antenna by **90°**.

### Key derived parameters from the measured pattern:

- **Half-Power Beamwidth (HPBW):** angular separation between the −3 dB points of the main beam.
- **First-Null Beamwidth (FNBW):** angular separation between the first two nulls flanking the main beam.
- **Front-to-Back Ratio (FBR):** ratio of main-lobe peak to the radiation in the exact rear direction (θ = 180°).
- **Sidelobe Level (SLL):** ratio (in dB) of the largest sidelobe peak to the main-lobe peak.
- **Beam Solid Angle** $\Omega_A$ (steradians):

$$\Omega_A = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} P_n(\theta,\phi)\,\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$$

## 2.3 Gain Measurement — The Two Fundamental Methods

### Method A — Gain Transfer (Comparison) Method

The simplest and most common method. A calibrated **standard gain horn** (whose gain $G_{\text{ref}}$ is known from the manufacturer) is first measured, then the AUT is swapped into the same position with identical range geometry. Using Friis transmission:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

Because the geometry and frequency are unchanged:

$$G_{\text{AUT}} = G_{\text{ref}} \cdot \frac{P_{r,\text{AUT}}}{P_{r,\text{ref}}}$$

In dB form:

$$G_{\text{AUT}}\big[\text{dBi}\big] = G_{\text{ref}}\big[\text{dBi}\big] + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{r,\text{AUT}}}{P_{r,\text{ref}}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This method requires only **one** known reference antenna and is the **default choice** in most KTU numerical problems.

### Method B — Absolute Gain (Three-Antenna / Two-Antenna) Method

When no calibrated reference is available, the absolute gain is recovered from Friis' equation itself.

**Two-identical-antenna case:** Use two identical unknown AUTs, one as transmit, one as receive.

$$G = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}}$$

**Three-antenna method:** Three unknown antennas (1, 2, 3) are paired in three transmit–receive combinations, giving three Friis equations with three unknowns $G_1$, $G_2$, $G_3$:

$$P_{r,ij} = P_{t,i}\, G_i G_j \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2} \quad \text{for } (i,j) = (1,2), (1,3), (2,3)$$

Solving gives explicit closed-form expressions for all three gains (derived in §3).

## 2.4 Directivity Measurement

Directivity $D_0$ is computed purely from the **shape** of the measured radiation pattern:

$$D_0 = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} P_n(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi}$$

Numerically, the integration is approximated by a **double Riemann sum** over the discrete angular grid of the measured pattern. Once $D_0$ and $G$ are known, the **radiation efficiency** follows immediately:

$$e_{rad} = \frac{G}{D_0}, \quad \text{equivalently} \quad e_{rad}\big[\text{dB}\big] = G\big[\text{dBi}\big] - D_0\big[\text{dBi}\big]$$

## 2.5 Polarization Measurement

Polarization describes the **time-varying orientation of the E-field vector** at a fixed observation point.

- **Linear polarization:** E-field oscillates along a fixed line. Measured by rotating a linearly-polarised probe around the boresight axis — a **null pattern** indicates the polarisation axis.
- **Circular / Elliptical:** characterised by **Axial Ratio (AR)** and **Sense of Rotation (RHCP / LHCP)**, measured using a **spinning-linear** or **dual-polarised (turnstile) probe**.
- **Cross-Polar Discrimination (XPD):**

$$\text{XPD}\big[\text{dB}\big] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\vert E_{\text{co}} \vert}{\vert E_{\text{cross}} \vert}\right)$$

## 2.6 Impedance / VSWR / Return Loss Measurement

Measured directly with a **Vector Network Analyser (VNA)** calibrated to the AUT feed port using SOLT (Short–Open–Load–Thru) or TRL techniques.

$$S_{11} = \Gamma = \frac{Z_{\text{in}} - Z_0}{Z_{\text{in}} + Z_0}$$

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert \Gamma \vert}{1 - \vert \Gamma \vert}, \qquad \text{Return Loss (dB)} = -20\log_{10}\vert \Gamma \vert$$

The reflection coefficient $\Gamma$ is plotted on a **Smith Chart** for visual impedance matching; bandwidth is the frequency interval over which VSWR ≤ prescribed limit (e.g. 2:1).

## 2.7 Antenna Test Ranges — The Three Principal Geometries

| Range Type | Geometry | Typical Use | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outdoor Open-Area Test Site (OATS)** | Elevated source antenna + ground-edge diffraction fences | UHF / VHF; large AUTs | Weather, RFI, ground reflection |
| **Indoor Anechoic Chamber** | Rectangular room lined with RF absorber pyramids | Microwave lab measurements | Cost, size limit (AUT $D < L/2$) |
| **Compact Range (CATR)** | Off-axis paraboloid converts spherical wave → plane wave | Large AUTs (radar reflectors, spacecraft) in small chamber | Surface accuracy, edge diffraction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **Compact Antenna Test Range (CATR)** is built around a **serrated or rolled-edge offset parabolic reflector** that collimates the spherical wave from a small feed into a **pseudo-plane wave** at the AUT. Quiet-zone quality depends on reflector surface RMS error (must be $\le \lambda / 100$).

## 2.8 Near-Field to Far-Field Transformation (NF-FF)

When the available range length is shorter than the Fraunhofer distance, the **planar / cylindrical / spherical near-field scan** is performed. The far-field pattern is then recovered by an FFT (planar/cylindrical) or spherical-wave expansion (spherical scan):

$$E_{\theta}^{ff}(r,\theta,\phi) = \frac{j k e^{-jkr}}{2\pi r}\big[T_x(\theta,\phi)\cos\phi + T_y(\theta,\phi)\sin\phi\big]$$

where $T_x, T_y$ are 2-D Fourier transforms of the measured tangential near-field components.

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{ff} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | Minimum far-field (Fraunhofer) distance |
| 2 | $R_{ff} = \dfrac{0.6 D^2}{\lambda}$ | Revised (more lenient) criterion for $D \gg \lambda$ |
| 3 | $P_r / P_t = G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation |
| 4 | $G_{\text{AUT}}[\text{dB}] = G_{\text{ref}}[\text{dB}] + 10\log_{10}(P_{r,\text{AUT}}/P_{r,\text{ref}})$ | Gain transfer method |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{P_r/P_t}$ | Absolute gain, two identical antennas |
| 6 | $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$ | Directivity from beam solid angle |
| 7 | $\Omega_A = \iint P_n(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$ | Beam solid angle definition |
| 8 | $e_{\text{rad}} = G / D_0$ | Radiation efficiency |
| 9 | $\text{VSWR} = (1+\vert\Gamma\vert)/(1-\vert\Gamma\vert)$ | VSWR in terms of $\Gamma$ |
| 10 | $\text{RL [dB]} = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | Return Loss in dB |
| 11 | $\text{XPD [dB]} = 20\log_{10}(\vert E_{\text{co}}\vert/\vert E_{\text{x}}\vert)$ | Cross-polar discrimination |
| 12 | $\text{AR [dB]} = 20\log_{10}(\text{major axis} / \text{minor axis})$ | Axial Ratio |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 2 — Antenna Measurements: Section 3

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fraunhofer (Far-Field) Distance

**Statement:** The minimum distance $R$ at which the wavefront across the AUT aperture may be approximated as a plane wave with phase error $\le \pi/8$ radian (22.5°) is

$$R_{ff} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

### Step 1 — Geometry of a point source illuminating a linear aperture

Let the AUT have a linear dimension $D$ (e.g. diameter of a parabolic dish). Place a source antenna at distance $R$ on the boresight axis, illuminating the AUT. Consider the worst-case phase point at the **edge** of the aperture, at a perpendicular distance $D/2$ from the axis.

The true path length from the source to this edge point is:

$$R' = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2}}$$

The **on-axis path length** is simply $R$. The **path difference** is:

$$\Delta R = R' - R = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^{2}} - R$$

### Step 2 — Binomial expansion for $R \gg D$

Since $R \gg D/2$ in practice (otherwise the far-field assumption is meaningless), use the binomial expansion:

$$R' = R\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{D}{2R}\right)^{2}}$$

$$R' \approx R\left[1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{D}{2R}\right)^{2} - \frac{1}{8}\left(\frac{D}{2R}\right)^{4} + \dots\right]$$

Keeping only the first two terms (sufficient for small $(D/2R)^2$):

$$R' \approx R + \frac{D^2}{8R}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta R = R' - R \approx \frac{D^2}{8R}$$

### Step 3 — Convert path difference to phase error

The corresponding **phase error** at the aperture edge is:

$$\Delta\beta = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta R = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\cdot\frac{D^2}{8R} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the maximum permissible phase error criterion

The universally adopted **Fraunhofer criterion** is $\Delta\beta \le \dfrac{\pi}{8}$ radian (22.5°):

$$\frac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R} \le \frac{\pi}{8}$$

Cancel $\pi$ and cross-multiply:

$$\frac{D^2}{4\lambda R} \le \frac{1}{8} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 8D^2 \le 4\lambda R \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R \ge \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{ff} = \frac{2D^{2}}{\lambda}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick numerical sanity check (KTU-favourite):** For a parabolic dish with $D = 2$ m operating at $f = 6$ GHz ($\lambda = 0.05$ m): $R_{ff} = 2(2^2)/0.05 = 160$ m. This is precisely why indoor anechoic chambers cannot test large apertures at high frequencies — and why **compact ranges** exist.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Absolute Gain by the Three-Antenna Method

**Statement:** When no calibrated standard-gain antenna is available, the absolute gains of three unknown antennas (1, 2, 3) can be determined from three Friis transmission measurements, provided $P_t$, $P_r$, $R$ and $\lambda$ are known.

### Step 1 — Three independent Friis equations

Let $G_1$, $G_2$, $G_3$ be the unknown gains. Pair the antennas in transmit–receive sets: (1,2), (1,3), (2,3). Let the received powers be $P_{12}$, $P_{13}$, $P_{23}$ respectively.

$$P_{12} = P_{t}\,G_{1}G_{2}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

$$P_{13} = P_{t}\,G_{1}G_{3}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

$$P_{23} = P_{t}\,G_{2}G_{3}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Multiply the first two equations and divide by the third

$$\frac{P_{12}\cdot P_{13}}{P_{23}} = \frac{(P_{t}G_{1}G_{2}k^2)(P_{t}G_{1}G_{3}k^2)}{P_{t}G_{2}G_{3}k^2} = P_{t}\,G_{1}^{2}\,k^{2}$$

where $k^{2} = (\lambda/4\pi R)^{2}$.

So:

$$G_{1}^{2} = \frac{P_{12}\,P_{13}}{P_{t}\,P_{23}}\cdot\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,G_{1} = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_{12}\,P_{13}}{P_{t}\,P_{23}}}\,}$$

By cyclic permutation of indices:

$$G_{2} = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_{12}\,P_{23}}{P_{t}\,P_{13}}}, \qquad G_{3} = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_{13}\,P_{23}}{P_{t}\,P_{12}}}$$

### Step 3 — Specialisation: Two identical antennas

If $G_1 = G_2 = G$ and we measure $P_{12}$:

$$P_{12} = P_{t}\,G^{2}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

$$G^{2} = \frac{P_{12}}{P_{t}}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,G = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_{12}}{P_{t}}}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Return Loss to VSWR Conversion

**Statement:** Express VSWR in terms of Return Loss, and vice versa.

### Step 1 — Start from the reflection coefficient magnitude

$$S_{11} = \Gamma, \qquad \text{Return Loss (RL)} = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$$

So:

$$\vert\Gamma\vert = 10^{-\text{RL}/20}$$

### Step 2 — VSWR definition

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert} = \frac{1 + 10^{-\text{RL}/20}}{1 - 10^{-\text{RL}/20}}$$

### Step 3 — Inversion: VSWR to Return Loss

Let $V = \text{VSWR}$. Then $\vert\Gamma\vert = (V-1)/(V+1)$, hence:

$$\text{RL} = -20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V-1}{V+1}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V+1}{V-1}\right)$$

**Sanity check (KTU favourite):** VSWR = 2:1 ⟹ $\Gamma = 1/3$ ⟹ RL = $20\log_{10}(3) = 9.54$ dB.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Beam Solid Angle and Directivity for a Symmetric Pencil Beam

**Statement:** For a symmetric pencil beam approximated by a circularly-symmetric pattern $P_n(\theta)$ with first null at $\theta_0$, the directivity is:

$$D_0 \approx \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}, \qquad \Omega_A \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\theta_{1}^{2}\,\,\text{(radians)}$$

### Step 1 — Beam solid angle for a narrow main beam

The beam solid angle (in steradians) is the "cone angle" of an equivalent conical beam carrying the same total power. For a symmetric beam with first null at $\theta_0$:

$$\Omega_A = 2\pi \int_{0}^{\theta_0} P_n(\theta)\sin\theta\,d\theta$$

If the pattern is approximated as uniform within the main-lobe cone ($P_n = 1$ for $\theta \le \theta_0$):

$$\Omega_A \approx 2\pi \int_{0}^{\theta_0} \sin\theta\,d\theta = 2\pi\big(1 - \cos\theta_0\big)$$

### Step 2 — Small-angle approximation

For $\theta_0 \ll 1$ radian: $\cos\theta_0 \approx 1 - \theta_0^{2}/2$, giving $\Omega_A \approx \pi\theta_0^{2}$.

### Step 3 — Directivity

$$\boxed{\,D_0 \approx \frac{4\pi}{\pi\theta_0^{2}} = \frac{4}{\theta_0^{2}}\,}$$

Equivalently in **degrees** (since $1$ rad $= 180/\pi$ deg):

$$D_0 \approx \frac{4\pi}{(\pi/4)\,\theta_{1}^{2}\text{[rad]}} = \frac{41253}{\theta_{1}^{2}\text{[deg]}}$$

(The constant $41253 = 4\pi (180/\pi)^2$.)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Answer Formula (very high yield):** $D_0 \approx \dfrac{41253}{\theta_{1}^{2}[\deg] \cdot \theta_{2}^{2}[\deg]}$ for a rectangular beam with HPBWs $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ in the two principal planes.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Fraunhofer Distance

**Problem:** An antenna of largest dimension $D = 1.5$ m is to be measured at $f = 10$ GHz. Calculate the minimum far-field distance. If this distance is unavailable in your lab, suggest a remedy.

**Solution:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3\times 10^{8}}{10\times 10^{9}} = 0.03\,\text{m}$$

$$R_{ff} = \frac{2D^{2}}{\lambda} = \frac{2\times(1.5)^{2}}{0.03} = \frac{4.5}{0.03} = 150\,\text{m}$$

A 150 m indoor chamber is impractical. **Remedy:** Use a **Compact Antenna Test Range (CATR)** with a parabolic reflector to create a **pseudo-plane wave** at the AUT, reducing the required physical length to ~5–10 m.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Three-Antenna Gain

**Problem:** Three identical unknown antennas are measured pairwise at $R = 5$ m, $f = 2$ GHz. The received/transmitted power ratios are $P_{12}/P_t = 0.0016$, $P_{13}/P_t = 0.0025$, $P_{23}/P_t = 0.0036$. Find $G_1$, $G_2$, $G_3$.

**Solution:**

$$\lambda = 0.15\,\text{m}, \quad k = \frac{\lambda}{4\pi R} = \frac{0.15}{4\pi(5)} = \frac{0.15}{62.83} = 2.387\times10^{-3}$$

For the symmetric case, all three gains should be equal:

$$G = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{P_{12}/P_t} = \frac{62.83}{0.15}\sqrt{0.0016} = 418.9\times 0.04 = 16.75$$

Converting to dBi: $G[\text{dBi}] = 10\log_{10}(16.75) \approx 12.24$ dBi.

The three pairwise products (e.g. $P_{12}P_{13}/P_{23} = 0.0016\times 0.0025 / 0.0036 = 1.111\times 10^{-3}$) all give $\sqrt{P_{12}P_{13}/(P_t P_{23})} = \sqrt{1.111\times 10^{-3}} = 0.0333$, so $G_1 = G_2 = G_3 \approx 16.75 \approx 12.24$ dBi. ✔

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 2 — Antenna Measurements: Section 4

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Antenna Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[RF Signal Source<br/>CW or Swept] --> node2[Source Antenna<br/>Standard Gain Horn]
    node2 --> node3[Test Zone<br/>Far Field Region]
    node3 --> node4[Antenna Under Test<br/>AUT on Positioner]
    node4 --> node5[Receiving Chain<br/>LNA and Cable]
    node5 --> node6[Measurement Receiver<br/>VNA or Power Meter]
    node6 --> node7[Control Computer<br/>and Data Logger]
    node7 --> node8[Processed Output<br/>Pattern, Gain, S11]
    node1 -.reference.-> node6
    node7 -.rotational control.-> node4
```

> The dashed lines indicate the **reference channel** (used to remove source-power drift) and the **positioner control feedback** (synchronises angle with measurement).

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing a Test Range

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start AUT Measurement] --> q1{Is AUT dimension D<br/>very large at lambda?}
    q1 -- Yes, Rff too big --> q2{Is anechoic chamber<br/>available indoors?}
    q1 -- No, Rff feasible --> q3[Use Indoor Anechoic Chamber]
    q2 -- Yes --> q4[Use Compact Range CATR<br/>with parabolic reflector]
    q2 -- No --> q5[Use Outdoor OATS with<br/>absorber fences and towers]
    q3 --> q6[Perform Far Field Pattern<br/>and Gain Measurement]
    q4 --> q6
    q5 --> q6
    q6 --> endA[Output: Gain, Pattern, S11, AR]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Architecture — The Three Range Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R1[Outdoor OATS]
        a1[Source Antenna on Tower] --> b1[Free Space Path]
        b1 --> c1[AUT on Positioner]
    end
    subgraph R2[Indoor Anechoic Chamber]
        a2[Source Antenna] --> b2[Anechoic Wall Absorber]
        b2 --> c2[AUT on Positioner]
    end
    subgraph R3[Compact Range CATR]
        a3[Feed Horn] --> b3[Offset Parabolic Reflector]
        b3 --> c3[Quiet Zone with AUT]
    end
```

## 4.4 Three-Antenna Method — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Antenna 1 Tx] --> nodeB[Free Space R]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Antenna 2 Rx]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Free Space R]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Antenna 3 Rx]
    nodeF[Antenna 2 Tx] --> nodeG[Free Space R]
    nodeG --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeH[Measure P12]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Measure P13]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Measure P23]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Solve 3 Friis Equations]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Extract G1, G2, G3]
```

## 4.5 Anechoic Chamber — Cross-Sectional Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHAMBER[Rectangular Anechoic Chamber]
        direction LR
        srcA[Source Horn] -->|spherical wave| reflB
        reflB[Walls Ceiling Floor<br/>Lined with RF Absorber<br/>Ferrite plus Foam Pyramids] --> quietC[Quiet Test Zone]
        quietC --> autD[AUT on Azimuth Positioner]
    end
    reflB -.suppresses.-> multE[Multipath Reflections]
    multE -.mimics.-> freeF[Free Space Conditions]
```

> The **RF absorber pyramids** (typically carbon-loaded polyurethane foam) provide a tapered impedance match to free space, attenuating wall reflections by **40 – 60 dB** at the design frequency. The **Quiet Zone** is the volumetric region where the residual reflection level is below the required amplitude taper (typically ±0.5 dB) and phase taper (typically ±5°).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 2 — Antenna Measurements: Section 5

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define an anechoic chamber. Why is it necessary for indoor antenna measurements? List the two main types of RF absorber materials used.**

**Model Answer:**

An **anechoic chamber** is a shielded indoor room whose internal walls, floor, and ceiling are lined with RF-absorbing material to simulate free-space propagation conditions by suppressing electromagnetic reflections.

It is **necessary** because:
1. Indoor environments suffer from **multipath reflections** that distort radiation patterns and corrupt gain measurements.
2. External **RFI (Radio Frequency Interference)** can be rejected by the **Faraday-cage** shielding of the chamber walls.

The two principal absorber materials are:
- **Ferrite tiles** (effective from ~30 MHz to ~1 GHz).
- **Carbon-loaded foam pyramids** (effective from ~1 GHz to ~40 GHz and beyond).

(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**What is the Fraunhofer distance? Derive the expression $R_{ff} = 2D^2/\lambda$ and state its significance in antenna measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fraunhofer distance** (or far-field distance) is the minimum separation $R$ between the source antenna and the AUT beyond which the spherical wavefront across the AUT aperture can be approximated as a plane wave with a maximum phase error of $\pi/8$ rad (22.5°).

**Derivation sketch:**
The path-length difference to the aperture edge is $\Delta R = R' - R = \sqrt{R^{2} + (D/2)^{2}} - R \approx D^{2}/(8R)$ for $R \gg D$. The corresponding phase is $\Delta\beta = (2\pi/\lambda)\Delta R = \pi D^{2}/(4\lambda R)$. Setting $\Delta\beta \le \pi/8$ gives:

$$R \ge \frac{2D^{2}}{\lambda}$$

**Significance:**
- It defines the **minimum range length** for accurate pattern, gain, and phase measurements.
- It determines whether an **indoor chamber** is feasible or whether a **compact range** or **outdoor site** is required.
- For large apertures at high frequencies, $R_{ff}$ can be hundreds of metres, dictating the use of CATRs.

(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Antenna Ranges and Pattern Measurement

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2, Choice A]**

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **setup and procedure for measuring the radiation pattern** of an antenna. List the precautions to be observed during measurement.

**(b) (7 marks)** Compare **Outdoor Open-Area Test Site (OATS)**, **Indoor Anechoic Chamber**, and **Compact Antenna Test Range (CATR)**. Mention at least four comparison parameters.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Block Diagram (refer §4.1):** RF source → Source horn → Test zone → AUT on positioner → LNA → Receiver (VNA) → Computer.

**Procedure:**
1. Mount the AUT on a precision **azimuth–elevation positioner** with its phase centre at the rotation axis.
2. Align the source antenna on the boresight axis at a distance $R \ge 2D^{2}/\lambda$.
3. Set the source to the desired frequency and power $P_t$.
4. Rotate the AUT in azimuth in small steps (typically 0.5° to 5°) over 360°; record the received power $P_r(\phi)$ at each step.
5. Repeat for the orthogonal polarisation to capture cross-polar pattern.
6. Normalise the recorded pattern to its maximum to obtain $P_n(\theta,\phi)$.
7. From $P_n$ extract HPBW, FBR, SLL, beam solid angle, and directivity.

**Precautions:**
- Maintain $R \ge 2D^{2}/\lambda$ (Fraunhofer criterion).
- Eliminate reflections (use absorbers or OATS geometry).
- Avoid RF leakage — use shielded cables and ferrite beads.
- Calibrate the receiver and use a reference channel to remove source drift.
- Ensure AUT phase centre is on the rotation axis.
- Repeat for both principal polarisations (co- and cross-pol).

**Mark split (a):** Block diagram — 2 marks; Procedure — 3 marks; Precautions — 2 marks.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

| Parameter | OATS | Anechoic Chamber | CATR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Test Environment** | Outdoor, open air | Indoor, absorber-lined | Indoor, with parabolic reflector |
| **Range Length Required** | $\ge 2D^{2}/\lambda$ | $\ge 2D^{2}/\lambda$ | $\sim$ 5 – 10 m (independent of $D$) |
| **RFI / Weather Sensitivity** | High (weather, RFI) | Low (shielded) | Low (shielded) |
| **Suitable AUT Size** | Large (aircraft, towers) | Small to medium | Large radar/spacecraft antennas |
| **Cost** | Moderate (land + fencing) | High (room + absorbers) | Very High (precision reflector) |
| **Multipath Suppression** | Good (with fences) | Excellent (40–60 dB absorber) | Excellent (with edge serrations) |
| **Frequency Range** | UHF to microwave | Microwave to mm-wave | Microwave to mm-wave |

**Mark split (b):** Comparison table — 5 marks; Conclusion / selection criterion — 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **CATR** with an ordinary anechoic chamber. Remember: a CATR uses an **offset parabolic reflector** to convert the **spherical wave** from a small feed into a **plane wave** in the quiet zone. The chamber itself is just the housing; the reflector is the *active* element.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Gain, Directivity and Three-Antenna Method

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, ESE Module 2, Choice B]**

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **two-antenna method** for absolute gain measurement. State the assumptions made and derive the working formula.

**(b) (7 marks)** Two identical antennas are used in a transmit–receive link at $R = 4$ m and $f = 3$ GHz. The transmitted power is $P_t = 10$ W and the received power is $P_r = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ W. Calculate the gain of each antenna in dBi. If the HPBW is 30°, estimate the directivity and radiation efficiency.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The two antennas are identical ($G_1 = G_2 = G$).
2. They are **polarisation-matched** (no polarisation loss factor).
3. The propagation is in the **far field** ($R \ge 2D^{2}/\lambda$).
4. The range is **free of multipath reflections** (anechoic or OATS).
5. $P_t$ and $P_r$ are accurately measured (cable and connector losses calibrated out).

**Derivation:**

Starting from Friis transmission equation:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_{t}G_{r}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

With $G_t = G_r = G$:

$$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G^{2}\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^{2}$$

Solving for $G$:

$$G^{2} = \frac{P_r}{P_t}\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,G = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}}\,}$$

**Mark split (a):** Assumptions — 2 marks; Derivation — 4 marks; Final formula — 1 mark.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength:**

$$f = 3\,\text{GHz} \Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3\times 10^{8}}{3\times 10^{9}} = 0.1\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute gain in linear scale:**

$$G = \frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\sqrt{\frac{P_r}{P_t}} = \frac{4\pi (4)}{0.1}\sqrt{\frac{4\times 10^{-4}}{10}}$$

$$G = \frac{50.27}{0.1}\sqrt{4\times 10^{-5}} = 502.7 \times 6.3246\times 10^{-3}$$

$$G = 3.179$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dBi:**

$$G\big[\text{dBi}\big] = 10\log_{10}(3.179) \approx 5.02\,\text{dBi}$$

**Step 4 — Directivity from HPBW:**

Using the pencil-beam formula (HPBW in degrees):

$$D_0 \approx \frac{41253}{\theta_{1}^{2}\cdot\theta_{2}^{2}}$$

For an azimuthally-symmetric pattern, $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 30°$:

$$D_0 = \frac{41253}{30 \times 30} = \frac{41253}{900} = 45.84$$

$$D_0\big[\text{dBi}\big] = 10\log_{10}(45.84) \approx 16.61\,\text{dBi}$$

**Step 5 — Radiation efficiency:**

$$e_{rad} = \frac{G}{D_0} = \frac{3.179}{45.84} = 0.0693$$

$$e_{rad}\big[\% \big] \approx 6.93\,\% \quad\text{or}\quad e_{rad}\big[\text{dB}\big] = 10\log_{10}(0.0693) \approx -11.59\,\text{dB}$$

**Mark split (b):** Gain calculation — 3 marks; Directivity — 2 marks; Efficiency — 2 marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** In the two-antenna method, students frequently forget to take the **square root** of the Friis ratio, leading to a gain overestimate by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ in linear scale (≈ 1.5 dB in dBi). This is the **single most common mistake** in KTU answer sheets.
> **Pitfall #2:** When converting HPBW to directivity, the constant is **41253** (not 4125 or 52525) — use the formula $D_0 = 41253/(\theta_1 \cdot \theta_2)$ with HPBW in **degrees**, or $D_0 = 4/(\theta_1\theta_2)$ with HPBW in **radians**. Mixing the two units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Antenna Measurements (Module 2)**

- **Far-field criterion:** $R \ge 2D^{2}/\lambda$ (or the relaxed $0.6D^{2}/\lambda$ for $D \gg \lambda$); always compute this **first** for any numerical problem.
- **Friis Transmission Equation:** $P_r/P_t = G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ — the **foundation** of all gain and range-loss problems.
- **Gain Transfer Method:** Single known reference horn; ratio of received powers in dB added to reference gain in dB.
- **Two-Antenna Method:** $G = (4\pi R/\lambda)\sqrt{P_r/P_t}$ — assumes two **identical** unknown antennas.
- **Three-Antenna Method:** $G_i = (4\pi R/\lambda)\sqrt{P_{jk}P_{il}/(P_t P_{mn})}$ — no calibration needed; solve three Friis equations.
- **Directivity:** $D_0 = 4\pi/\Omega_A$. For a narrow symmetric beam, $D_0 \approx 4/\theta_0^{2}$ (rad) or $41253/(\theta_1\theta_2)$ (deg).
- **Efficiency:** $e_{rad} = G/D_0 \le 1$ always.
- **Polarisation:** Co-pol, cross-pol, XPD (dB), AR (dB) for circular; **RHCP/LHCP** sense from a spinning-linear or dual-polarised probe.
- **Impedance / VSWR:** $\text{VSWR} = (1+\vert\Gamma\vert)/(1-\vert\Gamma\vert)$; $\text{RL [dB]} = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$. **VSWR 2:1 ↔ RL 9.54 dB** (a KTU-favourite numerical).
- **Test Ranges:**
  - *OATS* — outdoor, simple, weather/RFI sensitive.
  - *Anechoic chamber* — indoor, ferrite + foam pyramid absorbers (40–60 dB reflection suppression).
  - *CATR (Compact Range)* — uses **offset parabolic reflector** to create a plane wave in a short quiet zone; surface RMS error $\le \lambda/100$.
- **Near-Field to Far-Field:** Planar / cylindrical / spherical near-field scans + Fourier (or spherical-wave) transform.
- **KTU Numerical Shortcuts:**
  - $D_0$ (linear) from HPBW: $D_0 \approx 41253 / (\theta_1 \theta_2)$ with $\theta$ in **degrees**.
  - RL = $20\log_{10}[(V+1)/(V-1)]$ where $V$ = VSWR.
  - $\lambda$ in metres = $0.3 / f$ (GHz).
- **Examiner Watch-list:**
  - *Always* state the Fraunhofer distance before claiming a pattern is valid.
  - *Always* specify HPBW in **degrees** when using the 41253 formula.
  - *Never* use a single-antenna absolute gain method — it is impossible without calibration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Antenna Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Antenna Range ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Antenna Range** is a specially designed, controlled test facility (either indoor or outdoor) used to accurately measure the radiation characteristics of an Antenna Under Test (AUT) — such as its **radiation pattern, gain, directivity, polarization, and phase distribution** — under conditions that simulate free-space propagation, while minimizing the influence of unwanted reflected signals, environmental noise, and interference. In formal KTU terminology, an antenna measurement range is defined by the **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) Standard Test Procedures for Antennas (IEEE Std 149-2021)** as: > *"A facility consisting of a transmitting antenna, a receiving antenna (or AUT), instrumentation, and a signal path geometry configured to characterize antenna performance under repeatable, calibrated conditions approximating an ideal unbounded free-space environment."* The two primary classifications are: - **Far-Field Ranges** (Outdoor Elevated Ranges, Slant Ranges, Anechoic Chambers) - **Near-Field Ranges** (Compact Ranges, Spherical/Cylindrical/Planar Near-Field Scanning) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The focus is on *range geometry*, *far-field criterion (Fraunhofer distance)*, *indoor vs outdoor ranges*, *compact ranges (CATR)*, and the **Friis Transmission Equation** as applied to gain and pattern measurement. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in a **circular concert hall** and you want to measure how loud a singer's voice is in every direction around them. If the walls of the hall echo the sound back, your microphone will pick up not just the direct sound but also confusing reflections — your measurement will be wrong. So, what do you do? **Option A:** Take the singer to an **open football stadium** at midnight (no crowds, no walls) — this is analogous to an **Outdoor Antenna Range**. **Option B:** Build a special room whose walls are covered with **pyramid-shaped foam that absorbs all echoes** — this is an **Anechoic Chamber (Indoor Range)**. **Option C:** Use a **giant parabolic mirror** to bend the sound so it appears to come from very far away even in a small room — this is a **Compact Antenna Test Range (CATR)**. In every case, the goal is the same: ensure the **measuring antenna sees only the AUT's direct radiation**, as if both antennas were floating in empty space — an ideal condition called the **Far-Field (Fraunhofer) Region**. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Speed of light | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** | | Free-space impedance | $\eta_0$ | **$120\pi \approx 377 \ \Omega$** | | Maximum allowable phase taper | $\Delta\phi_{max}$ | **$\pi/8$ rad (22.5°)** for high accuracy | | Reflectivity of anechoic chamber | RCS | **$<-40$ dB** (good chamber) | | Minimum range length (Fraunhofer) | $R_{min}$ | **$2D^2/\lambda$** | > [!NOTE] > The choice of **$\pi/8$ radians** as the phase-taper tolerance ensures a maximum amplitude error of about **0.25 dB** in the measured pattern — the standard adopted by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Do We Need an Antenna Range? An antenna is a **reciprocal, passive, linear device** characterized by its behavior in the **far-field** (Fraunhofer) region. To characterize it, we must: 1. Illuminate it with a known uniform plane wave, **OR** 2. Measure the spherical wave it produces at a sufficiently large distance where the wavefront is locally planar. The challenge: in practice, true plane waves exist only at infinite distance. So we must engineer the measurement setup to *approximate* plane-wave illumination within acceptable error bounds. --- ### 2.2 Classification of Antenna Ranges **A. By Geometry** - **Far-Field (Fraunhofer) Ranges:** Direct measurement at $R \geq 2D^2/\lambda$. - **Near-Field Ranges:** Measure fields close to AUT, then mathematically transform to far-field using FFT-based algorithms. - **Compact (CATR) Ranges:** Use a parabolic reflector to collimate spherical waves into plane waves within a short distance. **B. By Environment** - **Outdoor Ranges:** Elevated, slant, or ground-reflection ranges — weather dependent, subject to RFI. - **Indoor Ranges (Anechoic Chambers):** RF absorber-lined rooms — 24/7 operation, secure from interference. --- ### 2.3 The Fraunhofer Distance (Far-Field Criterion) The minimum distance $R_{min}$ beyond which the wavefront from an antenna of largest dimension $D$ can be approximated as a plane wave (with maximum phase error of $\pi/8$ rad): $$R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$ **Why the factor of 2?** Geometrically, the path difference between the wave arriving at the center of the aperture and the wave arriving at the edge equals $\Delta R = D^2/(2R)$. Setting this equal to $\lambda/16$ (which corresponds to $\pi/8$ rad phase): $$\frac{D^2}{2R} = \frac{\lambda}{16} \implies R = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Formula:** For an antenna with $D = 1$ m operating at $f = 10$ GHz ($\lambda = 0.03$ m): > $$R_{min} = \frac{2 \times (1)^2}{0.03} = 66.7 \text{ m}$$ > This shows why far-field measurement of large antennas at high frequencies becomes physically impractical — driving the need for **compact ranges**. --- ### 2.4 Friis Transmission Equation — The Foundation of Range Measurements For two antennas separated by distance $R$ in free space, with transmitted power $P_t$ and received power $P_r$: $$\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$$ where: - $G_t$ = Gain of transmitting antenna - $G_r$ = Gain of receiving antenna - $\lambda$ = Operating wavelength - $R$ = Separation between antennas **Utility in antenna measurement:** - If $G_t$ is known and $P_r$ is measured, $G_r$ of the AUT can be computed. - Forms the basis of the **gain-substitution method**. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | Fraunhofer (far-field) distance | $R$ in m, $D$ in m, $\lambda$ in m | | 2 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{0.5 D^2}{\lambda}$ | Reactive near-field boundary | Begin of radiating near-field | | 3 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda} + D$ | Conservative KTU board range | $D$ added for finite AUT length | | 4 | $\dfrac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | Friis transmission equation | Free-space, polarization-matched | | 5 | $G_{AUT} = G_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{P_{AUT}}{P_{ref}}$ | Gain-substitution method | Same setup, identical range | | 6 | $\Delta R = \dfrac{D^2}{2R}$ | Path-length difference edge-to-center | Determines phase taper | | 7 | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta R$ | Phase error at edge of aperture | Radians | | 8 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Radar cross-section (RCS) link to aperture | For RCS ranges | | 9 | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$ | Free-space path loss | dB | | 10 | $R = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength-to-frequency conversion | $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write $R = D^2/\lambda$. This is the **radiating near-field** to **far-field** transition for looser accuracy. The **board-exam correct** formula is $R = 2D^2/\lambda$. --- ### 2.6 Indoor Anechoic Chamber Design Anechoic chambers are shielded rooms lined with **pyramidal RF absorber foam** (typically carbon-loaded polyurethane) that reflects incoming electromagnetic energy into the foam via multiple internal reflections, achieving reflectivity below **-40 dB** for typical operating frequencies. **Key design parameters:** - **Quiet zone:** The volume within the chamber where reflected energy is below -40 dB; AUT must lie inside. - **Wall reflectivity:** $\Gamma_{wall} = E_{reflected}/E_{incident}$. - **Absorber length:** Typically $L_{abs} \geq \lambda/2$ at the lowest operating frequency. --- ### 2.7 Outdoor Range Considerations - **Elevated range:** Both antennas mounted on towers to avoid ground bounce; minimum tower height $\approx \lambda$. - **Slant range:** Antennas at different heights creating a tilted path to minimize ground reflection. - **Ground-reflection range:** Deliberately uses ground bounce (e.g., for measuring low-side-lobe patterns). **Ground-bounce path difference:** $$\Delta R = h_t h_r \cdot \frac{2}{\lambda R}$$ (approx. for short heights) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Fraunhofer Distance $R_{min} = 2D^2/\lambda$ **Setup:** A linear aperture antenna of length $D$ is transmitting. Consider two rays from the aperture: - Ray 1: from the **center** of the aperture to the observation point at distance $R$. - Ray 2: from the **edge** of the aperture to the same observation point. **Step 1 — Geometry of path difference:** The line-of-sight distance from the edge to the observation point, $R_e$, by the Pythagorean theorem: $$R_e = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2}$$ **Step 2 — Path difference $\Delta R$:** $$\Delta R = R_e - R = \sqrt{R^2 + \frac{D^2}{4}} - R$$ **Step 3 — Binomial expansion for $R \gg D$:** Using the binomial identity $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + \dfrac{x}{2} - \dfrac{x^2}{8} + \dots$ for small $x$: $$\sqrt{R^2 + \frac{D^2}{4}} = R \sqrt{1 + \frac{D^2}{4R^2}}$$ $$\Delta R = R\left(1 + \frac{D^2}{8R^2} - 1\right) = \frac{D^2}{8R}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the maximum allowable phase-error criterion:** For the Fraunhofer approximation, the maximum tolerable phase difference between the edge and the center rays is $\Delta\phi_{max} = \pi/8$ rad: $$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta R = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{D^2}{8R} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R}$$ **Step 5 — Equate to $\pi/8$ and solve:** $$\frac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R} = \frac{\pi}{8}$$ $$\frac{D^2}{4\lambda R} = \frac{1}{8}$$ $$R = \frac{8 \cdot D^2}{4\lambda} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$ $$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Friis Transmission Equation **Step 1 — Isotropic transmitter assumption:** A hypothetical isotropic radiator emits total power $P_t$ uniformly. The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) at distance $R$: $$S_{iso} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 2 — Directional antenna gain:** A real antenna with gain $G_t$ concentrates power in a specific direction, increasing flux density by factor $G_t$: $$S = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 3 — Effective aperture of receiving antenna:** The receiving antenna intercepts power over its effective area $A_e$: $$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \, G_t \, A_e}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the antenna-aperture relation:** From antenna theory, $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$: $$P_r = \frac{P_t \, G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange:** $$\boxed{\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2}$$ **Step 6 — Express in decibels (dB form):** $$P_r(dBm) = P_t(dBm) + G_t(dBi) + G_r(dBi) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example — Minimum Range Length Calculation **Problem:** A parabolic dish antenna has diameter $D = 1.5$ m and operates at $f = 5.8$ GHz. Compute: 1. The Fraunhofer distance $R_{min}$. 2. The free-space path loss at this distance. 3. The required phase-taper-free separation for a $\pi/16$ criterion. **Step 1 — Compute wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{5.8 \times 10^9} = 0.0517 \text{ m} = 5.17 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 2 — Fraunhofer distance:** $$R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (1.5)^2}{0.0517} = \frac{4.5}{0.0517} \approx 87.04 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Free-space path loss:** $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 87.04}{0.0517}\right)$$ $$\frac{4\pi \times 87.04}{0.0517} = \frac{1093.69}{0.0517} \approx 21154.6$$ $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(21154.6) = 20 \times 4.325 = 86.5 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Distance for $\pi/16$ criterion (tighter tolerance):** For $\Delta\phi = \pi/16$: $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R} = \dfrac{\pi}{16}$ $$R = \frac{4D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \times 2.25}{0.0517} \approx 174.1 \text{ m}$$ This is exactly **double** the Fraunhofer distance — confirming the trade-off between accuracy and physical range length. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Range Types | Range Type | Geometry | Typical $D$ | Typical $R$ | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Outdoor Elevated** | Horizontal, both antennas on towers | Large ($>10\lambda$) | $2D^2/\lambda$ | High accuracy, large AUTs | Weather, RFI, expensive land | | **Anechoic Chamber** | Indoor, RF absorber lined | Small to medium | $2D^2/\lambda$ | All-weather, secure | Limited AUT size, expensive foam | | **Compact (CATR)** | Parabolic reflector collimator | Medium to large | $\sim$10 m fixed | Compact, far-field in small space | Edge diffraction, quiet zone limit | | **Near-Field Planar** | AUT scanned over planar grid | Any | $\sim 3\lambda$–$5\lambda$ | No reflector needed, very accurate | Time-consuming, complex math | | **Near-Field Spherical** | AUT on rotating positioner | Any | $\sim 3\lambda$–$5\lambda$ | Full-sphere coverage | Slow, requires spherical transform | | **Near-Field Cylindrical** | AUT scanned along cylinder | Long antennas | $\sim 3\lambda$–$5\lambda$ | Good for fan-beam antennas | Limited azimuth coverage | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Antenna Measurement Range ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeS[Signal Source
Synthesized CW or Swept] nodeT[Transmitter Feed
Power Amplifier and Filter] nodeTA[Transmitting Antenna
Known Gain Gt] nodeFS[Free Space Propagation
Far Field Region 2D squared by lambda] nodeAUT[Antenna Under Test
AUT on Positioner] nodeR[Receiver Feed
LNA and Downconverter] nodeSP[Spectrum Analyzer
or Network Analyzer] nodePC[Data Acquisition
and Post Processing] nodeCAL[Calibration Stage
Reference Horn Antenna] nodeS --> nodeT --> nodeTA nodeTA --> nodeFS nodeFS --> nodeAUT nodeAUT --> nodeR --> nodeSP --> nodePC nodeCAL -. replaces AUT for gain calibration .-> nodeAUT nodePC --> nodeOut[Pattern Gain Phase
Polarization Reports] classDef src fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px classDef ant fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px classDef proc fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px class nodeS,nodeT,nodeR,nodeSP src class nodeTA,nodeAUT,nodeCAL ant class nodeFS,nodePC,nodeOut proc ``` --- ### 4.2 Compact Antenna Test Range (CATR) Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SourceEnd["Feed End"] nodeF[Feed Antenna
Small Horn or Open Ended Waveguide] end subgraph Collimator["Main Reflector Assembly"] nodeR1[Parabolic Reflector
Offset Fed Geometry] nodeR2[Sub Reflector
for Double Bounce CATR] end subgraph QuietZone["Quiet Zone Region"] nodeQZ[Plane Wave Zone
Amplitude Taper less than 0.5 dB
Phase Taper less than pi by 8] nodeAUT2[AUT Mounted on
Azimuth Over Elevation Positioner] end nodeF -- Spherical Wave --> nodeR1 nodeR1 -- Collimated Plane Wave --> nodeQZ nodeQZ --> nodeAUT2 nodeAUT2 -. Received Power Fed to .-> nodeRec[Receiver and Recorder] classDef quiet fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px class nodeQZ,nodeAUT2 quiet ``` --- ### 4.3 Decision Flow for Range Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Start AUT Measurement] --> nodeB{Antenna Size D} nodeB -- D less than 50 lambda --> nodeC[Use Indoor Anechoic Chamber] nodeB -- D greater than 50 lambda --> nodeD{Facility Available} nodeD -- Outdoor Available --> nodeE[Use Elevated Far Field Range] nodeD -- Indoor Only --> nodeF[Use CATR Compact Range] nodeC --> nodeG{Measurement Goal} nodeE --> nodeG nodeF --> nodeG nodeG -- Full 3D Pattern --> nodeH[Spherical Near Field] nodeG -- 2D Cut Only --> nodeI[Far Field or Planar Near Field] nodeG -- RCS and Gain --> nodeJ[Far Field or CATR] classDef gate fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px class nodeB,nodeD,nodeG gate ``` --- ### 4.4 Annotated Range Geometry (ASCII Schematic) ``` R = 2D^2/lambda <-----------------------> [AUT] [TX Antenna] | | / \ | | D / Known \ | | / Gain Gt \ ---- ---- ---- Phase Center ray: R Reference Edge ray: R + D^2 / (8R) ------------------------------------------------ GROUND (Elevated Range - no reflection) ------------------------------------------------ ``` > [!NOTE] > The edge ray travels an extra distance $\Delta R = D^2/(8R)$ relative to the center ray. If $R = 2D^2/\lambda$, then $\Delta R = \lambda/16$, producing a phase shift of $\pi/8$ rad — the boundary of the Fraunhofer region. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the **Fraunhofer distance** of an antenna. Why is it important in antenna range measurements? **Model Answer:** The **Fraunhofer distance**, also known as the **far-field distance** or **minimum range length**, is the minimum separation between an antenna under test (AUT) and a measuring antenna beyond which the radiated wavefront can be approximated as a uniform plane wave with a maximum phase error of $\pi/8$ radians. Mathematically: $$R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$ where $D$ is the largest dimension of the AUT and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength. **Importance in range measurements:** 1. Ensures the measurement is performed in the **far-field (Fraunhofer) region** where the antenna's radiation pattern is fully formed. 2. Guarantees that the angular variation of the field with distance is negligible, so $1/r$ decay dominates. 3. Avoids errors due to **quadratic phase taper** across the aperture, which would distort the measured pattern. 4. Defines the **physical minimum length** of an antenna range, which is critical for facility design. (3 marks — *Awarded by KTU Examiners*) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between **indoor anechoic chambers** and **outdoor antenna ranges**. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Indoor Anechoic Chamber | Outdoor Antenna Range | |---|---|---| | **Environment** | RF-shielded room lined with pyramidal absorber foam | Open-air elevated test facility | | **Weather dependency** | None — 24/7 operation | Affected by rain, wind, temperature | | **RFI immunity** | High (shielded walls) | Susceptible to TV, cellular, radar interference | | **Maximum AUT size** | Limited by chamber dimensions | Can be very large | | **Cost** | Very high (anechoic foam + shielding) | High (land + towers) but lower than chamber | | **Ground bounce** | Eliminated by absorber walls | Requires elevated towers to avoid reflection | | **Typical accuracy** | $\pm 0.1$ dB | $\pm 0.3$ to $0.5$ dB | (3 marks) --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Fraunhofer distance** of an antenna. State clearly the assumption regarding maximum allowable phase error. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A horn antenna of aperture $D = 0.8$ m operates at $6$ GHz. Compute the minimum range length for far-field measurement and the corresponding free-space path loss. If the measurement tolerance is tightened to $\pi/16$ rad, what is the new range length? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Geometry:** Consider a linear aperture of length $D$. Two rays — center ray and edge ray — travel to a point at distance $R$. The edge-ray distance is: $$R_e = \sqrt{R^2 + \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2}$$ **Step 2 — Path difference:** $$\Delta R = R_e - R = \sqrt{R^2 + \frac{D^2}{4}} - R$$ **Step 3 — Binomial approximation** ($R \gg D$): $$\Delta R \approx \frac{D^2}{8R}$$ **Step 4 — Phase error:** $$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta R = \frac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R}$$ **Step 5 — Apply maximum allowable phase error $\pi/8$ rad:** $$\frac{\pi D^2}{4\lambda R} = \frac{\pi}{8} \implies R = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$ The **Fraunhofer distance** is therefore: $$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}}$$ **Assumption:** Maximum quadratic phase error across the aperture is $\pi/8$ rad (equivalent to $\lambda/16$ path-length error), producing $\leq 0.25$ dB amplitude error in the measured pattern. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 — Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 2 — Fraunhofer distance:** $$R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (0.8)^2}{0.05} = \frac{1.28}{0.05} = 25.6 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Free-space path loss at $R = 25.6$ m:** $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 25.6}{0.05}\right)$$ $$= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{321.7}{0.05}\right) = 20\log_{10}(6434) = 20 \times 3.808 = 76.17 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Range length for $\pi/16$ rad criterion:** For $\Delta\phi = \pi/16$ rad, the formula becomes $R = \dfrac{4D^2}{\lambda}$ (twice the Fraunhofer distance): $$R_{new} = \frac{4D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \times 0.64}{0.05} = 51.2 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5 — New FSPL:** $$FSPL_{new} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 51.2}{0.05}\right) = 20\log_{10}(12868) = 82.19 \text{ dB}$$ **Final answers:** - $R_{min} = 25.6$ m, $FSPL = 76.17$ dB - $R_{new} = 51.2$ m, $FSPL_{new} = 82.19$ dB --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **Friis transmission-based gain measurement** technique using the substitution method. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two horn antennas of gain $12$ dBi each are separated by $R = 10$ m at $f = 10$ GHz. The transmitted power is $P_t = 10$ dBm and the received power is $P_r = -42$ dBm. Determine whether the measurement satisfies the Fraunhofer condition (assume $D = 0.15$ m for the horn). If not, suggest a corrective method. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) **Step 1 — Concept:** The substitution method uses a reference antenna of known gain to determine the gain of the AUT. The technique requires two measurements with identical geometry — first with the reference antenna, then with the AUT — eliminating the need to know the exact transmitted power, range distance, or frequency precisely. **Step 2 — Block diagram (functional architecture):** ``` [Signal Source] → [Transmitting Horn (fixed)] → [Far-Field Path] → [Reference Antenna] → [Power Meter] (P_ref) | Replace with AUT | (P_AUT) ``` **Step 3 — First measurement with reference antenna:** $$P_{ref} = P_t + G_t + G_{ref} - FSPL \quad \text{(all in dB)}$$ **Step 4 — Second measurement with AUT (same $P_t$, $G_t$, and $R$):** $$P_{AUT} = P_t + G_t + G_{AUT} - FSPL$$ **Step 5 — Subtraction eliminates $P_t$, $G_t$, and $FSPL$:** $$P_{AUT} - P_{ref} = G_{AUT} - G_{ref}$$ $$\boxed{G_{AUT}(dBi) = G_{ref}(dBi) + \left[P_{AUT}(dBm) - P_{ref}(dBm)\right]}$$ **Step 6 — Validity conditions:** - Range must satisfy $R \geq 2D_{max}^2/\lambda$. - Polarization alignment between transmit and receive antennas. - No obstruction or multipath in the propagation path. --- #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1 — Given data:** - $G_t = G_r = 12$ dBi - $R = 10$ m - $f = 10$ GHz $\implies \lambda = 0.03$ m - $P_t = 10$ dBm, $P_r = -42$ dBm - $D = 0.15$ m **Step 2 — Compute Fraunhofer distance:** $$R_{min} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times (0.15)^2}{0.03} = \frac{0.045}{0.03} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$ Since $R = 10 \text{ m} > R_{min} = 1.5 \text{ m}$, **the Fraunhofer condition is satisfied** with significant margin. **Step 3 — Verify using Friis equation:** $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \times 10}{0.03}\right) = 20\log_{10}(4188.8) = 72.44 \text{ dB}$$ $$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL = 10 + 12 + 12 - 72.44 = -38.44 \text{ dBm}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the discrepancy:** - Predicted: $-38.44$ dBm - Measured: $-42$ dBm - Difference: $3.56$ dB — likely due to **polarization mismatch, misalignment, or cable losses**. **Step 5 — Corrective methods:** 1. Re-align polarization axes (rotate horns to maximize received signal). 2. Use precision laser alignment to center antennas on boresight. 3. Measure and subtract cable losses via calibration (VNA calibration kit). 4. Use a higher gain reference horn to boost signal-to-noise ratio. **Step 6 — Confirm Fraunhofer status:** $$\boxed{R = 10 \text{ m} \gg R_{min} = 1.5 \text{ m} \implies \text{Fraunhofer condition SATISFIED}}$$ The discrepancy is therefore **NOT** due to range geometry. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Confusing $D^2/\lambda$ with $2D^2/\lambda$:** Many students write the radiating-near-field boundary instead of Fraunhofer distance. The board-approved formula is $R = 2D^2/\lambda$. > 2. **Forgetting units:** When $D$ is given in cm and $\lambda$ in m, students forget to convert — always normalize units before substitution. > 3. **Misapplying Friis:** Polarization mismatch factor $\vert \hat{\rho}_t \cdot \hat{\rho}_r^* \vert^2$ is omitted — for a 14-mark question, include this term or state the assumption. > 4. **Skipping the calibration step:** In substitution method, examiners expect explicit mention that $P_t$, $G_t$, and $R$ are **identical** in both measurements. > 5. **Indoor chamber without "anechoic":** Simply writing "indoor room" loses marks; the correct term is **anechoic chamber with RF absorber lining**. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Fraunhofer distance (range length):** $R_{min} = 2D^2/\lambda$ — phase error bounded at $\pi/8$ rad. - **Reactive near-field boundary:** $R = 0.5\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ (less commonly needed). - **Friis equation (dB form):** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - 20\log_{10}(4\pi R/\lambda)$. - **Gain substitution method:** $G_{AUT} = G_{ref} + (P_{AUT} - P_{ref})$ in dB. - **Free-space path loss:** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(4\pi R/\lambda)$ in dB. - **Three classes of ranges:** Far-field, Compact (CATR), Near-field. - **Anechoic chamber:** Indoor, RF absorber, reflectivity < -40 dB. - **Outdoor elevated range:** Towers, weather-dependent, large AUTs. - **CATR:** Parabolic reflector converts spherical to plane wave — compact far-field. - **Minimum range length is conservative:** Often engineers add factor of 1.5–2 for safety. - **Phase-error criteria:** $\pi/8$ rad (Fraunhofer), $\pi/16$ rad (high accuracy), $\pi/32$ rad (precision). - **KTU commonly tested frequency:** $f = 1, 5.8, 10, 12, 15$ GHz. - **Wavelength conversion:** $\lambda(\text{m}) = 300/f(\text{MHz})$. - **Required tower height (outdoor):** $\geq \lambda$ to avoid ground reflection null at boresight. - **Polarization purity:** Must be matched; cross-polarization isolation > 25 dB recommended.

topics.title: Radiation Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **RADIATION PATTERN (KTU Formal Definition):**
> The **radiation pattern** of an antenna is defined as a *mathematical function or graphical representation of the radiation properties of the antenna as a function of space coordinates* $(\theta, \phi)$. In most cases, the radiation pattern is determined in the **far-field (Fraunhofer) region** and is represented as a function of the directional coordinates $\theta$ (elevation, measured from the $+z$ axis) and $\phi$ (azimuth, measured from the $+x$ axis in the $xy$-plane).

More explicitly, the radiation pattern is the spatial distribution of one (or both) of the following observable quantities:

- **Normalized (or absolute) power flux density** $\mathbf{S}(\theta,\phi)$ — measured in $\mathrm{W/m^2}$
- **Radiation intensity** $U(\theta,\phi)$ — measured in $\mathrm{W/(unit\ solid\ angle)}$

In KTU board examinations, the radiation pattern is conventionally plotted in **dB scale** as:
$$
P_{dB}(\theta,\phi) = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{max}}\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY DISTINCTION FOR KTU EXAMS:**
> A *radiation pattern plot* is essentially a 3-D surface in spherical coordinates. The **principal E-plane pattern** is the plot of the radiation in the plane containing the electric-field vector and the direction of maximum radiation. The **principal H-plane pattern** is the plot in the plane containing the magnetic-field vector and the direction of maximum radiation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lighthouse + Flashlight" Model

Imagine standing inside a lighthouse at night. You are holding a flashlight in one hand and a bare incandescent bulb in the other:

| Object | Analogy to Antenna Concept |
|---|---|
| Bare bulb (radiates uniformly in all directions) | **Isotropic radiator** — the theoretical reference with $U(\theta,\phi)=\text{constant}$ |
| Flashlight (radiates strongly in one direction, weakly elsewhere) | **Directional antenna** — energy is "sculpted" into preferred directions |
| Cone of light coming out of the flashlight | **Main lobe** of the radiation pattern |
| Light bleeding around the rim of the flashlight | **Side lobes** and **back lobe** |
| Region of complete darkness behind | **Nulls** of the pattern |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** Your Wi-Fi router antenna at home is a *directional* antenna. It concentrates RF energy horizontally toward your laptop rather than wasting it toward the ceiling or floor. This "concentration of energy" is exactly what a radiation pattern describes — where, in space, the antenna *chooses* to deliver its power.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

The following **standard metrics** are universally used by KTU examiners when evaluating radiation pattern problems:

- **Beam Solid Angle** $\Omega_A$ — measured in **steradians (sr)**
- **Directivity** $D$ — **dimensionless**, expressed in **dBi** when referenced to isotropic
- **Half-Power Beam Width (HPBW)** — measured in **degrees (°)**
- **First-Null Beam Width (FNBW)** — measured in **degrees (°)**
- **Front-to-Back Ratio (FTBR)** — measured in **dB**
- **Side Lobe Level (SLL)** — measured in **dB** (negative value)
- **Beam Efficiency** $\varepsilon_M$ — **dimensionless ratio** between 0 and 1

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 of PEECT865, students must be able to (i) draw and interpret radiation patterns in both **principal planes (E-plane and H-plane)**, (ii) identify **major lobes, minor lobes, side lobes, and nulls**, (iii) compute **beamwidths**, and (iv) relate **HPBW to directivity** through standard empirical relations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coordinate Framework

The radiation pattern is universally expressed in a **spherical coordinate system** $(r, \theta, \phi)$ centred on the antenna phase centre. The unit vector $\hat{r}$ points from the antenna to the observation point. Two angles uniquely fix the direction:

$$
\hat{r} = \sin\theta\cos\phi\,\hat{x} + \sin\theta\sin\phi\,\hat{y} + \cos\theta\,\hat{z}
$$

## 2.2 Types of Radiation Pattern Representations

There are **three standard representations** accepted in KTU exam answers:

1. **3-D Pattern** — Surface plot of $U(\theta,\phi)$ or $|F(\theta,\phi)|^2$
2. **2-D Principal-Plane Cuts** — E-plane (typically $\phi = 0°$ for a $z$-oriented dipole) and H-plane (typically $\phi = 90°$)
3. **Polar Plot vs. Rectangular Plot** — Polar plots are visually intuitive; rectangular (Cartesian) plots with dB scale are used for quantitative measurement of beamwidths and side-lobe levels.

## 2.3 Logical Breakdown of the Pattern Structure

Step 1 — **Identify the Reference:** Every antenna pattern is normalized to its **maximum value** $U_{max}$.
Step 2 — **Locate the Main Lobe:** The lobe containing the direction of **maximum radiation** ($U_{max}$).
Step 3 — **Identify Side Lobes:** All lobes **other than the main lobe**.
Step 4 — **Identify Back Lobe:** The lobe pointing in the **opposite direction** ($\theta = 180°$ generally).
Step 5 — **Locate Nulls:** Directions where the pattern value equals **zero** (theoretically infinite dB suppression).
Step 6 — **Quantify Beamwidths:** HPBW = angular separation between **$-3$ dB points**; FNBW = angular separation between **first nulls** flanking the main lobe.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:** Normalization removes dependence on transmit power and range, leaving only the *shape* of the pattern (a property of the antenna geometry alone). Main-lobe identification guides where to point the antenna. Nulls are exploited for interference rejection (e.g., pointing a satellite dish null toward an adjacent satellite).

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THESE EQUATIONS — they appear in nearly every KTU board question on radiation patterns.**

$$
\Omega_A = \iint_{4\pi} F_n(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega \quad [\text{steradians}]
$$

$$
U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{r^2}{2\,\eta}\,\left[\vert E_\theta\vert^2 + \vert E_\phi\vert^2\right] \quad [\text{W/sr}]
$$

$$
P_{rad} = \oint_{\Omega} U(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega = \iint_{4\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi \quad [\text{W}]
$$

$$
D = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi\,U_{max}}{P_{rad}} \quad [\text{dimensionless}]
$$

$$
D_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(D) \quad [\text{dBi}]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_M = \frac{\Omega_M}{\Omega_A} \quad [\text{dimensionless, }0 \le \varepsilon_M \le 1]
$$

$$
\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{41{,}000}{\Omega_A} \quad [\text{degrees}]
$$

$$
\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{30{,}000}{D} \quad [\text{degrees, for } D \ge 30 \text{ or so}]
$$

$$
\text{SLL (dB)} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{U_{sl}}{U_{max}}\right]
$$

$$
\text{FTBR (dB)} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left[\frac{U_{max}}{U_{back}}\right]
$$

### Summary Table — Radiation Pattern Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Defining Equation | Unit | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Radiation Intensity | $U(\theta,\phi)$ | $U = r^2 S_r$ | W/sr | Power per unit solid angle |
| Power Flux Density | $S_r$ | $\tfrac{1}{2\eta}\vert E\vert^2$ | W/m² | Far-field Poynting vector |
| Beam Solid Angle | $\Omega_A$ | $\iint F_n d\Omega$ | sr | "Effective" angular spread |
| Directivity | $D$ | $\tfrac{4\pi U_{max}}{P_{rad}}$ | dBi | Gain over isotropic |
| HPBW | $\Theta_{h}$ | $-3$ dB width of main lobe | degrees | Angular resolution |
| FNBW | $\Theta_{n}$ | First-null-to-first-null width | degrees | Main lobe extent |
| SLL | — | $\vert U_{sl}/U_{max}\vert$ in dB | dB | Stray-radiation indicator |
| FTBR | — | $\vert U_{max}/U_{back}\vert$ in dB | dB | Back-radiation indicator |
| Beam Efficiency | $\varepsilon_M$ | $\Omega_M / \Omega_A$ | ratio | Main-lobe fraction |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Communications:** The HPBW of a ground-station dish determines how precisely it must point toward a geostationary satellite (typical values: 0.1°–2°).
- **Radar:** A narrow main lobe gives fine angular resolution for target discrimination. Side-lobe level dictates radar's susceptibility to jamming.
- **5G Base Stations:** Sectoral antennas use shaped radiation patterns (typically 65° or 90° HPBW) to cover assigned azimuthal cells.
- **Radio Astronomy:** Ultra-low side-lobe levels (e.g., $-40$ dB) are critical to detect faint cosmic sources without contamination from terrestrial interference.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Radiated Field to Normalized Power Pattern

**Given:** In the far-field region, the electric field of any antenna can be written as
$$
\mathbf{E}(r,\theta,\phi) = \frac{e^{-jkr}}{r}\,\big[\hat{\theta}\,F_\theta(\theta,\phi) + \hat{\phi}\,F_\phi(\theta,\phi)\big]
$$

where $F_\theta$ and $F_\phi$ are complex *pattern functions* (functions of direction only, not range).

**Goal:** Derive the **normalized power pattern** $F_n(\theta,\phi)$ that KTU examiners expect in answer scripts.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the radial Poynting vector magnitude.**

Using $\eta = \sqrt{\mu/\varepsilon}$ as the intrinsic impedance of free space ($120\pi\ \Omega$ in vacuum), the time-average Poynting vector in the far field is
$$
\langle \mathbf{S} \rangle = \frac{\hat{r}}{2\eta}\,\big[\vert E_\theta\vert^2 + \vert E_\phi\vert^2\big]
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises because we use phasor (peak) representations and time-average a sinusoidal signal — average of $\cos^2$ is $\tfrac{1}{2}$.

---

**Step 2 — Express the magnitude of the radial Poynting vector.**

Since $\hat{r}\cdot\hat{r}=1$ and the cross terms vanish (orthogonality of $\hat{\theta},\hat{\phi}$), the magnitude simplifies to
$$
S_r(r,\theta,\phi) = \frac{1}{2\eta r^2}\,\big[\vert F_\theta(\theta,\phi)\vert^2 + \vert F_\phi(\theta,\phi)\vert^2\big]
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We replaced each $E_i$ by $F_i/r$ because the $e^{-jkr}/r$ factor outside gives $\vert e^{-jkr}\vert/r = 1/r$ after taking the magnitude squared.

---

**Step 3 — Define radiation intensity.**

By definition,
$$
U(\theta,\phi) \equiv r^2\,S_r(r,\theta,\phi)
$$

Multiplying $S_r$ by $r^2$ eliminates the range dependence:
$$
U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{1}{2\eta}\,\big[\vert F_\theta\vert^2 + \vert F_\phi\vert^2\big]
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the *power radiated per unit solid angle*. Crucially, $U$ does **not** depend on $r$ — it depends only on direction. That is why radiation patterns are direction-only functions.

---

**Step 4 — Introduce the normalised pattern function.**

Define the **normalised radiation intensity**:
$$
F_n(\theta,\phi) \equiv \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{max}} = \frac{S_r(r,\theta,\phi)}{S_{r,max}(r,\theta,\phi)}
$$

with the constraint
$$
0 \le F_n(\theta,\phi) \le 1
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $F_n$ is the ratio of intensity in direction $(\theta,\phi)$ to the *peak* intensity. Hence $F_n$ is dimensionless and reaches unity at the main-lobe peak.

---

**Step 5 — Convert to dB scale.**

The KTU board-standard dB representation is:
$$
P_{dB}(\theta,\phi) = 10\,\log_{10}\!\big[F_n(\theta,\phi)\big]
$$

At the peak, $F_n=1$, so $P_{dB}=0$ dB. The $-3$ dB contour is the set of directions where $F_n=0.5$ — this defines the **half-power beam width**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Beam Solid Angle and Directivity

**Given:** $F_n(\theta,\phi)$ is the normalized pattern.

**Goal:** Show that $D = 4\pi/\Omega_A$.

---

**Step 1 — Express total radiated power using $F_n$.**

$$
P_{rad} = \oint_{4\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega = U_{max}\oint_{4\pi} F_n(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of beam solid angle.**

By definition of the beam solid angle,
$$
\Omega_A \equiv \oint_{4\pi} F_n(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega
$$

Therefore
$$
P_{rad} = U_{max}\,\Omega_A
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the directivity formula.**

Directivity was defined earlier as
$$
D = \frac{4\pi\,U_{max}}{P_{rad}}
$$

Substituting $P_{rad} = U_{max}\Omega_A$:
$$
D = \frac{4\pi\,U_{max}}{U_{max}\,\Omega_A}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
\boxed{\,D = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A}\,}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This result shows that **directivity is purely geometric** — a tighter beam (smaller $\Omega_A$) yields a higher $D$. An isotropic antenna has $\Omega_A = 4\pi$ sr, giving $D = 1$ (0 dBi), as expected.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — HPBW from Beam Solid Angle

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Example (KTU board style):**
> An antenna has a main lobe that is well approximated by a circular cone of half-angle $\theta_0 = 30°$, with negligible radiation elsewhere. Compute (a) $\Omega_A$, (b) $D$, (c) HPBW.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the beam solid angle.**

For a circular cone of half-angle $\theta_0$, the beam solid angle is
$$
\Omega_A = 2\pi(1 - \cos\theta_0)
$$

Substituting $\theta_0 = 30°$:
$$
\cos(30°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.8660
$$

$$
\Omega_A = 2\pi\,(1 - 0.8660) = 2\pi\,(0.1340) \approx 0.8419 \text{ sr}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Geometric derivation: a cone of half-angle $\theta_0$ subtends a solid angle $2\pi(1-\cos\theta_0)$. The whole sphere corresponds to $\theta_0=180°$, giving $2\pi(1-(-1))=4\pi$ ✓.

---

**Step 2 — Compute directivity.**

$$
D = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_A} = \frac{4\pi}{0.8419} = \frac{12.566}{0.8419} \approx 14.93
$$

In dBi:
$$
D_{dB} = 10\,\log_{10}(14.93) \approx 11.74\ \text{dBi}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute HPBW.**

For a circular main lobe, HPBW $\approx \theta_0 \times 2 = 60°$ approximately. A more accurate empirical estimate is
$$
\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{41{,}000}{\Omega_A \text{ in deg}^2}
$$

Convert $\Omega_A$ to square degrees:
$$
\Omega_A \text{ [deg}^2] = 0.8419 \times \left(\frac{180}{\pi}\right)^2 = 0.8419 \times 3282.8 \approx 2763.9\ \text{deg}^2
$$

$$
\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{41{,}000}{2763.9} \approx 14.83°
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the discrepancy:** The first method (geometric cone) gives HPBW = 60° because we used *half-angle*; the second gives the *empirical estimate of HPBW* assuming a smooth main-lobe shape, which is different. In KTU answers, students must specify whether the question asks for "cone half-angle" or "HPBW between $-3$ dB points".

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Radiation Pattern

The following block diagram maps out how a radiation pattern is constructed from antenna geometry and how each lobe/null is identified.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Antenna Geometry and Excitation] --> B[Far Field Electric Field E_theta E_phi]
    B --> C[Compute Radial Poynting Vector S_r]
    C --> D[Derive Radiation Intensity U theta phi]
    D --> E[Find Peak Direction U_max]
    E --> F[Form Normalized Pattern F_n = U / U_max]
    F --> G{Choose Representation}
    G --> G1[3D Spherical Surface Plot]
    G --> G2[2D E Plane Cut]
    G --> G3[2D H Plane Cut]
    G1 --> H[Identify Lobes and Nulls]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> H1[Main Lobe Direction]
    H --> H2[Side Lobes]
    H --> H3[Back Lobe]
    H --> H4[Nulls]
    H1 --> I[Measure HPBW and FNBW]
    H2 --> J[Compute Side Lobe Level SLL]
    H3 --> K[Compute Front to Back Ratio]
    H4 --> L[Mark Null Directions for Interference Rejection]
    I --> M[Beam Efficiency and Directivity]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Antenna Field Regions

The radiation pattern is only defined in the far field. The diagram below shows how the field regions are partitioned around an antenna of largest dimension $D$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Antenna of Largest Dimension D] --> B[Reactive Near Field Region]
    A --> C[Radiating Near Field Fresnel Region]
    A --> D[Far Field Fraunhofer Region]
    B --> B1[Range: 0 to 0.62 times sqrt of D cubed over lambda]
    C --> C1[Range: 0.62 times sqrt of D cubed over lambda to 2D squared over lambda]
    D --> D1[Range: greater than 2D squared over lambda]
    D1 --> E[Radiation Pattern is Defined Here]
    E --> F[Power Density falls as 1 over r squared]
    E --> G[E and H fields are perpendicular to r and to each other]
    E --> H[Angular Field Distribution is Independent of r]
    B1 --> X[Energy Stored in Reactive Fields]
    C1 --> Y[Radiated Field Dominates but Pattern Still Varies with r]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Lobe Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Normalized Radiation Pattern F_n theta phi] --> M{Main Lobe}
    P --> S{Side Lobes}
    P --> BL{Back Lobe}
    P --> N{Nulls}
    M --> M1[Contains Direction of Maximum Radiation]
    M --> M2[HPBW measured at minus 3 dB points]
    M --> M3[FNBW measured at first nulls]
    S --> S1[Adjacent Lobes Outside Main Lobe]
    S --> S2[SLL measured in dB below main peak]
    BL --> BL1[Opposite Hemisphere to Main Beam]
    BL --> BL2[FTBR measured in dB]
    N --> N1[Directions of Zero Radiation]
    N --> N2[Exploited for Null Steering in Adaptive Antennas]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define radiation pattern. Distinguish between E-plane and H-plane patterns. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The radiation pattern of an antenna is a graphical/mathematical representation of the radiation properties of the antenna as a function of space coordinates. It is typically a plot of the normalized radiation intensity $F_n(\theta,\phi)$ or the field amplitude $\vert E(\theta,\phi)\vert$ at a fixed radius in the far-field region.

**E-plane vs H-plane (1 mark):**
- **E-plane:** The plane containing the **electric-field vector** $\mathbf{E}$ and the direction of maximum radiation. For a $z$-directed half-wave dipole, the E-plane is the $xz$-plane ($\phi = 0°$).
