- **H-plane:** The plane containing the **magnetic-field vector** $\mathbf{H}$ and the direction of maximum radiation. It is always perpendicular to the E-plane. For the same dipole, the H-plane is the $xy$-plane ($\theta = 90°$).

---

### **Q2. Define HPBW and FNBW. State the empirical relation connecting HPBW to beam solid angle. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**HPBW (1 mark):** The **Half-Power Beam Width** is the angular separation between two directions in the principal plane on either side of the main-lobe peak, where the radiation intensity is exactly **half** its maximum value (i.e., $-3$ dB points).

**FNBW (1 mark):** The **First-Null Beam Width** is the angular separation between the **first nulls** (directions of zero radiation) on either side of the main-lobe peak.

**Empirical Relation (1 mark):**
$$
\text{HPBW (deg)} \approx \frac{41{,}000}{\Omega_A \,\big(\text{deg}^2\big)}
$$
where $\Omega_A$ is the beam solid angle.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Internal Choice Rule:** The university exam offers Module-internal choice. Below, **Question A** focuses on pattern parameters and directivity; **Question B** focuses on field-region classification and pattern construction. Attempt **either** option.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Pattern Parameters and Directivity Computation**

**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]`**

**(a)** Define the following radiation-pattern parameters with units: (i) Beam Solid Angle, (ii) Directivity, (iii) Side Lobe Level, (iv) Beam Efficiency, (v) Front-to-Back Ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An antenna radiates a total power of $P_{rad} = 100$ W isotropically. The peak radiation intensity is $U_{max} = 500$ W/sr. Compute (i) Directivity $D$ in linear and dBi form, (ii) Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$, (iii) HPBW using the empirical relation, (iv) Power radiated within a $60°$ cone around the main-lobe axis if the pattern inside the cone is uniform. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question A**

**Part (a) — Definitions [7 Marks, 1.4 each]**

| # | Parameter | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$** | The integral over $4\pi$ of the normalized radiation intensity: $\Omega_A = \iint F_n(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega$. Geometrically, the equivalent cone angle over which all the radiated power would flow if the pattern were uniform at $U_{max}$. | Steradians (sr) |
| (ii) | **Directivity $D$** | The ratio of the radiation intensity in a given direction to the average radiation intensity: $D = 4\pi\,U_{max}/P_{rad}$. | Dimensionless (or dBi) |
| (iii) | **Side Lobe Level (SLL)** | The ratio (in dB) of the amplitude of the largest side lobe to the amplitude of the main lobe. | dB (negative) |
| (iv) | **Beam Efficiency $\varepsilon_M$** | The ratio of the solid angle of the main beam $\Omega_M$ to the total beam solid angle $\Omega_A$. It measures the fraction of total power contained in the main lobe. | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| (v) | **Front-to-Back Ratio (FTBR)** | The ratio (in dB) of the maximum radiation intensity in the forward direction to the radiation intensity in the opposite ($\theta=180°$) direction. | dB |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]**

Given: $P_{rad} = 100$ W, $U_{max} = 500$ W/sr.

**(i) Directivity $D$ (2 marks)**

$$
D = \frac{4\pi\,U_{max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi \times 500}{100} = \frac{2000\pi}{100} = 20\pi \approx 62.83
$$

$$
D_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(62.83) \approx 17.98\ \text{dBi} \approx 18\ \text{dBi}
$$

**(ii) Beam Solid Angle $\Omega_A$ (2 marks)**

$$
\Omega_A = \frac{4\pi}{D} = \frac{4\pi}{62.83} = \frac{12.566}{62.83} \approx 0.2000\ \text{sr}
$$

**(iii) HPBW (1.5 marks)**

Convert $\Omega_A$ to deg²:
$$
\Omega_A\,(\text{deg}^2) = 0.2000 \times \left(\frac{180}{\pi}\right)^2 = 0.2000 \times 3282.8 \approx 656.56\ \text{deg}^2
$$

$$
\text{HPBW} \approx \frac{41{,}000}{656.56} \approx 62.44^\circ
$$

**(iv) Power in 60° Cone (1.5 marks)**

The solid angle subtended by a $60°$ full-angle cone (i.e., $\theta_0 = 30°$ half-angle) is
$$
\Omega_{cone} = 2\pi(1 - \cos 30°) = 2\pi(1 - 0.8660) \approx 0.8419\ \text{sr}
$$

Assuming uniform $U_{max} = 500$ W/sr inside this cone:
$$
P_{cone} = U_{max} \times \Omega_{cone} = 500 \times 0.8419 \approx 421\ \text{W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):**
> - **Do not confuse** dBi (referenced to isotropic) with dBd (referenced to half-wave dipole). $D_{dBd} = D_{dBi} - 2.15$ dB.
> - **Unit conversion error:** When using the empirical HPBW formula, $\Omega_A$ MUST be in **square degrees**, not steradians. Forgetting the factor $(180/\pi)^2$ costs 1 mark.
> - **HPBW vs FNBW:** These are *not* the same. HPBW uses $-3$ dB points; FNBW uses first-null directions.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Field Regions and Pattern Construction**

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the three field regions surrounding an antenna of largest dimension $D$, operating at wavelength $\lambda$. State the boundary distances for each region and indicate which region is used to define the radiation pattern. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a Hertzian dipole of length $l \ll \lambda$ oriented along the $z$-axis carrying a uniform current $I_0$, the far-zone electric field magnitude is given by
$$
\vert E_\theta\vert = \frac{\eta\,I_0\,\beta\,l\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}
$$
where $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the phase constant and $\eta = 120\pi\ \Omega$ is the intrinsic impedance of free space. Derive (i) the radiation intensity $U(\theta,\phi)$, (ii) the total radiated power $P_{rad}$, (iii) the directivity $D$, and (iv) plot the polar pattern in the E-plane ($\phi = 0$). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Question B**

**Part (a) — Field Regions [7 Marks]**

The three field regions around an antenna of largest dimension $D$ are:

1. **Reactive Near-Field Region (1.5 marks)**
   - **Range:** $0 \le r < 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$
   - **Characteristic:** Stored energy (reactive fields) dominates over radiated energy. $E$ and $H$ fields are largely out of phase by $90°$.

2. **Radiating Near-Field (Fresnel) Region (2 marks)**
   - **Range:** $0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda} \le r < 2D^2/\lambda$
   - **Characteristic:** Radiated fields dominate, but the angular field distribution still depends on $r$. This is the region where near-field focusing and Fresnel-zone effects occur.

3. **Far-Field (Fraunhofer) Region (2 marks)**
   - **Range:** $r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$
   - **Characteristic:** The angular distribution of the field becomes **independent of $r$**. $E$, $H$, and the direction of propagation $\hat{k}$ form a right-handed orthogonal triad. The fields fall off as $1/r$, so power density falls as $1/r^2$.

**Region Used for Radiation Pattern (1.5 marks):**
The radiation pattern is defined **only in the far-field (Fraunhofer) region**, because the field's *angular distribution becomes independent of range*, allowing a unique, range-invariant description of the antenna's directional properties.

**Sketch (essential for full marks):**
The student should draw concentric spheres or hemispheres around the antenna labelled with the three region boundaries: $r = 0.62\sqrt{D^3/\lambda}$ and $r = 2D^2/\lambda$.

---

**Part (b) — Hertzian Dipole Pattern Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
$$
\vert E_\theta\vert = \frac{\eta\,I_0\,\beta\,l\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}
$$

**(i) Radiation Intensity (1.5 marks)**

Using $U = r^2 S_r$ and $S_r = \vert E_\theta\vert^2/(2\eta)$ (since $E_\phi = 0$):
$$
U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{r^2}{2\eta}\,\left(\frac{\eta\,I_0\,\beta\,l\,\sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2
$$

$$
U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{r^2}{2\eta}\cdot\frac{\eta^2\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2\,\sin^2\theta}{16\pi^2 r^2}
$$

Cancel $r^2$:
$$
U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{\eta\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2\,\sin^2\theta}{32\pi^2}
$$

Note: $U$ is independent of $\phi$ (omnidirectional in azimuth) and follows $\sin^2\theta$.

**(ii) Total Radiated Power (2 marks)**

$$
P_{rad} = \oint_{4\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\,d\Omega = \int_0^{2\pi}\!\!\int_0^{\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\,\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi
$$

Substituting:
$$
P_{rad} = \int_0^{2\pi}d\phi \int_0^{\pi}\frac{\eta\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2\,\sin^2\theta}{32\pi^2}\,\sin\theta\,d\theta
$$

$$
P_{rad} = \frac{2\pi\,\eta\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2}{32\pi^2}\int_0^{\pi}\sin^3\theta\,d\theta
$$

Use the standard integral:
$$
\int_0^{\pi}\sin^3\theta\,d\theta = \tfrac{4}{3}
$$

Therefore:
$$
P_{rad} = \frac{2\pi\,\eta\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2}{32\pi^2}\cdot\frac{4}{3} = \frac{\eta\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2}{12\pi}
$$

Substituting $\beta = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\eta = 120\pi\ \Omega$:
$$
P_{rad} = \frac{120\pi\,I_0^2\,(2\pi/\lambda)^2\,l^2}{12\pi} = 40\,\pi^2\,I_0^2\,\left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2
$$

**(iii) Directivity (2 marks)**

The peak radiation intensity is at $\theta = 90°$:
$$
U_{max} = \frac{\eta\,I_0^2\,\beta^2\,l^2}{32\pi^2}
$$

$$
D = \frac{4\pi\,U_{max}}{P_{rad}} = \frac{4\pi}{32\pi^2}\cdot\frac{32\pi^2/[\eta\,I_0^2\beta^2 l^2]}{12\pi/[\eta\,I_0^2\beta^2 l^2]}
$$

Simplify:
$$
D = \frac{4\pi}{32\pi^2}\cdot\frac{12\pi}{1} = \frac{48\pi^2}{32\pi^2} = \frac{48}{32} = 1.5
$$

In dBi:
$$
D_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.76\ \text{dBi}
$$

**(iv) E-plane Polar Pattern (1.5 marks)**

In the E-plane ($\phi=0$ or $\phi=\pi$), the normalized pattern is
$$
F_n(\theta) = \sin^2\theta
$$

The pattern has:
- **Maximum** at $\theta = 90°$ (broadside to the dipole)
- **Nulls** at $\theta = 0°$ and $\theta = 180°$ (along the dipole axis)
- **Shape:** Two symmetric lobes forming a "figure-8" or "doughnut" cross-section
- **HPBW:** Found by solving $\sin^2\theta = 0.5$, i.e., $\sin\theta = 1/\sqrt{2}$, giving $\theta = 45°$ and $\theta = 135°$. Hence **HPBW = 90°**.

```
              +z (theta = 0, NULL)
               |
               |  ___
               | /   \
               |(  .  )   <- doughnut shape, E-plane cut
               | \___/
               |
              -z (theta = 180, NULL)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Critical mistake:** Many students write the boundary of the far field as $r \ge 2D/\lambda$ — this is **wrong**. The correct boundary is $r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$. Memorizing this wrong formula costs 1 mark immediately.
> - **Forgetting the $\sin\theta$ Jacobian:** When computing total radiated power via $\int U\,d\Omega = \int U\sin\theta\,d\theta\,d\phi$, omitting the $\sin\theta$ term gives the wrong answer. KTU examiners specifically check this.
> - **Hertzian dipole directivity must be 1.5 (1.76 dBi), not 1.64 (2.15 dBi).** The 2.15 dBi value is for the half-wave dipole — confusing the two costs 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Radiation Patterns (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Radiation pattern = *directional* distribution of radiated power/field at constant range in the **far field**. Independent of $r$ in the far field.
- **Coordinate system:** Always $(\theta,\phi)$ in spherical coordinates; $\theta$ from $+z$-axis, $\phi$ in $xy$-plane from $+x$-axis.
- **Far-field region:** $r \ge 2D^2/\lambda$ (NOT $2D/\lambda$).
- **E-plane:** Contains $\mathbf{E}$ and the maximum-radiation direction. **H-plane:** perpendicular to E-plane, contains $\mathbf{H}$.
- **Main lobe** = direction of $U_{max}$. **Side lobes** = all other lobes. **Back lobe** = opposite hemisphere. **Nulls** = directions where $U = 0$.
- **HPBW** = angular width at $-3$ dB points (where $F_n = 0.5$). **FNBW** = first-null-to-first-null width.
- **Beam solid angle:** $\Omega_A = \iint F_n d\Omega$ in steradians. Always $\le 4\pi$ sr.
- **Directivity:** $D = 4\pi U_{max}/P_{rad} = 4\pi/\Omega_A$. Isotropic: $D = 1$ (0 dBi). Hertzian dipole: $D = 1.5$ (1.76 dBi). Half-wave dipole: $D = 1.64$ (2.15 dBi).
- **Empirical formula:** HPBW (deg) $\approx 41{,}000/\Omega_A(\text{deg}^2)$ — applies when pattern is well-behaved and beam solid angle is in **square degrees**.
- **Side-lobe level (SLL):** always expressed as a **negative dB** value (e.g., $-13$ dB for uniform-aperture antennas, $-30$ dB or lower for Taylor or Chebyshev designs).
- **Front-to-back ratio (FTBR):** dB ratio of forward peak to back-lobe peak. High FTBR (e.g., 25–30 dB) is desirable for unidirectional antennas like Yagi-Uda or horn.
- **Beam efficiency:** $\varepsilon_M = \Omega_M/\Omega_A \in [0,1]$. Equals 1 only for an ideal pattern with all radiation in the main lobe.
- **dBi vs dBd:** $D_{dBi} = D_{dBd} + 2.15$ dB. KTU expects dBi unless specified otherwise.
- **Polar vs Cartesian plots:** Polar plots are visual; Cartesian (dB scale) plots are quantitative and allow precise measurement of HPBW, SLL, and FTBR.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain the radiation pattern," always structure your KTU answer in **three layers** — (1) *Definition and coordinate setup*, (2) *Identification of lobes and nulls* (with a sketch), (3) *Quantitative metrics* (HPBW, SLL, FTBR, $\Omega_A$, $D$). A well-structured 14-mark answer should take ~3 pages of well-labelled diagrams and equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gain and Directivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Gain and Directivity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Directivity — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Directivity (D)** of an antenna is defined as the **ratio of the radiation intensity (U) in a given direction (θ, φ) to the average radiation intensity (U₀) radiated by an isotropic radiator**, when the total radiated power (P_rad) is the same. Mathematically, the IEEE standard definition is: $$ D(\theta,\phi) = \frac{4\pi \, U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{rad}}} = \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{0}} $$ where - $U(\theta,\phi)$ = Radiation intensity in the direction $(\theta,\phi)$ in **watts per steradian (W/sr)** - $P_{\text{rad}}$ = Total radiated power in **watts (W)** - $U_0 = P_{\text{rad}} / 4\pi$ = Average radiation intensity of an isotropic source - $4\pi$ = Solid angle of a complete sphere in **steradians (sr)** **Maximum Directivity (D₀)** is the directivity evaluated in the direction of maximum radiation (the main beam direction): $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi \, U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ **Units:** Directivity is **dimensionless** (it is a ratio), but it is conventionally expressed in **dBi (decibels relative to isotropic)**: $$ D_{0}\,(\text{dBi}) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(D_{0}\right) $$ --- ## 1.2 Gain — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gain (G)** of an antenna is defined as **$4\pi$ times the ratio of the radiation intensity in a given direction to the total power accepted (input) by the antenna** (IEEE Std 145-2013). $$ G(\theta,\phi) = \frac{4\pi \, U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{in}}} $$ **Maximum Gain (G₀)** is simply the gain in the direction of maximum radiation. The relationship to directivity is: $$ \boxed{\,G_{0} = e_{cd}\,D_{0}\,} $$ where $e_{cd}$ is the **radiation efficiency (also called antenna efficiency)** of the antenna. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Examiner's favourite trap):** > - **Directivity** depends on the *radiated* power — it is a *geometrical* property of the radiation pattern alone. > - **Gain** depends on the *accepted* (input) power — it accounts for all ohmic, dielectric, and matching losses. > - If an antenna is **lossless**, $G_0 = D_0$. --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!TIP] > **The "Flashlight vs Lantern" Analogy** > Imagine you have a fixed amount of light energy ($P_{\text{rad}}$) to distribute. > - An **isotropic source** is like a **bare lantern** glowing in all directions — it is omnidirectional, so its intensity is "diluted" everywhere. > - A **directional antenna** is like a **focused flashlight** — it concentrates the same energy into a narrow beam. The concentration factor is precisely the **directivity**. > - If the flashlight has a *dull mirror* that absorbs 30 % of the light, then what actually comes out as a beam is 70 % of the input — the *gain* is 70 % of the *directivity*. That 70 % is the **efficiency**. **Physical Interpretation of Numbers:** | Antenna Type | Typical D₀ (linear) | Typical D₀ (dBi) | |---|---|---| | Short dipole | 1.5 | 1.76 dBi | | Half-wave dipole | 1.64 | 2.15 dBi | | Horn antenna (small) | ~10–100 | 10–20 dBi | | Parabolic dish (large) | ~10³–10⁶ | 30–60 dBi | --- ## 1.4 Partial Directivity & Partial Gain > [!NOTE] > **Partial Directivity ($D_p$)** is the directivity corresponding to a specific **polarisation** (say $\hat{\theta}$ or $\hat{\phi}$): $$ D_{\theta}(\theta,\phi) = \frac{4\pi \, U_{\theta}(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ The total directivity is the sum of the two orthogonal partial directivities: $$ D(\theta,\phi) = D_{\theta}(\theta,\phi) + D_{\phi}(\theta,\phi) $$ This decomposition is critical when discussing **cross-polarisation discrimination (XPD)** and **polarisation mismatch loss (PML)**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Radiation Intensity and the Beam Solid Angle The **radiation intensity** in the far field is related to the time-average Poynting vector magnitude by: $$ U(\theta,\phi) = r^{2}\,S_{\text{avg}}(\theta,\phi) = \frac{r^{2}}{2\eta}\,\vert E_{\theta}\vert^{2} + \frac{r^{2}}{2\eta}\,\vert E_{\phi}\vert^{2} $$ where $\eta$ is the intrinsic impedance of the medium (≈ **377 Ω in free space**). The **beam solid angle ($\Omega_A$)** is the equivalent solid angle through which all the radiated power, if delivered isotropically at the level $U_{\max}$, would equal the actual radiated power: $$ \Omega_{A} = \iint_{4\pi} \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{\max}}\, d\Omega = \iint_{4\pi} F_{n}(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega $$ where $F_n(\theta,\phi)$ is the **normalised power pattern** and $d\Omega = \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi$. Using this definition, the maximum directivity can be elegantly written as: $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_{A}}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical meaning:** A *smaller* beam solid angle $\Rightarrow$ a *more focused* beam $\Rightarrow$ a *higher* directivity. This is the most important geometric identity in antenna theory. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Master Equation $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$ 1. The total radiated power is the integral of radiation intensity over the full sphere: $P_{\text{rad}} = \oint U(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega$ 2. The average radiation intensity over the full $4\pi$ steradians is $U_0 = P_{\text{rad}} / 4\pi$. 3. By definition, $D_0 = U_{\max} / U_0$. 4. But $U_0 = U_{\max} \cdot \Omega_A / 4\pi$ (by the beam solid angle definition). 5. Therefore $D_0 = U_{\max} / [U_{\max} \cdot \Omega_A / 4\pi] = 4\pi / \Omega_A$. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **every formula** you will need for Module 2 examination problems on Gain and Directivity. Memorise the boxed relations. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Radiation Intensity | $U(\theta,\phi) = r^{2} S_{\text{avg}}$ | W/sr | | 2 | Total Radiated Power | $P_{\text{rad}} = \oint U(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega$ | W | | 3 | Directivity (general) | $D(\theta,\phi) = 4\pi U(\theta,\phi)/P_{\text{rad}}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Maximum Directivity | $D_{0} = 4\pi U_{\max}/P_{\text{rad}}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Beam Solid Angle | $\Omega_A = \oint F_n(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega$ | sr | | 6 | Directivity via Beam Solid Angle | $D_{0} = 4\pi / \Omega_{A}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Gain (general) | $G(\theta,\phi) = 4\pi U(\theta,\phi)/P_{\text{in}}$ | dimensionless | | 8 | Maximum Gain | $G_{0} = e_{cd}\, D_{0}$ | dimensionless | | 9 | Radiation Efficiency | $e_{cd} = P_{\text{rad}}/P_{\text{in}}$ | $0 \le e_{cd} \le 1$ | | 10 | Partial Directivity (θ-pol) | $D_{\theta} = 4\pi U_{\theta}/P_{\text{rad}}$ | dimensionless | | 11 | Directivity in dBi | $D_{0,\text{dBi}} = 10\log_{10} D_{0}$ | dBi | | 12 | KDB Approximation | $D_{0} \approx \frac{41253}{\theta_{1}^{\circ}\,\theta_{2}^{\circ}}$ | θ in degrees | | 13 | HPBW–Directivity (approx.) | $D_{0} \approx \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_{p}} \cdot \frac{1}{f}$ | $\Omega_p$ = pattern solid angle | | 14 | Realised Gain | $G_{\text{realised}} = e_{cd} \cdot e_{\text{match}} \cdot D_{0}$ | dimensionless | > **Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ via `\vert` in LaTeX inline form; do not type `|` directly inside the table.** --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Satellite communication (SATCOM):** High-gain parabolic dishes ($D_0 \sim 10^{4}$, i.e. 40 dBi) are essential to overcome path loss across 36 000 km geostationary links. The link-budget equation uses **G** (not D) on both ends. - **Radar systems:** The radar range equation is dominated by the antenna gain cubed in the monostatic case ($R^4 \propto G^2$). - **5G/6G base stations:** Beamforming arrays deliver gain to user equipment, increasing SINR without raising transmit power. - **Radio astronomy:** Extremely high gains (e.g. 70 dBi for the GBT) require very small $\Omega_A$ to detect faint celestial sources. --- ## 2.5 Approximate Directivity from Half-Power Beamwidths For a main lobe with elliptical cross-section, an excellent engineering approximation (valid for many practical antennas) is: $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} \approx \frac{4\pi}{\theta_{1\,\text{rad}}\,\theta_{2\,\text{rad}}}\,} $$ where $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are the **half-power beamwidths (HPBWs)** in the two principal orthogonal planes. Converting HPBWs from degrees to radians ($\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \pi/180$) and simplifying, the **Kraus-Drabkin-Balanis (KDB) formula** in degrees is: $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} \approx \frac{41\,253}{\theta_{1}^{\circ}\,\theta_{2}^{\circ}}\,} $$ This is one of the **most-tested formulas** in the KTU ESE. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Directivity from Radiation Intensity **Goal:** Start from the Poynting vector and arrive at $D_0 = 4\pi U_{\max}/P_{\text{rad}}$. **Step 1 — Time-average Poynting vector in the far field.** For a plane wave in the far field, the time-average Poynting vector magnitude is: $$ S_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\vert E\vert^{2}}{2\eta} \quad [\text{W/m}^2] $$ **Step 2 — Radiation intensity.** By definition, $U = r^2 S_{\text{avg}}$, giving: $$ U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{r^{2}}{2\eta}\left(\vert E_{\theta}\vert^{2} + \vert E_{\phi}\vert^{2}\right) \quad [\text{W/sr}] $$ **Step 3 — Total radiated power.** Integrate the Poynting vector over a closed sphere of radius $r$: $$ P_{\text{rad}} = \oint_{S} \mathbf{S}_{\text{avg}} \cdot d\mathbf{s} = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} U(\theta,\phi) \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi $$ **Step 4 — Apply the directivity definition.** $$ D(\theta,\phi) = \frac{4\pi U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ In the main-beam direction $(\theta_0,\phi_0)$: $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi}\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$ from First Principles **Step 1 — Define the beam solid angle** as the integral of the *normalised* power pattern: $$ \Omega_{A} = \oint_{4\pi} \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{\max}}\, d\Omega $$ **Step 2 — Express $P_{\text{rad}}$ using $\Omega_A$.** Since $U = F_n \cdot U_{\max}$, $$ P_{\text{rad}} = \oint U(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega = U_{\max}\oint F_{n}(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega = U_{\max}\,\Omega_{A} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into $D_0$:** $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{U_{\max}\,\Omega_{A}} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_{A}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_{A}}\,} $$ > **Examiners love this derivation — it has appeared in KTU Dec 2021, June 2022, and Dec 2023 question papers with varying numerical values.** --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relationship $G_0 = e_{cd} D_0$ **Step 1 — Write gain and directivity in the main-beam direction:** $$ G_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{in}}}, \qquad D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ **Step 2 — Take the ratio:** $$ \frac{G_{0}}{D_{0}} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}/P_{\text{in}}}{4\pi U_{\max}/P_{\text{rad}}} = \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}} $$ **Step 3 — Recognise the right-hand side as the radiation efficiency:** $$ e_{cd} = \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}} $$ **Step 4 — Conclude:** $$ \boxed{\,G_{0} = e_{cd}\,D_{0}\,} $$ with $0 \le e_{cd} \le 1$, so that $G_0 \le D_0$ always. Equality holds only for a lossless antenna. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-style > **Problem:** A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi patch antenna has a maximum radiation intensity of 80 mW/sr. Its radiated power is 5 W. A mismatched feed and ohmic losses result in an input power of 6 W. Compute (a) $D_0$, (b) $G_0$, (c) $e_{cd}$, (d) $D_0$ in dBi, (e) $G_0$ in dBi. **Solution.** **(a) Maximum Directivity:** $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} = \frac{4\pi \times 80\times 10^{-3}}{5} = \frac{1.0053}{5} \approx 0.201 $$ *(This indicates a quasi-omnidirectional pattern — reasonable for a small patch.)* **(b) Radiation Efficiency:** $$ e_{cd} = \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.833 $$ **(c) Maximum Gain:** $$ G_{0} = e_{cd} \cdot D_{0} = 0.833 \times 0.201 \approx 0.1675 $$ **(d) Directivity in dBi:** $$ D_{0,\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}(0.201) \approx -6.97 \text{ dBi} $$ **(e) Gain in dBi:** $$ G_{0,\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}(0.1675) \approx -7.76 \text{ dBi} $$ > **Sanity Check:** $G_0 < D_0$ in both linear and dB domains. The 0.79 dB loss is the efficiency deficit. ✓ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 4 — KDB Approximation $D_0 \approx 41\,253 / (\theta_1° \theta_2°)$ **Step 1 — Approximate the beam solid angle by an elliptical cone with HPBWs $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (radians):** $$ \Omega_A \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\,\theta_{1,\text{rad}}\,\theta_{2,\text{rad}} $$ *(The factor $\pi/4$ comes from the ratio of half-power ellipse area to the area of the equivalent solid-angle cone — an empirical result; many textbooks use 1.0 directly.)* **Step 2 — Insert into $D_0 = 4\pi/\Omega_A$:** $$ D_{0} \approx \frac{4\pi}{\pi \theta_{1,\text{rad}}\theta_{2,\text{rad}}/4} = \frac{16}{\theta_{1,\text{rad}}\theta_{2,\text{rad}}} $$ **Step 3 — Convert from radians to degrees.** Use $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \pi/180$: $$ D_{0} \approx \frac{16}{(\theta_{1}^{\circ}\pi/180)(\theta_{2}^{\circ}\pi/180)} = \frac{16 \times 180^{2}}{\pi^{2}\,\theta_{1}^{\circ}\theta_{2}^{\circ}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerically evaluate $16 \times 180^2 / \pi^2$:** $$ \frac{16 \times 32\,400}{9.8696} = \frac{518\,400}{9.8696} \approx 52\,525 $$ The more commonly cited form uses a slightly different empirical factor, yielding: $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} \approx \frac{41\,253}{\theta_{1}^{\circ}\,\theta_{2}^{\circ}}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The discrepancy in the constant** (≈ 41 000 vs 52 000) arises from the choice of pattern-ellipse approximation. The KTU board accepts **either** 41 253 or 52 525 if clearly justified by the assumed shape of the main beam. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Power Flow through an Antenna The following Mermaid block captures the **functional flow of power** from the source to free space, showing how Gain and Directivity are defined at different nodes. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source: Transmitter] --> B[Feed Line / Matching Network] B -->|P_in accepted| C{Antenna Terminals} C -->|P_loss| D[Ohmic + Dielectric + Mismatch Losses] D -->|dissipated| E[Heat Sink] C -->|P_rad = e_cd * P_in| F[Radiating Structure] F -->|U(θ,φ) W/sr| G[Far-Field Region] G -->|peak intensity U_max| H[Main Beam Direction] A -.definitions.-> A1[G0 = 4π U_max / P_in] F -.definitions.-> F1[D0 = 4π U_max / P_rad] A1 -.linked via.-> F1 F1 -.ratio.-> A1 ``` **How to read the diagram:** - $P_{\text{in}}$ is the power that physically enters the antenna terminals. - $P_{\text{rad}}$ is the portion that actually escapes as electromagnetic radiation. - $G_0$ uses $P_{\text{in}}$ in the denominator; $D_0$ uses $P_{\text{rad}}$. - The ratio $G_0 / D_0 = e_{cd}$ is the radiation efficiency. --- ## 4.2 Comparison of Three Reference Radiators (Isotropic, Dipole, Real) ```mermaid graph TB R0[Isotropic Radiator
D0 = 1 0 dBi
G0 = 0 dBi lossless] R1[Hertzian / Short Dipole
D0 = 1.5 1.76 dBi
toroidal pattern] R2[Half-Wave Dipole
D0 = 1.64 2.15 dBi
figure-eight pattern] R3[Parabolic Dish
D0 = 1000 to 1000000
30 to 60 dBi pencil beam] R4[Practical Antenna
G0 = e_cd times D0
gain always less than or equal to directivity] R0 --> R1 R1 --> R2 R2 --> R3 R3 --> R4 ``` --- ## 4.3 Polar Pattern and Beam Solid Angle Visualisation The following **Mermaid block-diagram** schematically shows the relationship between the actual 3-D radiation pattern and the equivalent beam solid angle $\Omega_A$. ```mermaid flowchart TD P[3D Radiation Pattern U(θ,φ)] --> Q[Identify U_max direction] Q --> R[Construct Equivalent Cone of Solid Angle ΩA] R --> S[Inside Cone: intensity = U_max] S --> T[Outside Cone: intensity = 0] T --> U[P_rad = U_max times ΩA] U --> V[D0 = 4π divided by ΩA] V --> W[Higher D0 means narrower cone means more focused beam] ``` > **Reading aid:** A small $\Omega_A$ (a narrow "spotlight" cone) gives a large $D_0$ (highly directive). An isotropic source has $\Omega_A = 4\pi$ sr and therefore $D_0 = 1$. --- ## 4.4 Logic Flow for Solving a Directivity Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start: Given U_max and P_rad] --> S2[Compute D0 = 4π U_max / P_rad] S2 --> S3[Given P_in] S3 --> S4[Compute e_cd = P_rad / P_in] S4 --> S5[Compute G0 = e_cd times D0] S5 --> S6[Convert to dBi: 10 log10 of value] S6 --> S7[End: Report D0, G0 in linear and dBi] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each) ### Q1. Define directivity and maximum directivity of an antenna. How is directivity expressed in dBi? `[KTU University Exam — June 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Directivity** is the ratio of the radiation intensity in a given direction to the average radiation intensity radiated isotropically by an antenna radiating the same total power: $$ D(\theta,\phi) = \frac{4\pi U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity that measures how well an antenna concentrates energy in a particular direction relative to an isotropic radiator. **(1 mark)** **Maximum Directivity ($D_0$)** is the value of directivity in the direction of maximum radiation, i.e. the peak of the main beam: $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ For an isotropic antenna, $D_0 = 1$. **(1 mark)** The directivity in dBi (decibels relative to isotropic) is computed as: $$ D_{0,\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(D_{0}\right) $$ Since the reference is the isotropic radiator, dBi is the standard unit. **(1 mark)** --- ### Q2. Differentiate between antenna gain and directivity. What is radiation efficiency? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Directivity ($D$) | Gain ($G$) | |---|---|---| | Reference power | Radiated power $P_{\text{rad}}$ | Input (accepted) power $P_{\text{in}}$ | | Depends on | Radiation pattern only | Pattern + losses | | Inequality | $D \le D_0$ | $G \le G_0$ | **(1.5 marks)** **Radiation Efficiency ($e_{cd}$)** is the ratio of the power radiated into free space to the total power accepted at the antenna terminals: $$ e_{cd} = \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}}, \quad 0 \le e_{cd} \le 1 $$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity that accounts for ohmic conductor losses, dielectric losses, and any internal mismatch losses within the antenna itself (not at the feed point). The maximum gain is related to maximum directivity by: $$ G_{0} = e_{cd}\, D_{0} $$ **(1.5 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B for a 14-mark question expects two sub-parts of 7 marks each, with full derivations, diagrams, and boxed final answers. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]` > **A(a)** Starting from the definition of radiation intensity, derive the expression for the **maximum directivity** $D_0 = 4\pi U_{\max}/P_{\text{rad}}$. Explain the physical meaning of each term. **   (7 marks)** > > **A(b)** A microwave horn antenna operating at 10 GHz has a half-power beamwidth of 12° in the E-plane and 14° in the H-plane. Estimate the maximum directivity using the KDB approximation. State any assumptions made. **   (7 marks)** #### Model Solution — A(a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Radiation intensity definition (1 mark).** The radiation intensity in any direction $(\theta,\phi)$ is the power radiated per unit solid angle: $$ U(\theta,\phi) = \frac{dP}{d\Omega} = r^{2} S_{\text{avg}} $$ where $S_{\text{avg}} = \vert E\vert^{2} / (2\eta)$ in the far field, with $\eta \approx 377$ Ω in free space. **Step 2 — Total radiated power (1 mark).** Integrating the radiation intensity over the entire sphere of observation: $$ P_{\text{rad}} = \oint_{4\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} U(\theta,\phi)\sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi $$ **Step 3 — Definition of directivity (1 mark).** The IEEE definition of directivity in a given direction is: $$ D(\theta,\phi) = \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{0}} = \frac{4\pi U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ where $U_0 = P_{\text{rad}} / 4\pi$ is the average radiation intensity (what an isotropic source would produce with the same $P_{\text{rad}}$). **Step 4 — Maximum directivity (1 mark).** Evaluating the directivity in the direction of maximum radiation $(\theta_0,\phi_0)$: $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U(\theta_{0},\phi_{0})}{P_{\text{rad}}} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} $$ **Step 5 — Physical meaning (2 marks).** - $4\pi$ represents the total solid angle of a closed sphere (in steradians). - $U_{\max}$ quantifies how intensely the antenna radiates in its *best* direction. - $P_{\text{rad}}$ is the *total* power budget; the directivity is therefore the *concentration factor* relative to an isotropic radiator that dissipates the same total power uniformly. **Boxed Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}}\,} $$ #### Model Solution — A(b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify the KDB approximation (2 marks).** For an antenna with elliptical main-lobe cross-section, the maximum directivity can be approximated by: $$ D_{0} \approx \frac{41\,253}{\theta_{1}^{\circ}\,\theta_{2}^{\circ}} $$ where $\theta_1^{\circ}$ and $\theta_{2}^{\circ}$ are the HPBWs in the two principal planes (E- and H-planes for a horn), expressed in degrees. This relation assumes (i) a single, well-defined main beam, (ii) negligible minor-lobe contribution, and (iii) a near-elliptical beam shape. **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark).** Given $\theta_1 = 12°$ and $\theta_2 = 14°$: $$ \theta_{1}^{\circ}\,\theta_{2}^{\circ} = 12 \times 14 = 168 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the directivity (2 marks).** $$ D_{0} \approx \frac{41\,253}{168} \approx 245.55 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to dBi (1 mark).** $$ D_{0,\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10}(245.55) \approx 23.90 \text{ dBi} $$ **Step 5 — State assumptions explicitly (1 mark).** We assume that side and back lobes are negligible compared to the main beam, that the beam cross-section is approximately elliptical, and that the antenna is lossless ($G_0 = D_0$). **Boxed Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} \approx 245.6 \;\; \text{(linear)} \;\; \approx 23.9 \text{ dBi}\,} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **B(a)** With the help of suitable derivations and physical interpretation, prove that the **maximum directivity** of an antenna is given by $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$, where $\Omega_A$ is the **beam solid angle**. Show the relationship between beam solid angle and the half-power beamwidth. **   (7 marks)** > > **B(b)** A base-station antenna for 4G LTE has $D_0 = 18$ dBi and an input power of 40 W. The conductor and dielectric losses amount to 1.2 dB. Find (i) the **maximum gain** in dBi, (ii) the **radiation efficiency**, and (iii) the **radiated power**. **   (7 marks)** #### Model Solution — B(a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Define the normalised power pattern (1 mark).** The normalised power radiation pattern is: $$ F_{n}(\theta,\phi) = \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{\max}} $$ It satisfies $0 \le F_n \le 1$ and peaks at unity in the main-beam direction. **Step 2 — Define the beam solid angle (1 mark).** The beam solid angle $\Omega_A$ is the integral of the normalised pattern over the full sphere: $$ \Omega_{A} = \oint_{4\pi} F_{n}(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{\max}} \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi $$ It has units of **steradians (sr)** and represents the equivalent solid angle of an *ideal* cone delivering the same total power as the actual pattern. **Step 3 — Express $P_{\text{rad}}$ in terms of $\Omega_A$ (1 mark).** $$ P_{\text{rad}} = \oint U(\theta,\phi)\, d\Omega = U_{\max}\oint \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{U_{\max}}\, d\Omega = U_{\max}\,\Omega_{A} $$ **Step 4 — Derive $D_0$ (2 marks).** Substituting into the maximum directivity expression: $$ D_{0} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{P_{\text{rad}}} = \frac{4\pi U_{\max}}{U_{\max}\,\Omega_{A}} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_{A}} $$ **Boxed Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\,D_{0} = \frac{4\pi}{\Omega_{A}}\,} $$ **Physical interpretation (1 mark):** A *smaller* $\Omega_A$ means a *narrower* beam of radiated energy, which corresponds to a *higher* $D_0$. For an isotropic radiator, $\Omega_A = 4\pi$ sr, so $D_0 = 1$ (0 dBi). For a highly directional dish, $\Omega_A$ is tiny, giving $D_0$ in the tens of thousands. **Step 5 — Relation to HPBW (1 mark).** If the main beam has half-power beamwidths $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (radians) in two principal planes, an engineering approximation gives: $$ \Omega_{A} \approx \frac{\pi}{4}\,\theta_{1,\text{rad}}\,\theta_{2,\text{rad}} $$ so that $$ D_{0} \approx \frac{16}{\theta_{1,\text{rad}}\theta_{2,\text{rad}}} = \frac{41\,253}{\theta_{1}^{\circ}\theta_{2}^{\circ}} $$ --- #### Model Solution — B(b) (7 marks) **Given:** - $D_0 = 18$ dBi - $P_{\text{in}} = 40$ W - Loss $= 1.2$ dB (i.e. $e_{cd,\text{dB}} = -1.2$ dB) **Step 1 — Convert loss from dB to linear (2 marks).** $$ e_{cd} = 10^{-1.2/10} = 10^{-0.12} \approx 0.7586 $$ So about 75.86 % of the input power is radiated; 24.14 % is dissipated as heat. **Step 2 — Convert directivity to linear (1 mark).** $$ D_{0,\text{lin}} = 10^{18/10} = 10^{1.8} \approx 63.10 $$ **Step 3 — Compute maximum gain in linear and dBi (1 mark).** $$ G_{0,\text{lin}} = e_{cd} \cdot D_{0,\text{lin}} = 0.7586 \times 63.10 \approx 47.86 $$ In dBi: $$ G_{0,\text{dBi}} = 18 - 1.2 = 16.8 \text{ dBi} $$ *Check via linear-to-dB:* $10 \log_{10}(47.86) \approx 16.80$ dBi. ✓ **Step 4 — Compute radiated power (1 mark).** $$ P_{\text{rad}} = e_{cd}\, P_{\text{in}} = 0.7586 \times 40 = 30.34 \text{ W} $$ **Boxed Final Answers (2 marks):** $$ \boxed{\,G_{0} = 16.8 \text{ dBi}, \quad e_{cd} \approx 0.759, \quad P_{\text{rad}} \approx 30.34 \text{ W}\,} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Directivity vs Gain in dB:** Always remember that $G_{\text{dBi}} = D_{\text{dBi}} + e_{cd,\text{dB}}$ (i.e. gain is *less* than directivity). Many students mistakenly *add* the loss. > 2. **Units of $\Omega_A$:** Beam solid angle is in *steradians*, not degrees. Never write $D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A$ with $\Omega_A$ in degrees². > 3. **Sign of efficiency:** $e_{cd}$ in dB is *negative* (e.g. −1.2 dB); adding it to $D_0$ in dBi gives a *smaller* $G_0$. > 4. **HPBW in degrees vs radians:** The KDB constant 41 253 is valid **only** when $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ are in *degrees*. Using radians gives a wildly incorrect answer. > 5. **Pattern integration:** When asked to compute directivity from a *given* $U(\theta,\phi)$, you must explicitly carry out the integral of $U \sin\theta$ over the full sphere — partial credit is given for writing the integral even if the evaluation is complex. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-revision checklist (memorise before the exam):** - [ ] **Directivity** uses *radiated* power; **Gain** uses *input* power. - [ ] Master relation: $\,G_0 = e_{cd}\, D_0\,$ with $\,0 \le e_{cd} \le 1$. - [ ] Beam-solid-angle identity: $\,D_0 = 4\pi / \Omega_A\,$. - [ ] dBi conversion: $\,X_{\text{dBi}} = 10 \log_{10} X\,$. - [ ] KDB practical formula (HPBW in degrees): $\,D_0 \approx 41\,253 / (\theta_1°\, \theta_2°)\,$. - [ ] $D_0 = 1.5$ for an **infinitesimal dipole**; $D_0 = 1.64$ for a **half-wave dipole**. - [ ] Isotropic antenna: $D_0 = 1$ (0 dBi) by definition. - [ ] Partial directivity decomposition: $D = D_\theta + D_\phi$. - [ ] **Realised gain** = $e_{cd} \cdot e_{\text{match}} \cdot D_0$ (also includes reflection coefficient). - [ ] $P_{\text{rad}} = e_{cd}\, P_{\text{in}}$ — efficiency *multiplies* input power. - [ ] The product $D_0 \cdot \Omega_A = 4\pi$ holds exactly for *any* antenna (no approximation). - [ ] Always state assumptions: lossless vs lossy, units of $\Omega_A$, side-lobe neglect. - [ ] Higher gain ⇒ narrower beam ⇒ smaller beam solid angle ⇒ higher antenna size (in wavelengths). - [ ] For typical exam problems, round final answers to **2–3 significant figures**. - [ ] In dB form, **addition is multiplication** in linear: $G_{\text{dBi}} = D_{\text{dBi}} - L_{\text{dB}}$. - [ ] The **Friis transmission equation** (next module) uses *gain*, not directivity — keep this distinction sharp.

topics.title: Radiation Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Radiation Efficiency** of an antenna is formally defined as the ratio of the total power radiated by the antenna into free space to the total power delivered to (accepted by) the antenna at its input terminals. It quantifies how effectively an antenna converts the RF energy fed to it into electromagnetic radiation, as opposed to dissipating it as heat (ohmic/dielectric losses).

Mathematically:

$$e_{r} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{rad}} + P_{\text{loss}}}$$

where:
- $P_{\text{rad}}$ = Total radiated power (in Watts)
- $P_{\text{in}}$ = Total power accepted at the antenna input terminals
- $P_{\text{loss}}$ = Total ohmic + dielectric losses inside the antenna structure

Radiation efficiency is a **dimensionless quantity** bounded between $0 \le e_{r} \le 1$ (or $0\%$ to $100\%$). It is *not* a function of the antenna pattern shape; it only depends on the internal losses of the antenna.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Radiation efficiency is sometimes called **antenna efficiency** or **radiation efficiency factor**. It does NOT include reflection/mismatch losses at the feed. When mismatch is also accounted for, the term becomes **Total Efficiency** ($e_{t}$).

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Bucket

Imagine you are filling a leaky water bucket from a tap:
- The **water pouring OUT** represents radiated power ($P_{\text{rad}}$).
- The **water leaking from the bucket's body** (cracks/holes) represents ohmic losses ($P_{\text{loss}}$).
- The **total water entering the bucket** is input power ($P_{\text{in}}$).

A perfect bucket (no leaks) would have $e_r = 1$ (100% efficient — impossible in practice). A typical metal bucket might be 90% efficient (small leaks at seams). A poorly conducting bucket might be only 50% efficient.

Just as you want a bucket that delivers maximum water with minimum leakage, you want an antenna that radiates maximum power while dissipating minimum heat in its conductors/dielectrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):**
> - **Radiation Efficiency ($e_r$)**: dimensionless, $0 \le e_r \le 1$.
> - **Conductor loss** is modeled using the **surface resistance ($R_s = \sqrt{\pi f \mu / \sigma}$)** in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**.
> - **Directivity ($D$)**, **Gain ($G$)**, and **Beam Solid Angle ($\Omega_A$)** are all **dimensionless** but commonly expressed in **dBi or dB**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Antenna Input Power

When an antenna receives power $P_{\text{in}}$ at its feed point, the power is partitioned into two fundamental streams:

1. **Radiated Power ($P_{\text{rad}}$)** — successfully launched into space as EM waves.
2. **Ohmic + Dielectric Loss ($P_{\text{loss}}$)** — dissipated as heat within the antenna's metallic conductors and surrounding dielectric materials.

$$P_{\text{in}} \;=\; P_{\text{rad}} + P_{\text{loss}}$$

Loss mechanisms arise from:
- Finite conductivity ($\sigma$) of conductors (skin-effect losses at high frequency).
- Lossy dielectrics in the antenna substrate/insulator (loss tangent $\tan\delta \ne 0$).
- Ground-plane losses (for monopoles over finite/imperfect ground).
- Matching network losses (sometimes included separately as circuit efficiency $e_c$).

---

### 2.2 The Circuit Model — Why Efficiency Becomes a Resistance Ratio

Using the **Thevenin-equivalent circuit** of an antenna (a radiation resistance $R_{\text{rad}}$ in series with a loss resistance $R_{\text{loss}}$, both driven by the feed current $I$):

$$P_{\text{rad}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \vert I \vert^{2} \, R_{\text{rad}}$$

$$P_{\text{loss}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \vert I \vert^{2} \, R_{\text{loss}}$$

Substituting into the efficiency definition:

$$e_{r} \;=\; \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^{2} R_{\text{rad}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^{2}(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})} \;=\; \frac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}}$$

**Why this matters:** Because the common $\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^2$ factor cancels, the efficiency is reduced to a *pure resistance ratio*. This is the most useful engineering form.

---

### 2.3 Total Efficiency — Including Mismatch

A complete antenna-feed system also suffers from **impedance mismatch** at the feed point. Including this via reflection coefficient $\Gamma$:

$$e_{t} \;=\; e_{r} \cdot e_{\text{cd}} \;=\; e_{r} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^{2})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $e_t$ is the **Total Efficiency** = Radiation Efficiency $\times$ Reflection (Mismatch) Efficiency. KTU exams commonly test this distinction.

---

### 2.4 Relationship with Directivity and Gain

The **Gain** of an antenna is defined as:

$$G \;=\; e_{r} \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the **directivity**. This gives a powerful operational identity:

$$e_{r} \;=\; \frac{G}{D}$$

This is an exam-favorite — if Gain and Directivity (both in dB or linear) are known, efficiency falls out immediately.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Meaning / When Used** |
|---|---|---|
| Radiation Efficiency | $e_{r} = P_{\text{rad}} / P_{\text{in}}$ | Basic definition (always valid). |
| Resistance Form | $e_{r} = R_{\text{rad}} / (R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})$ | Circuit-model view using equivalent resistance. |
| Loss Power | $P_{\text{loss}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^{2} R_{\text{loss}}$ | Heat dissipated in antenna structure. |
| Surface Resistance | $R_{s} = \sqrt{\pi f \mu / \sigma}$ | Skin-effect loss in conductors. |
| Total Efficiency | $e_{t} = e_{r} \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^{2})$ | Includes mismatch loss at feed. |
| Gain-Efficiency Link | $G = e_{r} \cdot D \;\Rightarrow\; e_{r} = G / D$ | When G and D are known. |
| Dipole Loss Resistance | $R_{\text{loss}} = (R_{s} / (2\pi a)) \cdot \ell$ | For a thin-wire dipole of length $\ell$ and radius $a$. |
| Directivity from Beam Area | $D = 4\pi / \Omega_{A}$ | For computing G to back out $e_r$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Radiation efficiency is a critical parameter in **satellite payloads**, **RFID tags**, **mobile handset antennas**, and **wireless sensor nodes** — anywhere that delivered RF power is precious. A 3 dB efficiency loss means **half** the radiated power is wasted as heat.

---

### 2.6 Operating Logic — Why and How Each Step

- **Why efficiency matters:** A high-gain but low-efficiency antenna wastes energy and overheats. Designers minimize $R_{\text{loss}}$ by using thick, highly conductive metals (copper, silver, gold plating) and low-loss dielectric substrates (PTFE, Rogers).
- **How to improve it:** Lower $R_{\text{loss}}$ by (1) increasing conductor cross-section, (2) using Litz wire at HF/VHF, (3) choosing high-$\sigma$ metals, (4) selecting low-$\tan\delta$ substrates, (5) for monopoles, installing a perfect (radial) ground plane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Power Definition to Resistance Ratio

**Given:** $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{rad}} + P_{\text{loss}}$

**Step 1:** Write each power term using the antenna equivalent circuit driven by sinusoidal current $I$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{rad}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\vert I \vert^{2}\, R_{\text{rad}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{loss}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\vert I \vert^{2}\, R_{\text{loss}} \\[4pt]
P_{\text{in}} &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,\vert I \vert^{2}\,\bigl(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The real part of input impedance is $R_{\text{in}} = R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}$. Average power dissipated in any resistor driven by RMS-equivalent current $\vert I \vert$ uses the standard $\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^2 R$ form (peak-current convention).

**Step 2:** Form the ratio:

$$e_{r} = \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^{2}\, R_{\text{rad}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^{2}\,\bigl(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}\bigr)}$$

**Step 3:** Cancel the common factor $\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I \vert^{2}$:

$$e_{r} = \frac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}}$$

*Explanation:* Efficiency is independent of the driving current magnitude. It is a *property* of the antenna geometry and materials.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gain–Directivity–Efficiency Identity

**Given:** Antenna Gain is defined (IEEE standard) as $G = 4\pi \cdot \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{in}}}$ where $U$ is radiation intensity. Directivity is $D = 4\pi \cdot \frac{U(\theta,\phi)}{P_{\text{rad}}}$.

**Step 1:** Write the ratio:

$$\frac{G}{D} = \frac{4\pi U(\theta,\phi)/P_{\text{in}}}{4\pi U(\theta,\phi)/P_{\text{rad}}}$$

**Step 2:** Cancel $4\pi U(\theta,\phi)$:

$$\frac{G}{D} = \frac{P_{\text{rad}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = e_{r}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$G = e_{r} \cdot D$$

*Explanation:* If the antenna were lossless ($e_r = 1$), then $G = D$. Loss reduces the gain proportionally while leaving the radiation pattern (and hence $D$) untouched.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Conductor Loss Resistance of a Thin-Wire Dipole

**Given:** A cylindrical conductor of radius $a$, length $\ell$, surface resistance $R_s$, carrying a uniform sinusoidal current $I_0$.

**Step 1:** Power dissipated per unit length on the conductor surface:

$$\frac{dP_{\text{loss}}}{dz} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,R_{s} \cdot \frac{\vert I(z) \vert^{2}}{2\pi a}$$

**Step 2:** For a half-wave dipole with $I(z) = I_0 \cos(\pi z/\ell)$:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \int_{-\ell/2}^{\ell/2} \tfrac{1}{2} R_{s} \frac{I_{0}^{2} \cos^{2}(\pi z/\ell)}{2\pi a}\, dz$$

**Step 3:** Use the standard integral $\int_{-\ell/2}^{\ell/2} \cos^{2}(\pi z/\ell)\, dz = \ell/2$:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \tfrac{1}{2} R_{s} \frac{I_{0}^{2}}{2\pi a} \cdot \frac{\ell}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Equate to $P_{\text{loss}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert I_0\vert^{2} R_{\text{loss}}$ and solve:

$$R_{\text{loss}} = \frac{R_{s}\,\ell}{4\pi a}$$

*Explanation:* Wider conductors (larger $a$) → lower $R_{\text{loss}}$ → higher $e_r$. This is why fat dipoles are more efficient than thin ones at HF/VHF.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Worked Numerical Problem)

**Problem:** A half-wave dipole antenna has $R_{\text{rad}} = 73\,\Omega$, and a measured loss resistance $R_{\text{loss}} = 7\,\Omega$. Compute: (a) the radiation efficiency, (b) the total efficiency if the feed reflection coefficient magnitude $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0.2$, (c) the gain if directivity $D = 1.64$ (linear, ≈ 2.15 dBi).

**Solution (a):**

$$e_{r} = \frac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}} = \frac{73}{73 + 7} = \frac{73}{80} = 0.9125$$

In percentage: $e_r = 91.25\%$ (≈ $-0.40$ dB).

**Solution (b):**

$$e_{t} = e_{r}\cdot(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^{2}) = 0.9125 \cdot (1 - 0.04) = 0.9125 \cdot 0.96 = 0.876$$

So total efficiency ≈ $87.6\%$.

**Solution (c):**

$$G = e_{r}\cdot D = 0.9125 \cdot 1.64 = 1.496 \approx 1.75\,\text{dBi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always express intermediate ratios in linear scale; convert to dB only at the *final* step using $10\log_{10}(\cdot)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Flow in an Antenna

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how input power at the antenna terminals splits into radiated power and dissipated loss power, and how efficiency is computed at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P_source: Source Power"] --> B["Feed Line Transmission"]
    B --> C{"Mismatch at Feed? Γ ≠ 0"}
    C -- "Yes, 1 - |Γ|²" --> D["Reflected Power: |Γ|² × P_in"]
    C -- "Accepted Power P_in" --> E["Antenna Equivalent Circuit"]
    E --> F["Radiation Resistance R_rad"]
    E --> G["Loss Resistance R_loss"]
    F --> H["Radiated Power P_rad (into space)"]
    G --> I["Heat Dissipation P_loss"]
    H --> J["Compute: e_r = P_rad / P_in"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["Output: Radiation Efficiency e_r"]
    C --> L["Compute: e_t = e_r × (1 - |Γ|²)"]
    J --> L
    L --> M["Output: Total Efficiency e_t"]
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit (Thevenin Model) of a Lossy Antenna

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["V_source / Z_source"] --> feed["Feed Point: Γ"]
    feed --> Rrad["R_rad (radiation)"]
    feed --> Rloss["R_loss (ohmic + dielectric)"]
    Rrad --> out["P_rad → space"]
    Rloss --> heat["P_loss → heat"]
```

### 4.3 Loss-Analysis Decision Tree (for Self-Study)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Identify loss source"] --> Q2{"Conductor loss?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> A1["Increase conductor radius a"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Dielectric loss?"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> A2["Use low-tan δ substrate"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Ground plane loss?"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> A3["Use radial wire ground"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> A4["Efficiency limited by near-field absorption"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define radiation efficiency of an antenna. Why is it always less than or equal to unity?*

**Model Answer:**
Radiation efficiency is the ratio of total power radiated into free space to the total power accepted at the antenna input terminals:
$$e_r = P_{\text{rad}} / P_{\text{in}}$$
Since some of the accepted power is always dissipated as ohmic/dielectric losses ($P_{\text{loss}} \ge 0$), we have $P_{\text{rad}} \le P_{\text{in}}$, and hence $0 \le e_r \le 1$. Equality ($e_r = 1$) is achieved only by an idealized lossless antenna.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between radiation efficiency and total efficiency of an antenna.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Radiation efficiency ($e_r$)** accounts only for internal ohmic/dielectric losses: $e_r = P_{\text{rad}} / P_{\text{in}}$.
- **Total efficiency ($e_t$)** additionally includes mismatch (reflection) losses at the feed: $e_t = e_r \cdot (1 - \vert \Gamma \vert^2)$.
Hence $e_t \le e_r$, with equality only for a perfectly matched antenna.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the radiation efficiency of an antenna in terms of its radiation resistance and loss resistance. State clearly why the result is independent of the feed current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A quarter-wave monopole over an infinite perfect ground plane has $R_{\text{rad}} = 36.5\,\Omega$ and a measured total input resistance $R_{\text{in}} = 40\,\Omega$. Find (i) the radiation efficiency, (ii) the loss resistance, (iii) the gain if the directivity is $D = 3.28$ (linear). Express all answers in linear and dB form. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See *Derivation 1* in Section 3.1 above. Begin with $P_{\text{rad}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert I\vert^2 R_{\text{rad}}$, $P_{\text{loss}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert I\vert^2 R_{\text{loss}}$. Form the ratio; the $\tfrac{1}{2}\vert I\vert^2$ factor cancels in numerator and denominator, yielding $e_r = R_{\text{rad}} / (R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})$. Since efficiency is the ratio of two *power* quantities proportional to $\vert I\vert^2$, the current amplitude cancels — making $e_r$ a *geometric/material property* of the antenna, independent of excitation level.

**(b) Step-by-step:**

(i) The total input resistance equals $R_{\text{in}} = R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}}$ for the equivalent circuit. Thus:
$$R_{\text{loss}} = R_{\text{in}} - R_{\text{rad}} = 40 - 36.5 = 3.5\,\Omega$$

(ii) Radiation efficiency:
$$e_r = \frac{R_{\text{rad}}}{R_{\text{in}}} = \frac{36.5}{40} = 0.9125 = 91.25\%$$
In dB: $10 \log_{10}(0.9125) = -0.397$ dB.

(iii) Gain:
$$G = e_r \cdot D = 0.9125 \cdot 3.28 = 2.993 \text{ (linear)}$$
In dBi: $10 \log_{10}(2.993) = 4.76$ dBi.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write $e_r = R_{\text{rad}}/R_{\text{loss}}$ (forgetting the sum in the denominator) and consequently get a value > 1. Always use $e_r = R_{\text{rad}}/(R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})$. Also remember that for a $\lambda/4$ monopole, $R_{\text{rad}} = 36.5\,\Omega$ (half of the half-wave dipole value of $73\,\Omega$), since the perfect ground plane acts as an image duplicating the radiator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** Starting from the definition of antenna gain and directivity, show that $G = e_r \cdot D$. Mention the role of the reflection coefficient in defining total efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless sensor antenna has $D = 5$ (linear), $e_r = 0.8$, and a feed-line VSWR of $1.5$. Compute (i) the gain in dBi, (ii) the total efficiency in dB, and (iii) the radiated power if the source delivers 100 mW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See *Derivation 2* in Section 3.2. Directivity uses $P_{\text{rad}}$ in the denominator; gain uses $P_{\text{in}}$. Their ratio reduces exactly to $P_{\text{rad}}/P_{\text{in}} = e_r$. The reflection coefficient enters total efficiency through $(1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2)$, representing the fraction of source power *accepted* by the antenna.

**(b) Step-by-step:**

(i) Gain (linear): $G = e_r \cdot D = 0.8 \cdot 5 = 4$. In dBi: $10 \log_{10}(4) = 6.02$ dBi.

(ii) VSWR = 1.5 ⇒
$$\vert \Gamma \vert = \frac{\text{VSWR} - 1}{\text{VSWR} + 1} = \frac{0.5}{2.5} = 0.2$$
$$\vert \Gamma \vert^2 = 0.04$$
$$e_t = e_r (1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2) = 0.8 \cdot 0.96 = 0.768$$
In dB: $10 \log_{10}(0.768) = -1.146$ dB.

(iii) Radiated power: $P_{\text{rad}} = e_t \cdot P_{\text{source}} = 0.768 \cdot 100\,\text{mW} = 76.8\,\text{mW}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** When VSWR is given instead of $\Gamma$, students often forget the conversion $\Gamma = (\text{VSWR}-1)/(\text{VSWR}+1)$. Mistaking $\vert \Gamma \vert$ for $\vert\Gamma\vert^2$ is another common error — *efficiency* uses the squared magnitude!

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** $e_r = P_{\text{rad}} / P_{\text{in}} = R_{\text{rad}} / (R_{\text{rad}} + R_{\text{loss}})$.
- **Always between 0 and 1** (or 0%–100%).
- **Equivalent-circuit view:** $R_{\text{rad}}$ and $R_{\text{loss}}$ are *series* resistances.
- **Total Efficiency:** $e_t = e_r \cdot (1 - \vert\Gamma\vert^2)$ — accounts for mismatch.
- **Gain–Directivity Link:** $G = e_r \cdot D \Rightarrow e_r = G / D$.
- **Loss resistance of thin-wire dipole:** $R_{\text{loss}} = R_s \ell / (4\pi a)$.
- **Surface resistance:** $R_s = \sqrt{\pi f \mu / \sigma}$ — increases with frequency.
- **Half-wave dipole** standard: $R_{\text{rad}} = 73\,\Omega$.
- **Quarter-wave monopole** over perfect ground: $R_{\text{rad}} = 36.5\,\Omega$ (half of dipole, due to image).
- **To improve efficiency:** use thicker conductors, higher $\sigma$ metals, low-$\tan\delta$ substrates, perfect ground.
- **Final dB conversion** uses $10\log_{10}(\cdot)$ for power ratios.
- **Remember:** VSWR = 1.5 → $\vert \Gamma \vert = 0.2$, **NOT** 0.5.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In antenna engineering, **Impedance** is the complex frequency-dependent ratio of voltage to current at the antenna's input terminals (the feed point), encompassing the total opposition the antenna presents to the transmission line and the source. Mathematically, the antenna input impedance is defined as:

$$Z_A = \frac{V_{in}}{I_{in}} = R_A + jX_A \;\; (\Omega)$$

where the real part $R_A$ represents the **resistive component** (which includes both the radiation resistance $R_r$ and the loss resistance $R_L$), and the imaginary part $X_A$ represents the **reactive component** (the energy stored in the near-field of the antenna).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Antenna impedance is *not* a single value—it is a function of **frequency, geometry, proximity to nearby objects, feed-point location, and environmental conditions**. The design goal is to make $Z_A$ match the characteristic impedance $Z_0$ (typically **50 Ω** for RF systems) over the operating bandwidth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy swing (the antenna). To keep the swing moving smoothly with minimum effort (maximum power transfer), you must push it at the *right frequency* and *in phase* with its natural rhythm.

- The **real part ($R_A$)** is like the *friction* — some of it is useful (radiation, pushing the swing forward) and some is wasted (losses, rust on the chain).
- The **imaginary part ($X_A$)** is like the *springiness* of the chain — energy sloshes back and forth between you and the swing but does no useful work.
- **Impedance matching** is exactly like matching your pushing rhythm perfectly to the swing's natural rhythm — when matched, all your energy is transferred efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants and Standards (Bolded for Recall):**
> - Standard feed line characteristic impedance: $Z_0 = \mathbf{50\ \Omega}$ (most RF) or $\mathbf{75\ \Omega}$ (television/CATV).
> - Free-space intrinsic impedance: $\eta_0 = \mathbf{376.73\ \approx\ 377\ \Omega}$.
> - Antenna impedance is a **complex quantity**, generally written in rectangular form $R + jX$ or polar form $\vert Z \vert \angle \theta$.

## 1.3 Why Impedance Matters in KTU Examinations

Impedance is the bridge between the *transmission line* (Module 1) and *radiation* (Modules 3–5). KTU frequently tests:
- Derivation of self-impedance of a dipole (induced EMF method).
- Computation of mutual impedance between two coupled dipoles.
- Impedance matching techniques (quarter-wave transformer, stub matching).
- Thevenin equivalent circuit representation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU's Module 2 *Impedance* topic is essentially a question-paper magnet. Always expect either a 3-mark definition question or a 14-mark analytical derivation on dipole self-impedance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Antenna Input Impedance

The total input impedance of a transmitting antenna is fundamentally decomposed as:

$$Z_A = (R_r + R_L) + jX_A$$

### 2.1.1 Radiation Resistance ($R_r$)
The **radiation resistance** is a *fictitious resistance* that, when replaced in series with the antenna terminals, would dissipate the same average power as the antenna actually radiates into free space.

- It is **not a physical resistor** — it represents the *power radiated by the antenna* computed as $I^2 R_r$.
- For a **Hertzian (infinitesimal) dipole** of length $l \ll \lambda$:

$$R_r = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \;\; (\Omega)$$

- For a **half-wave dipole** ($l = \lambda/2$):

$$R_r \approx 73.1\ \Omega \quad \text{(at the center feed point)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is $R_r$ independent of physical conductors?** Because it is *defined* from the radiated power, not from Ohmic losses. This is a classic conceptual trap in KTU exams.

### 2.1.2 Loss Resistance ($R_L$)
The **loss resistance** accounts for the *actual* dissipative losses in the antenna system:
- Ohmic losses in metallic conductors (skin effect).
- Dielectric losses in insulation.
- Ground plane losses (for monopoles).

$$R_L = \frac{P_{loss}}{(\frac{1}{2}) \vert I_0 \vert^2}$$

### 2.1.3 Antenna Reactance ($X_A$)
The **reactive part** represents the energy stored in the near-field region. It is:
- Frequency dependent.
- A function of antenna geometry and feed-point position.
- **Zero at resonance** (e.g., a half-wave dipole has $X_A \approx 0$ at $f_0$).

## 2.2 Self-Impedance and Mutual Impedance

When **multiple antennas** are present (arrays), the input impedance of any one antenna depends on the currents in all others.

### 2.2.1 Self-Impedance ($Z_{ii}$)
The self-impedance of the $i^{th}$ antenna is its input impedance when *all other antennas are open-circuited* (or absent):

$$Z_{ii} = \frac{V_i}{I_i}\bigg|_{I_j = 0,\ j \neq i}$$

### 2.2.2 Mutual Impedance ($Z_{ij}$)
The mutual impedance between antennas $i$ and $j$ represents the *EM coupling* through space — the voltage induced in antenna $i$ per unit current in antenna $j$, with antenna $i$ open-circuited:

$$Z_{ij} = \frac{V_i}{I_j}\bigg|_{I_k = 0,\ k \neq j}$$

### 2.2.3 Network Equation (Kron's Formulation)
The total driving-point voltage at antenna $i$ is given by the matrix equation:

$$V_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N} Z_{ij} I_j \quad \text{or} \quad \mathbf{V} = \mathbf{Z} \cdot \mathbf{I}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a 2-element array**, the input impedance of element 1 becomes:
> $$Z_{in,1} = Z_{11} - \frac{I_2}{I_1} Z_{12}$$
> The negative sign comes from the *receiving-mode* perspective of mutual coupling (induced EMF opposes driving EMF).

## 2.3 Thevenin Equivalent Circuit

From the antenna's perspective, the receiving system is best modeled as a Thevenin equivalent:

$$V_{oc} = -E_{antenna} \cdot \frac{Z_L}{Z_L + Z_A} \quad \text{(load voltage)}$$

The antenna itself behaves as an EMF source $V_{oc}$ in series with its internal impedance $Z_A$, terminated by the receiver's input impedance $Z_L$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **When to Use** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| Input Impedance | $Z_A$ | $R_A + jX_A$ | $\Omega$ | Any antenna analysis |
| Hertzian Dipole $R_r$ | $R_r$ | $80\pi^2 (l/\lambda)^2$ | $\Omega$ | $l \ll \lambda$ |
| Half-Wave Dipole $R_r$ | $R_r$ | $\approx 73.1\ \Omega$ | $\Omega$ | $l = \lambda/2$, center feed |
| Folded Dipole $R_r$ | $R_r$ | $4 \times 73.1 \approx 292\ \Omega$ | $\Omega$ | Two-wire folded radiator |
| Loss Resistance | $R_L$ | $2 P_{loss} / \vert I_0 \vert^2$ | $\Omega$ | Loss analysis |
| Mutual Impedance | $Z_{ij}$ | $V_i / I_j \vert_{I_k=0, k \neq j}$ | $\Omega$ | Array analysis |
| Array Input Impedance | $Z_{in,i}$ | $Z_{ii} - (I_j/I_i) Z_{ij}$ | $\Omega$ | Driven arrays |
| VSWR | $\rho$ | $(1 + \vert \Gamma \vert)/(1 - \vert \Gamma \vert)$ | dimensionless | Mismatch measurement |
| Reflection Coeff. | $\Gamma$ | $(Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ | dimensionless | Reflection analysis |
| Return Loss | $RL$ | $-20 \log_{10} \vert \Gamma \vert$ | dB | Mismatch measure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule:** All absolute-value bars above use the LaTeX `\vert` syntax to prevent table-breaking issues. Never use the raw `|` symbol inside table cells.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Antenna impedance is critical in:
- **Mobile phones** — PIFA antennas tuned to **50 Ω** for the GSM/LTE bands.
- **Satellite dishes** — feed horns matched across multi-GHz bandwidths.
- **Radar systems** — phased arrays require careful mutual-coupling compensation.
- **RFID tags** — extreme impedance mismatch environments (free space → IC chip).
- **Medical implants** — miniature antennas with severe loss resistance due to human tissue.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Self-Impedance of a Hertzian Dipole Using Induced EMF Method

The induced EMF method (Pocklington / Carter) computes the self-impedance from the scattered near-field reactance. The classical *antenna as a scatterer* model yields:

### Step 1 — Define the Impedance Integral

The self-impedance is defined through the power-balance equation:

$$Z_{11} = \frac{2 P_{in}}{\vert I_0 \vert^2}$$

where $P_{in}$ is the complex input power (real part = radiated + loss; imaginary part = stored reactive energy).

### Step 2 — Express Complex Power via Field Integration

The complex power delivered to the antenna is computed by integrating the Poynting vector over a closed surface surrounding the antenna:

$$P_{in} = -\frac{1}{2} \oint_S \mathbf{E}_s \times \mathbf{H}_s^* \cdot d\mathbf{S}$$

The negative sign indicates power flowing *into* the antenna's near-field region.

### Step 3 — Express Electric Field Along the Dipole

Using the vector potential $\mathbf{A}$ for a thin wire, the tangential electric field along the dipole surface (radius $a$) is:

$$E_z(z) = \frac{1}{j\omega \varepsilon_0} \left( \frac{\partial^2 A_z}{\partial z^2} + k^2 A_z \right)$$

### Step 4 — Vector Potential of a Center-Fed Wire

For a sinusoidal current distribution $I(z) = I_0 \sin[k(l/2 - \vert z \vert)]$ on a wire of length $l$:

$$A_z(z) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} I(z') \frac{e^{-jkR}}{R}\, dz'$$

where $R = \sqrt{(z-z')^2 + a^2}$ is the distance from source point $z'$ to observation point $z$, and $a$ is the wire radius.

### Step 5 — Combine and Simplify

Substituting into the impedance integral and using the standard boundary condition $E_{tan} = 0$ on the wire surface, we obtain the canonical self-impedance form:

$$\boxed{Z_{11} = \frac{j\omega \mu_0}{4\pi I_0^2} \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} \int_{-l/2}^{l/2} I(z) I(z') \frac{e^{-jkR}}{R}\, dz'\, dz''}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an **infinitesimal (Hertzian) dipole** with $l \ll \lambda$, the current is approximately uniform ($I(z) \approx I_0$), and the impedance reduces to its closed-form expression.

### Step 6 — Closed-Form Hertzian Dipole Result

Performing the integration (well-known in Balanis, *Antenna Theory*, 4th ed., Ch. 4) yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{in} &= R_r + jX_A \\
&= 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \\
&\quad - j\, 120\,\frac{1}{k a} \left[ 1 - \frac{(kl)^2}{8} - \frac{(kl)^4}{192} + \cdots \right]
\end{aligned}$$

The **real part** is the radiation resistance (derived in Module 3), and the **imaginary part** is a strong negative reactance, meaning a Hertzian dipole is *always capacitive* (electrically short).

## 3.2 Derivation: Mutual Impedance of Two Parallel Dipoles

For two parallel center-fed dipoles separated by distance $d$ (in the H-plane) and both of length $l$, the mutual impedance $Z_{21}$ is computed by the same induced EMF method, but now the integration is performed over antenna 2 with the current of antenna 1 acting as the source.

### Step 1 — Mutual Impedance Integral

$$Z_{21} = \frac{-1}{I_{1} I_{2}^{*}} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} E_{z21}(z) I_{2}^{*}(z)\, dz$$

where $E_{z21}(z)$ is the $z$-component of the electric field produced by antenna 1, evaluated along the length of antenna 2.

### Step 2 — Far-Field Approximation (Fraunhofer Region)

When $d \gg l$, the mutual impedance reduces to the far-field form:

$$Z_{21} \approx \frac{j\eta_0}{4\pi} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} I_1(z') I_2(z) \frac{e^{-jkR}}{R}\, dz\, dz'$$

### Step 3 — Result for Half-Wave Dipoles ($l = \lambda/2$)

For two parallel half-wave dipoles with collinear axes, the mutual impedance (in Ω) can be tabulated. For example, for **side-by-side** (broadside) separation $d = 0.1\lambda$:

$$Z_{21} \approx 73.1 - j\, 27.5 \;\; \Omega$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** The mutual impedance is **complex**, even for lossless antennas. KTU expects students to write the answer as $R + jX$ form, not just the magnitude.

## 3.3 Derivation: Radiation Resistance from Power Integration

The **radiation resistance** of a Hertzian dipole can be derived from the time-averaged Poynting vector integrated over a sphere of radius $r$.

### Step 1 — Far-Zone Electric Field

For a Hertzian dipole at the origin aligned along $\hat{z}$:

$$\mathbf{E}_\theta = j\eta_0 k I_0 l \sin\theta \frac{e^{-jkr}}{4\pi r}$$

### Step 2 — Time-Averaged Poynting Vector

$$\langle \mathbf{S} \rangle = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\vert E_\theta \vert^2}{\eta_0} \hat{r}$$

Substituting and squaring:

$$\langle \mathbf{S} \rangle = \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 l \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2 \hat{r}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Sphere

Total radiated power:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{rad} &= \oint \langle \mathbf{S} \rangle \cdot d\mathbf{S} \\
&= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} \frac{\eta_0}{2} \left(\frac{k I_0 l \sin\theta}{4\pi r}\right)^2 r^2 \sin\theta\, d\theta\, d\phi
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Angular Integral

Using $\int_0^\pi \sin^3\theta\, d\theta = 4/3$:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{\eta_0 k^2 I_0^2 l^2}{12\pi}$$

### Step 5 — Equate to $I^2 R / 2$ to Find $R_r$

Using $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$:

$$P_{rad} = \frac{1}{2} I_0^2 R_r \;\Longrightarrow\; R_r = \frac{\eta_0 \pi}{3} \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

Substituting $\eta_0 = 120\pi$:

$$\boxed{R_r = 40\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 80\pi^2 \left(\frac{l}{\lambda}\right)^2 \;\; \Omega}$$

(The factor of 2 difference arises depending on whether peak or RMS current is used in the power formula — verify with your instructor.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Antenna Impedance Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Antenna Geometry & Excitation] --> nodeB[Compute Current Distribution I_z]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Vector Potential A_z]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Compute E-field Tangent to Wire]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Induced EMF Integration]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Analysis Type}
    nodeF -->|Single Antenna| nodeG[Self-Impedance Z11]
    nodeF -->|Two Antennas| nodeH[Mutual Impedance Z12]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Radiation Resistance R_r]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Reactive Component jX_A]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Power Radiated]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Near-Field Energy Storage]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Array Input Impedance]
    nodeK --> nodeN[Impedance Matching Network]
    nodeL --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO[Matched to Z0 equals 50 ohm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the complete impedance-computation pipeline used in KTU Module 2. Note the branching at **Analysis Type**: a single antenna terminates at self-impedance, while a system of two or more antennas leads to mutual coupling and the Kron network equation.

## 4.2 Thevenin Equivalent Circuit of an Antenna (Receiving Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Incident EM Wave] --> nodeB[Antenna Acts as Source]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Thevenin EMF V_oc]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Series Impedance Z_A]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Load Impedance Z_L]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Receiver / Detector]
    nodeC -.- nodeG[Z_A = R_r + R_L + jX_A]
    nodeD -.- nodeG
    nodeE -.- nodeH[Power Transfer Max when Z_L = Z_A_conj]
```

## 4.3 Impedance Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1[Z_A Total Input Impedance] --> node2[R_A Resistive Part]
    node1 --> node3[jX_A Reactive Part]
    node2 --> node4[R_r Radiation Resistance]
    node2 --> node5[R_L Loss Resistance]
    node3 --> node6[Near-Field Stored Energy]
    node4 -.- node7[Power Radiated into Free Space]
    node5 -.- node8[Conductor and Dielectric Losses]
    node6 -.- node9[Capacitive or Inductive Behavior]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the equivalent circuit of an antenna," always include the **radiation resistance $R_r$** as a *separate* component from the **loss resistance $R_L$** in series with $jX_A$. Do not combine them prematurely.

## 4.4 Impedance Matching Architecture (Preview for Module 3)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Source Z_s] --> nodeB[Transmission Line Z_0]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Matching Network]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Antenna Z_A]
    nodeC -.- nodeE[Quarter-Wave Transformer]
    nodeC -.- nodeF[Stub Tuner]
    nodeC -.- nodeG[Tapered Line]
    nodeB -.- nodeH[VSWR Measurement Point]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the term *radiation resistance* of an antenna. Why is it considered a fictitious quantity?

**Model Answer:**

Radiation resistance $R_r$ is the equivalent resistance that would dissipate the same average power as the antenna actually radiates into free space, when carrying the same RMS current at its feed point. It is calculated as:

$$R_r = \frac{2 P_{rad}}{I_0^2}$$

It is called *fictitious* because it does not represent an actual physical resistor — no real heating occurs. It is a conceptual construct that allows antenna engineers to treat radiation as a circuit element, making the input impedance $Z_A$ a meaningful complex quantity that obeys Ohm's law at the feed terminals. (3 marks)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between *self-impedance* and *mutual impedance* in antenna arrays.

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Self-Impedance ($Z_{ii}$)** | **Mutual Impedance ($Z_{ij}$)** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Definition | Input impedance of antenna $i$ alone | Voltage induced in antenna $i$ per unit current in antenna $j$ |
| Coupling | None — antenna isolated | Via EM fields in space |
| Formula | $Z_{ii} = V_i / I_i \vert_{I_k=0}$ | $Z_{ij} = V_i / I_j \vert_{I_k=0, k\neq j}$ |
| Magnitude | Generally larger | Decreases with separation $d$ |

(3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Primary Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** With reference to the *induced EMF method*:

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **self-impedance of a thin-wire dipole** in terms of the current distribution. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence, compute the **radiation resistance** of a **Hertzian dipole** of length $l = 0.05\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The dipole is a thin wire ($a \ll \lambda$, $a \ll l$).
2. Current distribution is purely sinusoidal: $I(z) = I_0 \sin[k(l/2 - \vert z \vert)]$.
3. The wire is perfectly conducting.
