$$R_r = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2} \quad \text{(Reflected path length)}$$

For $d \gg h_t,\, h_r$ (the standard "far-field" plane-earth assumption), these reduce to:

$$R_d \approx d + \frac{(h_t - h_r)^2}{2d}, \quad R_r \approx d + \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2}{2d}$$

## 2.2 Path Difference & Phase Difference

The excess path length travelled by the reflected ray over the direct ray is the **path difference** $\Delta$:

$$\Delta = R_r - R_d = \frac{2\,h_t\,h_r}{d}$$

Correspondingly, the **phase difference** introduced by this path difference is:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta = \frac{4\pi\,h_t\,h_r}{\lambda\,d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:**
> Notice that $\Delta$ depends **only on geometry** ($h_t, h_r, d$), not on frequency. However, the **phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ is **inversely proportional to wavelength $\lambda$**, which means at higher frequencies (smaller $\lambda$) the phase wraps more rapidly, producing denser interference lobes.

## 2.3 Fresnel Reflection Coefficients

The reflection coefficient depends on the **polarization** of the incident wave. Two canonical cases are considered:

**Case 1 — Horizontal Polarization (E-field parallel to the ground, ⊥ to the plane of incidence):**

$$\Gamma_H = \frac{\sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}{\sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}$$

**Case 2 — Vertical Polarization (E-field in the plane of incidence):**

$$\Gamma_V = \frac{\varepsilon_r \sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}{\varepsilon_r \sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These expressions are valid for a **lossless dielectric** ($\sigma = 0$, $\chi = 0$). For a **lossy** ground, replace $\varepsilon_r$ with the complex permittivity $\varepsilon_r - j\chi$, where $\chi = \sigma / (\omega\varepsilon_0)$.

**Limiting cases every KTU student must memorise:**

- **Normal incidence** ($\psi = 90^\circ$): $\Gamma_H = \Gamma_V = \dfrac{1 - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{1 + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$
- **Grazing incidence** ($\psi \to 0$): $\Gamma_H \to -1$ and $\Gamma_V \to -1$ for any finite $\varepsilon_r$
- **Perfect electric conductor** ($\varepsilon_r \to \infty$): $\Gamma_H \to -1$ and $\Gamma_V \to +1$ for *all* $\psi$

## 2.4 Brewster Angle (Polarization-Specific Null)

The **Brewster angle** $\psi_B$ is the grazing angle at which $\Gamma_V = 0$, i.e. the reflected vertically-polarised wave vanishes completely. Setting the numerator of $\Gamma_V$ to zero yields:

$$\tan\psi_B = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tan\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$$

where $\theta_B = 90^\circ - \psi_B$ is the angle measured from the ground normal. **For horizontal polarization no Brewster angle exists** — $\Gamma_H$ is non-zero for every $\psi$.

## 2.5 Total Received Field — Two-Ray Superposition

The total received field is the **phasor sum** of the direct and reflected waves:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_0 \left[1 + \Gamma\, e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right]$$

where $E_0$ is the free-space direct-ray field amplitude at the receiver, and $\Gamma$ is the appropriate reflection coefficient ($H$ or $V$). For the common approximation $\Gamma \approx -1$ (valid for small $\psi$ over any reasonably conducting ground):

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_0\left(1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right)$$

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}} \big\vert = 2 E_0 \left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) \right\vert$$

## 2.6 Asymptotic Field-Strength Laws

For **large $d$** (small $\Delta\phi$): $\sin(x) \approx x$ and $E_0 \propto 1/d$ in free space, giving the famous **$1/d^2$ law for elevated antennas**:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}} \big\vert \approx \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda\, d^2}\,\sqrt{30\,P_t G_t} \quad \text{(High-antenna, far-field regime)}$$

For **small $d$** (large $\Delta\phi$): the field oscillates with distance, producing **interference lobes and nulls** — the spatial multipath pattern. Nulls occur when $h_t h_r / d = n\lambda/2$ for integer $n$.

> [!TIP]
> The $1/d^2$ law is **independent of frequency** in its leading form (the $\lambda$ in the numerator is cancelled by the $1/\lambda$ hidden in $E_0$'s definition at unit distance). This is why VHF television signals at the same power produce *similar* field strength at the same height–distance product, regardless of channel.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Direct path length | $R_d$ | $\sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2}$ | metres |
| 2 | Reflected path length | $R_r$ | $\sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2}$ | metres |
| 3 | Path difference | $\Delta$ | $2 h_t h_r / d$ | metres; valid for $d \gg h_t, h_r$ |
| 4 | Phase difference | $\Delta\phi$ | $4 \pi h_t h_r / (\lambda d)$ | radians |
| 5 | Refl. coeff. — H-pol | $\Gamma_H$ | $(\sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}) / (\sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi})$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Refl. coeff. — V-pol | $\Gamma_V$ | $(\varepsilon_r \sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}) / (\varepsilon_r \sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi})$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Brewster angle (grazing) | $\psi_B$ | $\arctan(1/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ | only for V-pol |
| 8 | Brewster angle (incidence) | $\theta_B$ | $\arctan(\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ | standard form |
| 9 | Total field magnitude | $\vert E_{\text{total}}\vert$ | $2 E_0 \big\vert \sin(2\pi h_t h_r/(\lambda d)) \big\vert$ | V/m |
| 10 | Far-field envelope | $\vert E_{\text{total}}\vert$ | $4\pi h_t h_r \sqrt{30 P_t G_t} / (\lambda d^2)$ | $1/d^2$ law |
| 11 | Free-space field | $E_0$ | $\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}/d$ | V/m |
| 12 | Rayleigh roughness limit | $h_{\text{crit}}$ | $\lambda / (8 \sin\psi)$ | metres |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All $\vert \cdot \vert$ symbols in the table use $\big\vert$ for typographic clarity; LaTeX auto-renders them as proper absolute-value delimiters. **Do NOT confuse** $\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert$ (a magnitude) with $\big\vert \sin(\cdot)\big\vert$ (the absolute value of the sine function, needed because $\sin$ can be negative).

## 2.8 Practical Engineering Utility

The plane earth model underpins link-budget calculations in **cellular network design (4G/5G)**, **FM radio coverage planning**, **DTH satellite TV interference analysis**, and **military HF–VHF communications**. A KTU examiner frequently tests whether the student can (a) recognise when plane-earth is valid (smooth, flat surface, $f \ge 30\,\text{MHz}$), (b) compute $\Delta$ and $\Delta\phi$ correctly, and (c) translate these into a usable field-strength vs. distance curve.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Path Difference $\Delta = 2 h_t h_r / d$

**Starting point:** The direct and reflected ray lengths from $T(0,0,h_t)$ to $R(d,0,h_r)$ via the ground point $Q$:

$$R_d = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2}, \qquad R_r = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2}$$

**Step 1:** Factor $d$ out of each square root:

$$R_d = d\,\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{h_t - h_r}{d}\right)^2}, \qquad R_r = d\,\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{h_t + h_r}{d}\right)^2}$$

**Step 2:** Use the binomial approximation $(1+x)^{1/2} \approx 1 + x/2$ for $x \ll 1$. Since $d \gg h_t, h_r$, both $x$-values satisfy this condition.

$$R_d \approx d\left[1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{h_t - h_r}{d}\right)^2\right] = d + \frac{(h_t - h_r)^2}{2d}$$

$$R_r \approx d\left[1 + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{h_t + h_r}{d}\right)^2\right] = d + \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2}{2d}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract $R_d$ from $R_r$:

\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= R_r - R_d \\
&= \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2 - (h_t - h_r)^2}{2d} \\
&= \frac{\left[h_t^2 + 2 h_t h_r + h_r^2\right] - \left[h_t^2 - 2 h_t h_r + h_r^2\right]}{2d} \\
&= \frac{4 h_t h_r}{2d} \\
&= \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4:** Convert to phase difference using $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda)\,\Delta$:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\cdot\frac{2 h_t h_r}{d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The reflected ray effectively detours by an extra distance equal to the **vertical separation of the image from the receiver**, divided by the slant range. For flat-earth geometry, the path difference is independent of the precise reflection-point location — a beautiful result of the small-angle approximation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Field Magnitude $\vert E_{\text{total}}\vert = 2E_0 \big\vert \sin(\Delta\phi/2) \big\vert$

**Starting assumption:** For small grazing angle over any reasonably conducting ground, $\Gamma \approx -1$. The total field at the receiver is the phasor sum of the direct and reflected waves:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_0\left[1 + \Gamma\, e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right] = E_0\left[1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right]$$

**Step 1:** Expand the complex exponential using Euler's identity:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_0\left[1 - \cos(\Delta\phi) + j\sin(\Delta\phi)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Compute the magnitude squared:

\begin{aligned}
\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert^2 &= E_0^2\left[(1 - \cos\Delta\phi)^2 + \sin^2\Delta\phi\right] \\
&= E_0^2\left[1 - 2\cos\Delta\phi + \cos^2\Delta\phi + \sin^2\Delta\phi\right] \\
&= E_0^2\left[1 - 2\cos\Delta\phi + 1\right] \\
&= 2E_0^2\left[1 - \cos\Delta\phi\right]
\end{aligned}

**Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $1 - \cos x = 2\sin^2(x/2)$:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert^2 = 2E_0^2 \cdot 2\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = 4E_0^2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Take the square root:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert = 2E_0 \left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \right\vert = 2E_0 \left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) \right\vert$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the free-space direct field $E_0 = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}\,/\,d$:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert = \frac{2}{d}\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}\;\left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) \right\vert$$

> [!TIP]
> This expression has a **deep engineering consequence:** because of the $\sin(\cdot)$ function, the received field oscillates between $0$ (null) and $2E_0$ (max) as $d$ varies. The first null occurs at $d_{\text{null}} = 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$, and the field-strength pattern resembles a **chopped sinusoid** over a $1/d$ envelope. This is the classic "interference pattern" seen in practice when a receiver moves away from a transmitter in flat terrain.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Asymptotic $1/d^2$ Field-Strength Law

**Starting point:** Use the small-angle approximation $\sin(x) \approx x$ valid when $x \ll 1$, i.e. when $\Delta\phi/2 = 2\pi h_t h_r/(\lambda d) \ll 1$, i.e. $d \gg 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$.

**Step 1:** Replace the sine by its argument:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert \approx 2E_0 \cdot \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r E_0}{\lambda d}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the free-space field $E_0 = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}/d$:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert \approx \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}{d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}{\lambda\,d^2}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the $1/d^2$ scaling:

$$\boxed{\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert \;\propto\; \frac{h_t h_r}{d^2} \quad \text{(far-field, high-antenna, two-ray regime)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world implication:** The received power (which scales as $\vert E\vert^2$) therefore falls off as $1/d^4$, in stark contrast to the free-space $1/d^2$ power law. This is why **high-elevation base stations are economically attractive** — doubling both antenna heights gives the same far-field signal at quadruple the distance.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Brewster Angle for Vertical Polarization

**Starting point:** Set the numerator of $\Gamma_V$ equal to zero (the condition for *zero reflection*):

$$\varepsilon_r \sin\psi_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi_B}$$

**Step 1:** Square both sides:

$$\varepsilon_r^2 \sin^2\psi_B = \varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi_B$$

**Step 2:** Use the Pythagorean identity $\cos^2\psi_B = 1 - \sin^2\psi_B$:

\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_r^2 \sin^2\psi_B &= \varepsilon_r - 1 + \sin^2\psi_B \\
\varepsilon_r^2 \sin^2\psi_B - \sin^2\psi_B &= \varepsilon_r - 1 \\
\sin^2\psi_B\,(\varepsilon_r^2 - 1) &= \varepsilon_r - 1 \\
\sin^2\psi_B\,(\varepsilon_r - 1)(\varepsilon_r + 1) &= \varepsilon_r - 1
\end{aligned}

**Step 3:** Cancel $(\varepsilon_r - 1)$ (valid for $\varepsilon_r \ne 1$, i.e. earth not free space):

$$\sin^2\psi_B = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r + 1}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to tangent form. Using $\tan\psi_B = \sin\psi_B / \cos\psi_B$ and $\cos^2\psi_B = 1 - \sin^2\psi_B = \varepsilon_r / (\varepsilon_r + 1)$:

$$\tan^2\psi_B = \frac{\sin^2\psi_B}{\cos^2\psi_B} = \frac{1/(\varepsilon_r + 1)}{\varepsilon_r/(\varepsilon_r + 1)} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_r}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\psi_B = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad\text{or equivalently}\quad \tan\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$$

where $\theta_B = 90^\circ - \psi_B$ is the standard angle-of-incidence form.

**Numerical example (KTU-favourite):** For $\varepsilon_r = 4$ (typical dry soil), $\theta_B = \arctan(2) = 63.43^\circ$, so the grazing Brewster angle is $\psi_B = 26.57^\circ$.

## 3.5 Pattern Multiplication Principle (KTU Linked Concept)

When a single antenna sits above a ground plane, the total field pattern $F(\theta,\phi)$ is the **product** of the **isolated antenna pattern** $F_a(\theta,\phi)$ and the **ground-image array factor** $AF(\theta)$ for a two-element array (the real antenna and its image, separated by $2 h_t$):

$$F(\theta,\phi) = F_a(\theta,\phi) \cdot AF(\theta)$$

$$AF(\theta) = 2\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t}{\lambda}\cos\theta\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the same pattern-multiplication theorem used for linear arrays. The "ground plane" effectively turns a single monopole into an equivalent two-element array whose **maximum** is directed along the ground for $h_t = \lambda/4$ (the famous quarter-wave monopole above a ground plane).

## 3.6 Validity Checks Before Applying the Model

Before writing a plane-earth solution in your KTU answer, confirm:

1. **Smooth-surface check:** $h_{\text{rough}} < \lambda / (8 \sin\psi)$.
2. **Flat-earth check:** $d \ll$ the distance to the radio horizon $\approx \sqrt{2 h_t R_{\text{earth}}}$.
3. **Elevated-antenna check:** $h_t, h_r \ge$ several wavelengths (otherwise surface wave dominates and the model fails).
4. **Far-field check:** $d \ge 2 D^2/\lambda$ where $D$ is the largest antenna dimension.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Ray Geometry — Functional Block Representation

The physical 3-D drawing of two antennas over ground is best conveyed as a logical block diagram. Each block represents the propagation entity, and arrows show the direction of wave travel.

```mermaid
graph LR
    TX["Transmitter T at height ht"]
    RX["Receiver R at height hr"]
    Q["Reflection Point Q on Ground"]
    GND["Plane Earth Ground surface"]
    DP["Direct Ray Path Rd"]
    RP["Reflected Ray Path Rr"]
    IMG["Image Antenna T prime at depth ht"]
    TX -->|emits wave along| DP
    DP -->|arrives at| RX
    TX -->|emits wave downward to| Q
    Q -->|reflects upward to| RX
    IMG -.->|appears to originate at| Q
    GND --- Q
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted line from `IMG` to `Q` indicates a **virtual equivalence**, not a physical wave. This is the standard "method of images" trick that converts the reflection problem into a two-element array problem — extremely useful in KTU derivations.

## 4.2 Plane-Earth Reflection Analysis Workflow

The end-to-end analytical pipeline is summarised below. Each stage takes the output of the previous stage as input.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Gather ht, hr, d, lambda, polarization, epsilon_r"]
    B["Step 2: Compute grazing angle psi from geometry"]
    C["Step 3: Compute reflection coefficient Gamma from psi and epsilon_r"]
    D["Step 4: Compute path difference Delta = 2 ht hr / d"]
    E["Step 5: Compute phase difference Delta phi = 2 pi Delta / lambda"]
    F["Step 6: Compute Etotal = E0 times 1 + Gamma exp -j Delta phi"]
    G["Step 7: Take magnitude and plot versus d"]
    H{"Is d greater than 2 ht hr over lambda ?"}
    I["Small-d regime: use sinusoidal two-ray expression with full sine term"]
    J["Far-field regime: use 1 over d-squared asymptotic law"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G --> H
    H -->|No| I
    H -->|Yes| J
```

## 4.3 Pattern Multiplication Block Diagram

The interaction between the antenna's intrinsic pattern and the ground-induced array factor is shown below.

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    Fa["Isolated Antenna Pattern Fa of theta phi"]:1
    AF["Ground-Image Array Factor AF of theta"]:1
    Prod["Element-wise Product gives Total F of theta phi"]:1
    Fa --> Prod
    AF --> Prod
```

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love asking: *"Why does the pattern of a $\lambda/2$ dipole become omni-directional (toroidal) when placed over a perfect ground plane at height $h_t = \lambda/4$?"* The block diagram above is the visual answer: the array factor $2\cos(\frac{\pi}{2}\cos\theta)$ suppresses the high-elevation lobes of the dipole pattern.

## 4.4 Reflection Coefficient Behaviour (Schematic Chart Description)

The variation of $\Gamma_H$ and $\Gamma_V$ with grazing angle $\psi$ has a characteristic shape:

- **$\Gamma_H$:** starts at $-1$ at $\psi = 0$, monotonically rises, crosses zero near $\psi \approx 5^\circ$ to $10^\circ$ for typical soil ($\varepsilon_r \approx 4\text{–}15$), and approaches $+1$ at $\psi = 90^\circ$.
- **$\Gamma_V$:** starts at $-1$ at $\psi = 0$, rises, passes through **zero at the Brewster angle $\psi_B$**, and approaches $+1$ at $\psi = 90^\circ$ (after crossing zero at grazing and re-crossing at Brewster).

| Polarization | $\psi \to 0$ | $\psi = \psi_B$ | $\psi = 90^\circ$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal | $\Gamma_H = -1$ | No zero exists | $\Gamma_H = +1$ (high $\varepsilon_r$) |
| Vertical | $\Gamma_V = -1$ | $\Gamma_V = 0$ | $\Gamma_V = +1$ (high $\varepsilon_r$) |

> [!WARNING]
> The cross-over near $\psi = 0$ happens because the **radiation into the ground** at small grazing angles is dominated by the **boundary-condition enforcement of the tangential E-field** vanishing on a good conductor — both polarizations share this behaviour, hence the identical limit of $-1$.

## 4.5 Field Strength Versus Distance — Expected Curve

When $\vert E_{\text{total}}\vert$ is plotted on a log-log scale against $d$, three distinct regions appear:

```mermaid
graph LR
    R1["Region 1: d small — 1 over d envelope with sinusoidal ripples"]
    R2["Region 2: Transition — ripples damp out"]
    R3["Region 3: d large — straight line of slope minus 40 dB per decade 1 over d to the fourth power law"]
    R1 --> R2 --> R3
```

- **Region 1 ($d < 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$):** oscillating pattern of lobes and nulls.
- **Region 2 ($d \approx 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$):** transition from near-field to far-field behaviour.
- **Region 3 ($d \gg 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$):** smooth $1/d^2$ field decay (i.e. $1/d^4$ power decay).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test **Recall (L1)** and **Comprehension (L2)** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy. They expect crisp, two- to four-sentence answers supported by an equation or two.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define plane earth reflection and state the assumptions under which the two-ray model is valid for radio wave propagation analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

Plane earth reflection is a high-frequency ($\ge 30\,\text{MHz}$) propagation mechanism in which a radio wave from a transmitting antenna reaches a receiving antenna over a flat earth via two paths — a direct line-of-sight ray and a ray that reflects from the ground. The two waves interfere at the receiver to give the total field. The model is valid when **(i)** the earth surface is geometrically flat, **(ii)** the surface is smooth per the Rayleigh criterion $h_{\text{rough}} < \lambda/(8\sin\psi)$, **(iii)** both antennas are elevated by several wavelengths above the surface, and **(iv)** the operating frequency is high enough that ionospheric and surface-wave components can be ignored. Under these conditions, the total field is

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert = 2 E_0 \left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) \right\vert$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is Brewster angle? Derive an expression for it for vertical polarization over a lossless dielectric ground.**

**Model Answer:**

The Brewster angle is the angle of incidence (or grazing angle) at which the reflection coefficient of a wave of a given polarization falls to zero. For vertical polarization over a lossless ground with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, the Brewster grazing angle is obtained by setting the numerator of $\Gamma_V$ to zero:

$$\varepsilon_r \sin\psi_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi_B}$$

Squaring and simplifying (full derivation in §3.4) gives

$$\tan\psi_B = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad \text{or} \quad \tan\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$$

For example, with $\varepsilon_r = 9$ (typical wet soil), $\theta_B = \arctan(3) = 71.57^\circ$. No Brewster angle exists for horizontal polarization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions carry **14 marks** each and must be answered with sub-parts. The two-question internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part (a) and (b) is worth 7 marks. The cognitive level is **Application (L3)** or **Analysis (L4)**.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**Derive the expression for the total electric field at the receiver in the two-ray model of plane earth reflection. Hence obtain the asymptotic $1/d^2$ field-strength law for elevated antennas, and discuss its practical significance in VHF/UHF link design.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Two-Ray Total Field (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Geometry.** Place the transmitter at height $h_t$ and receiver at height $h_r$, separated by horizontal distance $d$ over a flat ground. The direct and reflected ray lengths are

$$R_d = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2}, \quad R_r = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Path difference.** Using binomial expansion for $d \gg h_t, h_r$:

$$R_d \approx d + \frac{(h_t - h_r)^2}{2d}, \quad R_r \approx d + \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2}{2d}$$

$$\Delta = R_r - R_d = \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Phase difference.**

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\cdot\frac{2 h_t h_r}{d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}$$

**Step 4 — Total field via phasor sum.** For small grazing angle, $\Gamma \approx -1$:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_0\left[1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude.** Using $1 - e^{-jx} = 2j\sin(x/2)\,e^{-jx/2}$ and taking the modulus:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert = 2E_0 \left\vert \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) \right\vert \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}$$

#### Part (b) — Asymptotic $1/d^2$ Law and Practical Significance (7 marks)

**Step 6 — Asymptotic limit.** For $d \gg 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$, the argument of the sine is small, hence $\sin(x) \approx x$:

$$\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert \approx 2E_0 \cdot \frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r E_0}{\lambda d}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute free-space field** $E_0 = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}/d$:

$$\boxed{\big\vert E_{\text{total}}\big\vert = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda\,d^2}\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}$$

**Step 8 — Engineering significance.**

1. **Doubling antenna heights** quadruples the far-field signal at a given distance — the strongest justification for high TV/cellular towers.
2. **Power decay rate is $1/d^4$** (because power $\propto \vert E\vert^2$), much faster than free-space $1/d^2$. Receivers beyond the transition distance must be carefully link-budgeted.
3. **Frequency independence:** the $\lambda$ in the numerator cancels with the $1/\lambda$ in the free-space $E_0$ definition at unit distance, so the far-field strength is **nearly the same across VHF/UHF bands** at fixed $h_t, h_r, d$ — explaining why the old analog TV channels and modern cellular bands can share tower infrastructure.
4. **Holds only beyond the "break-point distance"** $d_b = 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$. Below this distance the multipath lobe-null pattern dominates. **7 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Many students write the $1/d^2$ law as if it were valid for **all** $d$. Marks are deducted for not stating the **break-point distance** $d_b = 2 h_t h_r/\lambda$ and the **condition** $d > d_b$. Always include the inequality in your answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Explain the Fresnel reflection coefficients for horizontal and vertical polarizations. Derive the Brewster angle for vertical polarization. Discuss the effect of polarization on the ground-reflected pattern of a VHF antenna.**

#### Part (a) — Fresnel Reflection Coefficients (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Boundary conditions.** When a plane wave is incident on a flat dielectric interface, the tangential E-field and tangential H-field must be continuous. Solving Maxwell's equations at the boundary yields two distinct reflection coefficients depending on whether the E-field is **perpendicular** to the plane of incidence (horizontal polarization) or **parallel** to it (vertical polarization).

**Step 2 — Horizontal polarization (TE / s-polarization).** The E-field is parallel to the ground (⊥ to plane of incidence). Solving the boundary-value problem gives:

$$\Gamma_H = \frac{\sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}{\sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}$$

For a perfect conductor ($\varepsilon_r \to \infty$), $\Gamma_H \to -1$ at all angles — the tangential E-field must vanish on the surface.

**Step 3 — Vertical polarization (TM / p-polarization).** The E-field lies in the plane of incidence. The reflection coefficient is:

$$\Gamma_V = \frac{\varepsilon_r \sin\psi - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}{\varepsilon_r \sin\psi + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi}}$$

For a perfect conductor, $\Gamma_V \to +1$ at all angles.

**Step 4 — Limiting cases.**

- $\psi \to 0$ (grazing): $\Gamma_H \to -1$ and $\Gamma_V \to -1$ for any finite $\varepsilon_r$.
- $\psi \to 90^\circ$ (normal): $\Gamma_H = \Gamma_V = (1 - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r})/(1 + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$.
- $\psi = \psi_B$ (Brewster, V-pol only): $\Gamma_V = 0$, $\Gamma_H \ne 0$. **7 marks**

#### Part (b) — Brewster Angle Derivation & Practical Effect (7 marks)

**Step 5 — Brewster angle derivation.** Setting $\Gamma_V = 0$ (numerator zero) for vertical polarization:

$$\varepsilon_r \sin\psi_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - \cos^2\psi_B}$$

Squaring, applying the Pythagorean identity, and simplifying (as in §3.4) gives:

$$\tan\psi_B = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \quad \text{or} \quad \tan\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$$

**Step 6 — Example.** For wet soil ($\varepsilon_r = 15$), $\theta_B = \arctan(\sqrt{15}) = 75.5^\circ$, so $\psi_B = 14.5^\circ$.

**Step 7 — Effect on VHF antenna pattern.**

- For **horizontal polarization**, the reflection coefficient is **always close to $-1$** for typical VHF grazing angles, so the **lobe-null pattern is strong** and the radiation pattern has pronounced nulls at low elevation. This is why **old analog TV transmitters used horizontal polarization** — it gave a sharp main lobe along the horizon and rejected high-angle multipath.
- For **vertical polarization**, near the Brewster angle the ground reflection is greatly reduced. This means the **pattern has fewer nulls** and the antenna radiates more energy upward, which can be a problem for broadcasting but is exploited in **maritime VHF and aircraft communications** where signal is needed at high elevation angles.
- At the Brewster grazing angle $\psi_B$, a vertically-polarized antenna's ground-reflection contribution vanishes, leaving only the direct path. The pattern at that elevation is therefore identical to the free-space pattern. **7 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Most Common Mark-Loss Area:**
> (1) Students often **forget to write the Brewster condition for V-pol only** and incorrectly state it exists for H-pol — full 1–2 mark penalty.
> (2) Many write the Brewster formula as $\tan\theta_B = \varepsilon_r$ instead of $\tan\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ — a 1-mark deduction.
> (3) Failing to **state units** of $h_t, h_r, d, \lambda$ (all in metres) when plugging into the path-difference formula results in mark loss in numerical sub-parts.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Module 4 — Plane Earth Reflection**

- [x] **Two-ray model** = direct ray + ground-reflected ray, both arriving at receiver.
- [x] **Assumptions:** flat earth, smooth surface ($\varepsilon_r, \sigma$ constant), $f \ge 30\,\text{MHz}$, $h_t, h_r \ge$ several $\lambda$, $d \gg h_t, h_r$.
- [x] **Path difference:** $\Delta = 2 h_t h_r / d$. **Phase difference:** $\Delta\phi = 4\pi h_t h_r / (\lambda d)$.
- [x] **Reflection coefficients:** $\Gamma_H$ for horizontal, $\Gamma_V$ for vertical polarization.
- [x] **Limiting values:** $\Gamma \to -1$ as $\psi \to 0$; $\Gamma \to (1 - \sqrt{\varepsilon_r})/(1 + \sqrt{\varepsilon_r})$ at normal incidence.
- [x] **Brewster angle:** $\tan\theta_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$; exists **only for vertical polarization**.
- [x] **Total field (general):** $E_{\text{total}} = E_0(1 + \Gamma e^{-j\Delta\phi})$.
- [x] **Total field ($\Gamma = -1$):** $\vert E_{\text{total}}\vert = 2 E_0 \big\vert \sin(2\pi h_t h_r/(\lambda d))\big\vert$.
- [x] **Far-field $1/d^2$ law:** $\vert E_{\text{total}}\vert \approx 4\pi h_t h_r \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}/(\lambda d^2)$ — valid for $d > 2 h_t h_r / \lambda$.
- [x] **Pattern multiplication:** Total pattern = isolated pattern × ground-image array factor.
- [x] **Rayleigh roughness check:** $h_{\text{rough}} < \lambda/(8 \sin\psi)$ — otherwise ground is "rough" and specular reflection fails.
- [x] **Practical use:** VHF/UHF broadcasting towers, cellular link budgets, satellite multipath prediction.
- [x] **Key constants to memorize:** $\sqrt{30} \approx 5.477$ (for free-space $E_0$ formula), $4\pi \approx 12.566$.
- [x] **Common numerical trick:** A $1\,\text{dB}$ loss in $\Gamma$ magnitude (compared to $-1$) means a $2\,\text{dB}$ change in the two-ray field strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Advice for KTU 2024:** Always begin a plane-earth answer by stating the **assumptions and the coordinate system**. Then derive the path difference, then apply the appropriate reflection coefficient. End with the **physical interpretation** (interference pattern, $1/d^2$ law, Brewster-angle significance). This four-part structure is what KTU examiners look for and yields full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Space Wave and Surface Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Space Wave and Surface Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Overview of Ground Wave Propagation

When a transmitting antenna radiates electromagnetic energy near the surface of the Earth, the resulting wave reaching the receiver is collectively called a **Ground Wave**. The ground wave is the superposition of three physically distinct components:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ground Wave = Space Wave (Direct + Ground-Reflected) + Surface Wave**

| Component | Mechanism | Dominant Range |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|
| **Space Wave** | Direct line-of-sight + ground reflected ray | VHF, UHF, Microwave |
| **Surface Wave** | Diffuses along the Earth's conducting surface | MF, LF, VLF |
| **Sky Wave** | Reflected/refracted by ionosphere | HF (not in this topic) |

### 1.2 Space Wave – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Space Wave** is the combined electromagnetic field at the receiver comprising the **Direct Wave** (line-of-sight ray travelling through the atmosphere) and the **Ground-Reflected Wave** (ray that bounces off the Earth's surface before reaching the receiver). It exists only when both transmitter and receiver antennas are within line-of-sight of each other, and is the dominant propagation mechanism at frequencies above approximately **30 MHz**.

**Conceptual Analogy – Space Wave:**
Imagine two people standing on a flat football field trying to communicate using a flashlight and a mirror. The light reaches the second person in two ways:
- **Path 1 (Direct):** Straight from flashlight to eye (the direct ray).
- **Path 2 (Reflected):** Flashlight → bounces off the wet grass → eye (the reflected ray).

These two rays combine (interfere) at the receiver. The antenna heights act like the heights of the flashlight and the person's eye. This is exactly how space wave propagates.

### 1.3 Surface Wave – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Surface Wave** is the portion of the ground wave that is trapped along the conducting surface of the Earth due to the finite conductivity and permittivity of the ground. Its energy decays both with distance along the surface and with height above the surface. It is dominant in the **MF (300 kHz – 3 MHz)**, **LF (30 – 300 kHz)**, and **VLF (3 – 30 kHz)** bands.

**Conceptual Analogy – Surface Wave:**
Picture a heavy ball rolling along a slightly tilted, sticky tabletop. The ball is "hugging" the surface — friction with the surface slows it down progressively, but its energy stays close to the table. Similarly, the surface wave energy hugs the curved Earth, gradually attenuating as it travels. The higher the ground conductivity, the lower the attenuation (think: ocean surface vs dry sand).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$
> - **Typical ground conductivity:** $\sigma \approx 5 \times 10^{-3}$ S/m (average land), $5$ S/m (sea water)
> - **Typical ground relative permittivity:** $\varepsilon_r \approx 4$ to $15$
> - **Radio horizon distance:** $d_h \approx \sqrt{2 R_e h}$ where $R_e = 6370$ km

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Space Wave Propagation Mechanism

The space wave field at any point in space is the vector sum of:
1. **Direct ray** from transmitter to receiver.
2. **Reflected ray** from transmitter → ground → receiver.
3. **Surface wave** (often negligible at VHF/UHF).

**Key principles governing Space Wave:**

- The direct ray travels a straight-line distance $d$.
- The reflected ray bounces off the ground at a small grazing angle. For sufficiently large $d$ and modest antenna heights, the reflection coefficient of the ground approaches $\Gamma \approx -1$ (perfect conductor assumption at grazing incidence).
- The two rays arrive with a small **path difference** $\Delta r$ producing interference — sometimes **constructive**, sometimes **destructive**, depending on antenna heights $h_t$ and $h_r$, distance $d$, and wavelength $\lambda$.

**Why is it important?**
Space wave is the basis of **television broadcasting**, **FM radio**, **cellular mobile communication (4G/5G)**, **radar**, **satellite links**, and **microwave line-of-sight (LOS) links**. Without understanding space wave interference effects, you cannot design cellular networks or radar coverage diagrams.

### 2.2 Field Strength in Space Wave

The total electric field at the receiver for the two-ray model (with $\Gamma = -1$) is:

$$E_T = \frac{E_0}{d} \cdot 2 \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right)$$

where $E_0$ is the free-space reference field at 1 km from an isotropic radiator with transmitted power $P_t$:

$$E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30\, P_t\, G_t}}{1\ \text{km}}\ \ \text{V/m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked for received field strength at VHF/UHF, students almost always forget the factor of **2**. The two-ray interference gives a peak value of $2 E_0 / d$, NOT just $E_0 / d$.

### 2.3 Maximum and Minimum Field Regions

Setting $\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right) = 1$ (maximum) and $= 0$ (minimum):

| Condition | Result |
|-----------|--------|
| $h_t h_r = \dfrac{\lambda d}{4}$ | **Maximum field** |
| $h_t h_r = \dfrac{\lambda d}{2}$ | **Zero field (null)** |

### 2.4 Atmospheric Refraction – The k-Factor

Due to tropospheric refractivity gradient, radio waves bend slightly toward the Earth. This is modelled by replacing Earth's true radius $R_e$ with an effective Earth radius:

$$k = \frac{R_e'}{R_e}$$

For a standard atmosphere, $k = 4/3$. The radio horizon distance becomes:

$$d_h = \sqrt{2 k R_e h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Super-refraction** ($k > 1$) and **ducting** (when a temperature inversion creates a sharp refractivity gradient) can dramatically extend communication range beyond the optical horizon.

### 2.5 Surface Wave Propagation Mechanism

The surface wave is a guided wave bound to the Earth's surface. Its field components must satisfy the boundary condition at the air–ground interface:

$$E_{\text{tangential, air}} = E_{\text{tangential, ground}}$$

For a vertically polarized wave propagating along a finitely conducting flat earth, the surface wave:
- Tilts forward at an angle $\psi$ relative to the ground.
- Has horizontal component $E_x$ and vertical component $E_z$ related by the **tilt angle**.

### 2.6 Surface Wave Tilt Angle

$$\tan\psi = \frac{E_x}{E_z} = \frac{\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda}{\varepsilon_r \tan^2\theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle measured from the ground surface. As $\theta \to 0$ (grazing incidence along the surface):

$$\tan\psi \to \frac{\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda}{\varepsilon_r \cdot 0} \to \text{large}$$

The wave becomes nearly **vertical** (electric field perpendicular to ground), consistent with surface-wave guidance.

### 2.7 Numerical Distance and Attenuation Factor

The surface wave field at distance $d$ is the space-wave field multiplied by an **attenuation factor** $A$:

$$E = \frac{E_0}{d} \cdot A$$

where $A$ depends on the **numerical distance** $p$:

$$p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \left|\frac{1}{\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda}\right|$$

For large $p$ (far from transmitter):

$$A \approx \frac{1}{2p}$$

For small $p$ (near transmitter), $A \approx 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** As $p$ increases (i.e., farther distance or lower conductivity), the surface wave attenuates faster. Over **sea water** ($\sigma \approx 5$ S/m), attenuation is small — hence **transatlantic AM radio broadcasts** at LF/MF can be received thousands of km away.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|----------|---------|-------------------|
| Free-space path loss (FSPL) | $L_{fs} = (4\pi d/\lambda)^2$ | Isotropic antennas |
| Reference field at 1 km | $E_0 = \sqrt{30\,P_t\,G_t}$ V/m at 1 km | $P_t$ in Watts |
| Two-ray field (flat earth, $\Gamma=-1$) | $E_T = (2 E_0 / d)\,\sin\!\big(2\pi h_t h_r/(\lambda d)\big)$ | VHF/UHF |
| Path difference (approx.) | $\Delta r \approx 2 h_t h_r / d$ | $d \gg h_t, h_r$ |
| Radio horizon | $d_h = \sqrt{2 k R_e h}$ | $R_e = 6370$ km |
| Brewster angle (horiz. pol.) | $\sin\psi_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$ | Lossless ground |
| Surface wave tilt | $\tan\psi = (\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda)/(\varepsilon_r \tan^2\theta)$ | — |
| Numerical distance | $p = (\pi d/\lambda)\,\cos\theta \cdot \vert 1/(\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda)\vert$ | — |
| Attenuation factor (large $p$) | $A \approx 1/(2p)$ | $p \gg 1$ |
| Effective Earth radius | $R_e' = k R_e$ | $k \approx 4/3$ standard |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Field in Space Wave (Two-Ray Model)

Consider a transmitting antenna $T$ at height $h_t$ and a receiving antenna $R$ at height $h_r$, separated by ground distance $d$. Both are in the far field.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the direct ray:**

The direct ray travels the line-of-sight distance:

$$d_1 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t - h_r)^2}$$

For $d \gg h_t, h_r$ (typical case), we approximate:

$$d_1 \approx d + \frac{(h_t - h_r)^2}{2d}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the reflected ray:**

Using image theory, the reflected ray travels:

$$d_2 = \sqrt{d^2 + (h_t + h_r)^2} \approx d + \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2}{2d}$$

**Step 3 — Path difference:**

$$\Delta r = d_2 - d_1 = \frac{(h_t + h_r)^2 - (h_t - h_r)^2}{2d} = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{2d} = \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$$

**Step 4 — Phase difference:**

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta r = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d} = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}$$

**Step 5 — Apply reflection coefficient $\Gamma$:**

At grazing incidence over typical ground, $\Gamma \approx -1$ for both polarizations. The total field is the sum:

$$E_T = E_0\!\left[\frac{e^{-j\beta d_1}}{d_1} + \Gamma\,\frac{e^{-j\beta d_2}}{d_2}\right]$$

With $\Gamma = -1$ and $d_1 \approx d_2 \approx d$ in amplitude:

$$E_T = \frac{E_0}{d}\left[e^{-j\beta d_1} - e^{-j\beta d_2}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Factor and apply phasor identity:**

$$E_T = \frac{E_0}{d}\cdot e^{-j\beta \bar{d}}\left[e^{+j\beta \Delta r/2} - e^{-j\beta \Delta r/2}\right]$$

where $\bar{d} = (d_1+d_2)/2$. Using $2j\sin(x) = e^{jx} - e^{-jx}$:

$$E_T = \frac{E_0}{d}\cdot 2j\,e^{-j\beta\bar{d}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\beta \Delta r}{2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Magnitude (field intensity):**

$$\vert E_T\vert = \frac{2 E_0}{d}\sin\!\left(\frac{\beta \Delta r}{2}\right) = \frac{2 E_0}{d}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}\right)$$

This is the **two-ray space-wave field strength formula** shown in §2.2. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions used:** flat earth, $\Gamma = -1$ (perfectly conducting or grazing angle is small), $d \gg h_t, h_r$. In a university problem, state these assumptions explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Range and Minimum Antenna Height for Space Wave

From §2.3, the maximum field occurs when the argument of the sine equals $\pi/2$:

$$\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

Solving for $d_{\max}$:

$$d_{\max} = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda}$$

For example, with $h_t = 100$ m, $h_r = 10$ m, $\lambda = 1$ m: $d_{\max} = 4 \times 100 \times 10 / 1 = 4000$ m.

For $d > d_{\max}$, the field oscillates (maxima and minima) as $d$ changes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Surface Wave Tilt (Norton)

Let the field above a finitely conducting earth propagate in the $x$-direction along the surface. The vertical component of the electric field satisfies Laplace's equation in the air region; the tangential $E_x$ component must be continuous across the boundary.

**Step 1 — Field expressions:**

Above ground (air), the field has both vertical and horizontal components. From the boundary condition that the tangential $E$ is continuous:

$$E_x^{\text{air}}\Big|_{z=0} = E_x^{\text{ground}}\Big|_{z=0}$$

**Step 2 — Intrinsic impedance of each medium:**

- Air: $\eta_1 = \eta_0 = 120\pi\ \Omega$ (lossless)
- Ground: $\eta_2 = \eta_0 / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda}$

**Step 3 — Ratio of components:**

For a wave propagating at grazing angle $\theta$ along the interface, the ratio of horizontal to vertical field components is:

$$\frac{E_x}{E_z} = \frac{1}{\eta_1}\!\left[\frac{\eta_2 - \eta_1 \sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\right]$$

For highly conducting ground ($\eta_2 \to 0$), this reduces to:

$$\frac{E_x}{E_z} \approx -\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = -\tan\theta$$

For grazing propagation, $\theta$ is small, so the tilt becomes small — but the vertical component dominates. The angle $\psi$ between $E$ and the vertical satisfies:

$$\tan\psi = \frac{E_x}{E_z} = \frac{\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda}{\varepsilon_r \tan^2\theta}\ \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Attenuation Factor for Large Numerical Distance

When the numerical distance $p \gg 1$, the surface wave is well-separated from the space wave. The Sommerfeld–Norton integral reduces to:

$$A \approx \frac{1}{2 p} - \frac{1}{(2p)^2}\cdot\text{(correction terms)} \approx \frac{1}{2p}$$

Substituting $p$:

$$A \approx \frac{\lambda}{2\pi d \cos\theta}\cdot \left|\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda\right|$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** Doubling the distance reduces the field by 6 dB (since $1/d$ dependence). For surface wave specifically, the additional $1/(2p)$ factor means the field decays even faster — typically an extra 6 dB per doubling over poor ground, but only ~3 dB per doubling over seawater.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Space Wave | Surface Wave |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Frequency range | VHF, UHF, SHF (above ~30 MHz) | MF, LF, VLF (below ~3 MHz) |
| Mechanism | Free-space LOS + ground reflection | Diffusion along finitely conducting surface |
| Field vs distance | $E \propto 1/d$ at far distance | $E \propto 1/(d \cdot p)$ — faster decay |
| Antenna requirement | Elevated antennas (towers) | Ground-mounted vertical antennas |
| Earth conductivity impact | Minor (only reflection coeff.) | Dominant |
| Range | Optical horizon (~30–50 km) | 50–1000 km (sea) or 50–200 km (land) |
| Polarization | Both, but vertically pol. preferred for ground effect | Vertical only |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of Ground Wave

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX["Transmitter Antenna"] --> SPW["Space Wave"]
    TX --> SFW["Surface Wave"]
    SPW --> DIR["Direct Ray LOS"]
    SPW --> REF["Ground-Reflected Ray"]
    DIR --> RX1["Receiver Antenna via Direct Path"]
    REF --> RX1
    SFW --> RX2["Receiver Antenna via Earth Surface"]
    REF -.->|"Γ = -1 at grazing"| GR["Earth Ground Reflector"]
    SFW -.->|"σ, εr coupling"| SOIL["Ground Parameters"]
    GR --> SOIL
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Propagation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Radiated EM Wave from Antenna"] --> B0{"Frequency Band?"}
    B0 -->|"f < 3 MHz"| C1["Surface Wave Dominant"]
    B0 -->|"3 MHz to 30 MHz"| C2["Sky Wave Dominant Ionosphere"]
    B0 -->|"f > 30 MHz"| C3["Space Wave Dominant LOS"]
    C1 --> D1["Tilt angle, Numerical Distance"]
    C1 --> E1["Highly dependent on sigma and epsilon_r"]
    C3 --> D2["Direct Ray plus Reflected Ray"]
    C3 --> D3["Apply k-factor for refraction"]
    D2 --> F1["Two-Ray Field Formula"]
    D3 --> F2["Radio Horizon Formula"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Mermaid Sketch — Two-Ray Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TXpos["TX at height h_t"] -->|"Direct Ray d1"| RXpos["RX at height h_r"]
    TXpos -->|"Reflected Ray d2"| GR["Ground reflection point"]
    GR --> RXpos
    TXpos -->|"Image antenna -h_t"| IMG["Virtual Image of TX"]
    IMG -->|"Straight line = d2"| RXpos
```

### 4.4 Field Strength vs Distance Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Near region: A≈1, E~E0/d"] --> P2["Transition region: oscillations begin"]
    P2 --> P3["Far region: A~1/2p, rapid decay of surface wave"]
    P3 --> P4["Space wave: E~2E0/d sin interference pattern"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Note for the student:** When you reproduce these diagrams in your exam book, you only need to sketch the *direct ray + reflected ray + ground tangent*. Examiners reward clarity over artistic perfection. Always label the heights $h_t$, $h_r$ and the path difference $\Delta r$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between space wave and surface wave propagation. In which frequency range does each dominate?**

**Model Answer:**
**Space wave** is the combination of the direct line-of-sight ray and the ground-reflected ray between the transmitting and receiving antennas. It requires both antennas to be within line-of-sight of each other and is the dominant mechanism for frequencies **above 30 MHz** (VHF, UHF, microwave bands). Its field strength decays as $\sim 1/d$ in the far region.

**Surface wave** is the portion of the ground wave that diffuses along the finitely conducting surface of the Earth, hugging the curvature of the planet. It dominates in the **MF (300 kHz–3 MHz), LF (30–300 kHz), and VLF (3–30 kHz)** bands. Its field decays faster than $1/d$ due to ground losses, with decay rate governed by numerical distance $p$ and ground parameters $\sigma$, $\varepsilon_r$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Define the term "attenuation factor" in surface wave propagation and state its limiting values for small and large numerical distances.**

**Model Answer:**
The **attenuation factor** $A$ is the factor by which the surface wave field is reduced compared to the free-space field $E_0/d$ at the same distance, due to energy dissipation in the finitely conducting ground:

$$E = \frac{E_0}{d} \cdot A$$

**Limiting values:**
- For **small numerical distance** ($p \ll 1$, near transmitter): $A \to 1$ — surface wave field nearly equals free-space value.
- For **large numerical distance** ($p \gg 1$, far from transmitter): $A \approx 1/(2p)$ — surface wave attenuates rapidly with distance.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfalls students make on space-wave and surface-wave problems:
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the two-ray field formula — always write $E_T = (2 E_0/d)\sin(\ldots)$, not $E_0/d$.
> 2. **Mixing up numerical distance $p$** with actual physical distance $d$. $p$ is dimensionless and depends on wavelength, ground constants, and distance.
> 3. **Stating the tilt angle in degrees when the formula gives radians**, or vice versa — always quote $\psi$ explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring the assumption $\Gamma = -1$**. In KTU board answers, always state: "assuming perfectly conducting ground at grazing incidence, $\Gamma \approx -1$."
> 5. **Forgetting to mention antenna polarization** when discussing surface waves — surface wave practically exists only for **vertically polarized** waves (horizontal polarization gives no usable surface wave).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the total field strength $E_T$ at the receiving antenna in space wave propagation using the two-ray model, assuming flat earth and reflection coefficient $\Gamma = -1$. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transmitter operating at 150 MHz with a power of 1 kW feeds a half-wave dipole antenna of height 150 m. The receiving antenna is a half-wave dipole at a height of 20 m. Calculate: **(i)** the maximum field strength at the receiving antenna, and **(ii)** the distance at which the first null occurs. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation** — Already presented in detail in §3.1. The student should reproduce:

1. Geometry: $d_1 \approx d + (h_t-h_r)^2/(2d)$, $d_2 \approx d + (h_t+h_r)^2/(2d)$
2. Path difference: $\Delta r = 2 h_t h_r / d$
3. Phase difference: $\Delta\phi = 4\pi h_t h_r / (\lambda d)$
4. Total field: $E_T = (E_0/d)\left[e^{-j\beta d_1} - e^{-j\beta d_2}\right]$
5. Apply identity: $e^{jx} - e^{-jx} = 2j\sin x$
6. Final result: $E_T = (2 E_0/d)\sin[2\pi h_t h_r/(\lambda d)]$
7. State assumptions: flat earth, $\Gamma = -1$, far-field $d \gg h_t, h_r$, no atmosphere.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $f = 150$ MHz, $P_t = 1$ kW $= 1000$ W, $h_t = 150$ m, $h_r = 20$ m.
Dipole gain: $G_t = 1.64$ (2.15 dBi).
Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 150 \times 10^6 = 2$ m.

**(i) Maximum field strength:**

Compute $E_0$ at 1 km:

$$E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30\,P_t\,G_t}}{1000} = \frac{\sqrt{30 \times 1000 \times 1.64}}{1000}$$

$$\sqrt{30 \times 1000 \times 1.64} = \sqrt{49200} \approx 221.8$$

$$E_0 = \frac{221.8}{1000} = 0.2218\ \text{V/m at 1 km}$$

Maximum field occurs when $\sin(\cdot) = 1$, i.e., when the path-length phase difference is $\pi/2$:

$$\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d_{\max}} = \frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow d_{\max} = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda} = \frac{4 \times 150 \times 20}{2} = 6000\ \text{m} = 6\ \text{km}$$

Maximum field:

$$E_{T,\max} = \frac{2 \times 0.2218}{6} = 0.0739\ \text{V/m} \approx 73.9\ \text{mV/m}$$

**(ii) Distance for first null:**

First null when $\sin(\cdot) = 0$, first positive null at argument $= \pi$:

$$\frac{2\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d_{\text{null}}} = \pi \Rightarrow d_{\text{null}} = \frac{2 h_t h_r}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 150 \times 20}{2} = 3000\ \text{m} = 3\ \text{km}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

`[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of surface wave propagation along a finitely conducting flat earth. Derive the expression for the **surface wave tilt angle** and discuss its significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1 MHz AM broadcast transmitter radiates 50 kW via a quarter-wave monopole over flat ground with $\sigma = 0.01$ S/m and $\varepsilon_r = 10$. Compute (i) the numerical distance $p$ at $d = 100$ km for $\theta \approx 0$, and (ii) the approximate attenuation factor $A$. Comment on whether surface wave or space wave dominates here. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation and derivation:**

A surface wave exists when a vertically polarized EM wave propagates along a finitely conducting boundary. The wave's phase velocity is slower in the ground than in the air, causing the energy to bend back toward the surface — the wave is "trapped." This produces a tilt of the electric field vector from the vertical, given by (see §3.3):

$$\tan\psi = \frac{E_x}{E_z} = \frac{\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda}{\varepsilon_r \tan^2\theta}$$

**Significance:**
- Quantifies the strength of the surface wave — larger $\psi$ deviation from vertical means stronger ground coupling.
- For $\theta \to 0$ (grazing), the tilt becomes very large, supporting long-distance surface wave propagation.
- As conductivity $\sigma \to \infty$, tilt vanishes (perfect conductor).
- For horizontal polarization, tilt is zero — horizontal polarization does **not** support surface wave propagation; hence AM broadcast uses **vertical polarization**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $f = 1$ MHz, $P_t = 50$ kW, $\sigma = 0.01$ S/m, $\varepsilon_r = 10$, $d = 100$ km, $\theta \approx 0$.

**(i) Numerical distance $p$:**

Wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 10^6 = 300$ m.

Compute the complex term:

$$\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda = 10 - j\,(60 \times 0.01 \times 300) = 10 - j\,180$$

Magnitude:

$$\left|\varepsilon_r - j\,60\sigma\lambda\right| = \sqrt{10^2 + 180^2} = \sqrt{100 + 32400} = \sqrt{32500} \approx 180.28$$

For $\theta \approx 0$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$, so:

$$p = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{\vert\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda\vert} = \frac{\pi \times 100\,000}{300 \times 180.28}$$

$$p = \frac{314\,159.27}{54\,084} \approx 5.81$$

**(ii) Approximate attenuation factor:**

Since $p = 5.81 \gg 1$, use the asymptotic formula:

$$A \approx \frac{1}{2p} = \frac{1}{2 \times 5.81} = \frac{1}{11.62} \approx 0.086$$

**Comment:**
Since $f = 1$ MHz is in the MF band, **surface wave dominates**. The sky wave (ionospheric) may also be present at night. The space wave contribution is negligible at this frequency. The attenuation factor $A \approx 0.086$ means the surface wave field at 100 km is only about 8.6% of the free-space value, indicating significant loss over land — but reception is still possible for AM broadcast with high-power transmitters and sensitive receivers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Space Wave & Surface Wave**

- **Ground wave** = Space wave + Surface wave (and optionally Sky wave).
- **Space wave** = Direct ray + Ground-reflected ray; dominant **above 30 MHz**.
- **Two-ray flat-earth field formula (most important):** $E_T = (2 E_0 / d)\,\sin(2\pi h_t h_r / \lambda d)$.
- **Reference field at 1 km:** $E_0 = \sqrt{30 P_t G_t}\ \text{V/m}$.
- **Max field** when $h_t h_r = \lambda d / 4$; **null** when $h_t h_r = \lambda d / 2$.
- **Path difference** $\Delta r = 2 h_t h_r / d$ for $d \gg h_t, h_r$.
- **Reflection coefficient** $\Gamma \approx -1$ for grazing incidence — assume this unless told otherwise.
- **Radio horizon:** $d_h = \sqrt{2 k R_e h}$ with standard $k = 4/3$, $R_e = 6370$ km.
- **Surface wave** dominates **below ~3 MHz**; requires **vertical polarization**.
- **Surface wave tilt:** $\tan\psi = (\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda) / (\varepsilon_r \tan^2\theta)$.
- **Numerical distance:** $p = (\pi d / \lambda) \cos\theta \cdot \vert 1/(\varepsilon_r - j 60\sigma\lambda)\vert$.
- **Attenuation factor:** $A \approx 1$ for $p \ll 1$, $A \approx 1/(2p)$ for $p \gg 1$.
- **Better ground conductivity** $\sigma$ (sea vs land) gives **smaller attenuation** — hence long-range maritime LF/MF communications.
- **Curved-earth and atmospheric refraction** matter above ~30 MHz; use $k$-factor for range estimation.
- **Brewster angle** (horiz. pol.): $\sin\psi_B = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r/(\varepsilon_r+1)}$ — beyond this, ground reflection weakens.
- **Sky wave is NOT space or surface wave** — it uses ionospheric reflection at HF (3–30 MHz).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Duct Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Duct Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Duct Propagation** is a form of **anomalous tropospheric wave propagation** in which electromagnetic (radio) waves become **trapped within a horizontal layer (duct)** of the lower atmosphere due to a sharp negative gradient in the modified refractive index. The bending of the radio ray becomes sufficiently strong to exceed the curvature of the Earth, allowing the wave to travel well beyond the normal line-of-sight (LOS) horizon with very low attenuation.

The phenomenon occurs when the **refractivity gradient** $\frac{dN}{dh}$ is more negative than **−157 N-units/km**, which is the standard atmospheric condition. Such a steep gradient effectively forms a waveguide-like structure in the atmosphere.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Light in an Optical Fiber:**
> Think of the tropospheric duct as the Earth's natural version of an **optical fiber**. Just as a glass fiber traps light between its higher-refractive-index core and lower-index cladding using **total internal reflection**, an atmospheric duct traps radio waves between two layers where the refractive index decreases sharply with height. The radio wave "bounces" gently along the duct, sliding over the curvature of the Earth for hundreds — sometimes thousands — of kilometers.

**Real-world observable consequence:** During ducting, distant FM radio or UHF TV stations that normally cannot be received suddenly appear with strong signals, even when they are 500–1000 km away. Pilots also exploit this phenomenon for over-the-horizon radar and communication.

### Classification of Atmospheric Refraction

| Refraction Type | Refractivity Gradient $\frac{dN}{dh}$ (N/km) | Ray Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Sub-refraction | > 0 | Ray bends **upward** (away from Earth) |
| Standard refraction | ≈ −39 | Normal tropospheric bending |
| Super-refraction | between −39 and −157 | Ray bends **more than normal** |
| **Ducting (Trapping)** | **< −157** | **Ray bends more than Earth's curvature — trapped** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The threshold value **−157 N-units/km** is not arbitrary — it corresponds exactly to the curvature of the Earth (radius ≈ 6371 km), giving $1/a = 157 \times 10^{-9}$ m⁻¹. When the ray's rate of bending exceeds the Earth's curvature, **trapping** occurs.

### Standard Physical Constants

- Earth's radius: $a = 6371$ km
- $1/a = 157 \times 10^{-9}$ m⁻¹ (or 157 N-units/km)
- Speed of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Surface refractivity at standard atmosphere: $N_s \approx 315$ N-units

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Atmospheric Refractivity — Foundation

The **refractive index** $n$ of the troposphere is slightly greater than unity and varies with height. For engineering use, the **refractivity N** is defined as:

$$N = (n - 1) \times 10^6$$

This makes $N$ a manageable number on the order of a few hundred N-units near the surface.

The **modified refractivity M** is introduced to remove the Earth's curvature from the picture and convert a curved Earth geometry into a flat-Earth equivalent:

$$M = N + \frac{h}{a} \times 10^6 = N + 157h$$

where $h$ is height in meters. The vertical gradient of $M$ is therefore:

$$\frac{dM}{dh} = \frac{dN}{dh} + 157$$

### Conditions for Duct Formation

A **duct forms** when $\frac{dM}{dh} < 0$, i.e., when the modified refractivity *decreases* with height. Converting back to $N$:

$$\frac{dM}{dh} < 0 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \frac{dN}{dh} < -157 \text{ N-units/km}$$

This is the **ducting condition**, and it is the central KTU result for this topic.

### Types of Ducts

- **Surface Duct**: The trapping layer starts at the ground and extends upward. Both transmitter and receiver must lie inside (or one boundary at) the duct.
- **Elevated (Elevated Layer) Duct**: The duct is suspended above the ground; the lower boundary is formed by a temperature inversion. Waves enter only when they refract at the right angle from a transmitter located below.

### M-Curve Representation

> [!NOTE]
> An **M-curve** is a plot of modified refractivity $M$ versus height $h$. The slope of the M-curve in any layer immediately tells you whether standard refraction, super-refraction, or ducting is occurring in that layer. A **negative slope** of the M-curve = **trapping**.

### Critical Angle and Trapping

A wave launched from within the duct at a small elevation angle (just above the horizon) will be **totally internally reflected** at the top of the duct if its angle relative to the horizontal layer exceeds the critical angle $\theta_c$:

$$\theta_c = \arccos\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,top}}}\right)$$

For small index variations, this simplifies to:

$$\theta_c \approx \sqrt{2\,\Delta n}$$

where $\Delta n$ is the refractive index change across the duct boundary.

### Maximum Wavelength for Duct Propagation

For a duct of vertical thickness $h_d$ (in meters), the **maximum wavelength** that can be trapped is given by the empirical KTU/standard textbook formula:

$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times h_d^{3/2} \quad \text{(cm, with } h_d \text{ in meters)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\lambda_{max} = c_d \cdot h_d^{3/2}$$

where $c_d = 0.25$ when $\lambda_{max}$ is in cm and $h_d$ is in meters.

### Skip Distance

The **skip distance** $D_s$ is the minimum ground distance over which the trapped wave re-emerges:

$$D_s \approx 2\sqrt{2 a h_d}$$

where $a$ is Earth's radius and $h_d$ is the duct thickness.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Condition/Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refractivity | $N = (n-1) \times 10^6$ | N-units | $N \approx 250$–$400$ near surface |
| 2 | Modified refractivity | $M = N + 157h$ | N-units | $h$ in meters |
| 3 | Gradient relation | $\frac{dM}{dh} = \frac{dN}{dh} + 157$ | N/km | Flat-Earth equivalent |
| 4 | Ducting condition | $\frac{dN}{dh} < -157$ | N/km | Required for trapping |
| 5 | M-curve slope | $\frac{dM}{dh} < 0$ | N/km | Trapping indicator |
| 6 | Max. wavelength | $\lambda_{max} = 0.25\, h_d^{3/2}$ | cm (if $h_d$ in m) | Empirical |
| 7 | Skip distance | $D_s \approx 2\sqrt{2 a h_d}$ | meters | $a = 6371$ km |
| 8 | Critical angle | $\theta_c \approx \sqrt{2\,\Delta n}$ | radians | Small $\Delta n$ approx. |
| 9 | Earth's curvature term | $1/a$ | $= 157 \times 10^{-9}$ m⁻¹ | Fundamental constant |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Duct propagation is exploited in:
> - **Over-the-horizon (OTH) radar** for maritime and military surveillance
> - **Long-distance UHF/VHF telemetry** in remote areas
> - **Interference prediction** for cellular and broadcast network planning
> - **Climate sensing** (ducting indicates temperature inversions over oceans)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Modified Refractivity and the Ducting Condition

**Starting point:** The actual ray path in the real curved-Earth atmosphere has a curvature $\rho$ given by:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = -\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

For small deviations $n \approx 1$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} \approx -\frac{dn}{dh}$$

The Earth itself has curvature $1/a$. The ray **appears trapped** when the ray curvature exceeds Earth's curvature:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} > \frac{1}{a}$$

Substituting:

$$-\frac{dn}{dh} > \frac{1}{a}$$

Converting to refractivity $N$ where $n = 1 + N \times 10^{-6}$:

$$-\frac{1}{10^6}\frac{dN}{dh} > \frac{1}{a}$$

Solving for the gradient:

$$\frac{dN}{dh} < -\frac{10^6}{a}$$

Inserting $a = 6371 \text{ km} = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m:

$$\frac{dN}{dh} < -\frac{10^6}{6.371 \times 10^6} = -0.157 \text{ per m} = -157 \text{ per km}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dN}{dh} < -157 \text{ N-units/km}}$$

This is the **ducting condition** — a KTU high-yield result.

**Equivalence with M-curve:** Using $M = N + 157h$:

$$\frac{dM}{dh} = \frac{dN}{dh} + 157 < 0 \;\;\text{(since } \frac{dN}{dh} < -157\text{)}$$

Hence, the M-curve has a **negative slope** inside the duct.

### Derivation 2: Maximum Wavelength for Trapping in a Surface Duct

Consider a surface duct of thickness $h_d$ with a linear $N$-gradient. The duct behaves as a slab waveguide. For the lowest-order (fundamental) mode to propagate, the phase change across the duct must equal an integer multiple of $\pi$.

For a duct with refractive index dropping linearly from $n_1$ at the bottom to $n_2$ at the top, the standard ray-tracing result for the **maximum trapped wavelength** is:

$$\lambda_{max} = 2.5 \times h_d^{3/2} \quad \text{(with } h_d \text{ in meters, } \lambda_{max} \text{ in cm)}$$

**Detailed step-by-step:**

1. Express the index variation in the duct:
   $$n(h) = n_0 - \alpha h \quad \text{for } 0 \le h \le h_d$$

2. The horizontal ray parameter (Snell's invariant) is:
   $$n(h)\cos\theta(h) = \text{constant} = n_0\cos\theta_0$$

3. At the top of the duct ($h = h_d$), the ray must be horizontal ($\theta = 0$) for the **critical** condition:
   $$n(h_d) \cdot 1 = n_0 \cos\theta_c$$
   $$\theta_c = \arccos\left(1 - \frac{\alpha h_d}{n_0}\right)$$

4. For ducting, the total phase change for a round-trip across the duct thickness (geometric + Goos-Hänchen shift) must equal $2\pi$:
   $$2 k h_d \sin\theta_c + 2\phi = 2\pi$$

5. For a sharp boundary, the Goos-Hänchen shift contributes $\phi \approx \pi/2$, and for the lowest-order mode:
   $$2 k h_d \sin\theta_c = \pi$$

6. Using $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\sin\theta_c \approx \theta_c$ for small angles, and substituting for $\theta_c$ in terms of the index gradient, we obtain:
   $$\lambda_{max} \approx 2.5 \, h_d^{3/2} \quad \text{(cm)}$$

### Derivation 3: Skip Distance for a Surface Duct

The skip distance is the ground range from the transmitter to where the trapped ray **first returns to the surface**.

1. The ray travels upward to height $h_d$ and bends back, following an arc.
2. The arc has a radius of curvature $\rho$ where:
   $$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{a} - \left(-\frac{dn}{dh}\right) = \frac{1}{a} + \frac{dn}{dh}$$

3. The ray reaches maximum height $h_d$ and returns; the chord length on the ground is:
   $$D_s = 2 h_d \cot\left(\frac{L}{2\rho}\right)$$

4. For a small arc (since $h_d \ll a$): $\sin(L/2\rho) \approx L/2\rho$, and the geometric path length satisfies $L \approx 2h_d \rho$ ... leading after algebraic reduction to:
   $$D_s \approx 2\sqrt{2 a h_d}$$

5. Substituting $a = 6371$ km:
   $$D_s \approx 2\sqrt{2 \times 6371 \times h_d \text{ (km)}} \text{ km}$$
   $$D_s \approx 159.5 \sqrt{h_d \text{ (m)}} \text{ km}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A surface duct has thickness $h_d = 20$ m. Find (a) the maximum wavelength trapped, and (b) the skip distance.

**Solution (a):**

$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times h_d^{3/2}$$

$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times (20)^{3/2} \text{ cm}$$

$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times 20 \times \sqrt{20} = 0.25 \times 20 \times 4.472$$

$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times 89.44 = 22.36 \text{ cm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 22.36 \text{ cm} \;\;(\approx 1.34 \text{ GHz})}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$D_s = 2\sqrt{2 a h_d} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times 20}$$

$$D_s = 2\sqrt{2.5484 \times 10^8} = 2 \times 15964$$

$$D_s \approx 31.93 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{D_s \approx 31.9 \text{ km}}$$

**Interpretation:** Only frequencies below ≈ 1.34 GHz (wavelengths above 22 cm) will be efficiently ducted by this particular duct, and the first signal re-emergence occurs nearly 32 km away from the transmitter.

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scenario | $\frac{dN}{dh}$ (N/km) | $\frac{dM}{dh}$ (N/km) | Ray Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sub-refraction | +30 | +187 | Ray curves upward |
| Standard | −39 | +118 | Normal LOS bending |
| Super-refraction | −100 | +57 | Strong bending |
| Ducting (just at limit) | −157 | 0 | Ray follows Earth curvature |
| Strong ducting | −200 | −43 | Ray trapped |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: M-Curve and Duct Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Altitude h] --> B[Plot M vs h]
    B --> C{Is slope of M-curve negative?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Ducting Layer]
    C -- No --> E[Standard or Sub Refraction]
    D --> F[Wave Trapped Between Two Layers]
    F --> G[Propagation Beyond LOS Horizon]
    E --> H[Normal Tropospheric Propagation]
```

### Diagram 2: Block-Level Architecture of Duct Propagation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transmitter] --> A1[Wave Launched at Small Elevation Angle]
    A1 --> A2[Wave Enters Duct Lower Boundary]
    A2 --> A3[Refraction Bends Ray Toward Earth]
    A3 --> A4[Ray Reaches Duct Upper Boundary]
    A4 --> A5{Total Internal Reflection?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Ray Reflects Back Down]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Ray Escapes to Troposphere]
    A6 --> A8[Continues Zigzag Path Inside Duct]
    A8 --> A9[Emerges at Skip Distance from TX]
    A7 --> A10[Standard Tropospheric Loss]
    A9 --> RX[Receiver Beyond Horizon]
    A10 --> RX2[Receiver Within LOS Only]
```

### Diagram 3: Refraction Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Start: Atmospheric Layer] --> Q{What is dN/dh?}
    Q -- Greater than 0 --> R1[Sub Refraction]
    Q -- Between -39 and 0 --> R2[Sub Standard]
    Q -- Between -157 and -39 --> R3[Super Refraction]
    Q -- Less than -157 --> R4[Ducting and Trapping]
    R1 --> OUT1[Ray Bends Upward]
    R2 --> OUT2[Normal Bending]
    R3 --> OUT3[Strong Downward Bending]
    R4 --> OUT4[Wave Trapped in Duct]
```

### Diagram 4: Surface Duct vs Elevated Duct

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SD[Surface Duct]
        G1[Ground Level] --> L1[Lower Boundary at Ground]
        L1 --> M1[Duct Thickness hd]
        M1 --> U1[Upper Boundary - Inversion Layer]
    end
    subgraph ED[Elevated Duct]
        G2[Ground Level] --> LB[Lower Boundary Above Ground]
        LB --> M2[Duct Thickness hd]
        M2 --> UB[Upper Boundary - Inversion Layer]
    end
```

### Diagram 5: Ray Path Inside a Surface Duct

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Start Position] --> P1[First Reflection at Top]
    P1 --> P2[Second Reflection at Top]
    P2 --> P3[Third Reflection at Top]
    P3 --> P4[Continues to Skip Distance]
    P4 --> P5[Returns to Ground]
```

### Diagram 6: Skip Distance and Coverage Zone

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transmitter Inside Duct] --> A[Coverage Gap up to Skip Distance Ds]
    A --> B[Ducted Signal Arrives]
    B --> C[Receiver Beyond Horizon Receives Signal]
    TX -.-> D[No Direct Signal within Skip Zone]
    D -.-> A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define duct propagation. State the condition for ducting in terms of refractivity gradient.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Duct propagation** is the phenomenon in which electromagnetic waves get trapped within a horizontal layer of the lower atmosphere (called a duct) where the refractivity gradient is sufficiently negative, enabling over-the-horizon propagation with low attenuation.

**Condition for ducting:**
$$\frac{dN}{dh} < -157 \text{ N-units/km}$$

Equivalently, the M-curve slope is negative: $\frac{dM}{dh} < 0$.

---

**Q2. What is the modified refractivity? Why is it introduced?**

**Model Answer:**

The **modified refractivity** $M$ is defined as:
$$M = N + 157h$$
where $N$ is refractivity and $h$ is height in meters.

It is introduced to **convert the curved-Earth geometry into an equivalent flat-Earth problem**, which simplifies ray-tracing analysis and makes the ducting condition simply $\frac{dM}{dh} < 0$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the formation of a **surface duct** and an **elevated duct**. Discuss the role of the M-curve. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **surface duct** is formed when a sharp negative refractivity gradient exists starting from the ground itself, typically caused by:
- A temperature inversion near the surface
- A rapid decrease in humidity with height
- Advection of warm dry air over a cool moist surface (e.g., over oceans)

The trapping condition is met when $\frac{dN}{dh} < -157$ N/km from the surface up to height $h_d$. On the **M-curve** (M vs. h plot), this appears as a **negative slope** in that region, indicating trapping.

An **elevated duct** is a duct whose lower boundary is **above** the ground, formed by:
- A subsidence inversion (warm air sinking over cool air)
- A frontal inversion
- A elevated layer with sharp moisture gradient

The transmitter can be **outside** the duct, and waves enter only by refraction at the upper boundary if launched at a sufficiently shallow angle.

**Role of M-curve:**
- Slope $\frac{dM}{dh} > 0$ → standard or sub-refraction
- Slope $\frac{dM}{dh} = 0$ → ray follows Earth's curvature
- Slope $\frac{dM}{dh} < 0$ → **trapping/ducting**

The M-curve is thus the single most useful diagnostic tool to identify ducting regions from atmospheric sounding data.

**Sketch (textual representation):**
```
   h ↑      M-curve
     |      ___________
     |     /            ← Standard (positive slope)
     |    /
     |   /  ____________ ← Ducting region (negative slope)
     |  /  /
     | /  /
     |/__/   ← Upper boundary (trapping cap)
     |________________ → h
```

**(b)** Derive the condition for duct propagation in terms of the refractivity gradient. For a surface duct of thickness **30 m**, calculate the **maximum wavelength** that can be trapped and the **skip distance**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

The ray curvature in a medium with gradient $\frac{dn}{dh}$ is:
$$\frac{1}{\rho} = -\frac{dn}{dh}$$

For trapping, the ray curvature must exceed Earth's curvature $1/a$:
$$\frac{1}{\rho} > \frac{1}{a} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; -\frac{dn}{dh} > \frac{1}{a}$$

Converting $n = 1 + N \times 10^{-6}$:
$$-\frac{1}{10^6}\frac{dN}{dh} > \frac{1}{a}$$
$$\frac{dN}{dh} < -\frac{10^6}{a}$$

With $a = 6.371 \times 10^6$ m:
$$\boxed{\frac{dN}{dh} < -157 \text{ N-units/km}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $h_d = 30$ m:

Maximum wavelength:
$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times h_d^{3/2} = 0.25 \times (30)^{3/2}$$
$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times 30 \times \sqrt{30} = 0.25 \times 30 \times 5.477$$
$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times 164.3 = 41.08 \text{ cm}$$

Skip distance:
$$D_s = 2\sqrt{2 a h_d} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times 30}$$
$$D_s = 2\sqrt{3.8226 \times 10^8} = 2 \times 19552$$
$$D_s \approx 39.1 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 41.08 \text{ cm}, \quad D_s \approx 39.1 \text{ km}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **M-curve representation** of the troposphere. How does the M-curve distinguish between standard refraction, super-refraction, and ducting? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **M-curve** is a plot of the **modified refractivity** $M = N + 157h$ versus altitude $h$. The vertical addition of the linear term $157h$ accounts for the curvature of the Earth, transforming the curved geometry into an **equivalent flat-Earth problem**.

**Significance of slope:**

- $\frac{dM}{dh} > 0$ (positive slope): The modified refractivity increases with height. The ray bends toward the Earth **less** than the Earth curves away — this corresponds to **sub-refraction** or **standard refraction** depending on the magnitude.
- $\frac{dM}{dh} = 0$ (zero slope): The ray curves downward at exactly the same rate as the Earth curves away — the wave follows the Earth's surface. This is the **ducting threshold**.
- $\frac{dM}{dh} < 0$ (negative slope): The modified refractivity **decreases** with height. The ray bends downward **more** than the Earth curves — the wave is **trapped**, leading to **duct propagation**.

**Practical use:** Meteorologists plot M-curves from radiosonde data; communication engineers use these to predict duct occurrence, frequency of operation, and skip distances.

**(b)** Discuss the **types of atmospheric ducts** and explain the difference between a surface duct and an elevated duct. A duct of thickness **50 m** is observed. Calculate the **maximum usable frequency** and the **skip distance**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Atmospheric Ducts:**

1. **Surface Duct:** Lower boundary at the ground, upper boundary at a temperature inversion or rapid humidity drop. Forms over oceans at night (cooling surface) and over deserts (hot surface, cool air aloft).

2. **Elevated Duct:** Lower boundary above the ground, upper boundary at a higher inversion. Both boundaries are aloft. The transmitter may lie below the duct, and waves enter by refraction.

3. **Evaporation Duct:** A special surface duct over oceans caused by rapid humidity decrease just above the sea surface; typically a few meters to ~40 m thick. Critical for ship-to-ship and over-the-horizon radar.

**Differences:**

| Feature | Surface Duct | Elevated Duct |
|---|---|---|
| Lower boundary | Ground | Above ground |
| Transmitter position | Must be inside | May be below or inside |
| Common cause | Surface inversion | Subsidence/frontal inversion |
| Typical location | Oceans, deserts | Upper troposphere |

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given $h_d = 50$ m:

Maximum wavelength:
$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times (50)^{3/2} = 0.25 \times 50 \times \sqrt{50} = 0.25 \times 50 \times 7.071$$
$$\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \times 353.55 = 88.39 \text{ cm}$$

Maximum usable frequency:
$$f_{max} = \frac{c}{\lambda_{max}} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{0.8839}$$
$$f_{max} \approx 3.39 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \approx 339 \text{ MHz}$$

Skip distance:
$$D_s = 2\sqrt{2 a h_d} = 2\sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times 50}$$
$$D_s = 2\sqrt{6.371 \times 10^8} = 2 \times 25241$$
$$D_s \approx 50.48 \text{ km}$$
