$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \approx 88.4 \text{ cm}, \quad f_{max} \approx 339 \text{ MHz}, \quad D_s \approx 50.5 \text{ km}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the ducting condition — KTU expects "**N-units/km**" not just "per km".
> 2. **Mixing up $N$ and $M$** — always clarify which is being plotted or differentiated.
> 3. **Using $a$ in km in the skip distance formula** — convert to meters for consistency, or use the shortcut $D_s = 159.5\sqrt{h_d \text{(m)}}$ km.
> 4. **Plugging $\lambda_{max}$ into $f = c/\lambda$ using wrong units** — convert cm to m before computing frequency.
> 5. **Confusing elevated duct with surface duct** — remember the transmitter-receiver geometry constraint.
> 6. **Skipping the "−157" justification** — KTU values derivations; write $1/a = 157 \times 10^{-9}$ m⁻¹ explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Duct Propagation:**

- ✅ **Ducting Condition:** $\frac{dN}{dh} < -157$ N-units/km — this is THE key result.
- ✅ **Modified Refractivity:** $M = N + 157h$ — the constant **157** comes from $10^6/a$ with $a = 6371$ km.
- ✅ **M-curve slope:** negative slope = trapping; positive slope = standard refraction.
- ✅ **Maximum wavelength:** $\lambda_{max} = 0.25 \, h_d^{3/2}$ cm, with $h_d$ in meters.
- ✅ **Skip distance:** $D_s = 2\sqrt{2 a h_d}$ — or shortcut: $D_s \approx 159.5\sqrt{h_d \text{ (m)}}$ km.
- ✅ **Duct types:** Surface (ground as lower boundary), Elevated (both boundaries aloft), Evaporation (over oceans).
- ✅ **Refractivity categories:** Sub (−39 < dN/dh), Standard (−39), Super (−157 < dN/dh < −39), Ducting (dN/dh < −157).
- ✅ **Critical angle:** $\theta_c \approx \sqrt{2 \Delta n}$ for small index variations.
- ✅ **Real-world impact:** ducting causes distant UHF/VHF interference, over-the-horizon radar operation, and unexpected long-distance radio reception.
- ✅ **Key Earth constant:** $a = 6371$ km; $1/a = 157 \times 10^{-9}$ m⁻¹.
- ✅ **Unit discipline:** $N$ in N-units, $h$ in meters (or km — be consistent), $\lambda$ in cm (empirical formula) or m (physics formula), $f$ in Hz.
- ✅ **KTU formula to memorize:** the empirical $\lambda_{max} = 0.25 h_d^{3/2}$ — examiners love this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spherical Earth Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Spherical Earth Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spherical Earth Propagation** refers to the analysis and study of electromagnetic wave propagation over the curved (spherical) surface of the Earth, accounting for the geometric obstruction, atmospheric refraction, and diffraction effects that occur because real Earth is not flat. The curvature of the Earth introduces an effective **bulge height** that obstructs the direct line-of-sight (LoS) path between transmitting and receiving antennas, leading to three principal phenomena:

- **Earth Bulge Obstruction** – the apparent rise of the Earth's surface between two points.
- **Tropospheric Refraction (k-factor model)** – bending of radio waves due to the refractive index gradient in the troposphere, modeled by an equivalent Earth radius factor $k$.
- **Knife-Edge Diffraction** – additional signal beyond the geometric horizon due to the wave nature of EM energy bending over the curved surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** "Propagation over spherical earth – Earth bulge, line of sight distance, radio horizon, tropospheric refraction, effective earth radius, knife-edge diffraction and Fresnel zones."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "Boats on the Ocean":**
> Imagine you are standing on a beach looking out at the ocean. A small boat just leaving the harbor is fully visible, but as it sails farther, eventually only its mast remains visible, and finally it disappears entirely beyond the horizon — even though the boat is still very much there!
> This happens because **the Earth is curved**. The water surface "bulges up" between you and the boat, blocking the straight-line view.
> In radio communication, the antennas are the "people on the beach," and the EM wave is the "boat." Even with a perfectly straight path, the Earth's curvature can block the signal. This is exactly what **Spherical Earth Propagation** studies — how to predict and overcome this curvature-induced obstruction.
> Additionally, the atmosphere acts like a "lens" that bends the radio wave slightly downward, making the horizon appear farther than the geometric one — this is the **k-factor** effect.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are standard for spherical earth calculations:

- **Mean Earth Radius:** $R = 6371 \text{ km} \approx 6.371 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$
- **Standard k-factor (normal atmosphere):** $k = 4/3 \approx 1.333$
- **Effective Earth Radius:** $R_e = kR$
- **Speed of Light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

> [!NOTE]
> The value $k = 4/3$ corresponds to a **standard atmosphere** with a refractive index gradient of $-39 \text{ N-units/km}$, and is the most commonly assumed value in KTU problems unless stated otherwise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Earth Must Be Treated as a Sphere

For short-range line-of-sight links (a few km), the Earth can be approximated as flat with negligible error. However, for **terrestrial microwave links, satellite-ground links, and long-distance VHF/UHF communication**, the spherical geometry fundamentally limits the maximum range and the path clearance. The flat-earth assumption causes:

1. **Overestimation of signal strength** beyond the true horizon.
2. **Incorrect prediction of obstruction losses** due to terrain features.
3. **Improper planning of antenna tower heights** for a given link distance.

### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of Spherical Earth Propagation

**Step 1 – Establish the Geometry of Earth Bulge**
- Consider two points A and B on the Earth's surface separated by a great-circle distance $d$.
- The midpoint of the chord AB lies a vertical distance $h_b$ above the Earth's surface, called the **Earth Bulge**.
- $h_b$ represents the height of the "obstacle" the wave must clear, even if no terrain exists.

**Step 2 – Compute the Line-of-Sight (LoS) Distance**
- For an antenna of height $h$ above the surface, the maximum geometric LoS distance over a smooth spherical earth is:
$$d_{LoS} = \sqrt{2Rh + h^2} \approx \sqrt{2Rh} \quad (\text{since } h \ll R)$$

**Step 3 – Account for Atmospheric Refraction**
- The troposphere's refractive index decreases with height, causing the wave to bend slightly toward the Earth.
- This is modeled by multiplying the Earth's radius $R$ by a factor $k$, giving an **Effective Earth's Radius** $R_e = kR$.
- With $k = 4/3$, the horizon distance increases by a factor of $\sqrt{4/3} \approx 1.155$.

**Step 4 – Determine the Fresnel Zone Clearance**
- Even with a clear geometric LoS, the wavefront requires an elliptical clearance region (Fresnel zones) to propagate efficiently.
- The **first Fresnel zone radius** at a point distance $d_1$ from one end of a link of total length $d$ is:
$$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda \cdot d_1 \cdot d_2}{d}}$$

**Step 5 – Apply Diffraction Loss Correction (Knife-Edge Model)**
- When the path is partially obstructed by the Earth's bulge or terrain, use the **Fresnel-Kirchhoff knife-edge diffraction** parameter:
$$v = h \sqrt{\frac{2(d_1+d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}}$$
- Diffraction loss depends on the sign and magnitude of $v$ (negative = obstruction in lower half, positive = clear path).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Spherical Earth Propagation**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | Earth bulge at distance $d_1$ from A and $d_2$ from B | $h_b = \dfrac{d_1 d_2}{2 R}$ | $d_1, d_2$ along the surface, $R$ = true Earth radius |
| 2 | Earth bulge at midpoint ($d_1 = d_2 = d/2$) | $h_b = \dfrac{d^2}{8R}$ | When both antennas are at the same height |
| 3 | Geometric LoS distance (antenna height $h$) | $d = \sqrt{2Rh}$ (for $h \ll R$) | True spherical Earth, no atmosphere |
| 4 | Radio horizon distance with k-factor | $d_{hor} = \sqrt{2 k R h}$ | Effective Earth radius = $kR$ |
| 5 | Total LoS link (two antennas) | $d_{total} = \sqrt{2kR h_1} + \sqrt{2kR h_2}$ | Standard $k=4/3$ used unless stated |
| 6 | Effective Earth radius | $R_e = kR$ | $k$ varies from 1 (no refraction) to $\infty$ (ducting) |
| 7 | First Fresnel zone radius | $r_1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}}$ | $d = d_1 + d_2$, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 8 | Required clearance (60% rule) | $r_1 \cdot 0.6$ | Minimum clearance for negligible obstruction loss |
| 9 | Knife-edge diffraction parameter | $v = h\sqrt{\dfrac{2(d_1+d_2)}{\lambda d_1 d_2}}$ | $h$ = clearance of path above obstacle (negative if obstructed) |
| 10 | Diffraction loss (Fresnel integral) | $L_d (dB) = -20 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{1}{2}(C(v)-jS(v))\right]$ | $C, S$ = Fresnel integrals |
| 11 | Approximate diffraction loss ($v > -1$) | $L_d \approx 6.9 + 20 \log_{10}\left(\sqrt{v^2-1} + v\right) \text{ dB}$ | Valid for $v \ge 0$ |
| 12 | k-factor from refractivity gradient | $k = \dfrac{1}{1 + R \dfrac{dn}{dh}}$ | $n$ = refractive index of atmosphere |

> **Critical Substitution Note:** In the formula sheet, the value $6371 \text{ km}$ is to be used for $R$ unless the problem specifies a different effective radius. The standard $k = 4/3$ is **always assumed** unless otherwise stated.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microwave Link Design:** Telecom operators (BSNL, Airtel, Jio) use these formulas to design point-to-point microwave links at 6 GHz, 11 GHz, 23 GHz, etc.
- **Broadcast Tower Height Planning:** Doordarshan and All India Radio use the radio horizon formula to set the height of TV/radio broadcast towers for national coverage.
- **Radar Horizon:** Naval and air-traffic-control radars use $d_{hor} = \sqrt{2kRh}$ to compute the maximum detection range of low-flying targets.
- **Satellite Ground Station Siting:** Used to ensure LoS to geostationary satellites from any latitude.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Earth Bulge Height $h_b = \dfrac{d_1 d_2}{2R}$

**Statement to prove:** For a smooth spherical Earth of radius $R$, the height of the bulge above the chord joining two points on the surface at arc distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from a third point P is $h_b = d_1 d_2 / (2R)$.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider the center of the Earth O, the surface point P, and two antenna locations A and B. The arc distances from P to A and P to B are $d_1$ and $d_2$ respectively. The bulge height $h_b$ is the perpendicular distance from P to the chord AB.

**Step 2 — Use the property of a circle.**
For a circle, the perpendicular distance from any point on the circumference to a chord is given by a standard geometric relation. Let the angles subtended at the center by arcs PA and PB be:
$$\alpha = \frac{d_1}{R}, \quad \beta = \frac{d_2}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the perpendicular from the center to the chord.**
Let M be the midpoint of AB. The distance from O to the chord AB is:
$$OM = R \cos\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Express the height above the surface at P.**
P is on the circumference. The radius to P makes an angle of $(\alpha - \beta)/2$ with the line OM. So the perpendicular distance from P to AB is:
$$h_b = R - OM \cos\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)$$

Wait — we need a cleaner derivation. The classical textbook derivation is as follows:

**Alternative (clean) Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider the line from the center O of the Earth through P. The chord AB lies at a perpendicular distance $x$ from O. By the geometry of a circle:
$$x = R \cos\theta$$
where $\theta$ is the half-angle subtended by the chord AB at the center.

**Step 2:** The angle at the center corresponding to a chord of length $\ell$ in a circle of radius $R$ is $\theta = \ell/(2R)$ for small angles. The arc AB has length $d_1 + d_2$, so:
$$\theta = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2R}$$

**Step 3:** The bulge height is the difference between the radius $R$ and the distance $x$:
$$h_b = R - x = R - R\cos\theta = R(1-\cos\theta)$$

**Step 4:** For small $\theta$ (typical in radio links), use the small-angle approximation $\cos\theta \approx 1 - \theta^2/2$:
$$h_b \approx R \cdot \frac{\theta^2}{2} = R \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{d_1+d_2}{2R}\right)^2 = \frac{(d_1+d_2)^2}{8R}$$

This gives the **bulge at the midpoint** when the two arc distances from P to A and P to B are $d_1$ and $d_2$. To get the **bulge at an arbitrary point** $P$ at distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from A and B:

**Step 5:** The more general relation is:
$$h_b = \frac{d_1 d_2}{2R}$$

> This can be rigorously shown by considering the power of a point. The foot of the perpendicular from P to AB lies at distances $\frac{d_1(d_1+d_2)}{2(d_1+d_2)} = d_1/2$ from A and $d_2/2$ from B in flat geometry, but the spherical correction is small. The standard result used universally in radio engineering is:
> $$h_b = \frac{d_1 d_2}{2R}$$
> Setting $d_1 = d_2 = d/2$ gives $h_b = d^2/(8R)$, which is the well-known **midpoint bulge** formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** For a 50 km link, $h_b = (50 \times 10^3)^2 / (8 \times 6.371 \times 10^6) = 2.5 \times 10^9 / (5.097 \times 10^7) \approx 49 \text{ m}$. This is a **significant** bulge that must be considered for a 50 km link — confirms the importance of the spherical Earth model.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line-of-Sight (LoS) Distance $d = \sqrt{2Rh}$

**Statement to prove:** The maximum geometric distance to the horizon for an antenna at height $h$ above a spherical Earth of radius $R$ is $d = \sqrt{2Rh}$ (for $h \ll R$).

**Step 1 — Geometry.** Let the antenna be at point A, at height $h$ above the tangent point T on the Earth's surface. The line of sight is tangent to the Earth at T.

**Step 2 — Right-angled triangle.** Construct triangle OTC, where O is the center of the Earth, C is the antenna location, and T is the tangent point. The angle at T is $90°$ (radius is perpendicular to tangent).

**Step 3 — Apply the Pythagorean theorem.**
$$OC^2 = OT^2 + TC^2$$
$$(R+h)^2 = R^2 + TC^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for TC.**
$$TC^2 = (R+h)^2 - R^2 = R^2 + 2Rh + h^2 - R^2 = 2Rh + h^2$$

**Step 5 — Arc length approximation.**
Since the arc is short, the distance $d$ along the Earth's surface is approximately equal to the straight-line distance TC:
$$d \approx \sqrt{2Rh + h^2}$$

**Step 6 — Approximation for $h \ll R$.**
Since $h^2 \ll 2Rh$ (because $h \ll R$):
$$d \approx \sqrt{2Rh}$$

**Step 7 — With k-factor (refraction):**
$$d_{hor} = \sqrt{2 k R h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** For an antenna at $h = 100$ m, $d_{hor} = \sqrt{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6 \times 100} = \sqrt{1.274 \times 10^9} \approx 35.7 \text{ km}$ (geometric) and $\sqrt{4/3} \times 35.7 \approx 41.2 \text{ km}$ with standard atmosphere.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — First Fresnel Zone Radius $r_1$

**Statement to prove:** The radius of the first Fresnel zone at a point $P$ that is at distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from the transmitter and receiver, with total link distance $d = d_1 + d_2$, is $r_1 = \sqrt{\lambda d_1 d_2 / d}$.

**Step 1 — Definition.** The first Fresnel zone is the locus of points P such that the path length from T to P to R exceeds the direct line-of-sight path T-R by $\lambda/2$.

**Step 2 — Set up geometry.** Place the transmitter T and receiver R on a horizontal axis separated by $d$. Point P is at perpendicular distance $r$ from the line TR, located at distance $d_1$ from T and $d_2$ from R.

**Step 3 — Direct path length.**
$$L_0 = \sqrt{d^2} = d$$

**Step 4 — Reflected/diffracted path length.**
$$L_1 = \sqrt{d_1^2 + r^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + r^2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Fresnel condition.**
For the first Fresnel zone boundary:
$$L_1 - L_0 = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Use the small-$r$ approximation.** For $r \ll d_1, d_2$:
$$\sqrt{d_1^2 + r^2} \approx d_1 + \frac{r^2}{2 d_1}$$
$$\sqrt{d_2^2 + r^2} \approx d_2 + \frac{r^2}{2 d_2}$$

So:
$$L_1 \approx d_1 + d_2 + \frac{r^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{d_1} + \frac{1}{d_2}\right) = d + \frac{r^2}{2} \cdot \frac{d_1 + d_2}{d_1 d_2} = d + \frac{r^2 (d_1 + d_2)}{2 d_1 d_2}$$

**Step 7 — Apply the Fresnel condition.**
$$d + \frac{r^2 d}{2 d_1 d_2} - d = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
$$\frac{r^2 d}{2 d_1 d_2} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
$$r^2 = \frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}$$

**Step 8 — Final result:**
$$\boxed{r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}}}$$

This derivation shows that the first Fresnel zone is maximum at the midpoint ($d_1 = d_2 = d/2$), where:
$$r_1^{max} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\lambda d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 60% Rule of Thumb:** For negligible obstruction loss, the path must be clear of obstructions up to at least $0.6 \cdot r_1$ above the line of sight. This is the most commonly tested criterion in KTU exams.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effective Earth's Radius from Refractivity Gradient

**Statement to prove:** The relationship $k = \dfrac{1}{1 + R \dfrac{dn}{dh}}$ for the equivalent earth radius factor.

**Step 1 — Snell's law in the atmosphere.** For a stratified atmosphere with refractive index $n(h)$ varying with height, a ray follows:
$$n(h) \cdot R \cdot \cos\phi = \text{constant}$$

where $\phi$ is the angle of the ray with respect to the horizontal.

**Step 2 — Curvature of the ray.** The rate of change of the ray's slope with distance (its curvature) is:
$$\frac{d\phi}{ds} = -\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}\cos\phi$$

For a nearly horizontal ray ($\cos\phi \approx 1$):
$$\frac{1}{\rho} \approx -\frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

where $\rho$ is the radius of curvature of the ray.

**Step 3 — Combined geometry.** The Earth curves away from the ray at a rate $1/R$. The relative curvature is:
$$\frac{1}{R} - \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

**Step 4 — Define the effective Earth radius $R_e$ such that** the ray appears straight:
$$\frac{1}{R_e} = \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k = R_e/R$:**
$$\frac{1}{kR} = \frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$
$$\frac{1}{k} = 1 + \frac{R}{n}\frac{dn}{dh}$$

Since $n \approx 1$ in the troposphere:
$$\boxed{k = \frac{1}{1 + R \cdot \dfrac{dn}{dh}}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical evaluation for standard atmosphere.** For a standard atmosphere, $dn/dh = -39 \times 10^{-6}$ per km $= -39 \times 10^{-9}$ per m:
$$k = \frac{1}{1 + 6.371 \times 10^6 \times (-39 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.2485} = \frac{1}{0.7515} \approx 1.33 \approx \frac{4}{3}$$

This confirms the well-known $k = 4/3$ result.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Scenario | Antenna heights given? | k-factor? | Primary formula to use |
|----------|------------------------|-----------|-------------------------|
| One antenna at fixed height, other at sea level | Yes | Given $k$ | $d = \sqrt{2kRh}$ |
| Both antennas at finite heights | Yes | $k = 4/3$ | $d = \sqrt{2kRh_1} + \sqrt{2kRh_2}$ |
| Earth bulge at intermediate point P | $d_1, d_2$ given | Not required | $h_b = d_1 d_2 / (2R)$ |
| Earth bulge at midpoint | Total $d$ given | Not required | $h_b = d^2 / (8R)$ |
| Clearance / obstruction check | $d, \lambda, h_{obstacle}$ given | Not required | Compute $r_1$ and compare with $h_{clearance}$ |
| Diffraction over knife-edge | Path geometry given | Not required | Compute $v$, then $L_d$ from Fresnel integrals |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Spherical Earth Geometry with Antenna Heights and Earth Bulge

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Center of Earth O] --> A1[Earth radius R]
    A1 --> A2[Transmitter at A, height h1]
    A1 --> A3[Receiver at B, height h2]
    A1 --> A4[Earth Bulge at point P, height hb]

    B0[Transmitter T] --> B1[Direct LOS path]
    B2[Receiver R] --> B1
    B1 --> B3[Tangent to Earth at Horizon H]
    B4[Troposphere] --> B5[Refracted ray path]
    B5 --> B6[Bent downward toward Earth]
    B3 --> B7[Geometric Horizon]
    B6 --> B8[Radio Horizon farther than geometric]

    C0[Total LOS distance] --> C1[sqrt of 2 k R h1 plus sqrt of 2 k R h2]
    C2[First Fresnel Zone] --> C3[Ellipsoid around LOS axis]
    C3 --> C4[r1 = sqrt of lambda d1 d2 over d]
    C4 --> C5[60 percent clearance required]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Spherical Earth Propagation Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT1[Input parameters: d, h1, h2, f, k] --> PROC1[Compute Earth Bulge hb]
    PROC1 --> PROC2[Compute Radio Horizon]
    PROC2 --> DECIDE{Total link within horizon?}

    DECIDE -->|Yes| PROC3[Compute Fresnel Zone Radius r1]
    PROC3 --> DECIDE2{Path clearance sufficient?}

    DECIDE2 -->|Yes| RESULT1[Link Feasible: Negligible Loss]
    DECIDE2 -->|No| PROC4[Knife Edge Diffraction Loss]
    PROC4 --> RESULT2[Link Feasible with Calculated Loss]

    DECIDE -->|No| PROC5[Beyond Radio Horizon]
    PROC5 --> PROC6[Diffraction around Earth bulge]
    PROC6 --> PROC7[Compute v parameter]
    PROC7 --> PROC8[Fresnel Integral Lookup]
    PROC8 --> RESULT3[High Diffraction Loss - Link May Fail]

    INPUT1 --> REFCHECK[Verify k factor: 1 for vacuum, 4 by 3 for standard, infinity for ducting]
    REFCHECK --> DECIDE
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Ray Paths and Refraction Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NODE_TX[Transmitter Antenna] --> NODE_RAY1[Direct Geometric Ray]
    NODE_TX --> NODE_RAY2[Refracted Ray in Troposphere]
    NODE_TX --> NODE_RAY3[Diffracted Ray over Earth Bulge]

    NODE_RAY1 --> NODE_RX1[Reaches Receiver if within Geometric Horizon]
    NODE_RAY2 --> NODE_RX2[Reaches Receiver if within Radio Horizon]
    NODE_RAY3 --> NODE_RX3[Reaches Receiver with Diffraction Loss]

    NODE_GEOM[Geometric Horizon] --> NOTE1[Visible boundary with k = 1]
    NODE_RAD[Radio Horizon] --> NOTE2[Apparent boundary with k = 4 by 3]
    NODE_DIFF[Diffraction Zone] --> NOTE3[Beyond geometric horizon, attenuated signal]

    NODE_RAY1 --> NODE_GEOM
    NODE_RAY2 --> NODE_RAD
    NODE_RAY3 --> NODE_DIFF
```

### 4.4 Flowchart: Step-by-Step KTU Problem-Solving Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Spherical Earth Problem] --> S1[Step 1: Identify d, h1, h2, f, k]
    S1 --> S2{Step 2: Earth Bulge Required?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Compute hb = d1 d2 over 2R]
    S2 -->|No| S4[Step 3: Compute Radio Horizon dhor = sqrt of 2 k R h]

    S3 --> S5{Step 4: Is Path Obstructed?}
    S4 --> S5

    S5 -->|No| S6[Step 5: Compute Fresnel Zone r1]
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Step 5: Compute v parameter for knife edge]
    S6 --> S8[Step 6: Check 60 percent clearance rule]
    S8 --> S9[Conclusion: Link Feasible]
    S7 --> S10[Step 6: Use Fresnel Integral or Approximation]
    S10 --> S11[Conclusion: Diffraction Loss in dB]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define "Earth Bulge" in the context of radio wave propagation. Write the expression for the height of the bulge at the midpoint of a link of total length $d$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Earth Bulge:** Earth bulge is the height of the Earth's curved surface above the straight chord joining the transmitting and receiving points on the Earth's surface. It represents the effective obstruction to a line-of-sight radio path caused by the spherical shape of the Earth, even when no terrain is present.

**Expression at midpoint:**
$$h_b = \frac{d^2}{8R}$$
where $d$ is the total link distance and $R = 6371$ km is the Earth's radius.

**General expression at any point P (at distances $d_1, d_2$ from the two ends):**
$$h_b = \frac{d_1 d_2}{2R}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is meant by the "Effective Earth Radius" and the "k-factor"? Why is $k = 4/3$ used as a standard value?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Effective Earth Radius** $R_e$ is the radius of an imaginary Earth over which radio waves appear to travel in straight lines, accounting for the bending of waves due to atmospheric refraction.

It is related to the true Earth radius $R$ by:
$$R_e = kR$$
where $k$ is the **k-factor** (equivalent Earth radius factor).

**Why $k = 4/3$:** In a standard atmosphere, the refractive index decreases linearly with height with a gradient of $-39$ N-units/km. Substituting this in:
$$k = \frac{1}{1 + R \cdot \dfrac{dn}{dh}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.2485} \approx 1.333 = \frac{4}{3}$$

This value gives a 15.5% increase in the radio horizon distance and is universally adopted in radio link design.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q.A. (a)** With a neat diagram, derive the expression for the maximum line-of-sight distance over a smooth curved Earth of radius $R$ for an antenna at height $h$ above the Earth's surface. (7 Marks)

**Q.A. (b)** A transmitter antenna is at a height of 200 m above sea level and a receiver antenna is at 150 m. Calculate the maximum line-of-sight distance between them assuming a standard atmosphere ($k = 4/3$). Take $R = 6.4 \times 10^6$ m. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q.A. (a):

**Step 1 — Diagram construction (described):**
Draw a circle representing the Earth with center O and radius R. Place the antenna at point A, at height $h$ above the surface point T. The line of sight from A is tangent to the Earth at T. Draw OT = R perpendicular to the tangent AT.

**Step 2 — Geometry:**
The triangle OTA is right-angled at T.
- Hypotenuse OA = $R + h$
- Base OT = $R$
- Tangent AT = $d$ (LoS distance)

**Step 3 — Apply Pythagoras:**
$$(R + h)^2 = R^2 + d^2$$
$$d^2 = (R + h)^2 - R^2 = 2Rh + h^2$$
$$d = \sqrt{2Rh + h^2}$$

**Step 4 — Since $h \ll R$, ignore $h^2$:**
$$\boxed{d \approx \sqrt{2Rh}}$$

**Step 5 — With atmospheric refraction (k-factor):**
$$d = \sqrt{2kRh}$$

This is the maximum LoS distance from an antenna at height $h$ to the radio horizon.

---

### Model Solution for Q.A. (b):

**Given:** $h_1 = 200$ m, $h_2 = 150$ m, $k = 4/3$, $R = 6.4 \times 10^6$ m

**Formula:** Total LoS distance = $d_1 + d_2 = \sqrt{2kRh_1} + \sqrt{2kRh_2}$

**Step 1 — Compute $d_1$ (from transmitter):**
$$d_1 = \sqrt{2 \times \frac{4}{3} \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \times 200}$$
$$d_1 = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3} \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \times 200}$$
$$d_1 = \sqrt{3.4133 \times 10^9} = 58,422 \text{ m} \approx 58.42 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_2$ (from receiver):**
$$d_2 = \sqrt{2 \times \frac{4}{3} \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \times 150}$$
$$d_2 = \sqrt{\frac{8}{3} \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \times 150}$$
$$d_2 = \sqrt{2.56 \times 10^9} = 50,596 \text{ m} \approx 50.60 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Total LoS distance:**
$$d_{total} = d_1 + d_2 = 58.42 + 50.60 = 109.02 \text{ km}$$

**Final Answer:** The maximum line-of-sight distance between the two antennas is approximately **109.02 km**.

---

## Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q.B. (a)** Derive the expression for the first Fresnel zone radius at a point located at distances $d_1$ and $d_2$ from the transmitter and receiver of a radio link of total length $d$. (7 Marks)

**Q.B. (b)** A microwave link of length 50 km operates at 6 GHz. Calculate the first Fresnel zone radius at a point 20 km from one end of the link. Also determine whether the path is clear if an obstruction of height 25 m exists at that point. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q.B. (a):

**Step 1 — Definition:** The first Fresnel zone is the locus of points P such that the path length from T to P to R exceeds the direct line-of-sight path T-R by $\lambda/2$.

**Step 2 — Geometry:** Let P be at perpendicular distance $r$ from the line TR, with $d_1$ from T and $d_2$ from R, and $d = d_1 + d_2$.

**Step 3 — Direct path:** $L_0 = d$

**Step 4 — Indirect path:**
$$L_1 = \sqrt{d_1^2 + r^2} + \sqrt{d_2^2 + r^2}$$

**Step 5 — Fresnel condition:**
$$L_1 - L_0 = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Small-$r$ expansion:**
$$\sqrt{d_1^2 + r^2} \approx d_1 + \frac{r^2}{2d_1}, \quad \sqrt{d_2^2 + r^2} \approx d_2 + \frac{r^2}{2d_2}$$
$$L_1 \approx d + \frac{r^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{d_1} + \frac{1}{d_2}\right) = d + \frac{r^2 d}{2 d_1 d_2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute:**
$$\frac{r^2 d}{2 d_1 d_2} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$
$$r_1^2 = \frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}$$
$$\boxed{r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}}}$$

---

### Model Solution for Q.B. (b):

**Given:** $d = 50$ km, $f = 6$ GHz, $d_1 = 20$ km, $h_{obs} = 25$ m, $R = 6371$ km

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $d_2$:**
$$d_2 = d - d_1 = 50 - 20 = 30 \text{ km} = 30{,}000 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — First Fresnel zone radius at the obstruction point:**
$$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\lambda d_1 d_2}{d}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.05 \times 20{,}000 \times 30{,}000}{50{,}000}}$$
$$r_1 = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^4}} = \sqrt{600} \approx 24.49 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Check obstruction:**
The obstruction has height 25 m, but the LoS is at the chord (zero height by reference). So the **clearance required (60% rule)** is:
$$h_{clearance, required} = 0.6 \times r_1 = 0.6 \times 24.49 = 14.69 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**
- If the 25 m obstruction is **above** the LoS line, the actual clearance at that point is some value that needs to be compared with 14.69 m.
- If the obstruction just **equals the LoS height**, then the path is barely blocked (clearance = 0), and 0 m < 14.69 m ⇒ **path not clear**.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** Since the Fresnel zone radius at the obstruction point is 24.49 m and the required clearance is 14.69 m, the path is **NOT clear** if the obstruction is at or near the LoS line. Significant diffraction loss will occur.

**Additional — Compute the Earth bulge at this point:**
$$h_b = \frac{d_1 d_2}{2R} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 30{,}000}{2 \times 6.371 \times 10^6} = \frac{6 \times 10^8}{1.274 \times 10^7} \approx 47.1 \text{ m}$$

So the Earth's surface at 20 km from the transmitter is actually **47.1 m above the straight chord** joining T and R. This adds to the obstruction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $k = 4/3$:** Many students compute the geometric horizon ($k=1$) instead of the radio horizon ($k=4/3$) and lose **2 to 3 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Confusing midpoint bulge $d^2/(8R)$ with general bulge $d_1 d_2/(2R)$:** Use midpoint only when $d_1 = d_2$. For arbitrary P, use $d_1 d_2/(2R)$.
> 3. **Wrong Earth radius value:** Use $R = 6371$ km or $6.4 \times 10^6$ m. Some students incorrectly use $6 \times 10^6$ m — this gives noticeable error.
> 4. **Fresnel zone sign errors:** Remember that the clearance $h$ in the knife-edge formula is **positive if the path clears the obstacle** and **negative if the obstacle obstructs the path**.
> 5. **Forgetting the small-angle approximation:** In the Fresnel derivation, students often skip the expansion $\sqrt{d^2 + r^2} \approx d + r^2/(2d)$ and lose method marks.
> 6. **Units:** Always express link distances in **meters** when computing $r_1$ to keep $\lambda$ in meters consistent. Mixing km and m is a frequent error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Earth Bulge General Formula:** $h_b = \dfrac{d_1 d_2}{2R}$ — at midpoint becomes $d^2/(8R)$.
- ✅ **LoS Distance (geometric):** $d = \sqrt{2Rh}$ — derived from Pythagoras on right triangle with tangent at horizon.
- ✅ **Radio Horizon (with $k$):** $d = \sqrt{2kRh}$ — accounts for atmospheric bending.
- ✅ **Effective Earth Radius:** $R_e = kR$; standard value $k = 4/3 \approx 1.333$.
- ✅ **Total LoS for two antennas:** $d_{total} = \sqrt{2kRh_1} + \sqrt{2kRh_2}$.
- ✅ **Fresnel Zone Radius:** $r_1 = \sqrt{\lambda d_1 d_2 / d}$ — **maximum at midpoint**, decreases to zero at ends.
- ✅ **60% Clearance Rule:** Path clear if obstructions are below $0.6 \cdot r_1$ from the LOS line.
- ✅ **Knife-Edge Diffraction Parameter:** $v = h \sqrt{2(d_1 + d_2)/(\lambda d_1 d_2)}$ — sign of $h$ indicates obstruction.
- ✅ **Diffraction Loss (Lee's approximation, $v \ge 0$):** $L_d \approx 6.9 + 20\log_{10}(\sqrt{v^2 - 1} + v)$ dB.
- ✅ **Earth Radius:** $R = 6371$ km ($6.371 \times 10^6$ m) — memorize this value.
- ✅ **Faster Earth-bulge approximation (equal distances):** $h_b \approx \dfrac{d^2 \text{ (in km)}}{50.97}$ meters (using $R = 6371$ km, gives $h_b$ in meters).
- ✅ **Standard Atmosphere Refractivity Gradient:** $dn/dh = -39$ N-units/km ⇒ $k = 4/3$.
- ✅ **Range of $k$:** $k = 1$ (no refraction/vacuum) to $k = \infty$ (super-refraction/ducting); sub-refraction gives $k < 1$.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Microwave link design, broadcast tower height, radar horizon, satellite ground station siting.
- ✅ **Numericals Always Check:** (i) Did you use $k = 4/3$? (ii) Are units in meters? (iii) Is it midpoint or general bulge?

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tropospheric Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tropospheric Waves ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tropospheric Wave Propagation (Troposcatter):** A beyond-the-horizon (BTH) radio propagation mechanism in which VHF/UHF/SHF electromagnetic waves (typically **30 MHz – 10 GHz**) are directed by high-gain antennas toward the common volume in the lower atmosphere (troposphere, 0–10 km altitude). The radio energy is **scattered by refractive-index irregularities and turbulent eddies** within a small common volume in the troposphere, redirecting a small portion of the energy toward the distant receive antenna, enabling reliable communication over **100 km to 1000 km** well beyond the optical horizon. The mechanism is often referred to as **forward-scatter** or **troposcatter (tropospheric scatter) propagation**, and is formally classified under *Scatter Propagation Modes* in the KTU 2024 syllabus. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Flashlight Through Fog" Analogy:** Imagine two people standing on opposite sides of a thick foggy valley, out of sight of each other. Each person shines a powerful flashlight (high-gain antenna beam) slightly above the horizontal, crossing the beams in the middle of the valley where the fog is densest. The tiny water droplets and turbulent eddies in the fog scatter a small amount of light in **all directions** — including toward the other person's eyes. Although the receiver gets only a **minuscule fraction** of the transmitted power, the message can still be decoded. The troposphere plays the role of the fog, and the refractive-index irregularities act as the tiny scattering particles. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Effective Earth radius (4/3 model) | $a_e$ | **8493 km** | | Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | | Frequency range | $f$ | **30 MHz – 10 GHz** | | Typical path length | $d$ | **100 – 1000 km** | | Scatter angle range | $\theta_s$ | **0.5° – 5°** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the 4/3 Earth radius model?** The troposphere's refractive index decreases with height, causing radio rays to bend slightly **toward the Earth**. The standard atmosphere's ray-bending is mathematically equivalent to propagating in straight lines over an Earth whose radius is multiplied by a factor of 4/3, giving $a_e \approx 8493$ km. This is a KTU Board examination hot-spot. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Troposphere and Refractive Index The **troposphere** is the lowest atmospheric layer (0 – 10 km) where temperature decreases with altitude at a standard lapse rate of **6.5 °C/km** and refractive index is approximately: $$ n(h) = 1 + N_s \times 10^{-6} \cdot e^{-h/h_0} $$ where $N_s$ is the surface refractivity (≈ **315 N-units** at sea level) and $h_0$ ≈ 7 km is the scale height. Small turbulent fluctuations in $n$ form the scatterers. ### 2.2 Mechanism of Tropospheric Scatter — Structured Logic 1. **Transmission:** A high-gain, narrow-beam antenna (typical gain **30 – 50 dBi**) transmits a narrow pencil beam tilted slightly above the horizon. 2. **Beam Crossing:** At a great distance, the transmit beam and receive beam **intersect in a small "common volume"** in the troposphere, typically **1–3 km above ground**. 3. **Scattering by Irregularities:** Within this common volume, refractive-index irregularities of size comparable to $\lambda/2$ (turbulent eddies) **re-radiate** a small fraction of the incident energy in all directions (Rayleigh/Mie scatter). 4. **Reception:** A fraction of this scattered energy is directed toward the receive antenna, and the message is recovered despite the heavy path loss. 5. **Time-Variant Behavior:** Because turbulence fluctuates, the received signal exhibits **fast fading** (seconds) and **slow fading** (minutes/hours), requiring diversity techniques. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** and have appeared in previous KTU University Examinations. Memorize the variables and units carefully — the Examiner deducts marks for wrong units. | # | Formula (Description) | Equation | |---|---|---| | 1 | Effective Earth radius | $a_e = \dfrac{4}{3}\,a = \dfrac{4}{3} \times 6370 \approx 8493$ km | | 2 | Scatter angle (small-angle geometry) | $\theta_s = \dfrac{d}{a_e} + \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{d} \;\text{[radians]}$ | | 3 | Scatter angle in degrees | $\theta_s\,[°] = \theta_s\,[\text{rad}] \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | | 4 | Height of common volume (symmetric case) | $h_{cv} \approx \dfrac{d^2}{8\,a_e}$ | | 5 | Basic Transmission Loss (Path Loss) | $L_b\,[\text{dB}] = 30\log f + 20\log d + 30\log\theta_s + 19.8 - G_p$ | | 6 | Received Power | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_b$ (dB) | | 7 | Atmospheric absorption coefficient (specific attenuation) | $\gamma\,[\text{dB/km}]$: 0.001 dB/km @ 1 GHz, 0.01 dB/km @ 10 GHz | | 8 | Surface refractivity | $N_s \approx 315$ N-units (sea level) | > [!WARNING] > **Unit Vigilance (KTU Examiner's strict rule):** In the path loss formula, **$f$ is in MHz**, **$d$ is in km**, **$\theta_s$ is in degrees**, and **$G_p$ is the combined antenna gain in dB**. Mixing units (e.g., $f$ in Hz) is the **#1 reason for losing 2 marks** in KTU numerical problems. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility Troposcatter links are widely used for: - **Military strategic communication** in forward-deployed, terrain-blocked theatres (no satellites, no repeaters needed). - **Off-shore oil rig to mainland** data/voice links (hundreds of km over the sea). - **Over-the-horizon radar (OTHR)** surveillance. - **Naval ship-to-shore** tactical networks. - **Backup links** for fibre cuts in remote regions (e.g., Siberia, Canadian Arctic, deserts of Rajasthan). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Scatter Angle $\theta_s$ **Given:** Two antennas $T$ and $R$ separated by great-circle distance $d$ over an Earth of effective radius $a_e$, with antenna heights $h_1$ and $h_2$ above the tangent plane. **Step 1 — Earth's curvature contribution:** The line joining the Earth's center to each antenna makes a small angle with the local horizontal. For a flat Earth, this would be zero, but the curvature lifts the common volume. The two horizontals diverge by: $$ \theta_{\text{curv}} = \dfrac{d}{a_e} \;\text{radians} $$ The first term arises because the surfaces of the Earth at the two sites are not parallel; the tangent planes meet (above) at a height $\approx d^2/(8a_e)$, and the line from each antenna to that intersection makes an angle $d/(2a_e)$ with its local horizontal. The two angles add up to give the total curvature contribution $d/a_e$. **Step 2 — Antenna-height contribution:** Each antenna beam is pointed just above the horizon. Raising antenna $T$ by $h_1$ tilts its beam downward by an angle $h_1/d$ (small-angle). Similarly for $R$. The combined tilt required to point the beams at each other is: $$ \theta_{\text{height}} = \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{d} $$ **Step 3 — Total scatter angle:** Adding the curvature and height contributions: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_s &= \theta_{\text{curv}} + \theta_{\text{height}} \\ \theta_s &= \dfrac{d}{a_e} + \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{d} \quad \text{(radians)} \end{aligned} $$ To convert to degrees, multiply by $180/\pi$. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Common Volume Height $h_{cv}$ **Given:** Symmetric link — both antennas at equal heights $h$, separated by distance $d$. **Step 1:** The center of the common volume lies on the perpendicular bisector of the great-circle path, at the midpoint $d/2$ from each antenna. **Step 2:** Using the Pythagoras–Earth-curvature relation, the height of the midpoint above the chord $TR$ is: $$ h_{cv} - h = \dfrac{(d/2)^2}{2a_e} = \dfrac{d^2}{8a_e} $$ For $h \ll h_{cv}$ (typical case, $h \approx 10$ m, $h_{cv} \approx 1$ km), this simplifies to: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{cv} &\approx \dfrac{d^2}{8a_e} \\ h_{cv} &\approx \dfrac{d^2}{8 \times 8493} \quad \text{with } d \text{ in km} \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical check:** For $d = 300$ km: $$ h_{cv} \approx \dfrac{300^2}{8 \times 8493} = \dfrac{90000}{67944} \approx 1.32 \text{ km above the chord} $$ This is well within the troposphere, confirming the mechanism is physically realizable. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (typical 7-mark KTU question):** Two troposcatter terminals are separated by $d = 300$ km, with antenna heights $h_1 = h_2 = 30$ m above the Earth's surface. Operating frequency $f = 1$ GHz. Each antenna has gain $G = 40$ dBi and transmitted power $P_t = 1$ kW (60 dBm). Compute: (a) The scatter angle $\theta_s$ in degrees. (b) The basic transmission loss $L_b$. (c) The received power $P_r$. **Solution:** **(a) Scatter Angle:** Convert antenna heights to km: $h_1 = h_2 = 0.03$ km. $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_s &= \dfrac{d}{a_e} + \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{d} \\ &= \dfrac{300}{8493} + \dfrac{0.03 + 0.03}{300} \\ &= 0.03533 + 0.00020 \\ &= 0.03553 \text{ radians} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to degrees: $$ \theta_s = 0.03553 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 2.036° \approx 2.04° $$ **(b) Basic Transmission Loss:** Using $L_b = 30\log f + 20\log d + 30\log\theta_s + 19.8 - G_p$, with combined gain $G_p = G_t + G_r = 40 + 40 = 80$ dB: $$ \begin{aligned} L_b &= 30\log(1000) + 20\log(300) + 30\log(2.036) + 19.8 - 80 \\ &= 30 \times 3 + 20 \times 2.477 + 30 \times 0.309 + 19.8 - 80 \\ &= 90 + 49.54 + 9.27 + 19.8 - 80 \\ &= 168.61 - 80 \\ &\approx 88.61 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ **(c) Received Power:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_r &= P_t + G_t + G_r - L_b \\ &= 60 + 40 + 40 - 88.61 \\ &= 140 - 88.61 \\ &= 51.39 \text{ dBm} \approx 51.4 \text{ dBm} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to milliwatts: $P_r = 10^{51.4/10} \approx 1.38 \times 10^5$ mW = **138 W**. This is an excellent troposcatter received power, consistent with field measurements (typical values: −10 to +60 dBm for high-power, high-gain links). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Tropospheric Scatter Geometry — Mermaid Block Diagram The following Mermaid diagram captures the geometric architecture of a troposcatter link — beam paths, common volume, and the key angles/heights used in the derivations above. ```mermaid flowchart TD TxAntenna["TX ANTENNA HEIGHT h1"] --> TxBeam["TX NARROW BEAM
Tilted above horizon"] RxAntenna["RX ANTENNA HEIGHT h2"] --> RxBeam["RX NARROW BEAM
Tilted above horizon"] TxBeam --> CommonVol["COMMON VOLUME
in Troposphere
Height h_cv above ground"] RxBeam --> CommonVol CommonVol --> Scatter["REFRACTIVE INDEX
IRREGULARITIES
scatter energy in all directions"] Scatter --> RxReceived["RECEIVED SIGNAL
at Rx antenna"] EarthCurv["EARTH CURVATURE
contributes d over a_e to scatter angle"] -.-> CommonVol HeightContrib["ANTENNA HEIGHTS h1 h2
contribute h1 plus h2 over d"] -.-> CommonVol PathDist["PATH DISTANCE d"] --> EarthCurv PathDist --> HeightContrib ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Troposcatter Link ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Baseband
Signal"] --> B["Modulator"] B --> C["HP Power
Amplifier"] C --> D["High-Gain
Parabolic Dish
G = 40 dBi"] D --> E["Free Space
Troposphere
d = 100 to 1000 km"] E --> F["High-Gain
Parabolic Dish
G = 40 dBi"] F --> G["LNA Low Noise
Amplifier"] G --> H["Demodulator"] H --> I["Diversity
Combiner
Space or Frequency"] I --> J["Recovered
Baseband"] K["Fading and
Multipath"] -.-> E K -.-> I ``` > [!NOTE] > The **diversity combiner** is critical — troposcatter links always employ **space, frequency, or angle diversity** to combat the rapid fading caused by atmospheric turbulence. The most common KTU exam question on fading asks students to "list the diversity techniques" — memorize **Space, Frequency, Angle (Beam), and Polarization** diversity. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Define tropospheric scatter propagation. State any two of its salient features.** **Model Answer:** Tropospheric scatter (or troposcatter) propagation is a beyond-the-horizon (BTH) mechanism in which VHF/UHF/SHF radio waves are **scattered by refractive-index irregularities** (turbulent eddies) in a small common volume of the troposphere located at the intersection of the transmit and receive antenna beams. *Salient features:* 1. Operates over distances of **100 – 1000 km**, well beyond the optical horizon. 2. Requires **high-gain directional antennas** (30–50 dBi) to overcome the very high path loss (typically 80–250 dB). 3. Suffer from **rapid and slow fading**; mitigated using **diversity** techniques. 4. Independent of ionospheric conditions — useful in **polar and equatorial regions** where ionospheric propagation is unreliable. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **What is meant by the "common volume" in a troposcatter link? Why is it important?** **Model Answer:** The **common volume** is the small three-dimensional region in the troposphere where the main lobes of the transmitting and receiving antennas overlap. It is the **actual scattering region** for the radio wave. *Importance:* - All the energy that reaches the receiver must be **scattered from this region**. - Its **height** (typically 1–3 km) is determined by the path geometry and antenna heights. - The size of the common volume is set by the **antenna beamwidths**; narrower beams → smaller common volume → less scattered power, but **lower scatter angle** → less path loss. - A careful **trade-off** between antenna gain, beamwidth, and scatter angle governs the link design. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, modified] > **(a)** Derive the expression for the **scatter angle** in a tropospheric scatter link. State clearly the role of Earth's curvature and the antenna heights. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A troposcatter link operates at $f = 4$ GHz over a distance of $d = 400$ km. The antenna heights are $h_1 = 50$ m and $h_2 = 80$ m. Each antenna has a gain of $G_t = G_r = 42$ dBi. Compute the **scatter angle** in degrees and the **basic transmission loss** $L_b$ in dB. Transmitted power is 1 kW. Find the **received power** in dBm. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of scatter angle** — Refer to Section 3.1 above. The full step-by-step derivation yields: $$ \boxed{\;\theta_s = \dfrac{d}{a_e} + \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{d} \quad \text{(radians)}\;} $$ The **first term** $d/a_e$ is the contribution from Earth's curvature (using the effective Earth radius $a_e = 8493$ km). The **second term** accounts for the downward tilt of each beam due to finite antenna heights. **(b) Numerical solution:** Convert heights: $h_1 = 0.05$ km, $h_2 = 0.08$ km. Combined gain: $G_p = 84$ dB. Scatter angle in radians: $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_s &= \dfrac{400}{8493} + \dfrac{0.05 + 0.08}{400} \\ &= 0.04710 + 0.000325 \\ &= 0.04743 \text{ radians} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to degrees: $$ \theta_s = 0.04743 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \approx 2.718° $$ Basic transmission loss (using $f$ in MHz = 4000 MHz, $d$ in km = 400): $$ \begin{aligned} L_b &= 30\log(4000) + 20\log(400) + 30\log(2.718) + 19.8 - 84 \\ &= 30 \times 3.602 + 20 \times 2.602 + 30 \times 0.4342 + 19.8 - 84 \\ &= 108.06 + 52.04 + 13.03 + 19.8 - 84 \\ &= 192.93 - 84 \\ &\approx 108.93 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ Received power ($P_t$ = 60 dBm): $$ P_r = 60 + 84 - 108.93 = 35.07 \text{ dBm} \approx 35.1 \text{ dBm} $$ Converting: $P_r \approx 10^{3.507} \approx 3210$ mW ≈ **3.2 W**. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, modified] > **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a tropospheric scatter communication link**. List **four fading-mitigation techniques** used in such links. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Explain the **mechanism of tropospheric scatter propagation** in detail. Why are the antennas required to have very high gain and very narrow beamwidths? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Block diagram and fading mitigation:** Refer to the **Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.2** above. The block sequence is: Baseband → Modulator → High-Power Amplifier → High-Gain Dish → Troposphere (with multipath) → High-Gain Dish → LNA → Demodulator → Diversity Combiner → Recovered Baseband. *Four fading-mitigation techniques:* 1. **Space diversity** — multiple receive antennas spatially separated (typically 100λ apart). 2. **Frequency diversity** — transmitting the same information on two different carrier frequencies. 3. **Angle (beam) diversity** — multiple receive beams at different elevation angles. 4. **Polarization diversity** — receiving on orthogonal polarizations simultaneously. **(b) Mechanism and the need for high-gain antennas:** Refer to Section 2.2. The mechanism involves the **scattering of radio waves by refractive-index irregularities** in the troposphere. The transmitted beam and receive beam overlap in a small common volume, where turbulent eddies re-radiate a small fraction of energy toward the receiver. *Why high-gain, narrow-beam antennas?* 1. The **scattering cross-section** of the turbulent eddies is extremely small (typically 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁸ of the geometric cross-section), so the **path loss is enormous (80–250 dB)**. 2. **High gain** (40–50 dBi) compensates for this loss by focusing the transmitted energy into the small common volume and capturing as much scattered energy as possible at the receiver. 3. **Narrow beamwidths** reduce the size of the common volume, which **minimizes the scatter angle** $\theta_s$ — the dominant term in the path-loss formula — and hence reduces overall loss. 4. Narrow beams also **reject unwanted multipath** from outside the common volume, improving the signal-to-noise ratio. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert** antenna heights from metres to **kilometres** when using the path-loss formula. Lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Using $\theta_s$ in radians** instead of degrees inside the $\log$ term. Wrong answer. > 3. **Mixing up the 4/3 Earth-radius factor** — students often use $a_e = 6370$ km (actual radius) instead of **8493 km**, leading to wrong scatter angle and loss. > 4. Forgetting to **subtract the antenna gain** $G_p$ from the loss expression. > 5. Not stating that the path loss formula assumes **median (50%) values**; for high-availability links, additional fading margin (10–20 dB) must be added. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tropospheric Waves** - **Mechanism:** Forward scattering of radio waves by refractive-index irregularities (turbulent eddies) in the troposphere. The scattering region is the **common volume** at the intersection of the Tx and Rx beams. - **Frequency range:** 30 MHz – 10 GHz (best performance around **1–5 GHz**). - **Path length:** 100 – 1000 km. - **Key geometry constants:** Effective Earth radius $a_e = 8493$ km; surface refractivity $N_s \approx 315$. - **Scatter angle formula (MEMORIZE):** $$\theta_s = \dfrac{d}{a_e} + \dfrac{h_1 + h_2}{d} \;\;\text{[radians]}$$ - **Path-loss formula (MEMORIZE):** $$L_b = 30\log f + 20\log d + 30\log\theta_s + 19.8 - G_p \;\;\text{[dB]}$$ with $f$ in MHz, $d$ in km, $\theta_s$ in degrees, $G_p = G_t + G_r$ in dB. - **Common volume height:** $h_{cv} \approx d^2/(8a_e)$ for symmetric links. - **Fading:** Troposcatter suffers from **fast fading** (seconds, due to turbulence) and **slow fading** (minutes/hours, due to weather); mitigated by **diversity** (space, frequency, angle, polarization). - **Advantages:** Independent of ionosphere, works in any weather, suitable for harsh and remote regions. - **Disadvantages:** Very high path loss, large expensive antennas, susceptibility to deep fading. - **Applications:** Military BTH links, OTH radar, off-shore oil rig links, naval communications, remote-area backup links. - **Common exam blunders:** Unit mismatches (m vs km, rad vs deg), using actual Earth radius instead of 4/3 value, forgetting to subtract $G_p$, ignoring fading margin. ---

topics.title: Tropospheric Scatter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tropospheric Scatter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Tropospheric Scatter Propagation (Troposcatter / Forward-Scatter)** is a beyond-the-horizon (BHO) radio communication mechanism operating in the **VHF (30–300 MHz)**, **UHF (300 MHz – 3 GHz)**, and lower **SHF (3–10 GHz)** bands, in which electromagnetic energy launched at a low elevation angle from a highly directive transmitting antenna is scattered by spatial irregularities (turbulent eddies) in the **refractive index** of the common volume of the troposphere (the region extending roughly from 0 to **10 km** altitude), redirecting a small but usable fraction of the power toward a similarly directive receiver located far beyond the geometric optical horizon (typically 50 km to 1000 km).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Highlight:** Troposcatter is the most reliable **non-satellite, non-ionospheric** BHO propagation mode. It is a **tropospheric phenomenon** (not ionospheric), hence it works mostly during day-time, is independent of sunspot cycles, and uses **forward scattering** (scatter angle < 10°) rather than back scattering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Refractivity (N-units):** The refractive index of air, n, is conventionally expressed in parts-per-million as  
> N = (n − 1) × 10⁶  
> At the surface N ≈ 315, decreasing to ≈ 100 at 10 km altitude under standard conditions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine two flashlights pointed at a small puff of smoke at opposite angles, but the smoke puff is so far away that you cannot directly see the other person. The smoke cloud (the common volume of the troposphere) acts like a giant **fog screen** — it doesn't reflect the light perfectly, but it does **scatter** a tiny fraction of the light beam off in many directions, including toward the distant flashlight. The receiver captures this faint, scattered "echo" of energy. 

The **common volume** is the wedge-shaped intersection of the two antenna beams high in the troposphere. The small refractive-index "blobs" within this volume act like countless tiny scattering centers. The larger the volume, the more scatterers, but the larger the scatter angle, the less efficient the coupling.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application:** Troposcatter links were the backbone of the U.S. **DEW Line (Distant Early Warning Line)** across the Arctic, the **White Alice Communications System** in Alaska, and the **North American Air Defense (NORAD)** communications network. The **Indian Doordarshan** network used troposcatter relays extensively in the 1970s–80s for Kashmir and North-East India before satellite links became affordable.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier frequency band | **400 MHz – 10 GHz** (best: 1–4 GHz) | Hz |
| Path length (d = d₁ + d₂) | **50 – 1000** (typical 100–500) | km |
| Scatter angle (θₛ) | **0.5° – 10°** (forward scatter) | degrees |
| Antenna size | **9 m – 37 m** parabolic dishes | metres |
| TX power | **1 kW – 50 kW** (high power amplifiers) | W |
| Antenna gain (each end) | **30 – 50 dBi** | dB |
| Antenna beamwidth | **< 1°** half-power | degrees |
| Hop loss (median) | **180 – 220 dB** for 300 km hop | dB |
| Free-space loss for 300 km @ 2 GHz | ≈ 146 dB | dB |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Troposcatter Mechanism

### Pillar I — Atmospheric Turbulence and Refractive Index Irregularities

The troposphere is **not homogeneous**. Temperature, pressure, and water-vapour content fluctuate in small eddies (typical scale sizes `l` from a few cm to ~100 m). These fluctuations create **small-scale spatial variations in the refractive index n** around the mean value. The variance of the refractive-index fluctuations is described by the structure constant **Cₙ²**, which peaks near the surface and decreases with height (typical surface value: Cₙ² ≈ 10⁻¹⁴ m⁻²/³; average value at 1 km altitude ≈ 10⁻¹⁶ m⁻²/³).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why forward scatter, not back-scatter?** The scatter efficiency of an irregularity of scale `l` is maximum when the irregularity size is comparable to the **Fresnel scale** √(λd), where `d` is path length. For forward scatter the effective phase mismatch is small → strong coupling. For back-scatter the mismatch is large → vanishingly small amplitude.

### Pillar II — The Common Volume (CV)

The CV is the **intersection region** of the transmit and receive antenna beams. Its height above ground depends on:

- Earth curvature (Earth's effective radius factor k = 4/3)
- Hop length
- Antenna elevation angles (typically 0° to 1°)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classic Question:** *"Explain the concept of common volume in troposcatter propagation and its effect on received signal level."* — The CV increases with hop length (more scatterers, lower per-volume contribution) and increases with antenna gain (smaller CV, higher per-volume contribution). The two effects roughly cancel, giving a slow ~d³ path loss rather than d² (free space).

### Pillar III — Fading Mechanisms

Fading in troposcatter occurs due to interference between:

1. **Fast multipath fading (Rayleigh)** — from multiple scatterer sub-volumes.
2. **Slow fading** — diurnal and seasonal changes in Cₙ².
3. **Ducting/refraction variations** — changes in the refractive index profile.

Diversity techniques (frequency, space, angle) are routinely employed to combat these.

## 2.2 Engineering Utility

- **Strategic military communications** (jam-resistant due to narrow beams and high path loss).
- **Oil and gas exploration** in remote, harsh terrain (Alaska, Siberia, Arabian deserts).
- **Cross-border / island links** where laying cable is impractical.
- **Disaster recovery backbones** — independent of satellites and ionosphere.
- **Back-up for submarine cable landings** in geographically difficult regions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use \vert instead of \mid in all tables to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refractivity | N = (n − 1) × 10⁶ | N-units |
| 2 | Gradient for ducting | dN/dh ≤ −157 N-units/km | Trapping condition |
| 3 | Standard atmosphere gradient | dN/dh = −39 N-units/km | Reference (no duct) |
| 4 | Effective Earth radius | R_eff = k × 6370 ; k = 4/3 | km |
| 5 | Earth bulge (h_b) | h_b = d₁·d₂ / (12.75·k) | metres (d in km) |
| 6 | Line-of-sight max distance | d_LOS ≈ √(17·h_t) + √(17·h_r) | km (h in metres) |
| 7 | Common volume height | h_cv ≈ d₁·d₂ / (2·k·R) | km |
| 8 | Scatter angle (small-angle) | θₛ ≈ d / (k·R) rad | d = hop length |
| 9 | Free-space path loss | FSPL = 32.45 + 20·log₁₀(f) + 20·log₁₀(d) | dB (f in MHz, d in km) |
| 10 | Free-space loss @ 1 km, 1 GHz | ≈ 92.45 dB | dB |
| 11 | Troposcatter median loss (NBS empirical) | L_med = 30·log₁₀(f) + 20·log₁₀(d) + F(θₛ) − G_t − G_r | dB |
| 12 | Path aperture gain (F) | F(θₛ) ≈ 0.1·N_s + 5·log₁₀(d) ... | dB (semi-empirical) |
| 13 | N_s (refractivity variance) | N_s = 7.5 × 10⁻³ for median | (typical) |
| 14 | Median dB fade depth | Fade ≈ 10·log₁₀(f) − 30·log₁₀(d) + C | semi-empirical |
| 15 | Booker–Gordon scatter cross-section | σ(θ) = (2π·k⁴)⁻¹ · Σ φ(κ) | m²·m⁻³ per unit volume |
| 16 | Turbulent scale (Fresnel) | l_F = √(λ·d) | metres |
| 17 | Diversity gain (4-fold) | ≈ 12–16 dB at 99.9% reliability | dB |
| 18 | Antenna gain (parabolic) | G = 10·log₁₀(η·(π·D·f/c)²) | dBi |

## 2.4 Operational Frequency Bands (KTU Favourite)

| Band | Frequency | Troposcatter Use |
|---|---|---|
| VHF | 30–300 MHz | Marginal (high scatter loss) |
| UHF | 300–1000 MHz | Used (rain scatter overlap) |
| Lower SHF | 1–4 GHz | **Sweet spot** — best trade-off |
| SHF | 4–10 GHz | Used (rain attenuation grows) |
| > 10 GHz | > 10 GHz | Not used — rain attenuation dominates |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** *Troposcatter loves 1–4 GHz, dislikes > 10 GHz, and ignores VHF except in emergency.*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Common-Volume Height and Scatter Angle

Consider two antenna towers of heights h_t and h_r separated by an Earth-flat distance d = d₁ + d₂ (with d₁ from TX to CV, d₂ from CV to RX). The Earth is modelled as a smooth sphere of effective radius R = k·R₀ (R₀ = 6370 km, k = 4/3 standard atmosphere).

**Step 1 — Geometric setup.**  
The common volume lies at the intersection of two straight lines (tangent-like rays) drawn from each antenna just grazing the Earth-bulge horizon. For first-order geometry, the CV height h_cv above the chord connecting the two antenna bases is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{cv} &\;\approx\; \frac{d_1 \cdot d_2}{2 \cdot k \cdot R_0} \quad \text{[km, with d in km, R in km]} \\
       &\;\approx\; \frac{d_1 \cdot d_2}{2 \cdot (4/3) \cdot 6370} \\
       &\;\approx\; \frac{d_1 \cdot d_2}{16987} \quad \text{[km]}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a symmetric hop d₁ = d₂ = d/2:

$$
h_{cv} \;\approx\; \frac{d^2}{4 \cdot k \cdot R_0 \cdot 2} \;=\; \frac{d^2}{16987} \quad \text{km}
$$

**Step 2 — Scatter angle θₛ.**  
The scatter angle is the angle between the incoming and outgoing ray directions, measured at the CV. For a curved Earth, geometrically:

$$
\theta_s \;\approx\; \frac{d_1 + d_2}{k \cdot R_0} \;=\; \frac{d}{k \cdot R_0} \quad \text{[radians]}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta_s \;[\text{deg}] \;\approx\; \frac{180}{\pi} \cdot \frac{d}{k \cdot R_0} \;\approx\; \frac{d}{88.5} \quad \text{deg (for d in km, k = 4/3)}
$$

**Worked numerical example.**  
For a 300 km hop: θₛ ≈ 300/88.5 ≈ **3.39°** (a typical small forward-scatter angle).

**Step 3 — Why this matters.**  
The forward-scatter efficiency of a turbulent eddy of size l is maximum when

$$
l \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda}{2 \sin(\theta_s/2)} \;\approx\; \frac{\lambda}{\theta_s} \quad \text{(small angle)}
$$

This is the **Bragg scatter** condition. For λ = 0.15 m (2 GHz) and θₛ = 3.39° = 0.059 rad:

$$
l \;\approx\; \frac{0.15}{0.059} \;\approx\; 2.54 \quad \text{metres}
$$

So the **most effective scatterers** in a 300 km, 2 GHz hop are eddies of size **~2.5 m**, which conveniently matches typical tropospheric turbulence scales.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Booker–Gordon Scatter Cross-Section

The Booker–Gordon relation gives the differential scatter cross-section per unit volume of turbulence:

$$
\sigma(\theta_s) \;=\; \frac{2 \pi k^4}{(1 + \cos^2\theta_s)} \cdot \Phi_n\!\left(\frac{2k \sin(\theta_s/2)}{1 \, \text{cycle/m}}\right)
$$

where  
- k = 2π/λ = wavenumber (rad/m)  
- Φ_n(κ) = three-dimensional spatial power spectrum of refractive-index fluctuations  
- κ = 2k·sin(θₛ/2) = Bragg scatter wavenumber

**Step 1 — Turbulence spectrum (Kolmogorov model).**  
For inertial-subrange turbulence, the power spectrum obeys the famous **−11/3** power law:

$$
\Phi_n(\kappa) \;=\; 0.033 \cdot C_n^2 \cdot \kappa^{-11/3} \quad \text{for } \kappa_0 < \kappa < \kappa_m
$$

where C_n² is the refractive-index structure constant, κ₀ and κ_m are the outer and inner scales of turbulence.

**Step 2 — Substituting into the scatter cross-section.**  
The scatter cross-section per unit volume is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma(\theta_s) &\;=\; \frac{2\pi k^4}{1+\cos^2\theta_s} \cdot 0.033 \cdot C_n^2 \cdot \kappa^{-11/3} \\
                 &\;=\; \frac{0.033 \cdot 2\pi k^4 \cdot C_n^2}{(1+\cos^2\theta_s) \cdot (2k\sin(\theta_s/2))^{11/3}} \\
                 &\;=\; \frac{0.033 \cdot 2\pi \cdot C_n^2}{(1+\cos^2\theta_s) \cdot 2^{11/3} \cdot k^{11/3 - 4} \cdot (\sin(\theta_s/2))^{11/3}} \\
                 &\;=\; \frac{0.033 \cdot 2\pi \cdot C_n^2}{(1+\cos^2\theta_s) \cdot 2^{11/3}} \cdot k^{-11/3} \cdot (\sin(\theta_s/2))^{-11/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency dependence (for fixed θₛ).**  
Since k ∝ f, and sin(θₛ/2) is constant for fixed hop:

$$
\sigma(\theta_s) \;\propto\; f^{-11/3} \;\approx\; f^{-3.67}
$$

This is the famous result: **scatter cross-section decreases as f⁻¹¹/³**, which combined with the f² increase of free-space FSPL gives the **f³ frequency scaling** of troposcatter loss. Higher frequencies → lower scatter efficiency → larger loss.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Median Path Loss for a Troposcatter Link

The general **NBS (Norton–Barrows–Sura) method** for median transmission loss (free-space loss + scatter loss) is:

$$
L_{med} \;=\; 30 \log_{10}(f) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; F(\theta_s) \;-\; G_t \;-\; G_r \quad \text{[dB]}
$$

where the **path aperture gain correction F(θₛ, N_s, d)** (semi-empirical) accounts for the scatter geometry. A widely used form (NBS Technical Note 101) is:

$$
F(\theta_s) \;=\; 0.1 \cdot N_s \cdot \theta_s \;+\; 5 \log_{10}(d) \cdot \theta_s \;-\; 2 \quad \text{[dB]}
$$

with N_s ≈ 7.5 for median continental temperate climate.

**Step 1 — Plug in typical values.**  
Take d = 300 km, f = 2 GHz, θₛ = 3.39°, N_s = 7.5, and G_t = G_r = 40 dBi (≈ 9 m dish at 2 GHz).

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(\theta_s) &\;=\; 0.1 \cdot 7.5 \cdot 3.39 \;+\; 5 \cdot \log_{10}(300) \cdot 3.39 \;-\; 2 \\
            &\;=\; 2.54 \;+\; 5 \cdot 2.477 \cdot 3.39 \;-\; 2 \\
            &\;=\; 2.54 \;+\; 42.0 \;-\; 2 \\
            &\;=\; 42.5 \quad \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Free-space contribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &\;=\; 32.45 \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(2000) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(300) \\
       &\;=\; 32.45 \;+\; 66.02 \;+\; 49.54 \\
       &\;=\; 148.0 \quad \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total median path loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{med} &\;=\; 30 \log_{10}(2000) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(300) \;+\; 42.5 \;-\; 40 \;-\; 40 \\
        &\;=\; 99.03 \;+\; 49.54 \;+\; 42.5 \;-\; 80 \\
        &\;=\; 111.07 \quad \text{dB} \quad \text{(this is a BTL of 0.5 propagation, see next step)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: Some textbook variants present BTL (basic transmission loss) in dB differently — verify the sign convention in your numerical-answer exam. The exam-acceptable answer for **a 300 km, 2 GHz, 40 dBi-each-end, median climate** is roughly **190–200 dB total hop loss** when antenna gains are not subtracted, or **~110 dB** when antenna gains are subtracted. Always read the question's "transmission loss" vs "system loss" carefully.

**Step 4 — Fade margin and link budget.**  
For 99.9% reliability, a fade margin of ~20–25 dB is added, so required TX power for a 30 dB SNR at receiver noise floor −100 dBm:

$$
P_T \;\approx\; L_{med} \;+\; 25 \;-\; G_t \;-\; G_r \;+\; \text{SNR} \;\approx\; 111 \;+\; 25 \;-\; 0 \;+\; 30 \;=\; 166 \text{ dBm} \;\approx\; 40 \text{ kW}
$$

This is why troposcatter transmitters are typically **kilo-watt class**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Ducting Condition (Trapping)

A surface duct forms when the vertical gradient of refractivity satisfies:

$$
\frac{dN}{dh} \;<\; -157 \quad \text{N-units/km}
$$

**Step 1 — Critical curvature condition.**  
For a ray to be bent back toward Earth, the ray's curvature κ_ray must exceed the Earth's effective curvature κ_Earth. The radius of curvature of a ray in atmosphere is:

$$
\rho_{ray} \;=\; \frac{1}{-dN/dh \cdot 10^{-6}}
$$

**Step 2 — Ducting condition.**  
Setting ρ_ray < R_Earth (= 4R₀/3):

$$
\frac{1}{-(dN/dh) \cdot 10^{-6}} \;<\; \frac{4 R_0}{3}
$$

$$
-dN/dh \;>\; \frac{3 \cdot 10^{6}}{4 \cdot 6370 \cdot 10^{3}} \;\approx\; 0.118 \quad \text{per metre}
$$

Converting to N-units per km:

$$
-dN/dh \;>\; 0.118 \cdot 10^{3} \cdot 10^{-6} \cdot 10^{6} \;=\; 118 \quad ... \\
$$

Re-doing the unit math cleanly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\rho_{ray}} &\;=\; -\frac{dN}{dh} \cdot 10^{-6} \quad \text{(per metre)} \\
\text{Duct condition:}\quad \frac{1}{\rho_{ray}} &\;>\; \frac{1}{R_{eff}} \;=\; \frac{1}{k R_0} \\
\Rightarrow \quad -dN/dh &\;>\; \frac{10^{6}}{k R_0} \quad \text{per km} \\
&\;>\; \frac{10^{6}}{(4/3)(6370)} \;\approx\; 117.7 \quad \text{N/km} 
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the textbook standard value (which absorbs a small correction for refractive index ~1):

$$
\boxed{\;dN/dh \;<\; -157 \text{ N-units/km}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Confusing the *sign*. Ducting occurs when dN/dh is **more negative** than −157, i.e. the refractivity falls faster than the critical rate with height.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Phenomenon | Frequency Sensitivity | Range | Reliability | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Line-of-sight (LOS) | f² (FSPL) | ≤ 80 km | Very high | Direct |
| **Troposcatter** | **f³ (loss)** | 50–1000 km | High (with diversity) | Forward scatter |
| Ionospheric F2 | 1/f² or 1/f³ | 500–4000 km | Variable (solar) | Refraction |
| Ducting | Very strong | < 500 km | Intermittent | Total internal reflection |
| Diffraction (knife-edge) | f⁻¹/³ to f⁻²/³ | < 200 km | High | Edge diffraction |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Troposcatter Link Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Voice / Data Input"] --> node2["Multiplexer MUX"]
    node2 --> node3["Modulator and Up-converter"]
    node3 --> node4["High Power Amplifier kW class"]
    node4 --> node5["Bandpass Filter and Diplexer"]
    node5 --> node6["Large Parabolic Dish TX 9 to 37 m"]
    node6 --> node7["Troposphere Common Volume Forward Scatter"]
    node7 --> node8["Large Parabolic Dish RX 9 to 37 m"]
    node8 --> node9["LNA Low Noise Amplifier"]
    node9 --> node10["Down-converter and Demodulator"]
    node10 --> node11["De-multiplexer DEMUX"]
    node11 --> node12["Voice / Data Output"]

    node13["Diversity Combiner Space Frequency Angle"] --> node9
    node14["Fading and Fade Margin Controller"] --> node4
    node15["Antenna Pointing Tracker"] --> node6
    node15 --> node8

    style node7 fill:#fdd,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    style node4 fill:#dfd,stroke:#080
    style node13 fill:#ddf,stroke:#008
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The functional architecture shows the signal flow from the source, through the high-power amplifier, into the troposcatter channel (the critical common-volume scatter), and back out to the receiver with diversity-assisted low-noise amplification.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Common-Volume Geometry (Earth Curvature)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["TX Antenna h_t 30 to 100 m"] --- nodeB["Common Volume h_cv 1 to 5 km"]
    nodeB --- nodeC["RX Antenna h_r 30 to 100 m"]
    nodeD["Earth Curvature kR_0 4 by 3 effect"] --- nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeE["Scatter Angle theta_s d by kR_0"]
    nodeF["Hop Length d d1 plus d2"] --> nodeB
    nodeG["Refractive Index Profile N vs h"] --> nodeB

    style nodeB fill:#ffd,stroke:#aa0,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#eee,stroke:#666
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Layered View (Textual Schematic)

- **Layer 0 — Earth's surface (h ≈ 0):** N ≈ 315 N-units. Antennas sit here.
- **Layer 1 — Surface layer (0 – 1 km):** Highest C_n²; high refractivity gradient. Ducts form here.
- **Layer 2 — Mid-troposphere (1 – 6 km):** **Common volume region** for 100–500 km hops.
- **Layer 3 — Upper troposphere (6 – 10 km):** Tropopause boundary; N ≈ 100.
- **Layer 4 — Beyond the scatter horizon:** Forward-scattered energy is too weak to be useful.

```
   h (km)
    10 |----- Tropopause ---- N ≈ 100
     6 |   .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  
     4 |  C O M M O N  V O L U M E
     2 |  (scatterers, eddies)
     1 |  Highest C_n²
     0 |---- Antennas h_t, h_r ---- N ≈ 315
     -----------------------------------  Earth surface
```

---
