# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1  `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define tropospheric scatter propagation. State any four typical characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Tropospheric scatter is a beyond-the-horizon propagation mechanism in which VHF/UHF radio waves are scattered by refractive-index irregularities (turbulent eddies) in the lower atmosphere (troposphere) to allow communication over distances of 50–1000 km.

**Four characteristics:**
1. Operating band: VHF to lower SHF, typically 1–4 GHz (sweet spot).
2. Hop length: 50–1000 km (typical 100–500 km).
3. High transmitter power (1 kW to 50 kW) and large high-gain parabolic antennas (9–37 m) required.
4. Path loss varies roughly as f³ × d³ and exhibits Rayleigh and slow fading.
5. Independent of ionospheric and solar activity — works day and night.

*(Any four = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is the critical refractivity gradient for atmospheric ducting? Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The critical refractivity gradient for atmospheric ducting is **dN/dh = −157 N-units/km**.

**Significance:** When the actual vertical refractivity gradient is more negative than this critical value (i.e., refractivity falls faster than 157 N-units per km increase in height), the atmospheric stratification bends radio waves back toward the Earth — a phenomenon called **ducting or super-refraction**. Ducting can trap UHF/SHF signals and cause them to propagate hundreds of kilometres with anomalously low loss, but it is intermittent, terrain-dependent, and can cause co-channel interference on troposcatter links.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the geometry of a tropospheric scatter link, defining **common volume** and **scatter angle**. Derive the expression for the common-volume height `h_cv` in terms of hop length `d` and Earth's effective radius `k R₀`.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A troposcatter link operates at 3 GHz over a hop length of 250 km. The parabolic dish antennas at both ends have gains of 42 dBi. Estimate the median path loss using the NBS method. Take the scatter-aperture correction `F(θₛ) = 42.5 dB` (assume isotropic gain reference) and the structure-parameter `N_s = 7.5`.  *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Geometry and Derivation (7 marks):**

> **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw Earth as a circle of radius kR₀, two antenna towers at heights h_t, h_r separated by total path d = d₁ + d₂ along the chord. Mark the common volume (intersection of antenna beams) at height h_cv above the chord.

**Geometric setup (2 marks):**  
- The CV is the intersection of the two pencil beams of the TX and RX antennas.  
- The scatter angle θₛ is the angle between the forward-going TX ray and the backward-going RX ray, measured at the CV.  
- For symmetric hop (d₁ = d₂ = d/2), the CV lies at the midpoint of the chord and is the **maximum cross-section** of the two overlapping beams.

**Derivation (3 marks):**  
The CV height h_cv above the chord joining the two antenna bases satisfies the small-angle chord–sagitta relation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For chord length } d \text{ on a sphere of radius } k R_0: \\
h_{cv} &\;\approx\; R \left(1 - \cos\!\left(\frac{d}{2 k R_0}\right)\right) \\
       &\;\approx\; R \cdot \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{d}{2 k R_0}\right)^2 \quad \text{(small angle, cos expansion)} \\
       &\;=\; \frac{d^2}{8 \, k \, R_0} \\
\text{For asymmetric } d_1, d_2: \quad h_{cv} &\;\approx\; \frac{d_1 \, d_2}{2 \, k \, R_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

For k = 4/3 and R₀ = 6370 km:

$$
h_{cv} \;=\; \frac{d_1 d_2}{2 \cdot (4/3) \cdot 6370} \;=\; \frac{d_1 d_2}{16987} \quad \text{[km]}
$$

The **scatter angle** is then:

$$
\theta_s \;=\; \frac{d_1 + d_2}{k R_0} \;=\; \frac{d}{k R_0} \quad \text{[radians]} \;\approx\; \frac{d \cdot 180}{\pi \cdot k R_0} \quad \text{[deg]}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Path Loss (7 marks):**

**Given:** f = 3 GHz, d = 250 km, G_t = G_r = 42 dBi, F(θₛ) = 42.5 dB.

**Step 1 — Compute θₛ.**

$$
\theta_s \;\approx\; \frac{250}{(4/3)(6370)} \;\text{rad} \;=\; 0.0294 \text{ rad} \;=\; 1.686°
$$

(Verify F(θₛ) is consistent — they have given F = 42.5 dB in the problem, so we use it directly.)

**Step 2 — Free-space term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &\;=\; 32.45 \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(3000) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(250) \\
       &\;=\; 32.45 \;+\; 69.54 \;+\; 47.96 \\
       &\;=\; 149.95 \;\text{dB} \;\approx\; 150.0 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — NBS median transmission loss (before antenna gain).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{med} &\;=\; 30 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(d) \;+\; F(\theta_s) \\
        &\;=\; 30 \log_{10}(3000) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(250) \;+\; 42.5 \\
        &\;=\; 30 \cdot 3.477 \;+\; 47.96 \;+\; 42.5 \\
        &\;=\; 104.3 \;+\; 47.96 \;+\; 42.5 \\
        &\;=\; 194.76 \;\text{dB} \;\approx\; 195 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Net transmission loss with antenna gains (system loss).**

$$
L_{sys} \;=\; L_{med} \;-\; G_t \;-\; G_r \;=\; 194.76 \;-\; 42 \;-\; 42 \;=\; 110.76 \;\text{dB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Do **not** mix `30 log f` and `20 log f` — NBS uses **30 log f** for the f³ dependence, **NOT** the free-space 20 log f.
> 2. Do not forget to convert d to km and f to MHz.
> 3. Watch whether the question asks for **transmission loss** (includes gains) or **system loss** (subtracts gains) — they differ by G_t + G_r ≈ 84 dB.
> 4. Use k = 4/3 in the Earth radius unless specified otherwise.
> 5. Always include **units** in your final answer (dB).

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, describe the **refractive index structure of the troposphere** and explain the formation of **surface ducts** and **elevated ducts**. State the critical gradient condition.  *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 4 GHz troposcatter link has hop length of 400 km. Both antennas have diameter 18 m, efficiency 0.55. Estimate the antenna gains and median path loss if the effective scatter-aperture factor F(θₛ) = 48 dB. Use N_s = 8.0 and k = 4/3.  *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Refractive Index Structure and Ducts (7 marks):**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A graph of N vs h showing the standard exponential decrease, the critical slope −157 N/km, and labelled surface-duct and elevated-duct regions.

**Refractive index profile (2 marks):**  
The refractivity N = (n − 1) × 10⁶ decreases approximately exponentially with height:

$$
N(h) \;\approx\; 315 \cdot e^{-0.136 \, h} \quad \text{(h in km, surface value 315 N-units)}
$$

This gives a standard gradient of about −39 N/km near the surface. **Ducts** form when the actual gradient is more negative than the critical **−157 N/km** (or equivalently the M-curve shows a local minimum of M = N + 157h).

**Duct types (3 marks):**  
- **Surface (ground-based) duct:** Grounded at Earth's surface. Typical in hot, humid coastal regions (Arabian Sea, Persian Gulf). The duct traps energy between the surface and the trapping layer at h_t.
- **Elevated (subsurface) duct:** Suspended aloft, bounded above and below by layers of high refractivity gradient. Common in trade-wind regions.
- Both ducts act as **waveguides** for UHF/SHF and produce anomalous long-range propagation.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Estimate (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Antenna gain.**

$$
G \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\eta \left(\frac{\pi D f}{c}\right)^2\right) \quad \text{dBi}
$$

With η = 0.55, D = 18 m, f = 4 GHz, c = 3×10⁸ m/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\pi D f}{c} &\;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot 18 \cdot 4 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \;=\; \frac{226.2 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \;=\; 754 \\
G &\;=\; 10 \log_{10}(0.55 \cdot 754^2) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(0.55 \cdot 568516) \\
  &\;=\; 10 \log_{10}(312684) \;=\; 54.95 \;\text{dBi} \;\approx\; 55 \;\text{dBi}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Median path loss (NBS).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{med} &\;=\; 30 \log_{10}(4000) \;+\; 20 \log_{10}(400) \;+\; 48 \\
        &\;=\; 30 \cdot 3.602 \;+\; 52.04 \;+\; 48 \\
        &\;=\; 108.06 \;+\; 52.04 \;+\; 48 \\
        &\;=\; 208.1 \;\text{dB} \;\approx\; 208 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify F(θₛ) consistency.**

$$
\theta_s \;\approx\; \frac{400}{88.5} \;\approx\; 4.52°,\quad F \;\approx\; 0.1 \cdot 8.0 \cdot 4.52 \;+\; 5 \log_{10}(400) \cdot 4.52 \;-\; 2 \;\approx\; 3.62 \;+\; 47.71 \;-\; 2 \;\approx\; 49.3
$$

Close to the assumed F = 48 dB ✓.

**Step 4 — System loss.**

$$
L_{sys} \;=\; 208.1 \;-\; 55 \;-\; 55 \;=\; 98.1 \;\text{dB}
$$

With a 30 dB fade margin (99.9% reliability) and a 30 dB receiver SNR requirement, the required TX power is:

$$
P_T \;\approx\; 98.1 \;+\; 30 \;+\; 30 \;+\; (-100) \;\text{(noise floor)} \;=\; 58.1 \;\text{dBm} \;\approx\; 660 \text{ W}
$$

So a **~1 kW HPA** suffices for this link with comfortable margin.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision list — print this and pin it up.**

- [ ] Troposcatter is **forward scatter** from **refractive index irregularities** in the common volume.
- [ ] Typical **scatter angle θₛ < 10°**; the smaller, the stronger the received signal.
- [ ] **Common volume** = intersection of TX and RX antenna beams at height `h_cv ≈ d₁d₂ / (2kR₀)`.
- [ ] **Effective Earth radius factor** k = **4/3** (standard atmosphere), k = 1 (no refraction).
- [ ] **Critical gradient for ducting:** dN/dh = **−157 N-units/km**.
- [ ] **Standard gradient:** dN/dh = **−39 N-units/km**.
- [ ] **Booker–Gordon scatter cross-section** ∝ **f⁻¹¹/³** (Bragg condition with Kolmogorov −11/3 spectrum).
- [ ] **Median loss** ∝ **f³ · d³** (approximate) — verify with NBS formula.
- [ ] NBS formula: `L_med = 30 log f + 20 log d + F(θₛ) − G_t − G_r` (f in MHz, d in km).
- [ ] F(θₛ) is **positive** (path-aperture gain) and grows with θₛ, d, N_s.
- [ ] **Best frequency band:** 1–4 GHz (rain attenuation not too high, scatter still efficient).
- [ ] **Required hardware:** Large parabolic dishes (9–37 m, G = 40–55 dBi) + kW-class HPAs + LNA + diversity combiner.
- [ ] **Diversity types:** frequency, space (antenna), angle, polarisation — gain of 12–16 dB with 4-fold diversity.
- [ ] **Fading types:** Fast (Rayleigh, seconds), Slow (log-normal, hours/days).
- [ ] **Free-space path loss** formula: FSPL = 32.45 + 20 log f + 20 log d (f in MHz, d in km).
- [ ] **Line-of-sight max distance** (with k = 4/3): d_LOS ≈ √(17 h_t) + √(17 h_r) km, h in m.
- [ ] The **troposphere** extends 0–10 km; the **ionosphere** starts ~60 km. Troposcatter is unaffected by solar activity.
- [ ] Past, real-world examples: **DEW Line, White Alice, Indian Doordarshan, NORAD, oil/gas pipelines**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**  
> • If the question says *transmission loss*, give the **NBS value (with − G_t − G_r)**.  
> • If it says *path loss* or *propagation loss*, give **only the f³ + F term (without gains)**.  
> • If it says *system loss* or *net loss*, include the antenna gains.  
> **Always state which one you are calculating.** This is the #1 source of confusion and lost marks in KTU valuation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ Module 4: Ionospheric Propagation — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ionospheric Propagation** is the mechanism by which electromagnetic waves in the **HF (3–30 MHz)** and lower **VHF (30–300 MHz)** bands are bent (refracted) back toward the Earth's surface by the **ionized layers of the upper atmosphere (the ionosphere)**, enabling **over-the-horizon (OTH)** radio communication beyond the line-of-sight (LoS) limit.

The **ionosphere** is the region of the Earth's upper atmosphere, extending from approximately **60 km to 1000 km** altitude, where solar ultraviolet (UV) and X-ray radiation ionize neutral gas molecules (primarily $O_2$, $N_2$, and $O$), producing free electrons and positive ions in sufficient density to significantly affect the **refractive index** of the medium for radio waves.

The complex refractive index for an ionospheric medium (ignoring collisions and Earth's magnetic field) is given by the **Appleton equation** in its simplest form:

$$n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{f_p^2}{f^2}} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{N_e \, e^2}{4\pi^2 \, m \, \epsilon_0 \, f^2}}$$

Where:
- $n$ = refractive index of the ionosphere
- $f_p$ = plasma frequency of the layer (Hz)
- $f$ = operating frequency (Hz)
- $N_e$ = electron density (electrons/m³)
- $e$ = electron charge = $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$ C
- $m$ = electron mass = $\mathbf{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}$ kg
- $\epsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = $\mathbf{8.854 \times 10^{-12}}$ F/m

> [!NOTE]
> When $f > f_p$, the wave propagates through the layer. When $f < f_p$, the wave is **reflected back**. This frequency-dependent behavior is the foundation of ionospheric propagation physics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool, holding a flashlight just above the water surface and shining it diagonally into the water. The light ray bends (refracts) as it enters the denser medium and eventually **curves back toward the surface** if angled correctly. The ionosphere works on the same principle — except the "water surface" is a **gradient of free electrons** hanging 60–500 km above the Earth, and the "flashlight" is your HF radio signal.

**Plain English Explanation:**
The Sun bombards the upper atmosphere with high-energy particles and UV rays. This **ionizes** (strips electrons from) gas molecules, creating a vast natural "mirror" of free electrons. When a radio wave hits this electron cloud, the electrons **oscillate** in response and re-radiate the wave. If the wave frequency is low enough, the collective response of these electrons acts like a **dispersive mirror**, bending the wave back to Earth — even though the mirror is hundreds of kilometers away and invisible.

**Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of the ionosphere as a giant **invisible trampoline** suspended above the Earth. Low-frequency waves (like a heavy basketball) **bounce** off it easily, while high-frequency waves (like a ping-pong ball) pass right through. This is why your grandfather's AM radio could receive signals from across the ocean, but your Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz) cannot.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Important)

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electron Charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ | Coulomb |
| Electron Mass | $m$ | $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ | kg |
| Permittivity of Free Space | $\epsilon_0$ | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| Permeability of Free Space | $\mu_0$ | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ | H/m |
| Speed of Light | $c$ | $3 \times 10^8$ | m/s |
| Earth Radius | $R_E$ | 6371 | km |
| Typical Peak $N_e$ (F2 layer) | $N_{max}$ | $10^{12}$ | electrons/m³ |
| HF Band | — | 3–30 | MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **electron density ($N_e$)** is the single most important variable in ionospheric propagation. It varies with: **time of day, season, latitude, solar cycle (11-year), and sunspot activity.**

---

## 1.4 Layers of the Ionosphere — A Bird's-Eye View

The ionosphere is divided into four principal layers, each with distinct formation mechanisms and propagation characteristics:

| Layer | Altitude (km) | Peak Electron Density ($N_{max}$, m⁻³) | Day/Night | Affects Frequency Band |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **D Layer** | 60–90 | $\sim 10^9$ | Exists in **day only** | Absorbs MF/LF; reflects very low freq |
| **E Layer** | 100–150 | $\sim 10^{11}$ | Stronger in day, weak at night | Reflects HF (low end) |
| **Sporadic E ($E_s$)** | 100–150 | $\sim 10^{12}$ | Irregular patches | VHF (occasionally) |
| **F1 Layer** | 150–250 | $\sim 10^{11}$–$10^{12}$ | Day only (merges with F2 at night) | HF |
| **F2 Layer** | 250–400 | $\sim 10^{12}$ (peak) | Exists day and night | **Primary HF reflection layer** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** **"D-E-F"** in increasing altitude. Think: **"Dumb Earthworms Fly"** — from Dumb (lowest D) to Flying (highest F). D is the **absorber**, F2 is the **reflector**.

---

## 1.5 Mechanism of Ionospheric Propagation — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Ground-based HF transmitter** radiates a wave toward the ionosphere at some **angle of elevation $\Delta$**.
2. The wave enters the ionosphere and travels through regions of **progressively increasing electron density**.
3. Since $n$ decreases as $N_e$ increases (see formula above), the wavefront **bends continuously** (analogous to Snell's law in a graded-index medium).
4. At the layer where $f = f_p$ (i.e., $n \to 0$), the wave reaches its **point of reflection**.
5. The wave is bent back and exits the ionosphere, returning to Earth at a **skip distance** from the transmitter.
6. **Multiple reflections** between the ionosphere and the ground enable **multi-hop** long-distance communication.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚡ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Operational Concept — Structured Logic

### 🔹 Step A: Refractive Index Behavior in Plasma

A plasma (ionosphere) is a dispersive medium. For an electron oscillating under the influence of an EM wave (ignoring magnetic field and collisions), the equation of motion is:

$$m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -e E_0 \, e^{j\omega t}$$

This yields a **time-varying dipole moment**, and substituting into the wave equation gives the refractive index:

$$n^2 = 1 - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2} = 1 - \frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0 \omega^2}$$

Where the **plasma (angular) frequency** is:

$$\omega_p = \sqrt{\frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0}} \quad \text{rad/s}$$

In Hz:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0}} \approx 9\sqrt{N_e} \;\; \text{Hz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **9** comes from substituting all fundamental constants: $\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(1.602\times10^{-19})^2}{(9.11\times10^{-31})(8.854\times10^{-12})}} \approx 8.98 \approx 9$.
> This is the **most-used formula in KTU exams** for ionospheric numerical problems.

### 🔹 Step B: The "Why" Behind the Reflection

- If $f > f_p$: $n$ is **real and positive** → wave **propagates** through (slightly slowed).
- If $f = f_p$: $n = 0$ → wave is **reflected** (theoretically, wave amplitude becomes zero and reverses).
- If $f < f_p$: $n$ becomes **imaginary** → wave **evanesces** (exponentially decays).
- In a **real gradient** (continuously varying $N_e$), the wave is **bent gradually** and returns to Earth even before reaching the critical point — this is **gradual refraction**, not sharp reflection.

### 🔹 Step C: Critical Frequency ($f_c$)

The **critical frequency** is the highest frequency that can be reflected back to Earth when the wave is transmitted **vertically upward** ($\theta = 90°$). At vertical incidence, the wave reaches the layer where $N_e = N_{max}$, and reflection occurs when $f = f_p(N_{max})$:

$$\boxed{f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{max}} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

Where $N_{max}$ is in electrons/m³.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The critical frequency assumes **vertical incidence ($\theta_i = 0°$)**. For oblique incidence, the frequency that can be reflected is **higher** than $f_c$ — this is given by the **Secant Law**.

### 🔹 Step D: Secant Law (Oblique Incidence / Maximum Usable Frequency)

When a wave is transmitted at an angle $\theta_i$ to the normal (i.e., NOT vertical), it penetrates to a layer where the **effective electron density** is higher. The maximum frequency reflected at angle $\theta_i$ is:

$$\boxed{f_{\max} = f_c \cdot \sec \theta_i = \frac{f_c}{\cos \theta_i}}$$

This is known as the **Secant Law** or **Breit-Tuve Theorem**.

By Snell's law (applied to ionospheric layers), this is also expressed as:

$$f_{\max} = f_c \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{d}{h'}\right)^2} = f_c \cdot \sec \theta_i$$

Where:
- $d$ = ground distance (half skip distance)
- $h'$ = **virtual height** of the reflecting layer
- $\theta_i$ = angle of incidence at the ionosphere

### 🔹 Step E: Virtual Height ($h'$)

Since the ionosphere is a **gradually varying medium**, the wave is **bent continuously**, not reflected sharply. The **virtual height** is the height at which a *hypothetical sharp reflection* would have to occur to produce the same total time delay as the actual refracted path.

Measured by an **ionosonde** (pulsed HF sounder) using the relationship:

$$h' = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}$$

Where $\Delta t$ = round-trip time delay between transmitted pulse and received echo.

### 🔹 Step F: Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF) & Skip Distance

- **MUF** = the highest frequency that supports communication between two specific points via ionospheric reflection at a given time. Depends on path geometry, solar conditions, and layer characteristics.
- **Skip Distance ($D_s$)** = the minimum ground distance from the transmitter at which the sky-wave returns to Earth. Closer than $D_s$, only ground wave is received (creating a **skip zone** or **dead zone**).

Approximate relation:

$$D_s \approx 2 h' \sqrt{\left(\frac{f_{MUF}}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1}$$

### 🔹 Step G: Lowest Usable Frequency (LUF) & Optimum Working Frequency (OWF)

- **LUF**: Minimum frequency usable — limited by **ionospheric absorption** (mainly in D-layer). Below LUF, signal-to-noise ratio becomes unacceptable.
- **OWF (or FOT — Frequency of Optimum Traffic)**: Practically, ~**85% of MUF**. Provides the best balance between low absorption and reliable propagation.

$$\boxed{\text{OWF} \approx 0.85 \times \text{MUF}}$$

### 🔹 Step H: Effect of Earth's Magnetic Field — Magneto-ionic Theory

When the Earth's magnetic field ($B \approx 50 \,\mu T$) is included, the ionosphere becomes an **anisotropic medium**. The wave splits into two characteristic waves:

1. **Ordinary Wave (o-wave)**: Refracted/refracted according to the simple Appleton formula above.
2. **Extraordinary Wave (x-wave)**: Different refractive index; experiences different reflection conditions.

This phenomenon causes:
- **Faraday Rotation**: The polarization plane of a linearly polarized wave rotates as it propagates through the magnetized ionosphere. Total rotation:

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{e^3}{2 \epsilon_0 m^2 c f^2} \int_0^{path} N_e \, B \cos\theta \, ds}$$

Where $\theta$ is the angle between propagation direction and $\vec{B}$.

- **Whistlers**: Very Low Frequency (VLF) waves (1–30 kHz) that follow the Earth's magnetic field lines from one hemisphere to the other, producing descending-tone audio-frequency "whistles" in radio receivers. Generated by **lightning strikes**.

### 🔹 Step I: Multi-hop Propagation

For long-distance communication, the wave undergoes multiple reflections:

$$D_{total} = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + \ldots = n \cdot D_{hop}$$

Each hop incurs additional **path loss** and may involve different ionospheric layers (e.g., F2 + F2 + E).

---

## 2.2 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_p = 9\sqrt{N_e}$ | Plasma frequency | Hz, with $N_e$ in m⁻³ |
| 2 | $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ | Critical frequency (vertical) | Hz |
| 3 | $n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{f_p^2}{f^2}}$ | Refractive index | dimensionless |
| 4 | $f_{MUF} = f_c \cdot \sec\theta_i$ | MUF at oblique incidence | Hz |
| 5 | $f_{MUF} = f_c \sqrt{1 + (d/h')^2}$ | MUF in terms of geometry | Hz |
| 6 | $h' = c \Delta t / 2$ | Virtual height | m |
| 7 | $OWF \approx 0.85 \times MUF$ | Optimum working frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{(f/f_c)^2 - 1}$ | Skip distance | m |
| 9 | $\Omega = \frac{2.36 \times 10^4}{f^2} \int N_e B \, ds$ | Faraday rotation | radians |
| 10 | $\alpha \propto \frac{N_e}{f^2}$ | D-layer absorption coefficient | Np/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** When numerical answers are required, ALWAYS keep $N_e$ in **electrons/m³** and $f$ in **Hz**. If $N_e$ is given in **electrons/cm³**, convert: $1$ cm⁻³ $= 10^6$ m⁻³.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **International Broadcasting**: BBC World Service, Voice of America, All India Radio use HF sky-wave for transoceanic reach.
- **Maritime & Aeronautical Communication**: Ship-to-shore HF, aircraft over oceans.
- **Military & Diplomatic Comms**: Long-haul, infrastructure-independent links.
- **Amateur (Ham) Radio**: DX (long-distance) communication on HF/VHF.
- **Over-the-Horizon Radar (OTHR)**: Australian Jindalee, Russian Duga — uses ionospheric reflection to detect ships/aircraft thousands of km away.
- **GNSS Errors**: Ionospheric delay is the **largest error source** in GPS — dual-frequency receivers correct for it.
- **Space Weather Monitoring**: Sudden Ionospheric Disturbances (SIDs) caused by solar flares can disrupt HF communications globally.

# ⚡ Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Plasma Frequency from Electron Equation of Motion

**Starting Premise:** A free electron in the ionosphere is displaced by an oscillating electric field $E = E_0 e^{j\omega t}$ of the incident wave.

The force on the electron: $F = -eE = ma$

$$m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -e E_0 e^{j\omega t}$$

Assuming steady-state harmonic solution: $x = X e^{j\omega t}$, where $X$ is the complex amplitude.

$$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -\omega^2 X e^{j\omega t}$$

Substituting:

$$m(-\omega^2) X e^{j\omega t} = -e E_0 e^{j\omega t}$$

$$\Rightarrow X = \frac{e E_0}{m \omega^2}$$

This displacement produces a **dipole moment** per electron: $p = -eX = -\frac{e^2 E_0}{m \omega^2}$

For $N_e$ electrons per unit volume, the **polarization** $P = N_e p$:

$$P = -\frac{N_e e^2 E_0}{m \omega^2}$$

The electric displacement is $D = \epsilon_0 E + P = \epsilon_0 E \left(1 - \frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0 \omega^2}\right)$

By definition, $D = \epsilon E$, so the effective permittivity is:

$$\epsilon = \epsilon_0 \left(1 - \frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0 \omega^2}\right)$$

Since $n^2 = \epsilon / \epsilon_0$ (non-magnetic ionosphere, $\mu = \mu_0$):

$$n^2 = 1 - \frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0 \omega^2} = 1 - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2}$$

Defining plasma angular frequency:

$$\omega_p = \sqrt{\frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_p = \frac{\omega_p}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N_e e^2}{m \epsilon_0}}$$

Substituting the constants numerically:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2}{(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(8.854 \times 10^{-12})}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2.566 \times 10^{-38}}{8.066 \times 10^{-42}}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{3.181 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi}(56.40) = 8.976 \;\;\text{Hz} \cdot \sqrt{N_e}$$

$$\boxed{f_p \approx 9\sqrt{N_e} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

**Interpretation:** Each layer of the ionosphere has a *characteristic plasma frequency*. A wave of frequency $f$ will be reflected if it encounters a region where $N_e \geq N_{critical} = (f/9)^2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Frequency for Vertical Incidence

For a wave transmitted **straight up** ($\theta_i = 0$, vertical incidence):

The wave penetrates the layer and continues until it reaches the point where $N_e = N_{max}$ (the peak of the layer). At this peak, if the wave frequency equals the local plasma frequency, it is reflected.

Setting $f = f_p(N_{max})$:

$$f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}} \;\; \text{Hz}$$

**Example (KTU-style numerical):**

> **Problem:** The critical frequency of the F2 layer is observed to be 8 MHz. Find the maximum electron density of the layer.

**Solution:**

Given: $f_c = 8 \times 10^6$ Hz.

$$N_{max} = \left(\frac{f_c}{9}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{8 \times 10^6}{9}\right)^2$$

$$= (8.889 \times 10^5)^2 = 7.90 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{electrons/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{N_{max} \approx 7.9 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{electrons/m}^3}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Secant Law (Maximum Usable Frequency at Oblique Incidence)

Consider a wave transmitted from point T on the ground, striking the ionosphere at point A with an angle of incidence $\theta_i$ to the vertical (i.e., measured from the normal). It is reflected from the **virtual height** $h'$ and lands at point R.

By Snell's law applied to the curved ionospheric layer (assuming a flat ionosphere at height $h'$ and applying geometry):

$$\sin(90° - \Delta) = \sin \alpha$$

where $\Delta$ is the elevation angle at the ground.

The horizontal ground distance from T to the foot of the perpendicular from A is:

$$d = h' \tan \theta_i$$

The angle $\theta_i$ relates to the elevation angle $\Delta$:

$$\cos \theta_i = \sin \Delta$$

The frequency that can penetrate to a particular $\theta_i$ is given by the **equivalent vertical frequency** $f_v$ at that electron density:

$$f_v = f_c \cos \theta_i$$

But the actual oblique frequency is:

$$f_{oblique} = \frac{f_v}{\cos \theta_i} = \frac{f_c \cos \theta_i}{\cos \theta_i} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos \theta_i}$$

Wait — the rigorous derivation uses the **equivalent path theorem (Breit-Tuve)**:

A wave at oblique incidence on a curved layer is equivalent to a wave at vertical incidence on a **flat layer** at virtual height $h'$, provided the equivalent vertical frequency $f_v$ satisfies:

$$f_v = f_{oblique} \cos \theta_i$$

Since at vertical incidence, maximum frequency is $f_c$:

$$f_v = f_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = f_{oblique} \cos \theta_i$$

$$\Rightarrow \boxed{f_{oblique} = \frac{f_c}{\cos \theta_i} = f_c \sec \theta_i}$$

Now, since $\tan \theta_i = d/h'$:

$$\sec \theta_i = \sqrt{1 + \tan^2 \theta_i} = \sqrt{1 + (d/h')^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_{MUF} = f_c \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{d}{h'}\right)^2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Skip Distance Formula

The skip distance is the **minimum ground range** at which the sky wave returns to Earth. It occurs when the wave is transmitted at the **lowest possible elevation angle** that still allows reflection back to Earth (i.e., the wave grazes the Earth tangentially).

Using the MUF formula:

$$f_{MUF} = f_c \sqrt{1 + (D_s / (2h'))^2}$$

Solving for $D_s$:

$$\left(\frac{f_{MUF}}{f_c}\right)^2 = 1 + \left(\frac{D_s}{2h'}\right)^2$$

$$\left(\frac{D_s}{2h'}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{f_{MUF}}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1$$

$$\boxed{D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{\left(\frac{f_{MUF}}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Numerical — Multi-Hop Path Loss (Sample Problem)

> **Problem:** A 15 MHz signal is reflected from the F2 layer with virtual height 300 km. The critical frequency is 7 MHz. Calculate:
> (a) The skip distance
> (b) The MUF for a ground distance of 1500 km
> (c) The number of hops needed for a 4500 km path

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using $D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{(f/f_c)^2 - 1}$:

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{15}{7} = 2.143$$

$$\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1 = (2.143)^2 - 1 = 4.592 - 1 = 3.592$$

$$\sqrt{3.592} = 1.895$$

$$D_s = 2 \times 300 \times 1.895 = 1137 \;\; \text{km}$$

**(b)** Using $f_{MUF} = f_c \sqrt{1 + (d/h')^2}$:

$$\frac{d}{h'} = \frac{1500}{300} = 5$$

$$\sqrt{1 + 25} = \sqrt{26} = 5.099$$

$$f_{MUF} = 7 \times 5.099 = 35.69 \;\; \text{MHz}$$

**(c)** Number of hops = $\lceil 4500 / 1137 \rceil = \lceil 3.96 \rceil = 4$ hops.

$$\boxed{\text{4 hops required (using minimal skip distance per hop)}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow for KTU Problems

| Given | Find | Use Formula |
|---|---|---|
| $N_e$ | $f_p$ | $f_p = 9\sqrt{N_e}$ |
| $f_c$ | $N_{max}$ | $N_{max} = (f_c/9)^2$ |
| $f_c$, $h'$ | MUF for distance $d$ | $f_{MUF} = f_c\sqrt{1+(d/h')^2}$ |
| $f$, $f_c$, $h'$ | Skip distance | $D_s = 2h'\sqrt{(f/f_c)^2 - 1}$ |
| Path length $L$, $D_s$ | # of hops | $n = \lceil L/D_s \rceil$ |
| Time delay $\Delta t$ | Virtual height | $h' = c \Delta t / 2$ |
| $f$, path, $B$, $N_e$ | Faraday rotation | $\Omega = (2.36 \times 10^4 / f^2) \int N_e B \, ds$ |

## 4.1 Ionospheric Layer Architecture (Block-Level Functional Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SUN[Sun UV and X-ray Radiation] --> IONIZE[Atmospheric Ionization Process]
    IONIZE --> DLAYER[D Layer 60 to 90 km]
    IONIZE --> ELAYER[E Layer 100 to 150 km]
    IONIZE --> F1LAYER[F1 Layer 150 to 250 km]
    IONIZE --> F2LAYER[F2 Layer 250 to 400 km]

    DLAYER --> DABSORB[Absorbs MF and LF and Disturbs HF during day]
    ELAYER --> EREFLECT[Reflects low HF band partially]
    F1LAYER --> F1MERGE[Day only merges into F2 at night]
    F2LAYER --> F2REFLECT[Primary HF reflector day and night]

    TX[HF Transmitter on Ground] --> WAVEUP[Wave Radiated Upward]
    WAVEUP --> REFRACT[Continuous Refraction in Ionospheric Gradient]
    REFRACT --> REFLECT[Wave Returns to Earth at Skip Distance]
    REFLECT --> RX[Receiver at Ground]
    REFLECT --> MULTIHOP[Multiple Hops for Long Distance]
    MULTIHOP --> RX

    BFIELD[Earth Magnetic Field 50 microT] --> FARADAY[Faraday Rotation of Polarization]
    BFIELD --> SPLIT[Wave Splits into Ordinary and Extraordinary]

    SOLARCYCLE[11 Year Solar Cycle] --> IONIZE
    DAYNIGHT[Day Night Cycle] --> DLAYER
    DAYNIGHT --> F1LAYER
```

# ⚡ Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Critical Frequency" in ionospheric propagation. Derive its relationship with the maximum electron density of the ionospheric layer.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical frequency** ($f_c$) of an ionospheric layer is the highest frequency of a radio wave that can be reflected back to Earth when transmitted **vertically upward** (i.e., at normal incidence).

At vertical incidence, the wave propagates up and reaches the layer where electron density is maximum ($N_{max}$). Reflection occurs when the operating frequency $f$ equals the local plasma frequency $f_p$:

$$f = f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N_{max} e^2}{m \epsilon_0}}$$

Substituting physical constants and converting to Hz:

$$\boxed{f_c = 9 \sqrt{N_{max}} \;\; \text{Hz}}$$

Thus, the critical frequency is directly proportional to the square root of the peak electron density of the reflecting layer.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the "Skip Distance" in ionospheric propagation? Mention the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Skip Distance** ($D_s$) is the **minimum ground distance** from the transmitter at which the sky wave, reflected from the ionosphere, returns to the Earth. Within this distance from the transmitter, the sky wave does not return, and the ground wave may also be too weak — creating a **dead zone** (or **skip zone**).

**Factors on which Skip Distance depends:**

1. **Operating frequency** ($f$): Higher $f$ → larger $D_s$.
2. **Critical frequency** ($f_c$) of the reflecting layer: Higher $f_c$ → smaller $D_s$.
3. **Virtual height** ($h'$) of the reflecting layer: Higher $h'$ → larger $D_s$.
4. **Angle of radiation** (elevation angle $\Delta$): Lower elevation → larger $D_s$.
5. **Ionospheric conditions** (solar activity, time of day, season).

The relationship is:

$$D_s = 2h'\sqrt{\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1}$$

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of the ionosphere. Discuss the formation mechanism and characteristic features of each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A radio wave of frequency 12 MHz is transmitted at an angle of 60° to the vertical. If the critical frequency of the F2 layer is 8 MHz, calculate:
   (i) The maximum usable frequency (MUF) for this path.
   (ii) The skip distance, if the virtual height is 350 km.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Structure of the Ionosphere and Formation Mechanism:**

The ionosphere is the ionized region of the Earth's upper atmosphere, extending from **60 km to about 1000 km** above the surface. It is formed by the **photoionization** of neutral atmospheric gases (mainly $O_2$, $N_2$, $O$) due to **solar ultraviolet (UV) and X-ray radiation**.

The ionosphere is divided into four layers based on altitude and electron density profile:

**1. D Layer (60–90 km):**
- **Formation**: Ionized by hard X-rays and Lyman-$\alpha$ radiation.
- **Electron density**: $\sim 10^9$ electrons/m³.
- **Exists**: Only during the day; disappears at night due to recombination.
- **Role**: Acts as an **absorber** of MF and HF waves (high collision frequency causes energy loss).

**2. E Layer (100–150 km):**
- **Formation**: Ionized by soft X-rays and UV.
- **Electron density**: $\sim 10^{11}$ electrons/m³.
- **Exists**: Stronger in day; weakly present at night.
- **Role**: Reflects HF waves at the lower end of the band.

**3. F Layer (150–400 km):**
- Splits into **F1 (150–250 km)** and **F2 (250–400 km)** during the day.
- **F1 Layer**: Day-only; density $\sim 10^{11}$–$10^{12}$ electrons/m³.
- **F2 Layer**: Persistent day and night; **peak electron density** $\sim 10^{12}$ electrons/m³ — the **most important layer** for long-distance HF communication.

**4. Sporadic E ($E_s$):** Irregular, dense patches of ionization in the E-layer region that can occasionally reflect VHF signals.

Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for visual representation.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- Operating frequency $f = 12$ MHz = $12 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Angle to vertical $\theta_i = 60°$
- Critical frequency $f_c = 8$ MHz = $8 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Virtual height $h' = 350$ km = $3.5 \times 10^5$ m

**(i) Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF):**

Using the secant law:

$$f_{MUF} = \frac{f_c}{\cos \theta_i} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{\cos 60°} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{0.5}$$

$$\boxed{f_{MUF} = 16 \times 10^6 \;\; \text{Hz} = 16 \;\; \text{MHz}}$$

**Verification using alternative form:** We need to compute ground distance $d$ first:
$$d = h' \tan \theta_i = 350 \times \tan 60° = 350 \times \sqrt{3} = 606.2 \;\; \text{km}$$
$$f_{MUF} = f_c \sqrt{1 + (d/h')^2} = 8 \sqrt{1 + (\tan 60°)^2} = 8 \sec 60° = 16 \;\; \text{MHz} \;\; \checkmark$$

**(ii) Skip Distance:**

$$D_s = 2 h' \sqrt{\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1}$$

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{12}{8} = 1.5$$

$$\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1 = 2.25 - 1 = 1.25$$

$$\sqrt{1.25} = 1.118$$

$$D_s = 2 \times 350 \times 1.118 = 782.6 \;\; \text{km}$$

$$\boxed{D_s \approx 782.6 \;\; \text{km}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **Faraday Rotation** in ionospheric propagation. Discuss its causes and effects on satellite communication links.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sky-wave signal reflected from the F2 layer has a virtual height of 300 km. If the critical frequency of the layer is 6 MHz, determine:
   (i) The maximum electron density of the layer.
   (ii) The MUF for a ground distance of 1200 km.
   (iii) The skip distance for an operating frequency of 10 MHz.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Faraday Rotation:**

When a linearly polarized electromagnetic wave passes through the ionosphere in the presence of the **Earth's magnetic field**, the plane of polarization **rotates** progressively along the propagation path. This phenomenon is known as **Faraday Rotation**.

**Cause:**
- The Earth's magnetic field makes the ionosphere an **anisotropic (magneto-ionic) medium**.
- A linearly polarized wave can be decomposed into two circularly polarized components (left-hand and right-hand).
- These two components experience **different refractive indices** in the magnetized plasma, leading to a differential phase shift.
- Upon recombination at the receiver, the resultant linear polarization is rotated by an angle $\Omega$:

$$\Omega = \frac{e^3}{2 \epsilon_0 m^2 c f^2} \int_0^{path} N_e \, B \cos\theta \, ds$$

Where $\theta$ is the angle between the propagation direction and $\vec{B}$, and $B$ is the magnetic flux density.

**Numerical constant form:**

$$\Omega \approx \frac{2.36 \times 10^4}{f^2} \int N_e B \cos\theta \, ds \;\; \text{radians}$$

**Effects on Satellite Communication:**
1. **Polarization mismatch loss**: A linearly polarized receiving antenna may suffer deep fades as the polarization rotates.
2. **Mitigation**: Use **circular polarization** (RHCP or LHCP) at both transmitter and receiver — immune to Faraday rotation.
3. **Worst affected**: Lower frequencies (VHF, lower UHF) at GHz frequencies (~1–3 GHz), the rotation is small (few degrees).
4. **Solar activity** can dramatically increase rotation angle during geomagnetic storms.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- Virtual height $h' = 300$ km
- Critical frequency $f_c = 6$ MHz = $6 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Ground distance $d = 1200$ km (for part ii)
- Operating frequency $f = 10$ MHz (for part iii)

**(i) Maximum Electron Density:**

$$N_{max} = \left(\frac{f_c}{9}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{6 \times 10^6}{9}\right)^2$$

$$= (6.667 \times 10^5)^2 = 4.44 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{electrons/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{N_{max} \approx 4.44 \times 10^{11} \;\; \text{electrons/m}^3}$$

**(ii) MUF for ground distance of 1200 km:**

$$f_{MUF} = f_c \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{d}{h'}\right)^2}$$

$$\frac{d}{h'} = \frac{1200}{300} = 4$$

$$1 + (4)^2 = 17$$

$$\sqrt{17} = 4.123$$

$$f_{MUF} = 6 \times 4.123 = 24.74 \;\; \text{MHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{MUF} \approx 24.74 \;\; \text{MHz}}$$

**(iii) Skip Distance for $f = 10$ MHz:**

$$D_s = 2h' \sqrt{\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2 - 1}$$

$$\frac{f}{f_c} = \frac{10}{6} = 1.667$$

$$\left(\frac{f}{f_c}\right)^2 = 2.778$$

$$\sqrt{2.778 - 1} = \sqrt{1.778} = 1.333$$

$$D_s = 2 \times 300 \times 1.333 = 800 \;\; \text{km}$$

$$\boxed{D_s = 800 \;\; \text{km}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Students frequently use $N_e$ in cm⁻³ and forget to convert to m⁻³ — leading to $f_c$ off by a factor of 1000. ALWAYS use **electrons/m³**.
> 2. **Angle Confusion**: $\theta_i$ in the secant law is the angle from the **vertical (normal)**, NOT from the horizontal. The elevation angle $\Delta = 90° - \theta_i$. Verify before substitution.
> 3. **Vertical vs. Oblique**: Critical frequency formula $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ is **only for vertical incidence**. For oblique paths, you MUST apply the secant law.
> 4. **D-Layer Absorption Forgotten**: Students often state that "higher frequencies are better for ionospheric propagation." This is FALSE above MUF (wave passes through) but TRUE below LUF (gets absorbed). Operating window is between LUF and MUF.
> 5. **Virtual Height ≠ Real Height**: Virtual height is the height of an equivalent sharp reflector. The actual refraction occurs over a finite thickness of the layer.
> 6. **Skip Distance = Minimum distance**: Some students confuse it with maximum distance per hop. Each hop covers up to its skip distance.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ionospheric Propagation — Last-Minute Revision Bullets:**

- 🌐 **Ionosphere altitude range**: **60 km to 1000 km**; formed by solar UV/X-ray ionization.
- 📊 **Layer order (altitude ↑)**: **D → E → F1 → F2**.
- 🌞 **D-layer**: Day-only; **absorber** of MF/HF.
- 🌙 **F2-layer**: Exists day & night; **primary HF reflector**; $N_{max} \sim 10^{12}$ /m³.
- 🧮 **Plasma frequency formula**: $f_p = 9\sqrt{N_e}$ Hz (with $N_e$ in m⁻³) — **MUST remember constant 9**.
- 🪞 **Critical frequency**: $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ — **vertical incidence** only.
- 📐 **Secant Law**: $f_{MUF} = f_c \sec\theta_i = f_c \sqrt{1 + (d/h')^2}$.
- 📏 **Virtual height**: $h' = c \cdot \Delta t / 2$; measured by ionosonde.
- 🛰️ **Skip distance**: $D_s = 2h'\sqrt{(f/f_c)^2 - 1}$ — minimum distance for sky-wave reception.
- 📡 **OWF**: $\approx 0.85 \times MUF$.
- 🧲 **Faraday rotation**: $\Omega \propto \int N_e B \cos\theta \, ds / f^2$.
- 🎵 **Whistlers**: VLF waves guided along magnetic field lines; descending-tone audio.
- 🌗 **Solar cycle**: 11-year cycle; sunspot number affects $N_{max}$ significantly.
- ⏰ **Day vs Night**: D and F1 disappear at night; F2 persists with reduced density.
- 🔁 **Multi-hop**: Long distances use multiple reflections between ionosphere and ground.
- 📉 **D-layer absorption**: $\alpha \propto N_e / f^2$ — explains why LUF exists.

### 🎯 Common KTU Exam Question Patterns:

1. **Numerical on $f_p$, $f_c$, $N_{max}$** — using $f = 9\sqrt{N_e}$.
2. **Numerical on Secant Law / MUF** — given $f_c$, $\theta_i$, $h'$.
3. **Numerical on Skip Distance** — given $f$, $f_c$, $h'$.
4. **Derivation of plasma frequency** — from electron equation of motion.
5. **Short notes on layers, Faraday rotation, whistlers, sporadic E.**
6. **Compare ground wave, sky wave, space wave** propagation.
7. **Effect of solar cycle on HF communication.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Earth's Magnetic Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT865 - ANTENNA THEORY AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Effects of Earth's Magnetic Field # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Earth's Magnetic Field** is a complex, three-dimensional geomagnetic field produced primarily by the geodynamo action of molten iron in Earth's outer core, with secondary contributions from ionospheric currents and magnetospheric plasma. It is conventionally described by the geomagnetic flux density vector $\vec{B}$ whose magnitude at the surface is approximately **$B_0 \approx 50 \, \mu T$ (0.5 Gauss)** at the equator and increases to about **$60\text{--}70 \, \mu T$** at the magnetic poles. In **antenna theory and electromagnetic wave propagation**, the Earth's magnetic field transforms the ionospheric plasma (a free-electron medium) into a **magnetized plasma** or **gyrotropic medium**. In such a medium, the propagation characteristics of a plane electromagnetic wave become **anisotropic** — they depend on the direction of wave propagation relative to the steady magnetic field vector $\vec{B_0}$. This anisotropy is the origin of phenomena like **Faraday rotation, wave splitting (Ordinary and Extraordinary modes), and polarization changes**, all of which critically impact the radiation pattern, polarization purity, and direction-finding accuracy of antennas operating in the **HF, VHF, and lower UHF bands**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > This topic is evaluated under **Module 4 — Radio Wave Propagation & Antennas in Realistic Environments**. It directly tests a student's understanding of *ionospheric physics*, *magneto-ionic theory*, and their implications for **antenna radiation pattern distortion** and **polarization mismatch loss**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Magnetic Crystal" Picture Imagine throwing a pebble into a still pond — the ripples spread out uniformly in concentric circles because water is the same in every direction (**isotropic medium**). Now imagine throwing the same pebble into a pond filled with countless spinning tops, all spinning about the same vertical axis. Depending on whether the wave vibrates *with* or *against* the spinning direction, the wave will travel at *different speeds*. The medium no longer treats all directions equally — it is **anisotropic**. The **Earth's magnetic field acts exactly like that vertical spinning axis**: it forces every free electron in the ionosphere to **gyrate (spiral) around the magnetic field lines** at a characteristic frequency called the **gyrofrequency** $f_H \approx 1.4 \, \text{MHz}$ (for B = 50 µT). When a radio wave arrives, the electrons' spiral motion interacts with the wave's electric field in two distinct ways, splitting it into two characteristic modes — the **Ordinary (O) wave** and the **Extraordinary (X) wave** — that travel with slightly different velocities. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used in this Module:** > - $B_0$ (Surface geomagnetic flux density) $\approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$ T > - $f_H$ (Electron gyrofrequency at equator) $\approx 1.4$ MHz > - $f_p$ (Typical ionospheric plasma frequency) $\approx 3\text{--}15$ MHz > - $e$ (Electron charge) = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C > - $m$ (Electron mass) = $9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg > - $\varepsilon_0$ (Free-space permittivity) = $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mechanism — How $\vec{B_0}$ Modifies Wave Propagation When a plane EM wave enters the ionospheric plasma, the free electrons are forced into oscillation by the wave's electric field $\vec{E}$. The motion of each electron in the presence of the static magnetic field $\vec{B_0}$ is governed by the **Lorentz force equation**: $$m \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -e\vec{E} - e\vec{v} \times \vec{B_0}$$ **Key Physics:** - The first term $-e\vec{E}$ is the wave's driving force. - The second term $-e\vec{v} \times \vec{B_0}$ is the **magnetic restoring force** that couples electron motion to the static $\vec{B_0}$. - This coupling makes the **complex refractive index $n$** a function of the **angle $\theta$** between the wave propagation vector $\vec{k}$ and $\vec{B_0}$. ## 2.2 Magneto-Ionic Parameters (KTU High-Yield Notation) | Symbol | Definition | Mathematical Expression | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | $f$ | Operating frequency (Hz) | — | Wave frequency | | $f_p$ | Plasma frequency | $f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{Ne^2}{\varepsilon_0 m}}$ | Natural oscillation freq. of e⁻ cloud | | $f_H$ | Electron gyrofrequency | $f_H = \frac{eB_0}{2\pi m}$ | Spiral frequency of e⁻ about $\vec{B_0}$ | | $X$ | Normalized plasma parameter | $X = (f_p / f)^2$ | Strength of plasma effect | | $Y$ | Normalized gyro parameter | $Y = f_H / f$ | Strength of magnetic field effect | | $Y_L$ | Longitudinal component of $Y$ | $Y_L = Y \cos\theta$ | Component of $\vec{B_0}$ along $\vec{k}$ | | $Y_T$ | Transverse component of $Y$ | $Y_T = Y \sin\theta$ | Component of $\vec{B_0}$ perpendicular to $\vec{k}$ | | $Z$ | Collision parameter | $Z = \nu / \omega$ | Loss term due to e⁻ collisions | | $\theta$ | Angle between $\vec{k}$ and $\vec{B_0}$ | $0 \le \theta \le \pi$ | Propagation direction w.r.t. field | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Observation:** In the limit $B_0 \rightarrow 0$ (or $Y \rightarrow 0$), the magneto-ionic formula reduces to the **simple plasma formula** $n^2 = 1 - X$. This confirms that **the Earth's magnetic field is the sole cause of anisotropy**. ## 2.3 Appleton-Hartree Formula (The Master Equation) The **complete magneto-ionic dispersion relation** is the **Appleton-Hartree Formula**: $$n^2 = 1 - \frac{X}{1 - iZ - \frac{Y_T^2}{2(1 - X - iZ)} \pm \sqrt{\frac{Y_T^4}{4(1 - X - iZ)^2} + Y_L^2}}$$ **Two Refractive Indices → Two Modes:** - **"−" sign (in the $\pm$)** $\rightarrow$ **Ordinary (O) wave**, refractive index $n_O$ - **"+" sign (in the $\pm$)** $\rightarrow$ **Extraordinary (X) wave**, refractive index $n_X$ ## 2.4 Limiting Cases (Extremely Important for KTU) ### Case A: Quasi-Longitudinal (QL) Propagation ($\theta \approx 0$) When the wave travels nearly *along* $\vec{B_0}$, the formula reduces to: $$n_{O,X}^2 \approx 1 - \frac{X}{1 \pm Y_L} = 1 - \frac{X}{1 \pm Y\cos\theta}$$ This is the **dominant regime for sky-wave HF propagation** over long distances. ### Case B: Quasi-Transverse (QT) Propagation ($\theta \approx \pi/2$) When the wave travels *perpendicular* to $\vec{B_0}$: $$n_{O,X}^2 \approx 1 - \frac{X}{1 - \frac{Y_T^2}{2(1 - X)}} \quad \text{or} \quad 1 - X$$ This applies to **trans-equatorial and high-latitude propagation**. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Theory | Engineering Application | How $\vec{B_0}$ Affects It | |---|---| | **HF Sky-wave Communication** | Determines usable frequency, MUF, and polarization rotation | | **Over-the-Horizon Radar (OTHR)** | Beam direction is bent due to anisotropic refraction | | **Satellite Communication (UHF/SHF)** | Faraday rotation depolarizes the signal; mitigated by circular polarization | | **Direction-Finding Antennas** | Bearing errors of several degrees due to wave tilting | | **Space-weather Monitoring** | Ionospheric storms alter $B_0$-induced effects | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why should an antenna engineer care about $\vec{B_0}$?** > Even though the static magnetic field does **not** appear in Maxwell's curl equations at the antenna terminals, it **critically alters the boundary conditions and propagation medium** surrounding the antenna. An antenna designed assuming an isotropic (free-space) medium can suffer **polarization mismatch loss up to 3 dB** when deployed in an ionospheric link where Faraday rotation is active. ## 2.6 Critical Frequency and MUF At **vertical incidence**, the maximum usable plasma frequency for O-wave reflection is: $$f_c = f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N_{max}\, e^2}{\varepsilon_0 m}} \approx 9 \sqrt{N_{max}} \text{ Hz}$$ where $N_{max}$ is the peak electron density in $\text{m}^{-3}$. The **Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF)** at oblique incidence follows the **Secant Law**: $$\text{MUF} = f_c \cdot \sec \phi_0$$ where $\phi_0$ is the angle of incidence at the ionospheric layer base. ## 2.7 Faraday Rotation (Net Polarization Twist) For **longitudinal propagation** through the ionosphere, the plane of polarization rotates by: $$\Omega = \frac{e^3 B_0}{2 \varepsilon_0 m^2 c \omega^2} \int_{\text{path}} N(s)\, ds \quad \text{(radians)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Consequence:** A linearly polarized wave transmitted from a ground antenna arrives at the receiver **rotated** by $\Omega$. The receiving antenna sees a polarization mismatch loss factor of $\cos^2 \Omega$. This is why **circularly polarized antennas** are preferred for satellite and ionospheric links. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Electron Gyrofrequency **Statement:** A free electron in the ionospheric plasma, when disturbed, gyrates around a static magnetic field line $\vec{B_0}$ with a characteristic angular frequency $\omega_H = eB_0/m$. ### Step 1 — Equation of Motion in a Static Field (No Wave) When there is **no incident wave** ($\vec{E} = 0$), the only force on the electron is the magnetic Lorentz force: $$m \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B_0})$$ ### Step 2 — Choose a Convenient Geometry Let $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$. The cross product gives: $$\vec{v} \times \vec{B_0} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{x} & \hat{y} & \hat{z} \\ v_x & v_y & v_z \\ 0 & 0 & B_0 \end{vmatrix} = (v_y B_0)\hat{x} - (v_x B_0)\hat{y} + 0 \cdot \hat{z}$$ ### Step 3 — Write the Component Equations $$\begin{aligned} m \frac{dv_x}{dt} &= +eB_0 v_y \\ m \frac{dv_y}{dt} &= -eB_0 v_x \\ m \frac{dv_z}{dt} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Decouple by Differentiation Differentiating the first equation and substituting the second: $$m \frac{d^2 v_x}{dt^2} = eB_0 \frac{dv_y}{dt} = eB_0 \left(-\frac{eB_0}{m} v_x\right) = -\frac{e^2 B_0^2}{m^2} \cdot m \, v_x$$ ### Step 5 — Identify the Simple Harmonic Motion $$\frac{d^2 v_x}{dt^2} + \left(\frac{eB_0}{m}\right)^2 v_x = 0$$ This is SHM with angular frequency: $$\boxed{\omega_H = \frac{eB_0}{m}}$$ In Hz: $$f_H = \frac{\omega_H}{2\pi} = \frac{eB_0}{2\pi m}$$ ### Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation for the Earth Substituting $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $m = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $B_0 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ T: $$f_H = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{-5})}{2\pi (9.11 \times 10^{-31})} = \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-24}}{5.724 \times 10^{-30}}$$ $$f_H \approx 1.4 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz} = 1.4 \, \text{MHz}$$ > **Conclusion:** The electron spirals around $\vec{B_0}$ at approximately **1.4 MHz** in equatorial regions. This sets the **characteristic scale** below which the Earth's magnetic field has a dominant effect on radio wave propagation. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Plasma Frequency **Statement:** A neutral plasma slab, when displaced, oscillates collectively at the plasma frequency $f_p$. ### Step 1 — Model the Plasma Consider a slab of electrons with density $N$ (per $\text{m}^3$) displaced by a small distance $\Delta x$ from a fixed ion background. This creates a **surface charge density** $\sigma = Ne\Delta x$ on each face. ### Step 2 — Calculate the Restoring Electric Field By Gauss's law, the field between the displaced layers is: $$E = \frac{Ne\,\Delta x}{\varepsilon_0}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law The force per electron is $-eE$: $$m \frac{d^2 (\Delta x)}{dt^2} = -\frac{Ne^2}{\varepsilon_0} \Delta x$$ ### Step 4 — Identify the Oscillation Frequency $$\frac{d^2 (\Delta x)}{dt^2} + \frac{Ne^2}{\varepsilon_0 m}\, \Delta x = 0$$ $$\boxed{\omega_p = \sqrt{\frac{Ne^2}{\varepsilon_0 m}} \quad ; \quad f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{Ne^2}{\varepsilon_0 m}}}$$ ### Step 5 — Practical Form Substituting physical constants: $$f_p \approx 9 \sqrt{N} \quad \text{(Hz, with N in m}^{-3}\text{)}$$ > **Example:** For the F2 layer with peak $N_{max} \approx 10^{12}\,\text{m}^{-3}$, $f_p \approx 9 \times 10^6 = 9$ MHz. Waves with $f > 9$ MHz **pass through**; waves with $f < 9$ MHz are **reflected** back to Earth. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Faraday Rotation **Statement:** A linearly polarized wave traveling *parallel* to $\vec{B_0}$ through a magnetized plasma undergoes a progressive rotation of its plane of polarization by an angle $\Omega$ proportional to the line-integral of electron density. ### Step 1 — Decompose the Linear Wave into Two Circular Modes Any linear polarization can be expressed as a superposition of **left-hand circular (LHC)** and **right-hand circular (RHC)** components. ### Step 2 — Refractive Indices for QL Propagation Under the quasi-longitudinal approximation: $$n_L^2 = 1 - \frac{X}{1 - Y} \quad ; \quad n_R^2 = 1 - \frac{X}{1 + Y}$$ where the subscripts refer to the sense of rotation relative to $\vec{B_0}$. ### Step 3 — Phase Velocities Differ The two circular modes travel with **different phase velocities** $v_p = c/n$. After traversing a path of physical length $L$: $$\text{Phase lag of LHC} = \frac{\omega}{c} n_L L \quad ; \quad \text{Phase lag of RHC} = \frac{\omega}{c} n_R L$$ ### Step 4 — Relative Phase Shift Causes Plane of Polarization to Rotate The plane of polarization rotates by **half** the relative phase shift: $$\Omega = \frac{\omega L}{2c}(n_L - n_R)$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $n_L$ and $n_R$ (Binomial Approximation for $X, Y \ll 1$) Using $\sqrt{1 - \varepsilon} \approx 1 - \varepsilon/2$: $$n_L - n_R \approx \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{X}{1+Y} - \frac{X}{1-Y}\right] \cdot (-1) \cdot (-1) \approx \frac{XY}{1-Y^2} \approx XY$$ Thus: $$\Omega \approx \frac{\omega L}{2c} \cdot \frac{f_p^2 f_H}{f^3} \cdot \frac{1}{f} = \frac{\omega L}{2c} \cdot \frac{XY}{1}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute $X$ and $Y$ in SI Units $$X = \frac{f_p^2}{f^2} = \frac{Ne^2}{\varepsilon_0 m \omega^2} \quad ; \quad Y = \frac{f_H}{f} = \frac{eB_0}{2\pi m f}$$ Multiplying and simplifying: $$\Omega = \frac{e^3 B_0}{2 \varepsilon_0 m^2 c \omega^2} \int_0^L N(s)\, ds$$ > **Result:** Faraday rotation is directly proportional to **$B_0$** and the **Total Electron Content (TEC)** $\int N\,ds$ along the propagation path. This is why **GPS satellites** transmit circularly polarized signals at L-band (1.575 GHz) — to make the link immune to this $B_0$-induced rotation. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of the Critical Frequency (Snell's Law Applied) **Statement:** A wave incident vertically on the ionosphere is reflected at the height where $n = 0$. ### Step 1 — Start with the Refractive Index (no $\vec{B_0}$, simplified case) $$n^2 = 1 - \frac{f_p^2(h)}{f^2}$$ where $h$ is the altitude (since $N$ varies with height). ### Step 2 — Reflection Condition For a wave propagating upward, $n^2$ decreases as $N(h)$ increases. Reflection occurs when $n^2 = 0$: $$1 - \frac{f_p^2(h_r)}{f^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = f_p(h_r)$$ ### Step 3 — For a Given Frequency $f$, Reflection Occurs at the Height Where $f_p(h_r) = f$ The **critical frequency** is defined as the lowest frequency that just penetrates the layer at vertical incidence: $$f_c = f_p(N_{max}) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{N_{max}\, e^2}{\varepsilon_0 m}}$$ ### Step 4 — Oblique Incidence (Secant Law) For an oblique angle of incidence $\phi_0$, applying Snell's law $n \sin\phi = \text{const}$ with the condition $f = f_p$: $$\boxed{\text{MUF} = f_c \cdot \sec \phi_0}$$ This is the **classical Secant Law of ionospheric propagation**, valid for a flat Earth approximation. --- ## 3.5 Summary of Derived Results | # | Quantity | Final Formula | KTU Marks Weight | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gyrofrequency | $f_H = eB_0/(2\pi m)$ | High (2-mark derivations) | | 2 | Plasma frequency | $f_p = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{Ne^2/(\varepsilon_0 m)}$ | Very High | | 3 | Appleton formula (QL) | $n^2 = 1 - X/(1 \pm Y_L)$ | Very High | | 4 | Critical frequency | $f_c = f_p(N_{max})$ | High | | 5 | MUF (secant law) | $\text{MUF} = f_c \sec\phi_0$ | Medium | | 6 | Faraday rotation | $\Omega = (e^3 B_0 / 2\varepsilon_0 m^2 c \omega^2) \int N\,ds$ | High (Part B 14-mark) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture: EM Wave Interaction with Earth's Magnetized Ionosphere ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Transmitter Antenna
Linear or Circular Polarized] --> B[EM Wave Travels
Through Neutral Atmosphere

90 to 150 km

toward Earth

travel different paths] F -- Yes --> H[Wave Penetrates Ionosphere
and Escapes into Space] G --> I[F2 Layer Acts as Mirror
Wave Returns to Earth] I --> J[During Return Passage

No Sky-wave Reception] style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style E fill:#FF8080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style J fill:#80C0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style N fill:#FF9090,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style O fill:#80FF80,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Decomposition of the Magneto-Ionic Process ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputBlock [Input Stage] In1[Operating Frequency f] --> Proc1 In2[Electron Density N at altitude h] --> Proc1 In3[Static Magnetic Field B0] --> Proc1 In4[Angle theta between k and B0] --> Proc1 end Proc1[Compute Magneto-Ionic Parameters

at Receiver Antenna] end style Proc1 fill:#FFE0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Proc2 fill:#A0E0FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Proc3 fill:#FFA0A0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Out3 fill:#C0FFC0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Out5 fill:#FFC0C0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Polarization State Evolution (LHC vs RHC) under $\vec{B_0}$ ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Linearly Polarized EM Wave
