The **right subgraph (dotted arrows)** shows the *equivalent uniform* model that produces the same terminal EMF. Virtual Height is the bridge between them. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Current Distributions and Their Virtual Heights ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["HERTZIAN DIPOLE"] A1["Uniform Current"] A2["h_v = l"] end subgraph B["SHORT DIPOLE"] B1["Triangular Current"] B2["h_v = l divided by 2"] end subgraph C["HALF WAVE DIPOLE"] C1["Sinusoidal Current"] C2["h_v = lambda divided by pi"] end subgraph D["SMALL LOOP N TURNS"] D1["Uniform Aperture Field"] D2["h_v = 2 pi N A divided by lambda"] end A1 --> A2 B1 --> B2 C1 --> C2 D1 --> D2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: EMF Calculation Workflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Wave as Incident Wave participant Ant as Real Antenna participant Cur as Current Distribution participant Vh as Virtual Height Calculator participant Vm as Virtual Model Antenna participant Ld as Load Wave->>Ant: E field V per m arrives Ant->>Cur: Induces I of z Cur->>Vh: Send I of z profile Vh->>Vh: Compute h_v = integral I dz / I0 Vh->>Vm: Equivalent uniform antenna of length h_v Wave->>Vm: E field V per m arrives Vm->>Ld: EMF equals E times h_v Note over Vh,Vm: Both paths produce IDENTICAL terminal EMF Ld-->>Vh: Confirm received power matches ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram: Virtual Height as a Function of Antenna Length ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Hertzian: l much less than lambda Hertzian --> ShortDipole: l increases but still much less than lambda ShortDipole --> ResonantDipole: l approaches lambda divided by 2 ResonantDipole --> FullWave: l equals lambda FullWave --> LongDipole: l greater than lambda Hertzian: Hertzian Dipole h_v equals l ShortDipole: Short Dipole h_v equals l divided by 2 ResonantDipole: Half Wave h_v equals lambda divided by pi FullWave: Full Wave h_v equals 2 lambda divided by pi LongDipole: Long Dipole h_v oscillates with l divided by lambda ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Virtual Height" of a receiving antenna. State its significance.** #### Model Answer (3 marks): **Definition (2 marks):** The Virtual Height $h_v$ of a receiving antenna is defined as the ratio of the **open-circuit EMF** ($\varepsilon_{oc}$) induced at the antenna terminals to the **magnitude of the incident electric field** ($E$) at the antenna, when the antenna is oriented for maximum reception: $$h_v \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{oc}}{E} \quad [\text{meters}]$$ **Significance (1 mark):** It represents the **effective length** of the antenna assuming a *uniform current distribution* equal to the terminal current. It enables direct computation of received EMF via $\varepsilon = E \cdot h_v$ and links to the effective aperture $A_e$ through $A_e = h_v^2 \eta / (4 R_{rad})$. --- ### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Why is the virtual height of a short dipole equal to half its physical length?** #### Model Answer (3 marks): The current distribution along a short dipole is **triangular** (linear taper) with peak value $I_0$ at the center and zero at the ends (1 mark). The Virtual Height is the **area under this triangular current profile** normalized by $I_0$: $$h_v \;=\; \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} I_0 \left(1 - \frac{2\vert z \vert}{l}\right) dz \;=\; \frac{l}{2} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$ A triangle has exactly **half the area** of the enclosing rectangle of the same base and peak, so $h_v = l/2$ (1 mark). Physically, the **average current** along the antenna is $I_0/2$, so only half the physical length effectively contributes to the EMF. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the **virtual height of a short dipole antenna** of physical length $l$. Explain the geometric meaning of the result. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **half-wave dipole** is operating at 100 MHz in a region where the incident plane wave has an electric field intensity of **10 mV/m**. Calculate the **(i)** virtual height, **(ii)** open-circuit EMF at the dipole terminals, and **(iii)** maximum power delivered to a matched load. Assume the radiation resistance of the half-wave dipole is **73 Ω**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (1 mark):** State the current distribution of a short dipole. For a center-fed short dipole ($l \ll \lambda$), the current is **linearly tapered**: $$I(z) \;=\; I_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 \vert z \vert}{l}\right), \quad -\frac{l}{2} \leq z \leq +\frac{l}{2}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Write the definition of virtual height: $$h_v \;=\; \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} I(z) \; dz$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute the current: $$h_v \;=\; \frac{1}{I_0} \int_{-l/2}^{+l/2} I_0 \left(1 - \frac{2 \vert z \vert}{l}\right) dz$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Exploit even symmetry and simplify: $$h_v \;=\; 2 \int_{0}^{l/2} \left(1 - \frac{2z}{l}\right) dz \;=\; 2 \left[ z - \frac{z^2}{l} \right]_{0}^{l/2}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Evaluate the limits: $$h_v \;=\; 2 \left[ \frac{l}{2} - \frac{l}{4} \right] \;=\; 2 \cdot \frac{l}{4} \;=\; \frac{l}{2}$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** State the result: $$\boxed{h_v^{\text{short dipole}} \;=\; \frac{l}{2}}$$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** Geometric meaning: The triangular current profile occupies **half the area** of a rectangle of height $I_0$ and base $l$. Hence the effective (uniform-equivalent) length is half the physical length. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 marks **Given:** - $f = 100 \; \text{MHz} \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = c / f = 3 \times 10^8 / 100 \times 10^6 = 3 \; \text{m}$ - $E = 10 \; \text{mV/m} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V/m} = 0.01 \; \text{V/m}$ - $R_{rad} = 73 \; \Omega$ **(i) Virtual Height (2 marks):** $$h_v \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{3}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.9549 \; \text{m}$$ **(ii) Open-Circuit EMF (2 marks):** $$\varepsilon_{oc} \;=\; E \cdot h_v \;=\; 0.01 \times 0.9549 \;=\; 9.549 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V} \;\approx\; 9.55 \; \text{mV}$$ **(iii) Maximum Power Delivered to Matched Load (3 marks):** $$P_{r,\text{max}} \;=\; \frac{E^2 \, h_v^2}{4 R_{rad}} \;=\; \frac{(0.01)^2 \times (0.9549)^2}{4 \times 73}$$ $$P_{r,\text{max}} \;=\; \frac{10^{-4} \times 0.9118}{292} \;=\; \frac{9.118 \times 10^{-5}}{292} \;\approx\; 3.12 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{P_{r,\text{max}} \;\approx\; 0.312 \; \mu \text{W}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the **virtual height of a small N-turn circular loop antenna** of radius $a$ operating at wavelength $\lambda$. Hence compute the virtual height of a 10-turn loop of radius 0.5 m at 30 MHz. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **concept of effective length (virtual height) of a receiving antenna**. Discuss how it differs from the physical length and state **two factors** on which it depends. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 marks **Step 1 (2 marks):** For a small loop ($a \ll \lambda$) in a uniform incident field $E$, the induced EMF per turn follows from Faraday's law. The flux through the loop is $\Phi = B A \cos(\omega t)$ with $B = E/c$: $$\varepsilon_{\text{turn}} \;=\; N \cdot \omega B A \;=\; N \cdot \frac{2\pi f}{c} E A \;=\; \frac{2\pi N A}{\lambda} \cdot E$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Since $\varepsilon_{oc} = E \cdot h_v$, comparing: $$h_v \;=\; \frac{2\pi N A}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2\pi^2 N a^2}{\lambda}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Given: $N = 10$, $a = 0.5 \; \text{m}$, $f = 30 \; \text{MHz}$, so $\lambda = 3 \times 10^8 / 30 \times 10^6 = 10 \; \text{m}$. **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting: $$h_v \;=\; \frac{2\pi^2 \times 10 \times (0.5)^2}{10} \;=\; \frac{2\pi^2 \times 10 \times 0.25}{10} \;=\; 0.5 \pi^2$$ $$h_v \;\approx\; 0.5 \times 9.8696 \;\approx\; 4.935 \; \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{h_v^{\text{loop}} \;\approx\; 4.94 \; \text{m}}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Note: The loop's *physical* size is $2a = 1$ m, yet the *virtual height* is ~4.94 m — the loop effectively behaves like a wire antenna **~5 times longer than its physical extent**. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 marks **Diagram (2 marks):** A labelled figure showing an incident plane wave of field strength $E$ striking a receiving antenna with non-uniform current $I(z)$, and alongside it an *equivalent virtual* antenna of length $h_v$ with uniform current $I_0$, both producing identical terminal EMF. **Concept (2 marks):** The **effective length (virtual height)** $h_v$ of a receiving antenna is the length of an *equivalent* linearly polarized antenna with **uniform current distribution** (equal to the feed-point current $I_0$) that would deliver the **same open-circuit EMF** to a load as the actual antenna in the same incident field. **Difference from Physical Length (1 mark):** - **Physical length $l$** is the actual geometric extent of the conductor. - **Virtual height $h_v$** is the *electromagnetically effective* length accounting for the actual current distribution. - They are equal **only** for an Hertzian dipole. For all other antennas, $h_v < l$ (e.g., short dipole $h_v = l/2$). **Two Factors on Which $h_v$ Depends (2 marks):** 1. **Geometry of the antenna** (length, shape, area for loops). 2. **Current distribution** along the antenna, which is itself a function of **frequency** (wavelength $\lambda$) and **feed-point configuration**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Virtual Height Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing $h_v$ with $l$ for a short dipole (2 marks lost):** Students often write $h_v = l$ for any dipole. Always check whether the current is uniform (Hertzian) or triangular (short dipole). > > 2. **Forgetting the $N$ factor for multi-turn loops (2 marks lost):** The formula $h_v = 2\pi N A / \lambda$ requires the number of turns $N$. Single-turn formulas will be marked down. > > 3. **Using $E$ in wrong units (1 mark lost):** $E$ must be in **V/m**, not V or mV/m, when computing EMF in volts. Always convert: $10 \; \text{mV/m} = 0.01 \; \text{V/m}$. > > 4. **Ignoring the $\pi$ factor for half-wave dipole (2 marks lost):** The correct result is $h_v = \lambda/\pi \approx 0.318 \lambda$, **not** $\lambda/2$. Show the integral step explicitly. > > 5. **Sign error in delivered power formula (1 mark lost):** The denominator is $4 R_{rad}$, not $2 R_{rad}$ or $R_{rad}$ alone. This comes from the voltage-divider factor of 1/2 across the matched load. > > 6. **Mixing up Virtual Height with Effective Aperture (2 marks lost):** $h_v$ has units of meters; $A_e$ has units of square meters. They are related by $A_e = h_v^2 \eta / (4 R_{rad})$ — do not interchange them. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Virtual Height (Module 4, PEECT865)** - ☐ **Core Definition:** $h_v = \varepsilon_{oc} / E$ — the equivalent uniform-current length of a receiving antenna. - ☐ **Geometric Meaning:** Area under the normalized current distribution curve. - ☐ **Hertzian Dipole:** $h_v = l$ (current is uniform). - ☐ **Short Dipole:** $h_v = l/2$ (triangular current — half-area argument). - ☐ **Half-Wave Dipole:** $h_v = \lambda/\pi \approx 0.318\lambda$ (sinusoidal current). - ☐ **Full-Wave Dipole:** $h_v = 2\lambda/\pi$ (longer, but still less than physical length). - ☐ **Small Loop Antenna:** $h_v = 2\pi N A / \lambda$ (scales with area and turn count, inversely with wavelength). - ☐ **Received EMF:** $\varepsilon_{oc} = E \cdot h_v$ — direct, exam-favorite formula. - ☐ **Max Power to Matched Load:** $P_{r,\max} = E^2 h_v^2 / (4 R_{rad})$. - ☐ **Effective Aperture Link:** $A_e = h_v^2 \eta / (4 R_{rad})$, with $\eta = 120\pi \; \Omega$. - ☐ **Dependence:** $h_v$ depends on **antenna geometry**, **current distribution**, and **frequency (wavelength)**. - ☐ **Practical Use:** Computing received EMF, link budget analysis, RCS, EMC testing. - ☐ **Units Vigilance:** $E$ in V/m, $h_v$ in m, $\varepsilon$ in V, $R_{rad}$ in $\Omega$, $P_r$ in W. - ☐ **Symmetry Trick:** Always use $h_v = (2/I_0) \int_0^{l/2} I(z) dz$ for symmetric current distributions. - ☐ **Validity Condition:** Formulas for small loops and short dipoles require $l, a \ll \lambda$.
